8.1.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.1.title: Board Approval of Technology Risk Appetite
8.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the board actively oversees technology risk rather than merely receiving information passively. They look for evidence of board-level challenge, dedicated agenda time, approved budgets, and integration of technology risk into the enterprise risk management framework.

evidenceItems.name: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes show specific technology risk agenda items (not buried under AOB)
Evidence of board members asking questions and challenging management presentations
Clear action items with assigned owners and deadlines arising from technology risk discussions
Documented board decisions on technology risk matters such as budget approvals or risk acceptance
commonGaps: Technology risk mentioned only as a footnote or information item with no recorded discussion
No evidence of board members engaging, questioning, or challenging management
Missing action items or follow-up tracking from previous board technology risk discussions
Minutes are generic and do not reflect substantive deliberation on specific technology risks
suggestedSources: Board secretariat records
Board portal or collaboration platform (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect)
Company secretary archives

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Technology risk is explicitly identified as a distinct risk category within the ERM framework
Clear linkages between technology risk and other risk categories such as operational, credit, and reputational risk
Defined risk taxonomy that includes technology-specific risk types such as cyber risk, data risk, and system availability risk
Framework references BNM RMiT requirements and maps controls to specific clauses
commonGaps: Technology risk treated as a subset of operational risk without distinct identification or assessment criteria
ERM framework last updated more than 12 months ago with no evidence of annual review
No mapping between technology risk categories and business impact scenarios
Framework does not reference or align with BNM RMiT policy requirements
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management team documentation
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream)
Chief Risk Officer office records

evidenceItems.name: Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
whatGoodLooksLike: Dedicated line items for technology risk management in the annual budget, separate from general IT spending
Board resolution or minutes recording formal approval of the technology risk budget
Budget covers cybersecurity tools, risk personnel, training, third-party assessments, and incident response capabilities
Quarterly variance reports showing actual spend against approved budget presented to the board
commonGaps: Technology risk budget is bundled within general IT or operational budgets with no separate visibility
No formal board approval record for the technology risk budget allocation
Budget does not cover all critical areas such as incident response, training, or third-party risk assessments
No tracking of budget utilisation or variance reporting to the board
suggestedSources: Finance department budget records
Board meeting papers and resolutions
CFO office quarterly financial reports

evidenceItems.name: Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight
evidenceItems.description: Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight
whatGoodLooksLike: Terms of reference explicitly include technology risk oversight as a board responsibility
Defined frequency and format for technology risk reporting to the board
Clear delineation of board versus management responsibilities for technology risk
Terms of reference reviewed and updated within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Terms of reference do not mention technology risk or cybersecurity oversight responsibilities
No defined reporting cadence or format for technology risk matters
Responsibilities for technology risk oversight are vague or conflated with general IT governance
Terms of reference have not been reviewed or updated in more than two years
suggestedSources: Company secretary records
Board governance documentation
Corporate governance framework

auditTips: Request the last 4 quarters of board minutes and verify technology risk is a standing agenda item with dedicated discussion time, not buried under AOB
Cross-reference board-approved technology risk budget with actual spending reports to verify resource allocation matches stated commitments
Check whether the enterprise risk management framework explicitly identifies technology risk as a distinct category with its own assessment criteria
Verify that board terms of reference were reviewed within the last 12 months and explicitly cover technology risk oversight responsibilities
Look for evidence of board challenge by examining whether minutes record questions, disagreements, or requests for additional information from management

8.2.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.2.title: Board Oversight Responsibilities
8.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners verify that a dedicated board-level technology risk committee exists with qualified members, meets at least quarterly, and produces meaningful outcomes. They will scrutinise member competency profiles, attendance records, and whether the committee exercises genuine challenge rather than rubber-stamping management reports.

evidenceItems.name: Board committee charter or terms of reference
evidenceItems.description: Board committee charter or terms of reference
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter clearly defines the committee's mandate covering technology risk, cybersecurity, and IT resilience oversight
Specifies membership composition requirements including minimum technology or cybersecurity expertise
Defines quorum requirements, meeting frequency (at least quarterly), and reporting obligations to the full board
Includes authority to commission independent reviews and access any information necessary for its oversight role
commonGaps: Charter is generic and does not specifically address technology risk or cybersecurity oversight responsibilities
No minimum competency requirements for committee membership specified in the charter
Charter does not define the committee's authority to commission independent assessments or access information
Charter has not been reviewed or updated since initial approval
suggestedSources: Company secretary records
Board governance documentation repository
Corporate governance framework

evidenceItems.name: Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
evidenceItems.description: Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
whatGoodLooksLike: Each member has a documented profile showing technology, cybersecurity, or IT risk management qualifications or experience
Profiles include relevant certifications (e.g., CISSP, CISM, CRISC), academic qualifications, or senior industry experience
Competency gaps are identified with documented plans for addressing them through training or advisory support
Profiles are updated annually or upon membership changes
commonGaps: Committee members lack any documented technology or cybersecurity expertise or experience
Profiles are outdated and do not reflect current competencies or recent training undertaken
No formal competency assessment performed against the requirements of the committee charter
Reliance on a single member for all technology expertise, creating a single point of failure
suggestedSources: Human Resources department records
Company secretary nomination files
Board skills matrix documentation

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual meeting schedule published at the start of each year with at least four meetings planned
Attendance records show consistent quorum achievement across all meetings
Individual member attendance rates are tracked and reported, with minimum attendance thresholds enforced
Ad hoc meetings are convened when material technology risk events occur outside the regular schedule
commonGaps: Fewer than four meetings held in the calendar year without documented justification
Repeated failure to achieve quorum, with meetings proceeding without sufficient attendees
No tracking of individual member attendance rates or consequences for poor attendance
No ad hoc meetings convened despite significant technology incidents occurring during the year
suggestedSources: Company secretary meeting management records
Board portal scheduling system
Committee chairman's office records

evidenceItems.name: Committee meeting minutes and action items
evidenceItems.description: Committee meeting minutes and action items
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes reflect substantive discussion of technology risk topics with specific questions and challenges from committee members
Action items are clearly documented with assigned owners, deadlines, and priority levels
Follow-up on previous action items is a standing agenda item with status updates recorded
Decisions and recommendations to the full board are clearly documented and tracked through to board discussion
commonGaps: Minutes are brief summaries that do not capture the substance of discussions or member challenges
Action items lack assigned owners or deadlines, making follow-up impossible to track
No evidence of tracking or following up on action items from previous meetings
Committee recommendations to the board are not formally documented or tracked
suggestedSources: Company secretary minutes archive
Board portal or document management system
Action item tracking system or register

auditTips: Request the committee charter and verify it explicitly mandates technology risk and cybersecurity oversight with quarterly meeting requirements
Review member profiles against the charter's competency requirements and check for documented plans to address any identified gaps
Count the actual number of meetings held in the past 12 months and verify quorum was achieved at each meeting
Examine the last 4 quarters of meeting minutes for evidence of genuine committee challenge, not just acknowledgement of management reports
Trace at least 3 action items from assignment through to completion or escalation to verify the action tracking process works

8.3.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.3.title: Board-Level Technology Committee
8.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether board members possess or have access to adequate technology and cybersecurity knowledge. They look for structured competency assessments, documented training records, evidence of external advisory engagement, and whether training content is relevant to current threats facing Malaysian financial institutions.

evidenceItems.name: Board training records on technology and cybersecurity topics
evidenceItems.description: Board training records on technology and cybersecurity topics
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual training records maintained for each board member showing dates, topics, duration, and provider
Training topics are current and relevant, covering areas such as cyber threats, cloud risk, data protection, and AI governance
At least two technology-focused training sessions conducted per year for the full board
Training materials are retained alongside attendance records for audit verification
Post-training feedback or knowledge assessments are documented
commonGaps: No formal training records exist; training is claimed but undocumented
Training topics are outdated or generic and do not address current threat landscape or Malaysian regulatory context
Training is conducted only once or irregularly rather than on a sustained annual programme
Some board members have no recorded technology training attendance at all
suggestedSources: Company secretary training administration records
Learning management system (LMS) records
External training provider attendance certificates

evidenceItems.name: External advisor engagement records
evidenceItems.description: External advisor engagement records
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal engagement letters or contracts with qualified technology or cybersecurity advisory firms
Advisor qualifications and credentials are documented and demonstrate relevant expertise
Records of advisory sessions, reports delivered, and recommendations made to the board
Evidence that advisory input influenced board decisions or risk appetite calibration
commonGaps: No external advisors engaged despite identified competency gaps in the board skills matrix
Advisor engagement is informal with no documented terms of reference or deliverables
Advisory recommendations are received but not tracked or acted upon by the board
Advisors lack relevant financial services or cybersecurity credentials
suggestedSources: Procurement or vendor management records
Board secretariat advisory engagement files
External consulting firm reports and invoices

evidenceItems.name: Board competency assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Board competency assessment results
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured assessment covering technology domains such as cybersecurity, cloud computing, data governance, and digital banking risks
Individual results are documented with identified strengths and development areas for each board member
Assessment results are used to inform the annual board training plan and external advisory needs
Year-on-year comparison shows improvement in collective board technology competency
commonGaps: No formal competency assessment conducted; board technology knowledge is assumed rather than measured
Assessment is a generic self-evaluation with no objective measurement criteria
Results are not used to inform training plans or advisory engagement decisions
Assessment has not been updated to reflect new technology risk areas such as AI, open banking, or cloud adoption
suggestedSources: Board Nomination Committee records
Human Resources board development files
External board effectiveness review reports

evidenceItems.name: Annual training plan for board members
evidenceItems.description: Annual training plan for board members
whatGoodLooksLike: Training plan is approved at the start of each year with specific technology and cybersecurity topics scheduled
Plan is informed by the competency assessment results and addresses identified knowledge gaps
Plan includes a mix of internal briefings, external conferences, and specialist workshops
Completion tracking shows actual delivery against the planned schedule
commonGaps: No formal annual training plan exists; training is ad hoc and reactive
Plan exists on paper but actual delivery falls significantly short of planned sessions
Plan does not align with competency assessment findings or current threat landscape
Plan covers only generic governance topics without dedicated technology or cybersecurity content
suggestedSources: Company secretary annual planning documents
Board Nomination Committee development plans
Learning and development department records

auditTips: Request the board skills matrix and verify that technology and cybersecurity competency is explicitly assessed for each individual member
Cross-reference the training plan with actual training records to identify gaps between planned and delivered sessions
Verify that external advisors engaged have relevant qualifications and that their recommendations are traceable to board actions
Check whether training content is current and addresses threats specific to Malaysian financial services, not just generic cybersecurity awareness
Ask for evidence that competency assessment results directly informed the training plan and advisory engagement decisions

8.4.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.4.title: Board Discussion on Technology and Cyber Risk
8.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the technology risk appetite statement contains both quantitative and qualitative measures, has current board approval, is reviewed at least annually, and is operationally embedded through cascaded limits and monitoring. They will test whether threshold breaches trigger defined escalation procedures.

evidenceItems.name: Board-approved technology risk appetite statement
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved technology risk appetite statement
whatGoodLooksLike: Statement explicitly covers cybersecurity, system availability, data integrity, and third-party technology risk domains
Includes both quantitative metrics (e.g., maximum 4 hours RTO for critical systems, fewer than 3 P1 incidents per quarter) and qualitative statements (e.g., zero tolerance for customer data breaches)
Carries formal board approval with documented resolution, signatures, and approval date
Version-controlled with clear effective date and next review date
Aligned with the institution's overall enterprise risk appetite statement
commonGaps: Statement contains only qualitative platitudes without measurable thresholds or metrics
No formal board approval record; statement exists as a management document without board endorsement
Statement has not been reviewed or updated in more than 12 months
Technology risk appetite is not aligned with or cross-referenced to the enterprise risk appetite statement
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management team documentation
Board secretariat resolution records
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer)

evidenceItems.name: Risk appetite metrics and thresholds
evidenceItems.description: Risk appetite metrics and thresholds
whatGoodLooksLike: Each metric has clearly defined green, amber, and red thresholds with specific numerical values
Metrics cover key domains including system uptime, incident frequency, vulnerability remediation timelines, and data loss events
Thresholds are calibrated based on historical performance data and industry benchmarks for Malaysian financial institutions
Metrics are mapped to specific risk appetite statements for traceability
commonGaps: Thresholds are arbitrary and not based on historical data, industry benchmarks, or business impact analysis
Metrics do not cover all technology risk domains referenced in the risk appetite statement
No differentiation between warning (amber) and breach (red) thresholds
Metrics have not been recalibrated despite significant changes in the institution's technology environment
suggestedSources: Risk management department metric definitions
GRC or risk monitoring platform configuration
IT service management platform (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM)

evidenceItems.name: Annual review and approval records
evidenceItems.description: Annual review and approval records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented review process showing what was assessed, what changed, and the rationale for changes or reaffirmation
Evidence that the review considered changes in the threat landscape, business strategy, regulatory requirements, and past incidents
Board meeting minutes or resolution recording formal re-approval of the reviewed risk appetite statement
Clear version history showing progression from previous to current approved version
commonGaps: No evidence of annual review despite the statement being more than 12 months old
Review is perfunctory with no documented analysis of whether thresholds remain appropriate
Review does not consider external factors such as emerging threats, regulatory changes, or industry incidents
Board re-approval is not formally documented in meeting minutes or resolutions
suggestedSources: Board meeting minutes and resolutions
Risk management annual review documentation
Document management system version history

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal communication or memo from the CRO distributing the approved risk appetite statement to all relevant stakeholders
Acknowledgement records showing business unit heads and IT leaders have received and understood the risk appetite
Risk appetite is referenced in operational policies, procedures, and risk assessment templates
Training or briefing sessions conducted to explain risk appetite thresholds and their operational implications
commonGaps: Risk appetite statement resides only in board papers with no evidence of communication to operational teams
No formal acknowledgement process; communication assumed but not documented
Business units and IT teams are unaware of specific thresholds and cannot articulate the institution's technology risk appetite
Risk appetite is not referenced or embedded in operational risk management processes
suggestedSources: Chief Risk Officer office correspondence
Internal communication platform records (email, intranet)
Risk management training records

auditTips: Verify the risk appetite statement contains both quantitative metrics with specific thresholds and qualitative statements, as BNM explicitly requires both
Check the approval date and confirm the statement has been reviewed within the last 12 months with board re-approval documented
Ask three different operational managers to articulate the institution's technology risk appetite to test whether communication has been effective
Request evidence of at least one threshold breach in the past year and trace the escalation path from detection through to board notification
Compare stated risk appetite metrics against actual performance data to identify any persistent breaches that have not been escalated or addressed

8.5.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.5.title: Board Audit Committee Technology Oversight
8.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the board receives comprehensive, quarterly technology risk reports that include KRIs, incident summaries, risk mitigation progress, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments. They will check whether the board engages with the reports and whether resulting actions are documented and tracked.

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are delivered quarterly without gaps or delays in the reporting cycle
Each report covers all mandatory elements: KRIs, incident summaries, mitigation status, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments
Reports are written in business language accessible to board members, not technical jargon
Reports include executive summaries with clear risk ratings and recommended board actions
Trend analysis covers at least four consecutive quarters to show directional movement
commonGaps: Reports are missed or delivered late, with quarters without any board technology risk reporting
Reports cover only a subset of required elements, typically missing trend analysis or forward-looking assessments
Reports are overly technical and do not translate technology risks into business impact language
No executive summary or clear indication of what requires board attention or action
suggestedSources: Board secretariat paper archives
Board portal or document management system
Chief Risk Officer reporting team records

evidenceItems.name: Key risk indicator dashboards
evidenceItems.description: Key risk indicator dashboards
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboard includes clearly defined KRIs with green, amber, and red thresholds aligned to the risk appetite statement
KRIs cover all major technology risk domains: cybersecurity, availability, change management, and third-party risk
Historical trend lines are visible for each KRI spanning at least four quarters
Dashboard narrative explains the significance of any amber or red indicators and actions being taken
commonGaps: KRIs lack defined thresholds, making it impossible to determine whether the institution is within risk appetite
Dashboard shows only current-state indicators without historical trend data
Critical risk domains such as third-party technology risk or patch management are missing from the dashboard
No narrative or commentary accompanies the dashboard data, leaving the board to interpret raw numbers
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream)
SIEM or security monitoring platform dashboards
IT service management platform reporting

evidenceItems.name: Incident summary reports presented to board
evidenceItems.description: Incident summary reports presented to board
whatGoodLooksLike: Incidents are categorised by severity, type, root cause, and business impact
Summary includes both the number of incidents and their cumulative business impact (e.g., downtime hours, customers affected, financial loss)
Root cause analysis findings are summarised with systemic themes identified across incidents
Comparison with previous quarters shows whether incident frequency and severity are trending up or down
commonGaps: Only major incidents are reported; near misses and lower-severity incidents that reveal systemic issues are omitted
Incidents are counted but business impact is not quantified or communicated to the board
No root cause analysis themes are presented, missing the opportunity to highlight systemic weaknesses
Incident reporting is ad hoc rather than structured within the quarterly board report
suggestedSources: IT service management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Security operations centre incident records
CISO quarterly incident summary compilations

evidenceItems.name: Risk mitigation status reports
evidenceItems.description: Risk mitigation status reports
whatGoodLooksLike: All open risk treatment plans are listed with current status, percentage completion, and expected closure dates
Overdue treatment plans are clearly highlighted with explanations and revised timelines
Reports show the number of risks closed versus new risks identified during the reporting period
Board is informed of any risks where the treatment approach has changed (e.g., from mitigate to accept)
commonGaps: Risk mitigation progress is not reported to the board, or only high-level summaries are provided without specifics
Overdue treatment plans are not flagged or explained to the board
No tracking of risk closure rates or comparison between new and resolved risks
Changes in risk treatment approach are made without board visibility or approval where required
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk treatment module
Risk management team tracking spreadsheets or registers
Project management office milestone reports for risk-related projects

evidenceItems.name: Board acknowledgement or sign-off records
evidenceItems.description: Board acknowledgement or sign-off records
whatGoodLooksLike: Board meeting minutes explicitly record that the technology risk report was presented, discussed, and acknowledged
Minutes capture specific questions, challenges, or concerns raised by board members regarding the report content
Resulting board directives or action items are documented with assigned owners and deadlines
Follow-up on previous board directives related to technology risk is recorded in subsequent meeting minutes
commonGaps: Minutes note the report was tabled but do not record any discussion or acknowledgement
No evidence of board members questioning or challenging the reported information
Board directives arising from technology risk discussions are not documented as formal action items
Follow-up on previous board directives is not tracked or reported in subsequent meetings
suggestedSources: Board secretariat minutes archive
Board portal action item tracking module
Company secretary records

auditTips: Request the last 4 quarterly board reports and verify each contains all mandatory elements: KRIs, incident summaries, mitigation status, trend analysis, and forward-looking assessments
Check that KRI thresholds in the dashboard align with those defined in the board-approved risk appetite statement
Verify that incident summaries include business impact quantification, not just incident counts
Trace at least 2 board directives from technology risk report discussions through to completion or ongoing tracking
Confirm no quarters were missed in the reporting cycle and that reports were delivered to the board in advance of meetings

8.6.title: Senior Management Accountability for Technology Risk
8.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that senior management accountability for technology risk is formally documented, that a CISO or equivalent is formally appointed with direct board or committee reporting access, and that this reporting line is actively exercised. They will scrutinise the CISO's organisational placement and independence from IT operations.

evidenceItems.name: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
evidenceItems.description: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix assigns specific technology risk domains to named C-suite executives (e.g., CISO for cybersecurity, CTO for infrastructure, CRO for risk oversight)
Each accountability area has clearly defined responsibilities, decision authority limits, and escalation triggers
Matrix distinguishes between first line (risk ownership), second line (risk oversight), and third line (audit) responsibilities
Matrix is board-approved and reviewed annually or upon significant organisational changes
commonGaps: No formal accountability matrix exists; technology risk responsibilities are assumed based on job titles rather than documented
Accountability overlaps between roles without clear delineation of who is ultimately responsible for specific risk domains
Matrix does not reflect the three lines of defence model expected by BNM
Matrix has not been updated following organisational restructuring or leadership changes
suggestedSources: Chief Executive Officer's office documentation
Corporate governance framework documents
Human Resources organisational design records

evidenceItems.name: CISO appointment letter and job description
evidenceItems.description: CISO appointment letter and job description
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal appointment letter signed by the CEO or board chairman confirming the CISO designation
Job description explicitly covers cybersecurity strategy, incident response leadership, security architecture governance, and regulatory compliance
Mandate includes authority to halt or delay technology deployments that present unacceptable security risks
Role grade and reporting structure demonstrate appropriate seniority within the organisation
commonGaps: No formal appointment letter; the CISO role is assumed rather than formally designated
Job description is generic IT management rather than specific to information security leadership
CISO lacks documented authority to influence technology decisions on security grounds
Role is positioned too junior in the organisation to have the influence required by RMiT
suggestedSources: Human Resources appointment and contract records
CEO office correspondence
Board or committee appointment resolutions

evidenceItems.name: CISO reporting line documentation
evidenceItems.description: CISO reporting line documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart clearly shows the CISO with a direct reporting line to the board or board technology risk committee for cybersecurity matters
Dual reporting structure is documented if the CISO has both an administrative reporting line (e.g., to CEO) and a functional reporting line to the board committee
Reporting line demonstrates independence from the CTO or Head of IT operations
Documentation is current and reflects the actual organisational structure in practice
commonGaps: CISO reports exclusively to the CTO or Head of IT with no independent board access
Organisation chart shows a dotted line to the board but no evidence that this reporting line is exercised
Reporting structure has the CISO buried several layers below the C-suite, limiting influence and access
Organisation chart is outdated and does not reflect current reporting arrangements
suggestedSources: Human Resources organisational chart repository
Corporate governance structure documents
Board committee terms of reference

evidenceItems.name: Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting
evidenceItems.description: Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes show the CISO presenting directly to the board or committee at least quarterly
CISO presentations cover cybersecurity posture, incident trends, threat intelligence, and security programme progress
Evidence of board members engaging with the CISO directly, asking questions, and requesting follow-up information
CISO has the opportunity to raise concerns directly to the board without management filtering
commonGaps: No records of the CISO ever presenting directly to the board or committee despite having a documented reporting line
CISO presentations are delivered through the CTO or CRO rather than directly, undermining independence
CISO attendance at board or committee meetings is irregular with multiple quarters without direct reporting
Minutes do not show the CISO being given the opportunity to raise independent concerns
suggestedSources: Board secretariat minutes archive
Board portal presentation and document records
Committee chairman's meeting files

auditTips: Verify the CISO appointment is formally documented with a board-approved mandate and confirm the appointee has appropriate seniority and qualifications
Examine the organisation chart and confirm the CISO has an independent reporting line to the board or committee that does not pass through the CTO
Request the last 4 quarters of committee minutes and verify the CISO presented directly at each meeting, not through an intermediary
Check that the senior management accountability matrix reflects the three lines of defence model with clear boundaries between risk ownership and risk oversight
Ask the CISO whether they have the authority to delay technology deployments on security grounds and request evidence of this authority being exercised

8.7.title: Cross-Functional Technology Committee
8.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that an independent technology risk management function exists with adequate authority, resources, and access. They will scrutinise organisational independence from IT operations, assess staffing adequacy relative to the institution's technology complexity, and review whether the function actively produces risk assessments and reports.

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk management function charter
evidenceItems.description: Technology risk management function charter
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter clearly defines the function's mandate covering risk identification, assessment, monitoring, and reporting across all technology domains
Authority to access any technology system, data, or personnel necessary for risk assessment is explicitly stated
Independence from IT operations is explicitly mandated in the charter with board or committee approval
Charter specifies reporting obligations to the CRO, senior management, and board committee
Document is version-controlled with board approval date and next review date
commonGaps: No formal charter exists; the function operates based on informal understanding rather than documented authority
Charter does not explicitly mandate independence from IT operations or define access authority
Charter is outdated and does not reflect current scope of technology risk across cloud, digital, and third-party domains
Charter lacks board or committee approval, undermining the function's formal authority
suggestedSources: Chief Risk Officer office documentation
Board committee approval records
Corporate governance framework documents

evidenceItems.name: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations
evidenceItems.description: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart clearly shows the technology risk function reporting to the CRO or a risk leadership role separate from the CTO or Head of IT
No dotted or solid reporting line connects the function to IT operations leadership
Function head has an appropriate title and grade reflecting the seniority needed to exercise authority across the institution
Chart is current, dated, and reflects the actual organisational structure
commonGaps: Technology risk function reports to the CTO or Head of IT, creating a conflict of interest
Organisation chart is outdated and does not reflect recent restructuring or leadership changes
Function is embedded within the IT department with no structural independence
Function head is too junior in the organisation to effectively challenge IT operations leadership
suggestedSources: Human Resources organisational chart repository
Corporate governance structure documentation
Chief Risk Officer office records

evidenceItems.name: Staffing plan and competency matrix
evidenceItems.description: Staffing plan and competency matrix
whatGoodLooksLike: Staffing plan is proportionate to the institution's technology complexity and maps roles to the function's mandate
Competency matrix defines required skills across risk management, cybersecurity, cloud, data, and technology audit disciplines
Current staff competencies are assessed against the matrix with documented gaps and development plans
Staffing plan includes succession planning for critical roles within the function
Staff hold relevant certifications such as CRISC, CISM, CISSP, or ISO 27001 Lead Auditor
commonGaps: Function is staffed with only one or two individuals for an institution with a complex technology environment
No competency matrix exists; staffing is based on availability rather than required competencies
Identified competency gaps have no documented development plans or hiring timelines
Staff lack relevant risk management or technology certifications
suggestedSources: Human Resources workforce planning records
Head of Technology Risk personnel files and development plans
Professional development and certification tracking records

evidenceItems.name: Function mandate and authority documentation
evidenceItems.description: Function mandate and authority documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documentation confirms the function's authority to conduct risk assessments across all technology domains without restriction
Evidence of the function exercising its authority through completed risk assessments, audit findings, and risk advisories
Access rights to technology systems, SIEM, vulnerability scanners, and CMDB are documented and verified
Evidence of the function providing independent risk opinions on major technology projects or changes
commonGaps: Mandate exists on paper but there is no evidence of the function exercising its authority in practice
Function has been denied access to systems or information needed for risk assessments
No evidence of the function providing independent risk opinions on technology projects or changes
Function's authority has been overridden by IT operations leadership without proper escalation
suggestedSources: Technology risk assessment reports and work products
System access logs and permissions records
Risk advisory memos and project risk sign-off records

auditTips: Verify organisational independence by examining the actual reporting line of the technology risk function head and confirming it does not report to IT operations
Assess staffing adequacy by comparing the number of risk staff against the institution's technology complexity (number of critical systems, cloud deployments, third-party integrations)
Request examples of risk assessments completed by the function in the past 12 months to verify it is actively producing work products
Ask the function head whether they have ever been denied access to systems or information and how such situations were resolved
Review the function's output (risk reports, advisories, assessment findings) to determine whether it provides genuine independent challenge to IT operations

9.1.title: TRMF Integration with Enterprise Risk Management
9.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the technology risk management framework is comprehensive, proportionate to the institution's complexity, board-approved, annually reviewed, and aligned with the enterprise risk management framework. They look for a cohesive document that defines governance, risk appetite, assessment methodology, and reporting mechanisms as an integrated whole.

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk management framework document
evidenceItems.description: Technology risk management framework document
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive document covering governance structures, risk appetite, risk assessment methodology, risk treatment options, monitoring, and reporting mechanisms
Framework is proportionate to the institution's size and complexity, with clear justification for the scope and depth of coverage
References BNM RMiT requirements with traceability mapping showing which framework components address which regulatory clauses
Includes a risk taxonomy specific to technology risks with clear definitions and categorisation
Version-controlled with effective date, next review date, and document owner identified
commonGaps: Framework is a collection of disconnected policies rather than a cohesive, integrated document
Framework is generic and not tailored to the institution's specific technology environment and risk profile
No mapping to BNM RMiT requirements, making it impossible to verify regulatory coverage
Framework is overly theoretical with no practical guidance on how to apply the methodology in day-to-day operations
suggestedSources: Chief Risk Officer office documentation
GRC platform document repository
Enterprise risk management team records

evidenceItems.name: Board approval of the framework
evidenceItems.description: Board approval of the framework
whatGoodLooksLike: Board meeting minutes or resolution explicitly recording approval of the technology risk management framework
Evidence that the board received a briefing on the framework's key components before approving
Approval covers the current version of the framework with the version number and date referenced
Board re-approval documented after each annual review or material update
commonGaps: No board approval record exists; the framework was approved only by management
Board approved an earlier version and has not re-approved after significant updates
Board approval is inferred from general governance approvals rather than specific to the technology risk framework
No evidence that board members were briefed on the framework content before approval
suggestedSources: Board secretariat resolution records
Board meeting minutes archive
Company secretary approval tracking records

evidenceItems.name: Annual framework review records
evidenceItems.description: Annual framework review records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented review process with assessment of each framework component's effectiveness and currency
Review considers changes in the regulatory landscape (e.g., BNM updates), technology environment, business strategy, and incident learnings
Summary of changes made or rationale for no changes required is documented
Review findings are presented to the board or committee with recommendations for framework updates
commonGaps: No evidence of annual review despite the framework being more than 12 months since last update
Review is perfunctory, consisting of a date change without substantive assessment of framework effectiveness
Review does not consider external factors such as new BNM guidelines, industry incidents, or changes in the threat landscape
Review findings are not escalated to the board or committee for awareness and approval
suggestedSources: Risk management team review working papers
Board or committee meeting papers presenting review findings
Document management system version history and change logs

evidenceItems.name: Framework alignment with enterprise risk management
evidenceItems.description: Framework alignment with enterprise risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Technology risk management framework explicitly references and aligns with the enterprise risk management framework
Common risk taxonomy, rating scales, and reporting formats are used across technology and enterprise risk frameworks
Technology risk appetite is derived from and consistent with the enterprise risk appetite statement
Technology risk reporting feeds into enterprise-wide risk aggregation and reporting to the board
commonGaps: Technology risk framework operates in isolation with different methodologies, rating scales, and reporting formats from the enterprise framework
No documented mapping between technology risk categories and enterprise risk categories
Technology risk reports are not integrated into enterprise-wide risk dashboards or board reports
Risk appetite thresholds in the technology framework are inconsistent with those in the enterprise framework
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management framework document
Chief Risk Officer alignment and integration records
GRC platform configuration showing integrated risk taxonomy

auditTips: Request the full framework document and verify it covers all required components: governance, risk appetite, assessment methodology, treatment, monitoring, and reporting
Confirm the framework carries current board approval by checking the approval date and comparing it to the framework's version and last review date
Compare the technology risk framework's risk taxonomy and rating scales with the enterprise risk framework to verify alignment
Ask for the last annual review record and verify it conducted a substantive assessment of framework effectiveness, not just a date update
Check whether the framework is operationally implemented by requesting examples of risk assessments conducted using its defined methodology

9.2.title: TRMF Minimum Requirements
9.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution conducts systematic technology risk assessments at least annually using a documented methodology, covering all critical assets and services. They will check that assessments consider threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and impact, and that ad hoc assessments are triggered by significant changes.

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk assessment methodology document
evidenceItems.description: Technology risk assessment methodology document
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology defines clear risk identification techniques including threat modelling, vulnerability analysis, and scenario analysis
Likelihood and impact rating scales are defined with specific criteria for each level (e.g., 5-point scales with quantitative and qualitative descriptors)
Risk scoring methodology is transparent, showing how likelihood and impact combine to produce an overall risk rating
Methodology specifies when assessments must be performed (annual minimum plus change-triggered triggers)
Roles and responsibilities for conducting, reviewing, and approving risk assessments are clearly defined
commonGaps: No documented methodology; risk assessments are conducted ad hoc using different approaches by different teams
Rating scales are undefined or subjective with no criteria to distinguish between levels
Methodology does not specify triggers for ad hoc assessments beyond the annual cycle
No quality assurance or review process defined for completed assessments
suggestedSources: Technology risk management function documentation
GRC platform methodology configuration
Chief Risk Officer policy repository

evidenceItems.name: Completed risk assessment reports
evidenceItems.description: Completed risk assessment reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment reports cover all critical technology assets and services within the defined scope
Each risk is documented with identified threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood rating, impact rating, and overall risk score
Business impact is quantified or described in terms relevant to the institution (financial, operational, regulatory, reputational)
Assessment findings lead to documented risk treatment recommendations with proposed owners and timelines
Reports are signed off by the Head of Technology Risk and relevant risk owners
commonGaps: Assessments cover only a subset of critical technology assets, leaving significant gaps in coverage
Risk ratings are assigned without supporting justification or evidence of threat and vulnerability analysis
Business impact is described in purely technical terms without translation to business consequences
No treatment recommendations accompany identified risks, making the assessment purely academic
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk assessment module
Technology risk management function work products
Risk assessment project files and working papers

evidenceItems.name: Risk assessment schedule and completion records
evidenceItems.description: Risk assessment schedule and completion records
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual schedule published at the start of the year covering all critical technology domains and assets
Schedule shows planned assessment dates, assigned assessors, and scope for each assessment
Completion tracking shows actual versus planned progress with explanations for any delays
Full coverage of all critical technology domains is achieved within each annual cycle
commonGaps: No formal schedule exists; assessments are conducted reactively rather than proactively
Schedule exists but completion records show significant portions were not completed within the annual cycle
Certain technology domains (e.g., cloud services, third-party integrations) are consistently excluded from the schedule
No tracking mechanism to monitor whether planned assessments are completed on time
suggestedSources: Technology risk management function planning documents
GRC platform assessment scheduling module
Head of Technology Risk annual work plan

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of assessment upon significant change events
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of assessment upon significant change events
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented criteria defining what constitutes a significant change triggering an ad hoc risk assessment
Records of ad hoc risk assessments conducted following major system implementations, infrastructure changes, or security incidents
Change management process includes a risk assessment gate that triggers evaluation for significant changes
Assessment results from change-triggered assessments feed into the risk register and treatment planning process
commonGaps: No defined criteria for what constitutes a significant change requiring an ad hoc assessment
Major technology changes were implemented without a corresponding risk assessment
Change management process does not include a risk assessment trigger or gate
Ad hoc assessments are conducted but findings are not integrated into the main risk register
suggestedSources: Change management system records (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management)
Technology risk assessment records for specific change events
Project management office risk gate documentation

auditTips: Request the risk assessment methodology and verify it defines clear rating scales with specific criteria for each likelihood and impact level
Compare the assessment schedule against completion records to identify any assessments that were planned but not executed
Select 2-3 completed risk assessment reports and verify they follow the documented methodology with proper justification for assigned risk ratings
Identify major technology changes from the past 12 months (from the change management system) and verify whether corresponding ad hoc risk assessments were conducted
Check whether assessment findings resulted in documented risk treatment plans with assigned owners and timelines

9.3.title: Independent Technology Risk Management Function
9.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that every identified technology risk has a documented treatment plan with a named owner and timeline. They will pay particular attention to risk acceptance decisions, checking whether approvals were obtained at the appropriate authority level with documented justification. Overdue treatment plans are a common finding.

evidenceItems.name: Risk treatment plans with assigned owners
evidenceItems.description: Risk treatment plans with assigned owners
whatGoodLooksLike: Each identified risk has a documented treatment plan specifying the chosen treatment option (avoid, mitigate, transfer, or accept)
Plans include specific actions, milestones, resource requirements, and target completion dates
A named individual is accountable for each treatment plan with their role and reporting line documented
Expected residual risk level after treatment completion is documented alongside the current risk level
Plans are linked to the corresponding risks in the risk register for traceability
commonGaps: Identified risks in the register have no corresponding treatment plans or only generic remediation notes
Treatment plans lack specific milestones, making progress tracking impossible
No named owner assigned to treatment plans; accountability is assigned to teams rather than individuals
Treatment plans are static documents that have not been updated since initial creation despite changing circumstances
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk treatment module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer)
Risk management team treatment plan register
Project management office records for risk-related projects

evidenceItems.name: Risk acceptance approvals at appropriate authority level
evidenceItems.description: Risk acceptance approvals at appropriate authority level
whatGoodLooksLike: Each risk acceptance record documents the risk description, current risk level, residual risk after any partial mitigation, and business justification for acceptance
Approval is signed by the authority level defined in the risk treatment policy (e.g., CRO for high risks, board for critical risks)
Acceptance includes conditions such as compensating controls, monitoring requirements, and a defined review or expiry date
Accepted risks are flagged in the risk register with their acceptance status and next review date
commonGaps: Risks are accepted de facto by inaction rather than through a formal acceptance process with documented approval
Risk acceptance approvals are signed by managers below the authority level required by policy for the given risk severity
No business justification documented for why the risk is being accepted rather than mitigated
Accepted risks are never revisited; they remain accepted indefinitely without periodic revalidation
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk acceptance workflow and approval records
Risk management committee meeting minutes
Senior management or board resolution records

evidenceItems.name: Treatment plan progress tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Treatment plan progress tracking records
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular progress updates are recorded for each active treatment plan showing percentage completion and status changes
Overdue treatment plans are clearly flagged with explanations for delays and revised target dates
Escalation to senior management is documented when treatment plans are significantly overdue or face resource constraints
Quarterly summary reports on treatment plan status are included in board technology risk reports
commonGaps: No systematic progress tracking; treatment plans are created but never monitored for completion
Overdue plans accumulate without escalation or management visibility
Progress tracking is ad hoc and inconsistent, making it impossible to assess the institution's overall risk treatment velocity
Treatment plan status is not included in board or senior management risk reporting
suggestedSources: GRC platform treatment tracking module
Risk management team status reports and dashboards
Senior management and board committee reporting packs

evidenceItems.name: Risk mitigation implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Risk mitigation implementation evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Technical evidence of control implementation such as system configuration screenshots, deployment records, or tool activation logs
Post-implementation validation reports confirming the control reduces the risk as intended
Residual risk re-assessment performed after control implementation showing the updated risk level
Sign-off by the risk owner confirming the treatment plan has been satisfactorily completed
commonGaps: Treatment plans are marked as complete without supporting evidence of actual implementation
No post-implementation validation to confirm the control is effective in reducing the identified risk
Residual risk is not re-assessed after control implementation; the risk register still shows the original risk level
Implementation evidence is anecdotal rather than documented with verifiable artefacts
suggestedSources: IT operations deployment and change records
Security testing and validation reports
GRC platform control implementation records

auditTips: Select 5-10 risks from the risk register and verify each has a documented treatment plan with a named owner and specific timeline
Review all risk acceptance records and confirm approvals were signed at the authority level required by the risk treatment policy for the given risk severity
Check the treatment plan tracking system for overdue plans and assess whether appropriate escalation occurred
For recently completed treatment plans, request implementation evidence and verify that post-implementation validation was performed
Ask the Head of Technology Risk to provide the current ratio of open to closed treatment plans and the average time to closure over the past 12 months

9.4.title: Designation of Chief Information Security Officer
9.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has defined KRIs with specific thresholds, maintains continuous monitoring capabilities, and has documented escalation procedures for threshold breaches. They will test whether the escalation framework has been exercised in practice by requesting evidence of actual breach notifications and responses.

evidenceItems.name: Key risk indicator definitions and thresholds
evidenceItems.description: Key risk indicator definitions and thresholds
whatGoodLooksLike: Each KRI has a clear definition, data source, calculation methodology, and measurement frequency documented
Thresholds are defined with green (within appetite), amber (approaching breach), and red (breach) levels using specific numerical values
KRIs cover all critical technology risk domains including cybersecurity, availability, change management, data integrity, and third-party risk
Named owners are assigned to each KRI with accountability for data quality and escalation
KRI thresholds are aligned with the board-approved technology risk appetite statement
commonGaps: KRIs are defined at a high level without specific numerical thresholds, making breach determination subjective
KRI coverage is incomplete, missing critical domains such as third-party risk, cloud risk, or data protection
No named owners assigned to individual KRIs, leading to gaps in monitoring and accountability
KRI thresholds are not aligned with or traceable to the risk appetite statement
suggestedSources: Risk management KRI register or catalogue
GRC platform KRI configuration
Chief Risk Officer policy documentation

evidenceItems.name: Risk monitoring dashboard or reports
evidenceItems.description: Risk monitoring dashboard or reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboard displays current status of all defined KRIs with visual indicators showing green, amber, or red status
Historical trend data is available for each KRI spanning at least 12 months to enable trend analysis
Dashboard is updated at least monthly with data sourced from automated monitoring tools rather than manual input
Narrative commentary accompanies the dashboard explaining any amber or red indicators and actions being taken
commonGaps: Dashboard is static and updated infrequently, providing a stale view of the institution's risk posture
No historical trend data available, preventing identification of deteriorating risk trends
Dashboard relies on manual data input, introducing delays and potential inaccuracies
No narrative or context provided alongside dashboard metrics, leaving readers to interpret raw data
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk dashboard module
SIEM or security monitoring platform dashboards
IT service management reporting tools

evidenceItems.name: Escalation procedure documentation
evidenceItems.description: Escalation procedure documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented procedures define specific actions for amber (warning) and red (breach) threshold triggers
Escalation path identifies specific roles (not just teams) who must be notified at each severity level, with timeframes for notification
Procedures include requirements for root cause analysis, remediation planning, and follow-up reporting
Procedures are integrated with the institution's broader incident management and crisis management frameworks
commonGaps: No documented escalation procedures exist; responses to threshold breaches are ad hoc
Procedures identify teams but not specific roles, creating ambiguity about who is responsible for taking action
No defined timeframes for notification or response, allowing breaches to persist without timely attention
Procedures are not tested or rehearsed, raising doubts about their effectiveness in a real scenario
suggestedSources: Risk management procedure documentation
Incident management and escalation framework documents
Chief Risk Officer operational procedures

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of threshold breach escalations
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of threshold breach escalations
whatGoodLooksLike: Records of actual KRI threshold breaches with timestamps showing when the breach was detected and when escalation occurred
Documentation of escalation actions taken including notifications sent, recipients, and response timeframes achieved
Root cause analysis completed for each breach explaining why the threshold was breached and what corrective action was taken
Evidence that breach events were reported to senior management and, where appropriate, to the board or committee
commonGaps: No records of threshold breach escalations despite KRIs showing amber or red status in the monitoring dashboard
Breaches are detected but not escalated per the documented procedures, indicating the framework is not operational
No root cause analysis performed for breach events, missing the opportunity to prevent recurrence
Breach events are not included in board or senior management risk reporting
suggestedSources: GRC platform escalation and notification logs
Incident management system escalation records
Senior management and board reporting packs showing breach notifications

auditTips: Request the KRI catalogue and verify each indicator has defined numerical thresholds aligned with the risk appetite statement
Ask for the most recent monitoring dashboard and check whether it shows any current amber or red indicators, then request evidence of corresponding escalation actions
Review the escalation procedures and trace at least 2 actual breach events from detection through escalation to resolution to verify the process works in practice
Check whether KRI trend data is available for the past 12 months and whether deteriorating trends triggered proactive management attention before thresholds were breached
Verify that KRI monitoring data feeds into the quarterly board technology risk report as required by Clause 8.5

9.5.title: CISO Responsibilities for Information Protection
9.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will request the full technology risk register and verify it is comprehensive, current, and contains all required fields for each risk entry. They will check that quarterly reviews are documented, risk owners are assigned and actively engaged, and the register's change history demonstrates active maintenance rather than a static document.

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk register with all required fields
evidenceItems.description: Technology risk register with all required fields
whatGoodLooksLike: Register contains entries for all identified technology risks across infrastructure, applications, cybersecurity, data, cloud, and third-party domains
Each entry includes risk description, risk category, assessed likelihood, assessed impact, overall risk rating, named risk owner, treatment plan reference, treatment status, and residual risk level
Risk ratings are consistent with the documented risk assessment methodology and use the defined rating scales
Register distinguishes between inherent risk (before controls) and residual risk (after controls) for each entry
Register is maintained on a platform with version control and audit trails, not in standalone spreadsheets
commonGaps: Register covers only IT infrastructure risks and omits critical domains such as cloud, data, third-party, or application risks
Entries are missing required fields; many risks lack assigned owners, treatment plans, or residual risk assessments
Risk ratings are inconsistent across entries, suggesting different methodologies were applied
Register is maintained in a spreadsheet with no version control, making it impossible to verify when entries were added or modified
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk register module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream)
Technology risk management function records
Head of Technology Risk operational files

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly review records of the risk register
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly review records of the risk register
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented review outcomes for each quarter showing which risks were reviewed, updated, added, or closed
Review records demonstrate that risk assessments were revalidated and treatment plan progress was evaluated
Summary statistics are captured: total risks, new risks added, risks closed, risks with overdue treatment plans
Review findings are escalated to senior management through the quarterly technology risk report
Reviews are conducted by the technology risk management function with participation from risk owners
commonGaps: No documented quarterly review records despite the register existing; reviews are claimed but not evidenced
Reviews consist of a cursory check with no substantive reassessment of risk levels or treatment progress
Review records do not show which specific risks were reassessed or what changes were made
Reviews are conducted by the risk function alone without engagement from risk owners
suggestedSources: GRC platform review workflow and audit logs
Technology risk management function quarterly review reports
Risk committee meeting minutes where register reviews are discussed

evidenceItems.name: Risk owner acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.description: Risk owner acknowledgement records
whatGoodLooksLike: Each risk owner has formally acknowledged their assigned risks through signed acknowledgement records or system-based confirmation
Risk owners provide quarterly status updates on their assigned risks including treatment progress and any changes to risk levels
Acknowledgement records include the risk owner's understanding of their responsibilities for monitoring and managing the assigned risks
Records are maintained for all current risk owners and updated when ownership changes
commonGaps: No formal acknowledgement process; risk owners are assigned in the register but have never confirmed their accountability
Risk owners do not provide regular status updates, leaving the risk function to track treatment progress without owner input
Acknowledgement was obtained at initial assignment but not refreshed when risk circumstances changed significantly
Some risks have no assigned owner or the assigned owner has left the organisation without a successor being designated
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk assignment and acknowledgement workflows
Technology risk management function correspondence records
Email or system-based acknowledgement confirmations

evidenceItems.name: Risk register change history
evidenceItems.description: Risk register change history
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete audit trail showing all additions, modifications, and closures with timestamps, author identification, and change descriptions
Change history demonstrates regular updates throughout the year, not just bulk changes at review time
Justification is documented for significant changes such as risk level adjustments, owner changes, or risk closures
Change history is system-generated from the GRC platform rather than manually maintained
commonGaps: No change history available due to the register being maintained in a tool without audit trail capabilities
Change history shows long periods of inactivity suggesting the register is only updated around audit or review dates
Risk closures are not accompanied by documented justification explaining why the risk is no longer relevant
Changes are not attributable to specific individuals, making accountability for register modifications unclear
suggestedSources: GRC platform audit log and version history
Document management system version control records
Technology risk management function change tracking records

auditTips: Request the full risk register and verify it covers all critical technology domains, not just traditional IT infrastructure risks
Check completeness by verifying that every entry has all required fields populated: risk description, likelihood, impact, rating, owner, treatment plan, and status
Request the last 4 quarters of review records and verify that substantive reassessment occurred, not just date updates
Select 5 risk owners at random and verify they can articulate the risks assigned to them and the current treatment plan status
Examine the change history timeline and confirm the register is updated regularly throughout the year, not just in bulk before scheduled reviews or audits

10.1.title: Technology Project Governance
10.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal, board-approved IT project governance framework exists and is actively enforced, with particular scrutiny on whether major projects underwent independent risk assessment and received senior management approval before commencement.

evidenceItems.name: IT project governance framework document
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved framework defining project classification criteria, governance structures, roles and responsibilities, stage-gate approval requirements, and escalation procedures for all technology projects
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is version-controlled with clear approval history showing board or senior management sign-off
Defines specific criteria for classifying projects as major (e.g., budget above RM5 million, customer-facing system changes, regulatory impact)
Includes a RACI matrix mapping governance responsibilities to named roles across project phases
Stage-gate definitions include mandatory deliverables, approval authorities, and go/no-go criteria for each phase
Framework has been reviewed and updated within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Framework exists as a generic template without customisation to the institution's project landscape
No clear criteria for what constitutes a 'major' project, leading to inconsistent classification
Stage-gate requirements defined but not enforced in practice — projects proceed without documented approvals
Framework has not been updated since initial creation despite organisational or regulatory changes
suggestedSources: IT Governance / PMO document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Board or IT steering committee meeting minutes showing framework approval
Internal audit reports referencing project governance compliance

evidenceItems.name: Project approval records with stage-gate sign-offs
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that technology projects passed through defined stage-gates with documented approvals from authorised personnel at each phase transition
whatGoodLooksLike: Each stage-gate has a dated sign-off from the designated approval authority with clear pass/fail outcome
Sign-off records reference specific deliverables reviewed (e.g., requirements document, design review, test results)
Conditions or actions arising from gate reviews are documented with assigned owners and completion dates
Consistent use of standardised templates across all projects in the portfolio
commonGaps: Stage-gate approvals are backdated or completed retrospectively after the project has already moved to the next phase
Sign-offs lack specificity — generic 'approved' without reference to what was actually reviewed
No evidence of conditions tracking — issues raised at gates are not followed through to closure
Smaller projects bypass stage-gates entirely without documented justification
suggestedSources: Project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps, MS Project) workflow records
PMO stage-gate review meeting minutes and sign-off forms
Email trails or digital approval workflows from document management systems

evidenceItems.name: Independent risk assessment reports for major projects
evidenceItems.description: Risk assessment reports for major technology projects conducted by personnel independent of the project delivery team, covering security, availability, data integrity, and business impact
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment conducted by risk management function, internal audit, or external assessor independent of the project team
Covers all required risk dimensions: security, availability, data integrity, customer impact, and regulatory compliance
Includes specific risk ratings with likelihood and impact scoring aligned to the institution's risk taxonomy
Recommended mitigations are clearly linked to identified risks with assigned owners and target dates
Report is completed and presented before project commencement, not retrospectively
commonGaps: Risk assessment performed by the project team itself rather than an independent party
Assessment is a tick-box exercise with generic risks that do not reflect the specific project context
No evidence that risk assessment findings influenced project decisions or design changes
Assessment completed after the project has already commenced
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management system or GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC)
Internal audit or risk management department records
IT steering committee or board risk committee meeting packs

evidenceItems.name: Board or senior management approval records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that major technology projects received formal approval from the board, board risk committee, or designated senior management prior to project commencement
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or committee meeting minutes explicitly record the approval decision with project name, scope, and budget
Approval is dated prior to project kick-off or resource mobilisation activities
Minutes reference the independent risk assessment as input to the approval decision
Any conditions attached to the approval are documented with follow-up tracking
commonGaps: Board approval is inferred from silence rather than an explicit recorded decision
Approval obtained after the project has already started spending budget or mobilising resources
No evidence that the board reviewed the independent risk assessment before approving
Delegated approvals for major projects without formal board ratification
suggestedSources: Board secretariat records and meeting minutes
IT steering committee meeting packs and decision logs
Enterprise project portfolio management system approval workflows

auditTips: Request the complete project register for the last 12 months and verify that each project classified as 'major' has a corresponding independent risk assessment and board approval dated before the project start date
Select 3-5 recent projects across different sizes and trace them through the stage-gate process end-to-end, checking for dated sign-offs at each gate
Verify that the governance framework has a current version with approval history and has been reviewed within the last 12 months
Cross-reference the project classification criteria against actual project classifications to identify any projects that should have been classified as major but were not
Check for any projects that commenced before governance approvals were obtained, as this is a common finding that indicates the framework is not being followed in practice

10.2.title: Technology Project Risk Assessment
10.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will look for evidence that every technology project has a completed risk assessment covering security, availability, and data integrity, with explicit interdependency analysis and documented risk mitigation plans reviewed before project commencement.

evidenceItems.name: Project risk assessment template and methodology
evidenceItems.description: Standardised methodology and template used for assessing technology project risks, defining risk categories, scoring criteria, and documentation requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology document is version-controlled and approved by CISO or risk management function
Template includes mandatory sections for security, availability, data integrity, interdependencies, and customer impact
Risk scoring criteria use a defined likelihood-impact matrix aligned to the institution's enterprise risk framework
Template includes fields for risk mitigation actions, owners, and target dates
Methodology specifies when reassessment is required (e.g., at each stage-gate, on scope change)
commonGaps: Template exists but does not include interdependency analysis as a mandatory section
Risk scoring criteria are vague or inconsistent with the enterprise risk framework
No defined process for risk reassessment during the project lifecycle
Template has not been updated to reflect current threat landscape or regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: PMO or IT governance document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Enterprise risk management framework documentation
CISO or risk management function policy library

evidenceItems.name: Completed project risk assessment reports
evidenceItems.description: Completed risk assessments for recent technology projects demonstrating systematic identification and evaluation of risks to security, availability, and data integrity
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment follows the standardised template and methodology consistently
Specific risks identified are relevant to the project context, not generic boilerplate entries
Each risk has a clearly assessed likelihood, impact, and residual risk rating after mitigations
Assessment is dated before the project commencement date with approval signatures from the appropriate authority
Evidence of risk reassessment at subsequent stage-gates where applicable
commonGaps: Risk assessments are completed retrospectively after the project has already started
Generic risks copied from templates without project-specific analysis
No residual risk rating after mitigations, making it impossible to assess whether risk is within appetite
Assessment completed but no evidence of review or approval by risk authority
suggestedSources: Project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) attached documents
