GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC) risk register entries
PMO shared drive with project documentation folders

evidenceItems.name: Interdependency analysis records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of system interdependency analysis conducted as part of project risk assessment, mapping upstream and downstream system impacts
whatGoodLooksLike: Analysis includes architecture diagrams showing all systems and interfaces affected by the project
Identifies shared infrastructure components (databases, middleware, networks) that could be impacted
Maps third-party and vendor dependencies with their potential impact on project delivery and operations
Analysis informed by CMDB data or enterprise architecture repository, not solely by project team knowledge
Identifies specific risks arising from interdependencies with corresponding mitigation actions
commonGaps: Interdependency analysis limited to systems the project team is directly aware of, missing peripheral impacts
No reference to CMDB or enterprise architecture data to validate completeness
Third-party and vendor dependencies not considered in the analysis
Analysis performed but not linked back to the risk assessment or mitigation plans
suggestedSources: Enterprise architecture tools (TOGAF-based repositories, Sparx EA, LeanIX)
CMDB (ServiceNow, BMC Helix) relationship maps
Solution architecture documents from project deliverables

evidenceItems.name: Risk mitigation plans for identified project risks
evidenceItems.description: Documented plans for mitigating risks identified during project risk assessments, with assigned owners, implementation timelines, and status tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: Each identified risk has a corresponding mitigation action with a named owner and target completion date
Mitigation actions are specific and actionable, not generic statements like 'monitor risk'
Status tracking shows progress of mitigations through the project lifecycle
Residual risks after mitigation are formally accepted by the appropriate authority level
Mitigations are integrated into the project plan as tracked tasks
commonGaps: Mitigation plans exist on paper but are not tracked or integrated into project delivery
No named owners for mitigation actions, leading to diffused accountability
Mitigation status not updated as the project progresses, leaving stale records
Risk acceptance for residual risks not formally documented or approved
suggestedSources: Project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) risk and issue registers
Risk management system mitigation tracking modules
Project steering committee meeting minutes showing risk review discussions

auditTips: Select 5 recent technology projects and verify that each has a completed risk assessment dated before project commencement, using the standardised template
Check that interdependency analysis is present in each risk assessment and references architecture diagrams or CMDB data, not just project team assumptions
Trace risk mitigation actions from the risk assessment into the project plan to verify they were implemented, not just documented
Verify that risk assessments for projects with scope changes were reassessed and updated rather than left as the original version
Look for evidence that risk assessment findings influenced project design decisions, such as additional security controls or architecture changes

10.3.title: Board and Senior Management Project Reporting
10.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will check whether post-implementation reviews are consistently conducted within three months of go-live for significant projects, and whether lessons learned are documented and demonstrably fed back into improved processes.

evidenceItems.name: Post-implementation review reports
evidenceItems.description: Formal review reports completed after significant technology projects go live, assessing achievement of objectives, operational performance, and areas for improvement
whatGoodLooksLike: Report completed within three months of project go-live date as evidenced by document dates
Includes assessment against the original business case objectives and measurable success criteria
Covers both technical outcomes (performance, security, stability) and business outcomes (user adoption, customer impact)
Contains specific findings and recommendations with assigned action owners
Signed off by project sponsor and key stakeholders
commonGaps: Reviews conducted more than three months after go-live, reducing the value of recollections and observations
Report focuses only on delivery metrics (on time, on budget) without assessing business benefit realisation
No formal sign-off or evidence of stakeholder participation in the review process
Findings are generic rather than specific to the project context
suggestedSources: PMO document repository with completed review reports
Project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) project closure artefacts
IT steering committee meeting packs containing review presentations

evidenceItems.name: Lessons learned documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documented lessons learned from completed technology projects, capturing what went well, what did not, and actionable recommendations for future projects
whatGoodLooksLike: Lessons are specific and actionable rather than vague observations
Captures both positive outcomes to replicate and negative outcomes to prevent
Each lesson has an assigned action to incorporate the learning into processes, standards, or training
Lessons are recorded in a centralised register accessible to all project teams
Evidence that lessons from previous projects influenced the planning of subsequent projects
commonGaps: Lessons learned are documented but never reviewed or acted upon for future projects
Register exists but is not maintained or accessible to project teams
Lessons are too generic to be actionable (e.g., 'improve communication')
No mechanism to verify that lessons learned are incorporated into updated procedures
suggestedSources: Centralised lessons learned register or knowledge base (Confluence, SharePoint)
PMO retrospective meeting records
Project closure documentation packages

evidenceItems.name: Risk mitigation effectiveness assessments
evidenceItems.description: Assessment of whether risk mitigation measures implemented during the project are operating effectively in the production environment
whatGoodLooksLike: Each risk mitigation from the original project risk assessment is evaluated for effectiveness
Assessment references post-go-live data such as incident records, vulnerability scan results, and performance metrics
Mitigations found to be ineffective have documented remediation plans with owners and timelines
Assessment is conducted by personnel with appropriate technical expertise
commonGaps: Risk mitigations from the project phase are not revisited after go-live to verify effectiveness
Assessment is superficial, confirming controls exist without testing whether they actually work
No link between the original project risk register and the post-implementation assessment
Ineffective mitigations are identified but no remediation actions are assigned
suggestedSources: Security operations team post-deployment assessment reports
Incident management system records for the post-go-live period
Vulnerability scanning and penetration testing results

evidenceItems.name: Review completion tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Records tracking the completion status and timeliness of post-implementation reviews across the technology project portfolio
whatGoodLooksLike: Tracker shows all significant projects with their go-live dates and corresponding review completion dates
Completion rates are reported as a metric (e.g., 95% of significant projects reviewed within 3 months)
Overdue reviews are flagged and escalated with reasons for delay documented
Tracker is reviewed by management as part of portfolio governance reporting
commonGaps: No centralised tracking of post-implementation review completion across the portfolio
Reviews that are overdue are not escalated or followed up
No definition of which projects qualify as 'significant' requiring mandatory review
Tracking data exists but is not reported to management
suggestedSources: PMO project portfolio dashboard or tracker
IT steering committee quarterly reporting packs
Project management tool portfolio-level reporting

auditTips: Request the list of all significant technology projects that went live in the past 12 months and verify each has a post-implementation review completed within three months
Sample 3-4 post-implementation review reports and check they assess both business outcomes and technical effectiveness, not just delivery metrics
Verify the lessons learned register is actively maintained and that at least some lessons have demonstrably influenced subsequent project planning or process updates
Check that risk mitigation effectiveness assessments reference actual post-go-live data rather than simply confirming the control was implemented
Ask for evidence that post-implementation review findings have been reported to senior management or the IT steering committee

10.4.title: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework
10.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal SDLC framework exists with security explicitly integrated at every phase, and will sample recent projects to confirm that security checkpoints and quality gates are being followed in practice, not just documented.

evidenceItems.name: SDLC framework document
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved SDLC framework defining all development phases, security integration points, quality gates, and roles and responsibilities for each phase
whatGoodLooksLike: Document covers all SDLC phases: requirements, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance
Each phase has explicitly defined security activities (e.g., threat modelling at design, SAST at development, penetration testing at pre-deployment)
Quality gate criteria are specific and measurable, not vague descriptions
Framework is version-controlled with senior management approval and review within the last 12 months
Applicable to all development methodologies in use (Agile, waterfall, hybrid) with tailored guidance for each
commonGaps: Framework document exists but security is only mentioned in the testing phase, not integrated throughout
Quality gate criteria are subjective (e.g., 'code quality is acceptable') rather than measurable
Framework only covers waterfall methodology while the organisation predominantly uses Agile
Document has not been updated since adoption and does not reflect current tools or practices
suggestedSources: IT governance or development standards document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
CTO or CISO office policy library
Internal audit reports on SDLC compliance

evidenceItems.name: Security checkpoint definitions per phase
evidenceItems.description: Detailed definitions of mandatory security checkpoints at each SDLC phase, including required activities, responsible roles, and completion criteria
whatGoodLooksLike: Each SDLC phase has at least one defined security checkpoint with clear entry and exit criteria
Security activities are specific: threat modelling outputs, secure code review completion, SAST/DAST scan results, security configuration validation
Checkpoint completion criteria define what evidence must be produced before the phase can be considered complete
Roles responsible for conducting and approving each checkpoint are clearly specified
commonGaps: Security checkpoints are defined for some phases but missing for design or maintenance phases
Checkpoint descriptions are too vague to be consistently applied across project teams
No defined evidence requirements, making it difficult to prove checkpoints were completed
Security checkpoints for Agile sprints are not adapted from the waterfall model
suggestedSources: SDLC framework annexes or appendices detailing phase-specific activities
Security team procedural documents for secure development
CI/CD pipeline configuration showing automated security gates

evidenceItems.name: Quality gate criteria and sign-off records
evidenceItems.description: Defined quality gate criteria for phase transitions and records of sign-off decisions for recent development projects
whatGoodLooksLike: Gate criteria include specific thresholds (e.g., zero critical SAST findings, minimum 80% code coverage, performance targets met)
Sign-off records show the date, reviewer, outcome (pass/conditional pass/fail), and any conditions attached
Conditional passes have documented conditions with deadlines and tracking to closure
Consistent application of gate criteria across all projects in the portfolio
commonGaps: Gate criteria exist on paper but sign-off records show all projects passing without any conditions or failures
No evidence of conditional passes or failures suggests gates are rubber-stamped
Criteria differ between projects without documented justification for the variance
Sign-off records are missing for some projects or some phase transitions
suggestedSources: CI/CD pipeline logs showing automated gate checks (Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps)
Project management tool phase transition records
Quality assurance team gate review forms and meeting minutes

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of framework adherence in recent projects
evidenceItems.description: Compliance assessment records or project artefacts demonstrating that recent development projects followed the SDLC framework including all security checkpoints and quality gates
whatGoodLooksLike: Sample of 3-5 recent projects shows complete set of SDLC artefacts aligned to framework requirements
Periodic compliance assessments conducted by QA or internal audit confirm framework adherence
Non-compliance findings are tracked to remediation with evidence of corrective actions taken
Consistent framework application across different development teams and project types
commonGaps: No compliance assessments conducted to verify framework adherence in practice
Framework is followed for large projects but routinely bypassed for smaller changes or urgent fixes
Project artefacts are inconsistent across teams, suggesting different interpretations of the framework
Non-compliance findings identified but not tracked to resolution
suggestedSources: Internal audit SDLC compliance review reports
QA team periodic assessment records
Project documentation repositories showing artefact completeness

auditTips: Request the SDLC framework document and verify that security activities are defined for every phase, not just testing
Select 3-5 recent projects across different sizes and methodologies, and trace each through the SDLC to verify all security checkpoints and quality gates were completed
Review CI/CD pipeline configurations to confirm automated security gates are implemented and cannot be bypassed without documented approval
Check quality gate sign-off records for evidence of conditional passes or failures, as 100% pass rates may indicate rubber-stamping
Verify the framework has been reviewed and updated within the last 12 months and reflects the methodologies actually in use

10.5.title: System Development Life Cycle Methodology
10.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will specifically look for evidence that security and compliance requirements are explicitly documented at the start of development, traceable through to validation, and formally signed off before deployment. They will sample recent projects to verify traceability is maintained in practice.

evidenceItems.name: Security requirements specification documents
evidenceItems.description: Documents capturing explicit security and compliance requirements for development projects, including their regulatory or policy source references
whatGoodLooksLike: Security requirements are specific and testable (e.g., 'All API endpoints must enforce OAuth 2.0 authentication') rather than vague ('system must be secure')
Each requirement references its source: RMiT clause, PDPA section, PCI DSS requirement, or internal policy
Requirements cover key domains: authentication, authorisation, encryption, logging, data protection, and availability
Document is signed off by information security and compliance functions before design phase commences
Requirements are prioritised (mandatory vs. recommended) to support risk-based implementation decisions
commonGaps: Security requirements are absent or limited to generic statements like 'system must comply with security policy'
No regulatory or policy source references, making it impossible to verify completeness of coverage
Requirements document created retrospectively to satisfy audit rather than used during development
Information security team not involved in requirements review or sign-off
suggestedSources: Project documentation repositories (Confluence, SharePoint)
Requirements management tools (Jira, Azure DevOps, IBM DOORS)
Information security team review and sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Requirements traceability matrix
evidenceItems.description: Matrix mapping each security and compliance requirement through design elements, code implementation, test cases, and validation results
whatGoodLooksLike: Every security requirement has a corresponding design element, implementation reference, test case, and validation result
Matrix is maintained as a living document updated throughout the project lifecycle, not created at the end
No gaps in traceability chain — every requirement can be traced from source to validated implementation
Matrix includes status tracking showing requirements as pending, implemented, tested, or validated
Validated by QA lead with sign-off confirming complete traceability before deployment
commonGaps: Traceability matrix created at project end as a retrospective exercise rather than maintained throughout development
Requirements mapped to test cases but not to actual validation results, leaving verification incomplete
Some requirements have no corresponding test cases, indicating they were never validated
Matrix only covers functional requirements, with security requirements traced separately or not at all
suggestedSources: Project management and testing tools (Jira, Azure DevOps, TestRail, Zephyr)
QA team documentation and sign-off records
Requirements management tools with built-in traceability features

evidenceItems.name: Requirements validation records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that each security and compliance requirement was validated through testing prior to production deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Each security requirement has at least one test case with documented execution results (pass/fail)
Failed test cases have linked defect records showing remediation and successful retest
Validation records are dated before the production deployment date
Test coverage for security requirements is reported as a metric (e.g., 100% of security requirements validated)
Sign-off from QA lead confirming all security requirements have been validated
commonGaps: Security requirements listed as validated without corresponding test execution evidence
Test cases exist but execution results are not recorded or are incomplete
Failed security tests not retested after remediation, leaving validation status ambiguous
Validation conducted after deployment rather than before as required
suggestedSources: Test management tools (TestRail, Zephyr, qTest) execution reports
CI/CD pipeline test execution logs
QA team test summary reports per release

evidenceItems.name: Compliance requirements mapping
evidenceItems.description: Mapping of applicable regulatory and compliance requirements (RMiT, PDPA, PCI DSS, etc.) to specific system security requirements for each development project
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive mapping from each applicable regulatory clause to specific system requirements
Mapping covers all relevant regulations: RMiT, PDPA, PCI DSS (where applicable), and internal security policies
Each regulatory requirement maps to one or more specific, testable system requirements
Mapping is reviewed and validated by the compliance function
Updated when regulatory requirements change or new regulations become applicable
commonGaps: Compliance mapping is limited to one regulation and does not cover the full regulatory landscape
Mapping exists at a high level but does not translate into specific, testable system requirements
No compliance function involvement in reviewing or validating the mapping
Mapping is static and not updated when regulations are amended
suggestedSources: Compliance team regulatory requirements registers
GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC) compliance mapping modules
Project security requirements documents with regulatory cross-references

auditTips: Select 3-5 recent projects and request their requirements traceability matrices — verify that security requirements trace from source through to validated test results with no gaps
Check that security requirements specification documents were signed off by the information security function before the design phase, not retrospectively
Verify that compliance requirements mapping covers all applicable regulations, not just RMiT
Look for evidence that failed security requirement validations were remediated and retested before deployment
Ask development teams to demonstrate how their tools enforce traceability links and prevent requirements from being marked complete without validation

10.6.title: Rapid Development Security Requirements
10.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that secure coding standards exist based on recognised practices like OWASP, that all developers have been trained, and that compliance is verified through both code reviews and automated scanning tools with documented results.

evidenceItems.name: Secure coding standards document
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved secure coding standards based on OWASP and industry best practices, tailored to the programming languages and frameworks in use at the institution
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards explicitly reference OWASP Top 10 and OWASP ASVS as foundational sources
Language-specific sections for each technology in use (e.g., Java, .NET, Python, JavaScript) with concrete coding examples
Covers key domains: input validation, output encoding, authentication, session management, cryptography, error handling, and logging
Version-controlled with approval history and reviewed within the last 12 months
Includes a mapping to automated scanning tool rules showing how each standard is enforced
commonGaps: Standards are generic copies of OWASP guidelines without tailoring to institutional technologies and context
Document covers only one programming language while the institution uses multiple
Standards have not been updated to address recent vulnerability classes or OWASP guideline changes
No link between documented standards and the rules configured in automated scanning tools
suggestedSources: Development standards library (Confluence, SharePoint, internal wiki)
CISO or application security team document repository
Automated scanning tool rule configuration exports

evidenceItems.name: Developer training records on secure coding
evidenceItems.description: Records of secure coding training completed by all developers, including training content, completion dates, assessment results, and attendance for contractors and outsourced personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records for 100% of developers including permanent staff, contractors, and outsourced partners
Training content aligned to the institution's secure coding standards and covers OWASP Top 10
Annual completion tracked with dates showing refresher training within the last 12 months for each developer
Assessment or competency verification included (e.g., quiz scores, practical exercises) rather than just attendance
New developer onboarding includes mandatory secure coding training before access to development environments
commonGaps: Training records exist for permanent staff but not for contractors or outsourced development partners
Training is a one-time event with no annual refresh or update when standards change
No competency assessment — only attendance records, providing no assurance of actual learning
Training content is generic and not aligned to the institution's specific secure coding standards
suggestedSources: Learning management system (LMS) completion reports
HR training records database
Vendor management records for contractor training compliance

evidenceItems.name: Code review records referencing coding standards
evidenceItems.description: Records of code reviews conducted for recent development projects, showing that reviewers specifically assessed code against secure coding standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Code review records explicitly reference secure coding standards as review criteria
Review comments include security-specific observations, not just functional or stylistic feedback
All code changes have at least one peer review before merge, with no exceptions
Security-related findings in reviews are tracked to resolution before code is approved
Review coverage metrics are reported (e.g., percentage of commits reviewed)
commonGaps: Code reviews are conducted but focus only on functionality and code style, not security
No explicit reference to secure coding standards in review checklists or comments
Some code changes bypass review (e.g., 'hotfixes' merged without review)
Security findings in reviews are noted but not consistently tracked to resolution
suggestedSources: Source code repository pull request / merge request records (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket)
Code review tool reports (Crucible, Review Board, GitHub PR reviews)
Development team review metrics dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Automated scanning tool configuration and results
evidenceItems.description: Configuration documentation and scan results from SAST and SCA tools showing automated enforcement of secure coding standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Tool configuration shows rules aligned to the institution's secure coding standards and OWASP guidelines
Scanning is integrated into CI/CD pipeline and runs on every code commit or pull request
Results show trend data over time demonstrating improving compliance or consistent enforcement
Critical and high findings block pipeline progression until remediated
Regular tuning of rules to reduce false positives while maintaining detection accuracy
commonGaps: Scanning tools are installed but use default rule sets not aligned to institutional standards
Scans run periodically (e.g., monthly) rather than on every code change
Results show persistent unresolved critical findings indicating scanning without remediation
Tool output is not reviewed by developers or security team, making it a checkbox exercise
suggestedSources: SAST tool dashboards and reports (SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify, Snyk Code)
SCA tool reports (Snyk, Black Duck, WhiteSource/Mend)
CI/CD pipeline configuration showing tool integration

auditTips: Request the secure coding standards and verify they explicitly reference OWASP and are tailored to each programming language in use, not just a generic copy
Pull the developer training completion report and check for 100% coverage including contractors and outsourced developers, with evidence of annual refresh
Sample 10-15 recent pull requests and verify each has a documented code review with security-specific comments referencing the coding standards
Review SAST tool configuration to confirm rules are aligned to institutional standards and scan on every commit, not just periodically
Ask for trend data on automated scan findings over the last 6-12 months to assess whether the institution is genuinely improving code security or just scanning without acting

10.7.title: Production Environment Segregation
10.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that both peer code reviews and SAST scanning are mandatory for all code before production deployment, that critical and high findings are remediated before release, and that any exceptions have formal risk acceptance documentation signed by the appropriate authority.

evidenceItems.name: Code review policy and procedures
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy and procedures mandating peer code review and SAST for all application code prior to production deployment, including exception handling processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly states that both peer code review and SAST are mandatory for all code before production
Defines minimum reviewer qualifications (e.g., completed secure coding training, approved reviewer list)
Specifies what constitutes an exception and the formal risk acceptance procedure required
Covers all code types including internally developed, third-party customisations, and integration scripts
Approved by CISO or CTO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Policy exists for SAST but peer code review is not mandated or is discretionary
Policy exempts certain code types such as configuration scripts, database changes, or vendor patches
Exception handling is informal — no defined risk acceptance procedure or authority level
Policy not updated to cover new development platforms or languages adopted by the institution
suggestedSources: CISO or development standards policy library
IT governance document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Internal audit reports on code review compliance

evidenceItems.name: SAST tool configuration and scan results
evidenceItems.description: Configuration documentation showing SAST tools integrated into CI/CD pipelines with appropriate rule sets, and scan results for recent releases showing compliance with the mandatory gate policy
whatGoodLooksLike: SAST tool is integrated into the CI/CD pipeline and runs automatically on every code commit or pull request
Rule sets are configured to align with institutional secure coding standards and OWASP guidelines
Pipeline configuration shows that critical and high findings block deployment progression
Scan results for recent releases show zero unresolved critical/high findings at deployment time
Historical trend data shows declining finding counts, indicating genuine code quality improvement
commonGaps: SAST tool exists but is not integrated into the CI/CD pipeline, requiring manual execution
Default rule sets used without customisation to institutional standards
Pipeline gate can be easily bypassed by developers without documented approval
Scan results show persistent unresolved critical findings across multiple releases
suggestedSources: SAST tool dashboards (SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify, Snyk Code)
CI/CD pipeline configuration (Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps) showing SAST integration
Release gate compliance reports from deployment tools

evidenceItems.name: Remediation records for critical and high findings
evidenceItems.description: Records tracking the remediation of critical and high-severity SAST findings from identification through to verified resolution
whatGoodLooksLike: Each critical/high finding has a tracking record with identification date, assigned developer, target remediation date, and actual closure date
Remediation SLAs are met consistently (e.g., critical within 48 hours, high within 7 days)
Remediated findings are verified through rescan confirming the vulnerability is resolved
Remediation metrics are tracked and reported showing trends over time
No critical or high findings remain open beyond their defined SLA without documented escalation
commonGaps: Findings are identified but not assigned to specific developers for remediation
No defined SLAs for remediation timelines based on severity
Remediation is claimed but no verification rescan confirms the fix
Large backlog of unresolved critical/high findings indicating scanning without remediation
suggestedSources: SAST tool finding management and tracking reports
Issue tracking system (Jira, Azure DevOps) security defect records
Development team sprint retrospectives showing security remediation activities

evidenceItems.name: Risk acceptance records for exceptions
evidenceItems.description: Formal risk acceptance records for cases where code with known critical or high findings was deployed to production, including compensating controls and approval by the designated authority
whatGoodLooksLike: Each exception has a formal risk acceptance form documenting the specific vulnerability, business justification, and compensating controls
Risk acceptance approved by CISO or CTO (not by the development team requesting the exception)
Time-limited approval with a defined remediation deadline and follow-up tracking
Compensating controls are specific and verifiable (e.g., WAF rule, network segmentation, enhanced monitoring)
Exception register is regularly reviewed with expired exceptions escalated for remediation or re-approval
commonGaps: Exceptions are granted informally via email without standardised risk assessment
Risk acceptance approved by development managers rather than CISO or equivalent authority
No defined expiry or remediation deadline for accepted exceptions, creating permanent risk
Compensating controls are vague (e.g., 'we will monitor') without specific implementation details
suggestedSources: Risk acceptance forms or register maintained by CISO office
GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC) exception management module
Change management system exception records

auditTips: Request CI/CD pipeline configurations and verify that SAST is configured as a mandatory gate that blocks deployment for critical/high findings
Sample 10 recent production deployments and verify each has both peer code review records and SAST scan results with no unresolved critical/high findings
Review the risk acceptance register for any exceptions and verify each has proper documentation, CISO approval, compensating controls, and a remediation deadline
Check SAST finding trend data over the last 12 months — a flat or increasing trend of critical findings suggests remediation is not keeping pace with new code
Attempt to identify code that was deployed without review or scanning by checking for direct commits to production branches or pipeline bypass patterns

10.8.title: System Testing Methodology
10.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that comprehensive testing standards exist covering functional, integration, performance, and security testing, and will sample recent releases to confirm that test plans were documented, executed, and formally approved before production deployment.

evidenceItems.name: Testing standards document
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved testing standards defining mandatory test types, acceptance criteria, documentation requirements, and approval procedures for all production deployments
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards explicitly require functional, integration, performance, and security testing for every production deployment
Defines specific acceptance criteria for each test type (e.g., zero critical defects, response time thresholds, security scan pass criteria)
Specifies test documentation requirements including test plans, test cases, execution results, and defect reports
Approved by CTO or Head of IT and reviewed within the last 12 months
Addresses both manual and automated testing approaches with guidance on when each is appropriate
commonGaps: Standards mention all four test types but only functional testing has detailed criteria; others are vague
Performance testing criteria are generic and not aligned to actual production workload profiles
Security testing is not explicitly mandated as part of the standard testing cycle
Standards have not been updated to reflect current testing tools and practices
suggestedSources: QA team standards library (Confluence, SharePoint)
IT governance document repository
Internal audit testing compliance review reports

evidenceItems.name: Test plan templates and completed test plans
evidenceItems.description: Standardised test plan templates and completed test plans for recent releases showing comprehensive coverage of all mandatory test types
whatGoodLooksLike: Template includes sections for each mandatory test type: functional, integration, performance, and security
Completed plans define specific test scenarios, test data requirements, expected results, and pass/fail criteria
Performance test plans specify load profiles based on production traffic patterns and peak scenarios
Security test plans reference OWASP testing guide and institution-specific threat scenarios
Test plans are reviewed and approved before test execution commences
commonGaps: Test plans focus heavily on functional testing with minimal coverage of integration, performance, and security
Plans use generic test scenarios rather than project-specific scenarios based on requirements
No performance testing load profiles, making it impossible to verify production readiness
Test plans created retrospectively to satisfy audit rather than used to guide testing
suggestedSources: Test management tools (TestRail, Zephyr, qTest, Azure Test Plans)
Project documentation repositories with test deliverables
QA team shared drives with completed test plans per release

evidenceItems.name: Test execution results and defect reports
evidenceItems.description: Documented test execution results showing pass/fail outcomes for all test cases, and defect reports for failed tests with remediation and retest evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Test execution results show individual test case outcomes with timestamps and tester identification
Pass rates are calculated and reported for each test type
Failed test cases have linked defect records in the issue tracking system
Defect reports show severity classification, root cause, fix implementation, and successful retest
All defects are resolved or formally accepted before deployment approval
commonGaps: Test execution recorded at summary level only, without individual test case results
Failed tests noted but defect records not created or linked back to the test case
Defects raised but not retested after remediation, leaving test outcomes ambiguous
Results show 100% pass rate without any defects, which is unrealistic and suggests inadequate testing
suggestedSources: Test management tools execution reports and defect reports
Issue tracking system (Jira, Azure DevOps) defect entries with test case links
CI/CD pipeline automated test execution logs

evidenceItems.name: Test result approval records
evidenceItems.description: Formal approval records showing that test results were reviewed and approved by QA lead and business representative before production deployment was authorised
whatGoodLooksLike: Approval form or record shows sign-off from both QA lead and designated business representative
Approval is dated before the production deployment date
Approval references the specific test summary report and confirms all acceptance criteria were met
Any outstanding defects at time of approval are documented with agreed severity and risk acceptance
Conditional approvals clearly state the conditions and deadlines for resolution
commonGaps: Test results approved by development team rather than independent QA function
No business representative sign-off, only technical approval
Approval obtained after deployment rather than before, indicating test gates are not enforced
Generic approval without reference to specific test results or acceptance criteria
suggestedSources: Release management tool approval workflows
Change management system (ServiceNow) test approval records
QA team sign-off forms and email approvals

auditTips: Select 5 recent production deployments and verify each has documented test plans covering functional, integration, performance, and security testing
Check that test result approvals were obtained before deployment dates, not retrospectively — compare sign-off dates against deployment logs
Review performance test plans and verify they include load profiles based on actual production traffic, not arbitrary numbers
Look for evidence of security testing (DAST, vulnerability scanning) as part of the standard release cycle, not only for major releases
Examine defect records linked to test failures and confirm remediation was verified through retesting before deployment approval

10.9.title: Scope of System Testing
10.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that UAT is conducted by business users in an environment mirroring production, that results are formally signed off by business owners before deployment, and that production data in UAT environments is appropriately masked or anonymised.

evidenceItems.name: UAT procedures and sign-off requirements
evidenceItems.description: Formal UAT procedures defining environment standards, testing process, acceptance criteria, data masking requirements, and business owner sign-off process
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures specify that UAT must be conducted by business users, not IT or development staff
Defines mandatory environment requirements including production parity standards
Includes data masking requirements with specific techniques (tokenisation, anonymisation, synthetic data)
Sign-off process requires named business owner approval with specific fields confirming acceptance criteria met
Procedures approved by Head of IT and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Procedures exist but do not mandate business user participation — UAT is conducted by IT staff
No specific environment parity requirements, allowing UAT in significantly different environments
Data masking requirements mentioned but no specific techniques or verification procedures defined
Sign-off process is informal — email approval rather than structured sign-off with acceptance criteria confirmation
suggestedSources: QA team procedures library (Confluence, SharePoint)
IT governance document repository
Project management methodology documentation

evidenceItems.name: UAT environment specification and comparison to production
evidenceItems.description: Documentation specifying the UAT environment configuration and formal comparison against the production environment to demonstrate parity
whatGoodLooksLike: Side-by-side comparison document showing UAT vs production configurations for infrastructure, software versions, and integrations
Documented differences with justification and risk assessment for any variance from production
Environment refresh schedule showing regular updates to maintain production parity
Evidence of environment validation checks before each UAT cycle commences
Network architecture diagram showing UAT environment topology
commonGaps: No formal comparison document — environment parity is assumed rather than verified
UAT environment significantly behind production in software versions or patches
Integration points in UAT use stubs or mocks instead of actual system connections, reducing test validity
Environment specification is outdated and does not reflect recent production changes
suggestedSources: Infrastructure team environment specification documents
CMDB (ServiceNow, BMC) environment configuration records
Cloud management platform (AWS, Azure) environment comparison reports

evidenceItems.name: UAT sign-off records from business owners
evidenceItems.description: Formal sign-off records from designated business owners confirming UAT completion and acceptance of system readiness for production deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Sign-off form includes the business owner's name, role, date, and explicit confirmation that acceptance criteria are met
References the specific UAT test results and any outstanding defects with agreed disposition
Sign-off dated before the production deployment date
Business owner is the appropriate authority (department head or product owner, not a junior delegate)
Consistent use of standardised sign-off forms across all projects
commonGaps: Sign-off obtained after deployment rather than before, indicating the gate is not enforced
Generic approval without reference to specific test results or acceptance criteria
Sign-off delegated to IT staff rather than a genuine business owner
No documentation of outstanding defect disposition at the time of sign-off
suggestedSources: Project management tool UAT completion records
Release management approval workflows (ServiceNow, Jira)
PMO project closure documentation with UAT sign-off forms

evidenceItems.name: Data masking procedures for UAT environments
evidenceItems.description: Procedures and evidence for masking or anonymising production data used in UAT environments, including masking techniques, verification steps, and compliance records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented masking procedures specify techniques for each data type (e.g., tokenisation for card numbers, pseudonymisation for names, synthetic generation for test data)
Masking is applied consistently using automated tools rather than manual processes
Verification checks confirm masking effectiveness before data is made available in UAT
Audit trail showing when data was masked, by whom, and verification results
Periodic compliance checks confirm no unmasked production data exists in UAT environments
commonGaps: Production data used in UAT without any masking, exposing real customer information
Masking applied to some data fields but not comprehensively — sensitive fields (IC numbers, phone numbers) left unmasked
Manual masking process prone to error and inconsistency
No verification step to confirm masking was applied correctly before UAT commences
suggestedSources: Data masking tool configurations and execution logs (Informatica, Delphix, IBM Optim)
DBA team data provisioning procedures and records
PDPA compliance audit reports covering non-production data handling

auditTips: Request UAT sign-off records for 5 recent deployments and verify sign-off dates precede deployment dates, with named business owners as signatories
Ask to see the UAT environment configuration and compare it against production — look for significant version differences or missing integration points
Verify data masking by requesting evidence of masking tool execution logs or sample masked data from the UAT environment
Check whether UAT was conducted by actual business users by reviewing UAT test execution logs for tester identities
Ask for the data masking verification report and confirm that all sensitive data types (IC numbers, account numbers, contact details) are covered

10.10.title: Source Code Review for Critical Systems
10.10.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal change management policy exists covering all production change types, that changes are risk-assessed and approved before implementation, and that a complete change log is maintained. They will sample recent changes to verify the process is followed in practice.

evidenceItems.name: Change management policy document
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved policy governing all changes to production systems, defining change categories, risk assessment requirements, approval workflows, and documentation standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy scope explicitly covers applications, infrastructure, databases, network configurations, and security controls
Defines clear change categories (standard, normal, major, emergency) with criteria for classification
Specifies approval authorities for each change category with named roles or positions
Includes requirements for risk assessment, testing, rollback planning, and post-change verification
Approved by the board or CTO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Policy covers application changes but not infrastructure, database, or configuration changes
Change categories defined but classification criteria are vague, leading to inconsistent categorisation
Emergency change procedures referenced but not defined (see clause 10.11)
Policy has not been updated to cover cloud infrastructure changes or DevOps practices
suggestedSources: IT governance policy library (SharePoint, Confluence)
Board or IT steering committee meeting minutes showing policy approval
Internal audit change management compliance reports

evidenceItems.name: Change advisory board terms of reference
evidenceItems.description: Terms of reference for the Change Advisory Board defining membership, meeting frequency, scope of authority, and decision-making procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Membership includes IT operations, information security, affected business units, and risk management representatives
Meeting frequency is defined (e.g., weekly) with provisions for ad-hoc meetings for urgent changes
Scope of authority clearly defines which change categories require CAB review vs. delegated approval
Decision-making procedures include quorum requirements and escalation paths for disputed changes
Terms of reference are current and reflect actual CAB composition and practices
commonGaps: CAB exists but membership does not include information security or risk management representation
Terms of reference are outdated and do not reflect current membership or meeting practices
CAB reviews all changes regardless of risk, creating bottlenecks, or reviews none, providing no oversight
No documented decision-making criteria — CAB approvals are based on informal discussion without structured assessment
suggestedSources: CAB terms of reference document
CAB meeting minutes and attendance records
IT governance committee structure documentation

evidenceItems.name: Sample change records with risk assessment and approvals
evidenceItems.description: Representative change records demonstrating the complete change lifecycle including request, risk assessment, testing evidence, approval, implementation, and post-change verification
whatGoodLooksLike: Each change record contains a completed risk assessment with impact and likelihood ratings
Testing evidence is attached or referenced showing the change was tested before deployment
Approval is documented from the appropriate authority level based on the change category
Rollback plan is documented and post-change verification results are recorded
Change records from different categories (standard, normal, major) demonstrate proportionate controls
commonGaps: Risk assessments are present but consist of generic ratings without specific impact analysis
Testing evidence missing or limited to 'tested in dev' without detailed results
Approvals obtained after implementation rather than before, indicating process bypass
Post-change verification not recorded, making it impossible to confirm successful implementation
suggestedSources: Change management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
CAB meeting minutes with change review decisions
Release management tool deployment records

evidenceItems.name: Change log or register
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive log of all production changes recording the complete change lifecycle from request through post-implementation verification
whatGoodLooksLike: Log includes every production change regardless of category, with no gaps or missing entries
Each entry records change ID, description, category, risk rating, requester, approver, implementation date, and outcome
Log is searchable and filterable by date range, category, system, and outcome
Failed changes are recorded with root cause and remediation actions
Log reconciles with deployment records and infrastructure change evidence to verify completeness
commonGaps: Change log only includes formally raised changes, missing ad-hoc or emergency changes
Log entries are incomplete — missing risk assessments, approvals, or implementation outcomes
Infrastructure changes (firewall rules, server patches, network changes) not recorded in the change log
No reconciliation between change log and actual production deployments, allowing unrecorded changes
suggestedSources: Change management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) change log exports
Infrastructure change tracking tools
Deployment pipeline logs cross-referenced with change records

auditTips: Pull the complete change log for the last 6 months and verify that each change has a risk assessment, approval, and post-change verification recorded
Cross-reference the change log against deployment pipeline logs to identify any production deployments that do not have corresponding change records
Sample 10 recent changes across different categories and trace each through the full lifecycle to verify process compliance
Check that the change management policy scope covers all change types including infrastructure, database, and configuration changes, not just application deployments
Review CAB meeting minutes for the last quarter and verify attendance includes security and risk management representation

10.11.title: Independent Review of System Changes
10.11.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether emergency change procedures have strict eligibility criteria, whether all emergency changes undergo retrospective review and approval within defined timeframes, and whether emergency change frequency is monitored and reported to management.

evidenceItems.name: Emergency change procedure document
evidenceItems.description: Formal procedure defining the criteria, approval process, implementation requirements, and retrospective review process for emergency changes that bypass normal change management
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear, restrictive criteria for emergency classification (e.g., active security breach, critical service outage, imminent regulatory violation)
Defines minimum approval requirements (e.g., two authorised personnel from IT management and security)
Specifies mandatory documentation even during the emergency (change description, justification, approver, implementer)
Retrospective review timeline is defined (e.g., within five business days) with escalation for non-compliance
Procedure is distinct from and cross-referenced with the standard change management policy
commonGaps: Emergency criteria are broad and subjective, allowing routine changes to be classified as emergencies
Single-person approval sufficient, creating inadequate oversight for high-risk changes
No defined timeline for retrospective review, allowing emergency changes to remain permanently unreviewed
Procedure does not address how to handle emergency changes that subsequently fail or cause additional issues
suggestedSources: Change management policy annexes or companion procedures
IT operations procedure library (SharePoint, Confluence)
CAB terms of reference (section on emergency changes)

evidenceItems.name: Emergency change records with retrospective approvals
evidenceItems.description: Records of emergency changes implemented with evidence of retrospective review and formal approval by the CAB or designated authority
whatGoodLooksLike: Each emergency change has a record capturing the emergency justification, real-time approval, implementer details, and implementation timestamp
Retrospective review is documented within the defined timeframe (e.g., five business days) with CAB meeting minutes as evidence
Retrospective review assesses whether the emergency classification was appropriate and the change was implemented correctly
Retrospective approval is formally recorded with the reviewing authority's sign-off
Changes that fail retrospective review have documented remediation actions
commonGaps: Emergency changes are implemented but retrospective reviews are not completed within the defined timeframe
Retrospective review is a formality — all emergency changes are approved without genuine scrutiny of the emergency classification
No documentation of the original emergency justification, making retrospective assessment impossible
Some emergency changes have no retrospective review record at all
suggestedSources: Change management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) emergency change records
CAB meeting minutes showing retrospective emergency change reviews
Incident management system records linked to emergency changes

evidenceItems.name: Management reports on emergency change frequency
evidenceItems.description: Regular reports to management on the volume, nature, and trends of emergency changes, including analysis of root causes and process compliance
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly reports showing emergency change count, percentage of total changes, and trend over time
Analysis of emergency change root causes (e.g., production incidents, security vulnerabilities, failed standard changes)
Retrospective review completion rates reported as a compliance metric
Trend analysis identifying systemic issues (e.g., increasing emergency changes for a particular system indicating instability)
Reports presented to IT steering committee or senior management with documented acknowledgement
commonGaps: No regular reporting on emergency change frequency to management
Reports show counts only without analysis of root causes or trends
High emergency change rates reported but no investigation into underlying causes
Retrospective review compliance rates not tracked or reported
suggestedSources: Change management system reporting and dashboards
IT operations monthly management reports
IT steering committee meeting packs

evidenceItems.name: Retrospective review records
evidenceItems.description: Detailed records of retrospective reviews conducted for emergency changes, assessing appropriateness of the emergency classification and correctness of the implementation
whatGoodLooksLike: Each review assesses whether the change genuinely met emergency criteria or should have been handled through standard process
Review evaluates the technical adequacy of the change implementation and any post-change issues
Findings from reviews feed into process improvement actions (e.g., if root cause was inadequate testing, testing standards are updated)
Reviews completed within the defined timeframe with documented attendance and decisions
Inappropriately classified emergency changes are flagged with corrective action for the requesting team
commonGaps: Retrospective reviews are superficial, confirming the change was successful without assessing classification appropriateness
Reviews completed well beyond the defined timeframe with no escalation
No mechanism to identify or flag inappropriately classified emergency changes
Review findings are not linked to process improvement actions
suggestedSources: CAB meeting minutes with emergency change retrospective agenda items
Change management system retrospective review workflow records
Post-incident review reports that triggered emergency changes

auditTips: Request all emergency changes from the last 6 months and verify each has a retrospective review completed within the defined timeframe
Calculate the emergency change ratio (emergency vs. total changes) and investigate if it exceeds 10%, which often indicates process abuse
Sample 5 emergency changes and assess whether the emergency justification genuinely met the defined criteria or whether standard process could have been used
Verify that the emergency change procedure defines strict eligibility criteria, not broad statements like 'urgent business need'
Check for any emergency changes that were not subsequently reviewed by the CAB, as this indicates a governance gap

10.12.title: Third Party Service Provider System Changes
10.12.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a structured release management process exists with mandatory rollback procedures, deployment verification steps, and defined success criteria for every production release. They will sample recent releases to confirm these elements are present and followed.

evidenceItems.name: Release management procedure document
evidenceItems.description: Formal procedure governing the controlled deployment of changes to production environments, including planning requirements, approval gates, and post-deployment verification
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure defines mandatory release plan contents: rollback procedure, deployment steps, verification checks, and success criteria
Specifies approval gates including who must approve before deployment can proceed
Includes release scheduling guidelines (approved release windows, blackout periods for critical business dates)
Addresses different release types (major release, minor release, hotfix) with proportionate requirements
Approved by Head of IT and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Procedure exists for major releases but hotfixes and minor changes bypass the release management process
Rollback procedures mentioned as a requirement but no guidance on what constitutes an adequate rollback plan
No defined release windows or blackout periods, allowing deployments during high-risk business periods
Procedure does not address automated deployment pipelines or CI/CD practices
suggestedSources: IT operations procedure library (SharePoint, Confluence)
Change management policy companion documents
DevOps team runbook and deployment documentation

evidenceItems.name: Sample release plans with rollback procedures
evidenceItems.description: Completed release plans for recent production deployments including documented rollback procedures, deployment steps, and success criteria
whatGoodLooksLike: Release plan includes step-by-step deployment instructions with responsible personnel identified
Rollback procedure is specific and tested, with clear trigger criteria for when rollback should be initiated
Success criteria are measurable (e.g., application response time below 200ms, zero error rate for key transactions, all monitoring green)
Communication plan identifies stakeholders to be notified before, during, and after deployment
Release plan is approved before deployment execution with sign-off dates preceding deployment dates
commonGaps: Release plans are generic templates without project-specific rollback procedures
Rollback procedure is simply 'restore from backup' without specific steps, timing, or verification
Success criteria are vague (e.g., 'system is working') rather than measurable thresholds
Release plan created after deployment as a retrospective documentation exercise
suggestedSources: Release management tool or change management system attached documents
DevOps team deployment runbooks
Project management tool release deliverables

evidenceItems.name: Deployment verification records
evidenceItems.description: Records of post-deployment verification checks conducted after each production release, confirming that the deployment was successful and the system is operating correctly
whatGoodLooksLike: Verification records show each check performed, expected result, actual result, and pass/fail outcome
Checks cover application health, key transaction validation, integration endpoints, and monitoring baseline
Verification completed within the defined post-deployment window (e.g., within 2 hours of deployment)
Failed verification checks have documented escalation and resolution actions
Automated verification results from deployment pipelines are captured and stored
commonGaps: No formal verification records — deployment success assumed unless incidents are reported
Verification limited to 'application is up' without functional or performance validation
Verification checks not defined in advance, leading to inconsistent post-deployment assessment
Automated verification exists in the pipeline but results are not stored for audit purposes
suggestedSources: CI/CD pipeline post-deployment verification logs (Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps)
Monitoring system health check dashboards (Grafana, Datadog, New Relic)
Operations team deployment checklists and sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Release success criteria documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation defining the success criteria for production releases including measurable thresholds and the process for confirming criteria are met
whatGoodLooksLike: Success criteria defined per release with specific, measurable thresholds (response times, error rates, availability percentages)
Criteria agreed with business stakeholders before deployment
Post-deployment assessment against criteria is documented with timestamps
Process for handling releases that do not meet success criteria is defined (rollback trigger, incident escalation)
Historical success criteria and outcomes are tracked for trend analysis
commonGaps: No defined success criteria — deployment considered successful unless a major incident occurs
Criteria are technical only without business-level success measures
Criteria defined but not assessed post-deployment, making them purely theoretical
No defined process for what happens when success criteria are not met
suggestedSources: Release plan documentation with success criteria sections
Service level agreement documentation for production systems
Operations team post-deployment assessment reports

auditTips: Sample 5 recent production releases and verify each has a documented release plan with specific rollback procedures, not generic templates
Check that deployment verification records exist for each release and include functional checks, not just 'application is running' confirmation
Verify that release plans were approved before deployment by comparing sign-off dates to actual deployment timestamps
Ask for evidence of a release that required rollback to verify the rollback procedure was actually tested and executable
Review release scheduling records to confirm that deployments during high-risk business periods (month-end, salary days) are avoided or specifically risk-assessed

10.13.title: Critical System Decommissioning
10.13.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that development, testing, staging, and production environments are strictly separated with enforced access controls, that code migration follows an auditable process through defined environment tiers, and that no production data is accessible from non-production environments without masking.

evidenceItems.name: Environment separation architecture documentation
evidenceItems.description: Architecture documentation showing the separation between development, testing, staging, and production environments including infrastructure, network, and access control designs
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear architecture diagrams showing distinct infrastructure for each environment tier
Network segmentation details including firewall rules, VLANs, or cloud security group configurations
Database separation documented with distinct connection strings and credentials per environment
Code migration pathways clearly illustrated showing the promotion path between environments
Documentation is current and reflects the actual deployed infrastructure (verified within the last 12 months)
commonGaps: Architecture documentation exists but is outdated and does not reflect current infrastructure
Diagrams show logical separation but physical infrastructure is shared (e.g., same database server for dev and production)
Cloud environments not covered — on-premises separation documented but cloud deployments use shared resources
No documentation of network segmentation rules between environments
suggestedSources: Enterprise architecture repository (Sparx EA, LeanIX, Visio diagrams)
Cloud management console screenshots (AWS VPC, Azure VNet, GCP VPC configurations)
Network team firewall rule documentation

evidenceItems.name: Access control lists per environment
evidenceItems.description: Documented access control configurations for each environment showing role-based access restrictions and segregation between development and production personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Access control lists clearly show different user groups per environment with production restricted to operations personnel
Developers have no standing access to production — only temporary, approved access through privileged access management
Quarterly access review records confirm compliance with the defined access model
Service account access is documented and segregated per environment
Access control configurations match the documented policy requirements
commonGaps: Developers have standing access to production environments, violating segregation of duties
Access control lists exist but are not regularly reviewed, leading to access creep
Shared service accounts used across environments, undermining separation controls
Production access granted on a temporary basis but never revoked after the need expires
suggestedSources: Identity and access management system (Active Directory, Azure AD, AWS IAM) group membership reports
Privileged access management tool (CyberArk, BeyondTrust) access logs
Quarterly access review reports from CISO or IT security team

evidenceItems.name: Code migration procedures and audit trail
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures for migrating code between environments and audit trail records showing all code promotions through the environment tiers
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures mandate that code follows the promotion path: development to testing to staging to production
CI/CD pipeline configurations enforce the promotion path and prevent direct deployment to production from development
Audit trail shows every code migration with timestamp, migrated artefact, environment source and target, and initiating user
No evidence of direct production deployments bypassing intermediate environments
Pipeline bypass requires documented emergency change approval
commonGaps: Procedures exist but developers can deploy directly to production outside the pipeline
Audit trail incomplete — not all code migrations are logged, particularly manual deployments
No automated enforcement of the promotion path — policy compliance relies on developer discipline
Emergency deployments bypass environment tiers without documented approval
suggestedSources: CI/CD pipeline configuration and execution logs (Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps)
Source code repository branch protection rules and merge logs
Deployment tool audit logs showing deployment history per environment

evidenceItems.name: Network segmentation evidence between environments
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence of network-level separation between environments including firewall rules, VLAN configurations, and security group settings
whatGoodLooksLike: Firewall rules explicitly block direct communication between development/testing and production networks
Each environment has dedicated network segments (VLANs, subnets, or cloud VPCs) with documented IP ranges
Security group rules in cloud environments restrict inter-environment traffic to approved integration points only
Regular network segmentation testing confirms that controls are effective
Network configuration matches the documented architecture
commonGaps: Logical environment separation exists but environments share the same network segment
Firewall rules allow broad access between environments rather than restricting to specific required paths
Cloud security groups are overly permissive, allowing any instance to communicate across environments
No regular testing to verify that network segmentation controls are functioning as intended
suggestedSources: Firewall configuration exports (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco ASA)
Cloud management console security group and VPC configurations
Network segmentation penetration testing reports

auditTips: Request network diagrams showing environment separation and cross-reference with actual firewall rules or cloud security group configurations to verify enforcement
Check production access control lists for developer accounts — any developer with standing production access is a finding
Review CI/CD pipeline configurations to confirm that direct deployment from development to production is technically prevented, not just procedurally discouraged
Sample recent code migrations and trace them through the environment promotion path to verify the defined process was followed
Request evidence of quarterly production access reviews and verify that temporary access grants were revoked after their approved period

10.14.title: Automated Development and Security Tools
10.14.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that source code is stored in access-controlled, version-controlled repositories with complete audit trails, that access is restricted to authorised personnel, and that source code is not present in production environments.

evidenceItems.name: Source code management policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining requirements for source code protection including access controls, version control, backup, audit logging, and prohibition of source code in production
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy covers all aspects: access control, version control, backup, audit logging, and production exclusion
Defines role-based access requirements specifying who can read, write, and administer repositories
Includes requirements for outsourced development scenarios covering vendor access controls and code ownership
Specifies audit log retention period of at least seven years
Approved by CISO or CTO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Policy covers version control but does not address access control, backup, or production exclusion requirements
Outsourced development code protection requirements not addressed
No specific requirements for audit log retention, leaving it to default system settings
Policy has not been updated to cover cloud-hosted repositories (GitHub, GitLab SaaS, Azure DevOps)
suggestedSources: CISO or development standards policy library
IT governance document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Information security policy framework

evidenceItems.name: Repository access control configurations
evidenceItems.description: Configuration evidence showing access controls on source code repositories with role-based permissions restricting access to authorised development personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Repository access configured with role-based groups (e.g., developers, reviewers, administrators) with appropriate permissions
No anonymous or public access to any repositories containing institutional source code
Service accounts with repository access are documented with their purpose and owning team
Quarterly access review evidence shows regular verification of repository access lists
Multi-factor authentication required for repository access
commonGaps: Repositories have overly broad access — all IT staff can access all repositories regardless of project involvement
Former employees or role-changed staff retain repository access
Service accounts with broad access are not documented or regularly reviewed
No MFA requirement for repository access, especially for cloud-hosted repositories
suggestedSources: Source code repository platform access settings (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps)
Identity provider group membership reports for repository access groups
Quarterly access review records from development management

evidenceItems.name: Version control system logs
evidenceItems.description: Audit logs from version control systems showing all code changes, access events, and administrative actions with user identification and timestamps
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs capture all commits, merges, branch creation/deletion, permission changes, and access events
Each log entry includes user identity, timestamp, action performed, and affected repository/branch
Logs are retained for a minimum of seven years in tamper-evident storage
Log aggregation into a central SIEM for monitoring and alerting on suspicious activities
Regular review of logs for anomalous patterns (e.g., bulk code downloads, off-hours access)
commonGaps: Version control logs capture commits but not access events or administrative actions
Logs retained only for default system retention periods (e.g., 90 days) rather than seven years
No centralised log collection — logs reside only on the version control platform
No monitoring or alerting on version control log events
suggestedSources: Source code repository platform audit log exports
SIEM system (Splunk, QRadar, Wazuh) with version control log feeds
Git server or hosting platform administrative logs

evidenceItems.name: Evidence that source code is not in production environments
evidenceItems.description: Verification evidence confirming that production environments do not contain source code, with only compiled or built deployment artefacts present
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated scanning or manual inspection reports confirm no source code files (.java, .py, .js source, .cs) on production servers
CI/CD pipeline configuration shows that only compiled artefacts (JAR, WAR, Docker images, minified bundles) are deployed to production
Deployment process documentation explicitly excludes source code from production deployment packages
Periodic verification checks are conducted (e.g., quarterly file system audits of production servers)
For interpreted languages, verification confirms that source code is minified/obfuscated and not in raw form
commonGaps: Source code deployed directly to production servers (common with interpreted languages like Python or Node.js)
No verification process exists — source code exclusion is assumed but never confirmed
Git repositories cloned onto production servers for deployment rather than using built artefacts
Debug or development files left on production servers after troubleshooting sessions
suggestedSources: CI/CD pipeline deployment stage configurations showing artefact-only deployment
Production server file system audit reports
Infrastructure security scanning reports covering production environments

auditTips: Request repository access reports and verify that access is restricted to current development team members, with no former employees or non-development personnel having access
Check branch protection rules on main/release branches to confirm that direct commits are blocked and pull request review is enforced
Verify that version control audit logs are retained for seven years and include access events, not just commit history
Ask for evidence that production servers have been inspected for source code — look for git repositories, source files, or development tools on production
Review CI/CD pipeline deployment configurations to confirm that only built artefacts (not source code) are deployed to production environments

10.15.title: Third Party Software Supply Chain Risk
10.15.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution maintains an accurate CMDB with complete asset inventory and interdependency mapping, that configuration baselines are established for critical assets, and that deviations are detected, assessed, and remediated through a governed process.

evidenceItems.name: Configuration management policy and procedures
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy and procedures defining configuration management requirements including CMDB maintenance, baseline management, deviation handling, and compliance reporting
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures define CMDB scope covering all technology asset categories: hardware, software, network, cloud resources
Baseline establishment process includes approval by the relevant technical authority
Deviation management workflow defines detection, assessment, escalation, and remediation steps
CMDB maintenance responsibilities and data quality requirements are clearly assigned
Procedures approved by Head of IT and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Procedures cover CMDB maintenance but do not address configuration baselines or deviation management
No defined process for baseline approval — baselines are set informally without governance
Deviation handling is reactive (addressed only when incidents occur) rather than proactive
Cloud resources not included in the CMDB scope, creating a growing blind spot
