suggestedSources: IT operations procedure library (SharePoint, Confluence)
ITSM framework documentation (ITIL-aligned processes)
IT governance policy repository

evidenceItems.name: Configuration management database or inventory
evidenceItems.description: The CMDB or technology asset inventory containing records of all technology assets, their configurations, relationships, ownership, and lifecycle status
whatGoodLooksLike: CMDB contains records for all technology assets including servers, databases, network devices, applications, and cloud services
Each record includes current configuration details, owning team, lifecycle status, and criticality rating
Relationship mapping shows interdependencies between assets (e.g., application X depends on database Y on server Z)
Regular reconciliation with automated discovery tools ensures completeness and accuracy
CMDB data quality metrics are tracked (e.g., completeness above 95%, accuracy verified quarterly)
commonGaps: CMDB exists but is incomplete — significant asset categories (cloud resources, network devices) are missing
Configuration details are outdated and do not reflect current deployed states
Interdependency mapping is minimal or absent, limiting the value for change impact analysis
No automated discovery integration — CMDB relies on manual updates that quickly become stale
suggestedSources: CMDB platform (ServiceNow, BMC Helix, Freshservice) asset reports
Automated discovery tool outputs (SolarWinds, Qualys, AWS Config)
Cloud management platform asset inventories

evidenceItems.name: Configuration baseline documents
evidenceItems.description: Approved configuration baselines for critical technology asset categories defining the standard, hardened configuration against which compliance is measured
whatGoodLooksLike: Baselines exist for all critical asset categories: servers (Windows, Linux), databases, network devices, cloud services
Baselines reference industry standards such as CIS Benchmarks with institution-specific customisation documented
Each baseline has formal approval from the relevant technical authority and information security
Baselines are version-controlled and updated when new security recommendations or patches change the standard
Baseline compliance measurement criteria are defined and automated
commonGaps: Baselines exist for servers but not for databases, network devices, or cloud services
Baselines are outdated and do not reflect current security hardening best practices
No formal approval process for baselines — they are created by technical teams without security review
Baseline compliance is not measured, making deviations invisible
suggestedSources: Information security team hardening standards library
Configuration management tool baseline definitions (Ansible playbooks, Chef cookbooks, Terraform modules)
CIS Benchmark compliance reports from scanning tools

evidenceItems.name: Deviation detection and remediation records
evidenceItems.description: Records of configuration deviations detected through monitoring, including assessment, risk rating, and remediation or risk acceptance documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated monitoring reports showing deviations detected with timestamps and affected assets
Each deviation has a documented assessment including severity, risk rating, and impact analysis
Remediation records show corrective actions taken with verification that the configuration was restored to baseline
Deviations that cannot be immediately remediated have formal risk acceptance with compensating controls
Trend analysis shows decreasing deviation rates over time, indicating improving configuration discipline
commonGaps: No automated deviation detection — deviations discovered only during periodic manual audits
Deviations detected but not assessed for risk or severity
Remediation is ad-hoc without formal tracking to closure
Large numbers of persistent deviations indicate that the baseline is impractical or monitoring is ignored
suggestedSources: Configuration monitoring tool reports (Qualys, Nessus, AWS Config, Azure Policy)
IT operations deviation management tickets
Security compliance dashboard showing baseline adherence metrics

auditTips: Request the CMDB and verify it includes all technology asset categories with current configuration data, not just a hardware inventory
Sample 10 critical assets from the CMDB and verify their recorded configurations match actual deployed configurations
Check configuration baseline documents for currency — baselines based on outdated CIS Benchmarks or vendor defaults are a common finding
Request deviation monitoring reports and verify that detected deviations are being assessed and remediated, not just reported
Verify that CMDB interdependency mapping is accurate by selecting a critical system and tracing its dependencies against actual infrastructure

10.16.title: Shadow IT Risk Management
10.16.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will check whether the institution has a defined capacity planning process with documented thresholds, regular monitoring, and evidence of proactive scaling actions taken before capacity limits are reached rather than reactively after service degradation.

evidenceItems.name: Capacity planning policy or procedure
evidenceItems.description: Formal procedure defining the capacity planning process including monitoring requirements, threshold management, review cycles, and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure covers all critical resource types: compute, storage, network, database, and application-specific metrics
Defines roles responsible for capacity monitoring, planning, and procurement actions
Includes requirements for annual capacity reviews aligned to business planning cycles
Specifies escalation procedures when thresholds are breached
Reviewed and approved within the last 12 months
commonGaps: No formal capacity planning procedure — capacity is managed reactively when issues arise
Procedure covers on-premises infrastructure but not cloud resources
No defined escalation path for capacity issues, leading to delayed response
Procedure exists but is not actively followed by infrastructure teams
suggestedSources: IT operations procedure library (SharePoint, Confluence)
Infrastructure team operational documentation
ITSM framework capacity management process documentation

evidenceItems.name: Capacity monitoring reports and dashboards
evidenceItems.description: Regular capacity monitoring reports and dashboard screenshots showing utilisation trends, threshold breaches, and resource consumption patterns
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboards show real-time utilisation for CPU, memory, storage, network, and database resources across production systems
Historical trend data available for at least 12 months to support capacity forecasting
Threshold indicators clearly shown on dashboards with colour-coded warning and critical levels
Monthly or quarterly reports summarise utilisation trends, threshold breaches, and recommended actions
Reports are distributed to IT management with evidence of review
commonGaps: Monitoring exists but only covers basic metrics (CPU, memory) without database connections, application queue depths, or network bandwidth
No historical trend data available beyond 30 days, limiting forecasting capability
Dashboards exist but are not regularly reviewed by management or used for planning
No regular reports — monitoring data is only examined when problems occur
suggestedSources: Infrastructure monitoring platforms (Grafana, Datadog, New Relic, Zabbix, SolarWinds)
Cloud provider monitoring tools (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Monitoring)
Monthly IT operations reports to management

evidenceItems.name: Capacity threshold definitions
evidenceItems.description: Documented capacity thresholds for each monitored resource type defining warning and critical levels with associated response actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Thresholds defined for each critical resource type with warning and critical levels (e.g., storage warning at 70%, critical at 85%)
Each threshold has a defined response action (e.g., warning triggers capacity review, critical triggers emergency scaling)
Thresholds are customised per system based on criticality and performance requirements, not just generic defaults
Automated alerting configured to notify relevant teams when thresholds are breached
Thresholds reviewed and adjusted at least annually based on utilisation trends
commonGaps: No documented thresholds — monitoring alerts use vendor defaults or ad-hoc settings
Thresholds defined but no associated response actions or escalation procedures
Same thresholds applied to all systems regardless of criticality or performance requirements
Thresholds have not been reviewed since initial setup despite changing utilisation patterns
suggestedSources: Monitoring platform alert configuration exports
Capacity management procedure annexes with threshold tables
Infrastructure team threshold management documentation

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of capacity scaling actions
evidenceItems.description: Records of capacity scaling or procurement actions taken in response to capacity monitoring, demonstrating proactive capacity management
whatGoodLooksLike: Scaling actions are linked to threshold breaches or forecast data, demonstrating proactive rather than reactive management
Records show the timeline from threshold breach to scaling action completion
Both upscaling and rightsizing actions are documented, showing efficient resource management
Scaling actions for critical systems completed before service degradation occurred
Annual capacity review produces a forward-looking procurement plan with budget requests
commonGaps: No records of proactive scaling — all capacity actions triggered by incidents rather than monitoring
Scaling actions documented but timeline shows significant delays between threshold breach and action
Only ad-hoc scaling records — no evidence of planned, proactive capacity management
No annual capacity forecast or procurement plan linked to business growth projections
suggestedSources: Infrastructure change management records for capacity-related changes
Cloud auto-scaling logs and manual scaling action records
Annual IT budget requests showing capacity-related procurement

auditTips: Request capacity monitoring dashboards and verify they cover all critical resource types, not just basic CPU and memory
Ask for evidence of threshold breaches in the last 12 months and trace each to a corresponding scaling action to verify proactive management
Check whether the annual capacity review was conducted and produced actionable recommendations that were followed through
Verify that capacity thresholds have been customised for critical systems rather than using default monitoring tool settings
Look for any capacity-related service incidents and assess whether proactive capacity planning could have prevented them

10.17.title: Vulnerability and End-of-Life System Management
10.17.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal patch management policy exists with defined severity classification and deployment timelines, that patches are tested before production deployment, and that compliance metrics demonstrate the institution is meeting its defined patching SLAs across all technology assets.

evidenceItems.name: Patch management policy document
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved policy defining the end-to-end patch management process including identification, evaluation, classification, testing, deployment, verification, and exception handling
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy scope covers all technology asset categories: operating systems, applications, databases, network devices, middleware, firmware, and cloud services
Defines patch classification criteria aligned with CVSS scoring with clear severity levels
Specifies maximum deployment timeframes for each severity level (e.g., critical 14 days, high 30 days, medium 90 days)
Includes mandatory testing requirements before production deployment
Approved by CTO or CISO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Policy covers server patching but excludes network devices, databases, firmware, or cloud services
Deployment timelines not defined or defined only for critical patches with no SLAs for other severities
No mandatory testing requirement before production deployment
Policy has not been updated to cover cloud-native patching requirements or containerised environments
suggestedSources: CISO policy library or IT governance document repository
Board or IT steering committee meeting minutes showing policy approval
Internal audit patch management compliance reports

evidenceItems.name: Patch classification criteria and timelines
evidenceItems.description: Documented criteria for classifying patches by severity and the corresponding maximum deployment timelines for each classification level
whatGoodLooksLike: Classification criteria reference CVSS scores with clear mapping to institutional severity levels (critical, high, medium, low)
Maximum deployment timelines specified for each level: critical within 14 days, high within 30 days, medium within 90 days, low within next maintenance window
Criteria include consideration of exploitability, exposure (internet-facing vs. internal), and business criticality of the affected asset
Documented exceptions process for when timelines cannot be met
Classification criteria aligned with the institution's risk appetite and approved by CISO
commonGaps: Classification relies solely on vendor-provided severity without considering institutional context or exposure
Timelines defined but not differentiated based on asset criticality or exposure
No criteria for zero-day vulnerabilities or actively exploited vulnerabilities requiring accelerated response
Classification criteria not documented separately — buried in the policy document and not easily applied by operational teams
suggestedSources: Patch management policy annexes with classification tables
Vulnerability management procedure documentation
CISO team operational guidelines for patch prioritisation

evidenceItems.name: Patch deployment records and compliance metrics
evidenceItems.description: Records of patch deployments across the technology estate with compliance metrics showing adherence to defined deployment timelines by severity level
whatGoodLooksLike: Deployment records show each patch applied with identification date, classification, target deadline, actual deployment date, and affected assets
Compliance metrics report the percentage of patches deployed within SLA by severity level (target above 95% for critical)
Non-compliant patches are individually tracked with documented reasons for delay and remediation plans
Trend data available for at least 12 months showing improving or sustained compliance rates
Metrics cover all asset categories, not just servers
commonGaps: Deployment records exist for server patches but not for network devices, databases, or applications
Compliance metrics not calculated against defined timelines — only raw deployment counts reported
Large numbers of overdue critical patches indicate the process is not functioning effectively
No trend analysis to identify systemic issues with patching specific asset types or business units
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform reports (Qualys, Nessus, Rapid7, Tenable)
Patch management tool dashboards (WSUS, SCCM, Ansible, AWS Systems Manager)
Monthly patch compliance reports to management

evidenceItems.name: Patch testing procedures
evidenceItems.description: Procedures for testing patches in non-production environments before production deployment, including test scope, environment requirements, and approval process
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure mandates testing in a non-production environment that mirrors production configuration for the affected systems
Test scope covers functionality verification, compatibility testing, and regression testing for critical applications
Defines criteria for when testing can be abbreviated (e.g., vendor-certified patches for standard components) vs. full testing required
Approval process requires sign-off from the system owner and QA before production deployment proceeds
Emergency patching procedure defined for zero-day or actively exploited vulnerabilities where normal testing timelines cannot be followed
commonGaps: No formal testing procedure — patches are deployed directly to production or tested informally
Testing environment does not match production, leading to compatibility issues discovered only after deployment
All patches follow the same testing process regardless of risk, creating bottlenecks for low-risk patches
No defined emergency patching procedure for urgent security patches
suggestedSources: Patch management procedure documentation
QA team testing process documentation
Change management records showing patch testing evidence

auditTips: Request the patch management policy and verify it defines specific deployment timelines for each severity level, not just 'as soon as possible'
Pull patch compliance reports for the last 6 months and calculate the actual compliance rate for critical and high patches against the defined SLAs
Verify that patch coverage extends beyond servers to include network devices, databases, applications, and cloud services
Sample 5 recent critical patches and trace each from identification through testing to production deployment, verifying the timeline met the policy requirement
Check for any critical patches older than 14 days that remain undeployed and verify whether formal exception documentation exists with compensating controls

10.18.title: Patch and EOL Management Framework
10.18.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that critical patches are deployed within 14 days and high-severity patches within 30 days, and that any exceptions are backed by documented compensating controls and formal risk acceptance at the appropriate authority level.

evidenceItems.name: Patch deployment timeline standards
evidenceItems.description: Documented standards defining maximum deployment timeframes for each patch severity level, aligned with RMiT requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Explicit statement of 14-day maximum for critical patches and 30-day maximum for high-severity patches, consistent with RMiT clause 10.18
Timeframes measured from vendor release date or public disclosure, whichever is earlier, with clear start-date definition
Standards address different asset categories with appropriate adjustments (e.g. network device firmware may require maintenance windows)
Emergency patching process for actively exploited zero-day vulnerabilities with accelerated timelines (e.g. 48-72 hours)
Standards formally approved by CISO or CTO and reviewed at least annually
commonGaps: Timeline standards exist but do not match RMiT requirements (e.g. 30 days for critical instead of 14)
Start date for timeline measurement not clearly defined, leading to ambiguous compliance calculations
No differentiated timelines for different asset types, causing impractical expectations for devices requiring maintenance windows
No emergency patching process for zero-day vulnerabilities
suggestedSources: Patch management policy or standards document
IT security standards library
IT governance committee approval records

evidenceItems.name: Patch deployment tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Detailed tracking records showing actual patch deployment dates against required timeframes for all critical and high-severity patches
whatGoodLooksLike: Tracking log capturing: patch identifier (CVE/KB), vendor release date, severity classification, target deployment date, actual deployment date, and compliance status
Records cover all technology asset categories: servers, endpoints, network devices, databases, and cloud infrastructure
Dashboard or report showing compliance rates against 14-day (critical) and 30-day (high) deployment targets
Non-compliant deployments flagged with root cause analysis and linked to exception or compensating control records
Historical tracking data available for at least the past 12 months
commonGaps: Tracking records incomplete; only cover Windows patches and exclude network devices, databases, or Linux systems
Vendor release date not captured, making it impossible to calculate compliance against deployment timeframes
Non-compliant deployments not flagged or investigated; no root cause analysis for breached timelines
Tracking performed manually in spreadsheets with gaps and inconsistencies
suggestedSources: Patch management platform reports (SCCM/MECM, WSUS, Ivanti, ManageEngine)
Vulnerability management platform (Qualys, Tenable, Rapid7) showing remediation timelines
IT service management platform patch-related change records

evidenceItems.name: Compensating control documentation for delayed patches
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of compensating controls implemented when patches cannot be deployed within the required timeframes
whatGoodLooksLike: Each delayed patch has a documented compensating control record specifying: affected systems, reason for delay, controls implemented, and target remediation date
Compensating controls are proportionate to the risk: e.g. network isolation, WAF rules, enhanced monitoring, or IPS signatures for the specific vulnerability
Controls validated as effective through testing (e.g. vulnerability scan confirming mitigation, network ACL verification)
Time-bound with defined expiry dates; compensating controls are not treated as permanent alternatives to patching
Regular review of outstanding compensating controls to ensure they remain effective and patching is progressing
commonGaps: No compensating controls documented; patches simply deployed late without interim mitigation
Compensating controls are generic statements (e.g. 'firewall in place') without specificity to the vulnerability being mitigated
Controls not validated as effective; no evidence they actually mitigate the exploitability of the unpatched vulnerability
Compensating controls become permanent; systems remain unpatched indefinitely with no remediation plan
suggestedSources: IT risk management exception register
Vulnerability management platform exception records
Change advisory board meeting minutes documenting patch deferrals

evidenceItems.name: Risk acceptance records for patch exceptions
evidenceItems.description: Formal risk acceptance records signed by the appropriate authority level for patches that cannot be deployed within required timeframes
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk acceptance signed by an authority at or above the level specified in the institution's risk acceptance framework (typically Head of IT Risk or CISO for high-severity exceptions)
Risk acceptance record includes: vulnerability details, affected systems, business justification for delay, compensating controls, residual risk assessment, and target remediation date
Risk acceptance is time-bound with a defined expiry date, requiring re-acceptance if the patch remains undeployed
Risk acceptance register maintained with regular review by IT risk committee
Escalation to Board Risk Committee for risk acceptances exceeding defined thresholds
commonGaps: No formal risk acceptance; patches deferred without documented approval from appropriate authority
Risk acceptance signed by operational staff rather than the authority level required by the risk framework
Risk acceptance records are open-ended with no expiry date or target remediation date
No central register of outstanding risk acceptances; individual records scattered across teams
suggestedSources: IT risk acceptance register or GRC platform (ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream)
IT risk committee meeting minutes and decision logs
CISO or CTO approval records for patch exceptions

auditTips: Prepare a compliance report showing actual deployment times for all critical and high-severity patches over the past 12 months against the 14-day and 30-day targets
For any patches that breached deployment timelines, have the compensating control documentation and risk acceptance records readily available
Ensure risk acceptance records are signed at the appropriate authority level as defined in your risk framework, not by operational staff
Demonstrate that compensating controls are validated as effective and time-bound with target remediation dates
Be prepared to show the end-to-end timeline for a sample of recent critical patches: from vendor release to production deployment

10.19.title: Technology Effectiveness Monitoring
10.19.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a comprehensive inventory of EOL and EOS assets exists, that each has a migration or replacement plan with defined timelines, and that any EOL systems still in use have documented compensating controls and formal risk acceptance.

evidenceItems.name: End-of-life inventory and tracking register
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive register of all technology assets approaching or past end-of-life and end-of-support dates, with tracking of vendor support status
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all asset types: operating systems, databases, middleware, application frameworks, network device firmware, and hardware platforms
Each entry includes: asset name, version, vendor EOL/EOS dates, current support status, criticality rating, and owning business unit
Forward-looking view showing assets approaching EOL within the next 12-24 months to enable proactive planning
Register linked to the CMDB or IT asset management system for automated updates when vendor EOL announcements are published
Regular review cycle (at least quarterly) with evidence of updates and additions
commonGaps: Inventory exists but is incomplete; excludes middleware, development frameworks, or network device firmware
EOL dates not tracked proactively; assets discovered as EOL only after vendor support has already ended
Register maintained manually in spreadsheets with no linkage to CMDB, leading to stale or inaccurate data
No forward-looking view; only assets already past EOL are tracked, missing those approaching EOL
suggestedSources: IT asset management system or CMDB (ServiceNow, BMC Helix)
Vendor lifecycle and support notification tracking
IT operations inventory and configuration management documentation

evidenceItems.name: Migration or replacement plans for EOL systems
evidenceItems.description: Documented migration or replacement plans for each technology asset at or approaching end-of-life, with defined timelines and resource allocation
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual migration plan for each EOL asset specifying: target platform or version, migration approach, timeline with milestones, resource requirements, and budget allocation
Plans include risk assessment of the migration itself, including rollback procedures and business impact during transition
Timelines are realistic and funded, with executive sponsorship and project governance in place
Plans prioritised based on asset criticality and risk exposure (internet-facing EOL systems prioritised over internal low-risk systems)
Progress tracked and reported to IT governance committee on a regular cadence
commonGaps: Migration plans exist on paper but have no budget allocation or executive sponsorship, making them aspirational rather than actionable
Plans do not include timelines or milestones; stated as 'to be migrated' without specific dates
High-criticality EOL systems deprioritised behind lower-risk projects due to competing business demands
Plans not updated to reflect delays or changes in vendor roadmaps
suggestedSources: IT programme management office project portfolio
Technology roadmap and strategic planning documentation
IT governance committee reporting packs and decision logs

evidenceItems.name: Compensating control documentation for legacy systems
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of compensating controls implemented for EOL systems that remain in production use, mitigating the risks of unsupported technology
whatGoodLooksLike: Each EOL system in production has a documented compensating control record specifying: network isolation measures, enhanced monitoring, hardening configurations, and access restrictions
Controls are proportionate to the risk: internet-facing EOL systems have stricter compensating controls than isolated internal systems
Compensating controls validated through testing (e.g. vulnerability assessment confirming isolation, penetration test confirming controls are effective)
Regular review of compensating controls (at least quarterly) to ensure they remain effective as the threat landscape evolves
Controls linked to the migration plan with a defined sunset date when the EOL system will be decommissioned
commonGaps: No compensating controls documented; EOL systems continue operating with no additional security measures
Controls are generic (e.g. 'behind firewall') without specificity to the risks of running unsupported software
Controls not validated through testing; assumed effective without verification
No regular review; compensating controls set once and never reassessed
suggestedSources: IT risk management exception register
Network security team isolation and hardening documentation
Vulnerability assessment reports for EOL systems

evidenceItems.name: Risk acceptance records for continued EOL system use
evidenceItems.description: Formal risk acceptance records authorising the continued use of EOL systems, signed by the appropriate authority level
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk acceptance signed by an authority at or above the level specified in the institution's risk acceptance framework (typically CTO, CISO, or CRO depending on risk tier)
Record includes: system details, EOL/EOS date, business justification for continued use, compensating controls in place, residual risk assessment, and target migration date
Risk acceptance is time-bound (typically 6-12 months) with mandatory re-acceptance if the system remains in use beyond the expiry date
Aggregate EOL risk exposure reported to Board Risk Committee as part of technology risk reporting
Risk acceptance linked to the corresponding migration plan, enabling oversight of remediation progress
commonGaps: No formal risk acceptance; EOL systems remain in use without documented authorisation
Risk acceptance signed by operational managers rather than the authority level required for the risk tier
Open-ended risk acceptances with no expiry date, allowing EOL systems to remain indefinitely without re-assessment
No aggregate reporting of EOL risk exposure to senior management or the Board
suggestedSources: IT risk acceptance register or GRC platform (ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream)
CTO or CISO approval records
Board Risk Committee or IT steering committee reporting packs

auditTips: Prepare the complete EOL inventory with current vendor support status for each asset, including those approaching EOL within the next 12 months
For each EOL system still in production, have the compensating control documentation and risk acceptance records readily available as a package
Ensure risk acceptances are current (not expired) and signed at the appropriate authority level
Demonstrate that migration plans are funded, have realistic timelines, and are being actively tracked through project governance
Be prepared to explain why specific EOL systems have not yet been migrated, with evidence of compensating controls proportionate to the risk

10.20.title: Cryptography Policy and Standards
10.20.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal cryptographic policy exists with specific approved algorithms and key lengths, is aligned with recognised international standards (NIST, ISO), and has been reviewed and updated within the past 12 months with documented evidence of the review process.

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic policy document
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy document defining the institution's cryptographic standards, approved algorithms, key lengths, and protocols for protecting data at rest and in transit
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly lists approved symmetric algorithms (AES-256), asymmetric algorithms (RSA-2048+, ECC P-256+), hash functions (SHA-256+), and protocols (TLS 1.2+)
Clear data classification mapping showing which cryptographic standards apply to each data tier
Board or senior management approval with dated sign-off and version control
Explicit prohibition of deprecated algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4, MD5, SHA-1) with migration timelines for any legacy usage
Defined exception process for systems that cannot yet comply with minimum standards
commonGaps: Policy references 'industry standard encryption' without specifying approved algorithms or minimum key lengths
No version control or approval signatures; document appears to be an informal IT guideline rather than a formal policy
Policy has not been reviewed or updated in over 12 months despite evolving cryptographic threats
No mention of quantum computing preparedness or post-quantum cryptography roadmap
suggestedSources: Information Security Policy Management System (policy repository)
Board IT Committee or CISO office (approval records)
IT governance document management system

evidenceItems.name: Approved algorithm and key length specifications
evidenceItems.description: Detailed technical specifications of all approved cryptographic algorithms, key lengths, modes of operation, and protocols, organised by use case and data classification
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured table mapping each use case (data at rest, data in transit, key exchange, digital signatures, hashing) to specific approved algorithms and minimum key lengths
Cipher suite specifications for TLS configurations with priority ordering
Approved modes of operation for block ciphers (GCM, CBC with HMAC) with guidance on when each is appropriate
Clear distinction between mandatory minimums and recommended best practices
commonGaps: Specifications are vague, listing algorithm families (e.g. 'AES') without specifying key lengths or modes of operation
No cipher suite ordering guidance for TLS implementations, leading to inconsistent configurations
Specifications do not cover all use cases, missing areas such as database encryption or API security
No guidance on algorithm selection for different data classification levels
suggestedSources: CISO office or security architecture team
Enterprise architecture documentation repository
Security standards working group minutes

evidenceItems.name: Annual policy review records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the annual cryptographic policy review process including review participants, findings, decisions made, and resulting policy updates
whatGoodLooksLike: Dated review meeting minutes with named attendees including CISO and relevant technical leads
Assessment of current policy against latest NIST publications, CVEs affecting approved algorithms, and industry developments
Clear documentation of decisions to add, remove, or modify approved algorithms with rationale
Evidence of review of deprecated algorithm remediation progress
Action items from the review with assigned owners and completion dates
commonGaps: No documented review activity; policy date unchanged for multiple years
Review recorded as a checkbox exercise without substantive assessment of cryptographic developments
Review does not consider emerging threats such as quantum computing impact on current algorithms
No tracking of action items arising from previous reviews
suggestedSources: Information security governance meeting minutes
Policy management system version history
CISO office records

evidenceItems.name: Alignment mapping to international standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal mapping document showing how the institution's cryptographic policy aligns with recognised international standards such as NIST SP 800-57, NIST SP 800-131A, and ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.10
whatGoodLooksLike: Clause-by-clause mapping between the cryptographic policy and NIST SP 800-57 key management recommendations
Gap analysis identifying any areas where the institution's standards deviate from international recommendations with documented justification
Cross-reference to ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.10 cryptographic controls requirements
Evidence that the mapping has been updated following publication of new NIST guidance or ISO standard revisions
commonGaps: No formal mapping exists; policy merely states 'aligned with international standards' without evidence
Mapping references outdated versions of NIST publications that have since been superseded
Gaps identified in the mapping have no documented remediation plan or risk acceptance
Mapping covers only a subset of relevant standards (e.g. NIST but not ISO, or vice versa)
suggestedSources: Security architecture team documentation
Compliance or IT risk management records
External audit or consultancy deliverables

auditTips: Prepare a side-by-side comparison of your approved algorithm list against NIST SP 800-131A transitioning recommendations to preemptively address alignment questions
Ensure the policy review date is within the last 12 months and that you can demonstrate substantive changes or explicit confirmation that no updates were needed
Have the cryptographic algorithm inventory ready showing which algorithms are actually deployed across systems, not just what is approved in policy
Prepare evidence of how deprecated algorithm usage is being tracked and remediated, including specific timelines and progress metrics
Be ready to explain your post-quantum cryptography strategy or roadmap, as this is an increasingly common examiner question

10.21.title: Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence
10.21.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a comprehensive key management framework governs the full key lifecycle, that HSMs are deployed for critical system keys, and that key custodian roles with dual-control and split-knowledge principles are formally assigned and enforced.

evidenceItems.name: Key management framework document
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive framework governing the entire lifecycle of cryptographic keys including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework covers all lifecycle phases with specific procedures for each: generation (approved RNG, ceremony procedures), distribution (encrypted channels, split knowledge), storage (HSM for critical, encrypted keystore for others), rotation (defined schedules by key type), revocation (immediate process, CRL/OCSP), destruction (cryptographic erasure, audit trail)
Key classification scheme linking key types to storage requirements and rotation schedules
Dual-control and split-knowledge requirements clearly defined for critical key operations
Named key custodian roles with backup assignments and segregation of duties requirements
Incident response procedures specific to key compromise events
commonGaps: Framework addresses key generation and storage but omits destruction procedures, leaving no defined process for secure key disposal
No distinction between key types; all keys treated identically regardless of criticality or classification
Dual-control requirements stated in policy but not enforced in practice, with single individuals having full key access
No defined key rotation schedules; keys remain unchanged until systems are replaced
suggestedSources: CISO office or cryptographic standards team
HSM management console and administration records
Key ceremony documentation and witness logs

evidenceItems.name: HSM deployment and configuration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of hardware security module deployment for critical systems including configuration documentation, FIPS certification, and tamper-evidence monitoring records
whatGoodLooksLike: HSM inventory listing all deployed units with model, firmware version, FIPS 140-2/3 certification level, and assigned critical systems
Configuration documentation showing HSM access controls, partition assignments, and audit logging settings
FIPS 140-2 Level 3 (or equivalent) certification certificates for all HSMs protecting critical system keys
Tamper-evidence monitoring logs showing regular physical inspection of HSM tamper seals
HSM backup and recovery procedures with tested restore processes
commonGaps: Critical system keys stored in software keystores or configuration files rather than HSMs
HSMs deployed but running outdated firmware with known vulnerabilities
No FIPS certification evidence available for deployed HSMs
HSM access not restricted through dual-control; single administrators have full access to key material
suggestedSources: HSM management console (Thales Luna, Utimaco, nCipher)
Data centre equipment inventory and asset management system
Vendor certification documentation

evidenceItems.name: Key rotation schedule and records
evidenceItems.description: Defined rotation schedules for each key type and records of actual key rotation activities performed
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented rotation schedule specifying frequency by key type: symmetric encryption keys (annual), asymmetric keys (biennial), session keys (per-session), signing keys (annual)
Rotation activity logs showing date, key identifier, old key reference, new key reference, operator identity, and verification of successful rotation
Evidence of automated rotation where applicable (e.g. TLS certificate key rotation, database encryption key rotation)
Exception tracking for any keys that could not be rotated within the scheduled timeframe with documented justification
commonGaps: No defined rotation schedule; keys rotated only when systems are upgraded or replaced
Rotation records incomplete or missing for certain key types or systems
Rotation performed but old keys not properly decommissioned, remaining accessible in backup systems
No automated rotation capability; all rotations require manual intervention with associated risk of human error
suggestedSources: HSM management console audit logs
Key management system (KeySecure, Venafi)
Change management system records for key rotation activities

evidenceItems.name: Key destruction procedures and records
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures for secure cryptographic key destruction and records of key destruction activities including verification of complete removal
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented destruction procedure specifying cryptographic erasure methods, dual-custodian witness requirements, and verification steps
Destruction records with key identifier, destruction date, method used, operator and witness identities, and confirmation that all copies (primary, backup, escrow) were destroyed
Verification evidence confirming destroyed keys cannot be recovered from any storage location
Alignment with NIST SP 800-88 media sanitisation guidelines where physical media is involved
commonGaps: No formal destruction procedure; keys simply deleted from active systems without addressing backup copies
Destruction records lack witness signatures or dual-control evidence
Backup copies of destroyed keys remain on backup tapes or in escrow without being addressed
No verification step to confirm keys are unrecoverable after destruction
suggestedSources: Key ceremony documentation and witness logs
HSM management console audit logs
IT asset disposal and data sanitisation records

auditTips: Prepare an inventory of all critical systems and map each to its key storage mechanism (HSM vs software keystore), as examiners will specifically ask which critical systems use HSMs
Have key custodian assignment records readily available showing named individuals, backup custodians, and evidence of dual-control enforcement
Ensure key rotation compliance reports are current, showing percentage of keys rotated within their defined schedule and any overdue rotations with justification
Prepare evidence of key destruction activities from the past 12 months, including destruction certificates with dual-custodian witness signatures
Be ready to demonstrate that the key management framework was reviewed in the last 12 months with evidence of any updates made

10.22.title: Cryptographic Protocol Implementation
10.22.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that all sensitive data is encrypted at rest and in transit using minimum AES-256 (symmetric) and RSA-2048 (asymmetric), that a comprehensive algorithm inventory exists, and that any deprecated or weak algorithms have been identified with active remediation plans.

evidenceItems.name: Encryption standards document
evidenceItems.description: Technical standards document specifying minimum encryption requirements for data at rest and in transit across all system classifications
whatGoodLooksLike: Explicit minimum standards: AES-256 for symmetric encryption, RSA-2048+ or ECC P-256+ for asymmetric, TLS 1.2+ for data in transit
Standards mapped to data classification tiers showing which encryption requirements apply at each level
Specific cipher suite configurations for TLS implementations with secure ordering
Clear list of prohibited algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4, MD5, SHA-1) with mandatory remediation timelines
Standards covering all data states: at rest (database, file system, backup), in transit (network, API, email), and in use where applicable
commonGaps: Standards document references 'strong encryption' without specifying minimum key lengths or approved algorithms
Data in transit requirements specify TLS but do not mandate minimum version (allowing TLS 1.0/1.1) or restrict weak cipher suites
Backup media encryption not addressed in the standards
No standards for database-level encryption (TDE) or application-level field encryption
suggestedSources: CISO office or security architecture team
IT standards library or policy management system
Enterprise architecture documentation

evidenceItems.name: Data encryption implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that encryption is implemented across databases, file systems, backup media, network communications, and APIs in accordance with defined standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Database encryption configuration screenshots showing TDE enabled with AES-256 for all databases containing sensitive data
TLS configuration evidence from web servers, application servers, and API gateways showing TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites
File system encryption evidence (BitLocker, LUKS, or equivalent) for servers and endpoints handling sensitive data
Backup encryption configuration showing all backup media encrypted with approved algorithms
SSL/TLS scan results (e.g. Qualys SSL Labs) showing A+ ratings on customer-facing services
commonGaps: Encryption enabled on customer-facing channels but not on internal network communications between application tiers
Database connections between application servers and databases using unencrypted channels
Backup tapes or cloud backups stored without encryption
Legacy systems still using TLS 1.0 or 1.1 without a migration timeline
suggestedSources: Database administration team (TDE configuration reports)
Infrastructure team (server and network encryption configurations)
SSL/TLS scanning tools (Qualys SSL Labs, SSLyze, testssl.sh)

evidenceItems.name: Inventory of encryption algorithms in use
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive inventory of all cryptographic algorithms, key lengths, and protocols currently deployed across the technology estate
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured inventory listing each system, the algorithms and key lengths in use for data at rest and in transit, and compliance status against minimum standards
Colour-coded compliance dashboard showing compliant (green), non-compliant with remediation plan (amber), and non-compliant without plan (red)
Coverage of all system types including applications, databases, network devices, middleware, and third-party integrations
Regular update cadence (at least quarterly) with evidence of new systems being added as deployed
commonGaps: Inventory covers only major systems, missing smaller applications, middleware, or network device encryption configurations
No regular update process; inventory is a point-in-time snapshot that quickly becomes outdated
Third-party and cloud service encryption configurations not included in the inventory
Inventory identifies non-compliant algorithms but does not track remediation progress
suggestedSources: Security architecture team or CISO office
Configuration management database (CMDB)
Vulnerability scanning tools with cryptographic assessment capabilities (Nessus, Qualys)

evidenceItems.name: Deprecated algorithm remediation records
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the identification, prioritisation, and remediation of deprecated or weak cryptographic algorithms across the technology estate
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation tracker listing each instance of deprecated algorithm usage with system name, algorithm details, risk rating, remediation plan, target date, and current status
Evidence of completed migrations from weak to approved algorithms with before/after configuration evidence
Risk acceptance documentation for any deprecated algorithms that cannot be immediately replaced, with compensating controls
Trend reporting showing decreasing deprecated algorithm usage over time
commonGaps: Deprecated algorithms identified in vulnerability scans but no formal remediation programme established
Remediation plans with unrealistic or repeatedly extended timelines, indicating no real commitment to resolution
Legacy systems running deprecated algorithms with no risk acceptance or compensating controls documented
Remediation focused on internet-facing systems only, ignoring internal system encryption weaknesses
suggestedSources: IT risk register or remediation tracking system
Vulnerability management platform (Nessus, Qualys)
Change management system records for algorithm migration projects

auditTips: Run SSL/TLS scans on all customer-facing services before the audit and ensure all achieve at least an A rating on Qualys SSL Labs
Prepare the algorithm inventory with clear compliance status indicators so examiners can quickly see overall encryption posture
Have specific examples ready of deprecated algorithm remediation completed in the past 12 months to demonstrate active management
Ensure database TDE configurations are documented and screenshots are current, as examiners often request evidence of encryption at rest
Be prepared to demonstrate that backup media encryption is in place, as this is a commonly overlooked area that examiners specifically check

10.23.title: Public Key Certificate Management
10.23.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will check for a complete certificate inventory with expiry tracking, evidence of certificate transparency log monitoring, documented issuance and renewal procedures using trusted CAs, and whether any certificate-related outages have occurred in the past 12 months.

evidenceItems.name: Certificate management procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure governing the lifecycle management of digital certificates including issuance, renewal, revocation, and monitoring processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Step-by-step procedures for certificate request, approval, issuance, installation, renewal, and revocation
Defined list of approved certificate authorities with justification for each trusted CA
Automated renewal workflow with triggers at 90, 60, and 30 days before expiry
Emergency revocation procedure with defined steps, responsible parties, and maximum response time
Certificate naming conventions and standards for different use cases (web server, API, code signing, internal)
commonGaps: No formal procedure; certificates managed ad hoc by individual system administrators
Procedure does not specify approved CAs, allowing any CA to be used without governance
No emergency revocation procedure defined; revocation handled reactively with no predefined process
Procedure does not cover wildcard certificate usage controls or certificate pinning requirements
suggestedSources: CISO office or PKI team
Certificate management platform (Venafi, DigiCert CertCentral, Let's Encrypt)
IT operations procedure library

evidenceItems.name: Certificate inventory with expiry dates
evidenceItems.description: Complete inventory of all digital certificates in use across the technology estate with issuance details, expiry dates, and responsible owners
whatGoodLooksLike: Centralised inventory showing certificate subject, issuing CA, issuance date, expiry date, key algorithm, key length, and assigned system/service
Dashboard or report highlighting certificates expiring within 30, 60, and 90 days
Coverage of all certificate types: public SSL/TLS, internal PKI, code signing, client authentication, and API mutual TLS
Regular automated discovery scans to identify any certificates deployed outside the managed inventory
Assigned ownership for each certificate with contact details for renewal coordination
commonGaps: Inventory maintained in a spreadsheet that is not regularly updated and contains stale entries
Only internet-facing certificates tracked; internal certificates, code signing certificates, and client certificates not inventoried
No automated discovery process; certificates deployed by development teams outside the PKI team's awareness
No ownership assignment; when a certificate expires, there is no clear responsible party for renewal
suggestedSources: Certificate management platform dashboard and reports
Certificate discovery scan results (Qualys, Venafi, CertSpotter)
PKI team certificate database

evidenceItems.name: Certificate transparency monitoring evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of ongoing monitoring of certificate transparency logs for all institution-owned domains to detect unauthorised certificate issuance
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration evidence showing CT log monitoring enabled for all institution-owned domains
Alert records demonstrating that CT log alerts are being generated and reviewed
Incident records for any unauthorised certificate issuance detections with investigation and response documentation
Integration with security operations for real-time alert triage and investigation
commonGaps: No certificate transparency monitoring in place for any domains
Monitoring configured but alerts not reviewed or investigated, rendering the capability ineffective
Only primary domains monitored; subsidiary domains, partner-facing domains, or recently acquired domains not covered
No documented response procedure for when an unauthorised certificate is detected
suggestedSources: CT monitoring platform (CertSpotter, Facebook CT Monitoring, crt.sh)
Security operations centre alert and incident logs
DNS and domain management records for complete domain inventory

evidenceItems.name: Certificate renewal and revocation records
evidenceItems.description: Records of certificate renewal activities and any certificate revocation events with associated investigation and response documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Renewal activity log showing certificates renewed, dates, old and new certificate details, and confirmation of successful installation
Revocation records with reason for revocation, revocation date, CRL/OCSP update confirmation, and impact assessment
Zero certificate-related service outages in the reporting period, or documented incident reports for any that occurred
Metrics showing average renewal lead time and percentage of certificates renewed before the 30-day warning threshold
commonGaps: Renewal activities not logged; evidence of renewal exists only in CA portal records that may not be readily accessible
Certificates renewed at the last minute or after expiry, indicating reactive rather than proactive management
Revocation records incomplete; no confirmation that CRL/OCSP was updated or that dependent systems stopped trusting revoked certificates
Certificate-related outages occurred but were not tracked as incidents or linked to certificate management process failures
suggestedSources: Certificate management platform activity logs
CA portal records (DigiCert, Entrust, GlobalSign)
Incident management system (for any certificate-related outages)

auditTips: Run a certificate discovery scan across all networks before the audit to ensure no unknown or expired certificates exist outside the managed inventory
Prepare a report showing zero certificate-related outages in the past 12 months, or documented incident reports with root cause and remediation for any that occurred
Demonstrate the CT monitoring dashboard showing active monitoring for all institution domains and any alert history
Have the approved CA list documented and ready, with evidence that certificates from non-approved CAs are not in use
Prepare renewal metrics showing average lead times and the percentage of certificates renewed well before expiry as evidence of proactive management

10.24.title: Data Centre Resilience Objectives
10.24.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify multi-layered physical security controls at data centres including perimeter security, progressive access controls, CCTV with minimum 90-day retention, and formal visitor management procedures with complete logs.

evidenceItems.name: Data centre physical security policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy document defining physical security requirements for all data centre facilities including access control standards, CCTV requirements, and visitor management procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines multiple security layers: perimeter (fencing, barriers), building (card access), floor (card + PIN), server room (card + biometric)
CCTV requirements specifying minimum 90-day retention, coverage of all entry/exit points and sensitive areas, and minimum resolution standards
Visitor management requirements including pre-approval, ID verification, continuous escort, and sign-in/sign-out logging
Board or senior management approval with version control and annual review date
Security incident response procedures for physical breaches or detected intrusions
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not specify minimum CCTV retention period, or specifies less than 90 days
No multi-layer access control defined; single card swipe provides access directly to server rooms
Visitor management procedures informal or not consistently enforced across all facilities
Policy not reviewed or updated within the past 12 months
suggestedSources: Facility management team or CISO office
Corporate security policy library
Board IT Committee approval records

evidenceItems.name: CCTV coverage maps and retention configuration
evidenceItems.description: Maps showing CCTV camera placement covering all data centre areas and system configuration evidence confirming minimum 90-day footage retention
whatGoodLooksLike: Floor plan diagrams showing camera positions covering all entry/exit points, corridors, server rooms, loading docks, and exterior perimeter
CCTV system configuration screenshots showing 90-day (or greater) retention settings
Camera resolution specifications meeting minimum standards for person identification
Evidence of regular CCTV system health checks including camera functionality, recording integrity, and storage capacity monitoring
Tamper-evident recording with protected access to footage archives
commonGaps: CCTV coverage gaps in corridors, loading docks, or emergency exits where camera placement is missing
Retention configuration set to less than 90 days due to storage constraints
Cameras non-functional or providing insufficient resolution for identification, with no maintenance process to detect and remediate failures
CCTV recordings accessible to unauthorised personnel without proper access controls
suggestedSources: CCTV management platform (Milestone, Hikvision, Axis)
Facility management team floor plans and camera maps
Data centre infrastructure management (DCIM) system

evidenceItems.name: Access control system configuration
evidenceItems.description: Configuration documentation for the physical access control system showing multi-layer access requirements, biometric integration, and access logging capabilities
whatGoodLooksLike: System configuration showing progressive security zones with escalating authentication requirements at each layer
Integration evidence between card access, biometric systems, and the centralised access management platform
Access logging configuration showing all access events (granted, denied, alarms) recorded with timestamps and individual identification
Anti-tailgating measures documented and tested (mantrap doors, turnstiles, video analytics)
Integration with CCTV for correlated access event and video evidence
commonGaps: Single-factor access (card only) throughout the facility, including server rooms
Access logs not retained for sufficient duration or not regularly reviewed
No anti-tailgating measures; shared entry points allow unauthorised persons to follow authorised personnel
Biometric systems installed but not consistently required (e.g. biometric bypass available during maintenance windows)
suggestedSources: Physical access control system administration console (HID, Lenel, Honeywell)
Facility security operations team
Biometric system vendor management reports

evidenceItems.name: Visitor management logs
evidenceItems.description: Logs of all visitor access to data centre facilities including pre-approval records, identification verification, escort assignments, and entry/exit times
whatGoodLooksLike: Electronic visitor management system (not just a paper logbook) with pre-approval workflow, photo ID capture, badge issuance, and automatic entry/exit recording
Complete records showing visitor name, company, purpose, host, pre-approval reference, ID verification, badge number, entry time, exit time, and escorting officer
No unescorted visitor access to any data centre area; all visitors accompanied by authorised personnel at all times
Visitor badge system that visibly distinguishes visitors from permanent staff and provides zone-restricted access
commonGaps: Paper-based visitor logbook with illegible entries, missing exit times, or unsigned records
Visitors not consistently escorted; evidence of visitors left unaccompanied in sensitive areas
Pre-approval not required; visitors can arrive unannounced and gain access through on-the-spot approval
Visitor badges do not restrict zone access; visitors receive the same badge type as permanent staff
suggestedSources: Visitor management system (Envoy, Proxyclick, or equivalent)
Security desk logbooks and records
Access control system visitor badge logs

evidenceItems.name: Physical security assessment reports
evidenceItems.description: Annual physical security assessment reports covering all data centre facilities with findings, risk ratings, and remediation tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: Independent assessment conducted within the past 12 months by qualified physical security professionals
Assessment covering perimeter security, access controls, CCTV coverage, visitor management, and security operations
Risk-rated findings with specific remediation recommendations and target completion dates
Evidence of previous assessment findings having been remediated and verified
commonGaps: No independent physical security assessment conducted in the past 12 months
Assessment conducted but findings not tracked to remediation
Assessment scope limited to server rooms only, not covering perimeter, corridors, or supporting areas
Previous assessment findings remain open without remediation progress
suggestedSources: Internal audit reports on data centre physical security
External security consultant assessment deliverables
IT risk register entries for physical security findings

auditTips: Verify CCTV retention is configured for at least 90 days by obtaining a system configuration screenshot dated within the past week showing retention settings
Prepare a sample of visitor log entries from the past 3 months demonstrating complete records with all required fields populated
Have the access control system configuration ready showing multi-factor authentication requirements at each security zone level
Ensure the most recent physical security assessment report is available with evidence that critical findings have been remediated
Be prepared for an examiner site visit; ensure all physical security controls are operational and staff at security desks can demonstrate visitor management procedures

10.25.title: Data Centre Redundancy
10.25.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will check for continuous environmental monitoring with defined thresholds for temperature, humidity, and water detection, automated alerting with documented escalation procedures, and evidence that environmental conditions have been maintained within acceptable ranges.

evidenceItems.name: Environmental monitoring system documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the environmental monitoring system including sensor placement, monitored parameters, system architecture, and integration with building management systems
whatGoodLooksLike: System architecture diagram showing sensor types, placement locations, data collection infrastructure, and alerting platform
Sensor placement maps for each data centre room showing temperature sensors at intake, exhaust, hot aisle, and cold aisle positions
Water leak detection sensor placement under raised floors, near cooling units, and along water supply routes
Integration documentation showing how environmental data feeds into the building management system (BMS) and alerting platform
Redundancy provisions for monitoring infrastructure ensuring monitoring continues even during partial system failures
commonGaps: Only room-level temperature monitoring with no granularity for hot/cold aisle or rack-level monitoring
No water leak detection sensors installed beneath raised floors or near cooling infrastructure
Environmental monitoring system is standalone with no integration into BMS or centralised alerting
Single points of failure in monitoring infrastructure; a network switch failure could blind all environmental monitoring
suggestedSources: Data centre infrastructure management (DCIM) platform (Nlyte, Sunbird, Schneider EcoStruxure)
Building management system (BMS) documentation
Data centre facilities team technical documentation

evidenceItems.name: Temperature and humidity threshold settings
evidenceItems.description: Documented threshold configurations for temperature and humidity monitoring with approved ranges and alert trigger points
whatGoodLooksLike: Formally approved threshold settings aligned with ASHRAE recommendations: temperature 18-27 degrees Celsius recommended, humidity 40-60% RH
Tiered alert thresholds: warning (approaching limits), critical (at limits), emergency (exceeding limits) with different response actions at each tier
Threshold settings documented in the BMS or DCIM configuration with evidence of formal approval
Seasonal adjustment procedures for threshold settings if applicable to the facility location
commonGaps: Threshold settings not formally documented or approved; configured by individual engineers without governance
Only a single alert threshold configured with no tiered escalation based on severity
Thresholds set too broadly (e.g. 15-35 degrees Celsius) providing insufficient early warning
Humidity monitoring not configured or thresholds not defined despite sensors being installed
suggestedSources: BMS or DCIM configuration interface screenshots
Data centre operations standard operating procedures
Environmental monitoring system configuration documentation

evidenceItems.name: Alert configuration and escalation procedures
evidenceItems.description: Configuration of environmental alert rules and the escalation procedures defining who is notified at each severity level and what response actions are required
whatGoodLooksLike: Alert rules configured in the monitoring platform with defined thresholds, notification channels (email, SMS, pager), and recipient groups for each severity level

Escalation matrix showing warning alerts to on-site operations, critical alerts to duty manager and on-call engineer, and emergency alerts to senior management with automated zone shutdown consideration
Evidence of alert delivery testing showing that notifications reach intended recipients reliably
Documented response procedures for each alert type including immediate actions, investigation steps, and reporting requirements
commonGaps: Alerts configured but sent only to a shared email inbox that is not actively monitored outside business hours
No escalation matrix; all alerts go to the same recipients regardless of severity
Alert fatigue from poorly tuned thresholds causing frequent false alarms that are routinely ignored
No after-hours alerting capability; environmental events outside business hours go undetected until staff arrive
suggestedSources: DCIM or BMS alerting configuration
IT operations on-call roster and escalation procedures
Alert testing and delivery confirmation records

evidenceItems.name: Environmental monitoring reports
evidenceItems.description: Regular reports showing environmental conditions over time, threshold breach events, alert response actions, and trend analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly reports with graphs showing temperature and humidity trends for each monitored zone over the reporting period
Summary of all threshold breach events with timestamps, duration, peak values, root cause, and response actions taken
Trend analysis identifying any deteriorating environmental conditions (e.g. gradually increasing baseline temperatures indicating cooling capacity issues)
Confirmation that all environmental parameters were maintained within approved thresholds for the majority of the reporting period
commonGaps: No regular environmental reports generated; data exists in the monitoring system but is never analysed or reported
Reports show only current readings without historical trend analysis
Threshold breaches recorded but no root cause analysis or corrective action documented
Reports not reviewed by management; generated but filed without action on identified issues
suggestedSources: DCIM reporting module or BMS data exports
Data centre operations monthly reporting pack
IT operations management meeting minutes

auditTips: Obtain current-day screenshots from the environmental monitoring dashboard showing real-time temperature and humidity readings within approved thresholds
Prepare a 12-month trend report demonstrating consistent environmental control with minimal threshold breaches
Have the water leak detection sensor placement map ready and be prepared to show sensor locations during a potential site visit
Demonstrate the alerting mechanism works by showing recent alert test records or actual alert-and-response examples from the past quarter
Ensure HVAC maintenance records and service contracts are available as supporting evidence for environmental control reliability

10.26.title: Production Data Centre Physical Security
10.26.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will specifically verify redundant power supply design, UPS capacity and configuration, backup generator capability with minimum 72-hour runtime, and evidence of regular power failover testing demonstrating successful transitions.

evidenceItems.name: Power redundancy design documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation of the data centre power redundancy architecture including single-line diagrams, capacity calculations, and failover design
whatGoodLooksLike: Single-line power distribution diagram showing dual utility feeds (where available), ATS configuration, UPS topology, and generator connections
2N or N+1 redundancy design with independent power paths to critical equipment racks
Capacity calculations showing total critical load, UPS capacity, generator capacity, and redundancy margins
Formal approval of the power architecture design by the CTO or Head of Infrastructure
Design review records showing annual assessment of power architecture adequacy
commonGaps: No formal power architecture documentation; design knowledge held only by facilities personnel
Single utility feed without redundancy at the utility supply level
Power capacity running at over 80% with insufficient headroom for growth or N+1 redundancy
Architecture documentation outdated and not reflecting recent infrastructure additions
suggestedSources: Data centre facilities engineering documentation
Electrical contractor design documents and as-built drawings
DCIM power monitoring reports

evidenceItems.name: UPS capacity and configuration records
evidenceItems.description: UPS system specifications, capacity records, battery health reports, and maintenance history
whatGoodLooksLike: UPS inventory listing all units with model, rated capacity (kVA), actual load, battery type, and battery age
Battery health reports from recent load testing showing actual runtime under load exceeds minimum requirements (15+ minutes for generator start-up)
UPS configuration documentation showing redundancy topology (N+1, 2N, or parallel) and failover behaviour
Preventive maintenance records showing regular battery replacement, capacitor inspection, and firmware updates
Monitoring configuration evidence showing real-time capacity, battery health, and alert thresholds
commonGaps: UPS batteries past their recommended replacement date with no scheduled replacement
Actual battery runtime under load significantly less than rated runtime due to battery degradation
No regular UPS load testing to validate battery runtime under actual conditions
UPS capacity insufficient for current load with no headroom for growth or redundancy
suggestedSources: UPS management console (APC, Eaton, Vertiv)
Preventive maintenance records from UPS vendor service contracts
DCIM power monitoring module

evidenceItems.name: Generator capacity and fuel supply records
evidenceItems.description: Backup generator specifications, fuel capacity calculations demonstrating 72-hour runtime, fuel supply agreements, and fuel level monitoring records
whatGoodLooksLike: Generator specifications showing rated capacity (kW), fuel consumption rate at full load, and fuel tank capacity
Calculation demonstrating that fuel tank capacity supports minimum 72 continuous hours of operation at expected load levels
Fuel supply agreements with at least two independent suppliers for emergency refuelling during extended outages
Fuel level monitoring records showing regular level checks and top-up activities
Generator maintenance records including regular load bank testing and preventive maintenance
commonGaps: Fuel tank capacity insufficient for 72-hour runtime at full load; calculation based on partial load assumptions
No fuel supply agreements for emergency refuelling beyond initial tank capacity
Fuel level not monitored; risk of generators running dry during extended outages
Generator maintenance overdue or load bank testing not conducted regularly
suggestedSources: Data centre facilities engineering records
Fuel supplier contracts and delivery records
Generator vendor maintenance and service reports

evidenceItems.name: Power failover test records
evidenceItems.description: Records of power failover tests demonstrating successful transfer from utility power to UPS to generator and back, with measured transfer times and any issues identified
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly test records showing full failover sequence: utility failure simulation, UPS assumption of load, generator start-up and load transfer, and return to utility power
Measured transfer times at each stage (utility to UPS: instantaneous, UPS to generator: under 10 seconds)
Load measurements during failover showing all critical systems maintained without interruption
Documentation of any anomalies observed during testing with root cause analysis and remediation
Sign-off by the responsible engineer and data centre manager on each test result
commonGaps: No power failover testing conducted in the past 12 months
Testing limited to generator start-up only, without full load transfer and return to utility
Test anomalies identified but not remediated before the next test cycle
Tests conducted under reduced load conditions that do not represent actual production load
suggestedSources: Data centre operations failover test reports
Generator vendor service reports
DCIM power event logs during failover tests

auditTips: Have the 72-hour fuel capacity calculation ready with clear assumptions about load levels and fuel consumption rates, as this is a specific BNM numerical requirement
Prepare the most recent power failover test report showing successful full failover with measured transfer times
Ensure UPS battery health reports are current (within the past 6 months) showing actual runtime under load meets or exceeds minimum requirements
Have fuel supply agreements available showing at least two independent suppliers with defined emergency delivery response times
Be prepared to show the power monitoring dashboard with current load levels, capacity utilisation, and redundancy margins

10.27.title: Data Centre Operations Control Procedures
10.27.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that fire detection and suppression systems are appropriate for electronic equipment protection (gas-based, not water-based in server rooms), are tested at least annually with documented results, and that staff are trained on emergency response procedures.

evidenceItems.name: Fire suppression system specifications
evidenceItems.description: Technical specifications and design documentation for fire detection and suppression systems installed in data centre facilities
whatGoodLooksLike: System specifications showing gas-based suppression (FM-200, Novec 1230, or inert gas) deployed in all server rooms and critical areas
VESDA or equivalent aspirating smoke detection system deployed for very early smoke detection in server rooms
System design documentation showing coverage areas, discharge calculations, and room integrity requirements
Integration documentation showing how fire detection links to HVAC shutdown, access control (door release), and notification systems
Compliance certificates showing adherence to local fire safety regulations (BOMBA in Malaysia)
commonGaps: Water-based sprinkler systems in server rooms instead of gas-based suppression appropriate for electronic equipment
No VESDA or aspirating detection; reliance on standard point-type smoke detectors that activate too late for data centre environments
Suppression system coverage does not extend to all areas (e.g. cable risers, under-floor spaces, above-ceiling areas)
No integration between fire detection and HVAC shutdown, allowing smoke to spread through ventilation
suggestedSources: Fire protection system vendor documentation and certificates
Data centre facilities engineering records
Fire safety compliance certificates (BOMBA approval)

evidenceItems.name: Annual fire suppression testing records
evidenceItems.description: Records of annual fire detection and suppression system testing including detector sensitivity tests, gas cylinder pressure checks, and system activation tests
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual test reports from qualified fire protection engineers covering all detection and suppression zones
Detector sensitivity testing records showing all detectors within manufacturer specifications
Gas cylinder weight and pressure verification confirming adequate suppression agent levels
System activation tests (simulated, not live discharge) confirming proper sequence: detection, alarm, HVAC shutdown, pre-discharge warning, suppression activation
Room integrity testing (door fan test) confirming gas retention capability
