commonGaps: No annual testing conducted; last test records are more than 12 months old
Testing limited to detection only, without testing the complete suppression activation sequence
Gas cylinders not weighed or pressure-checked to verify adequate agent levels
Room integrity not tested; gas leakage through gaps in walls, floors, or ceiling could render suppression ineffective
suggestedSources: Fire protection system maintenance contractor test reports
Facility management maintenance records
Fire safety inspection certificates

evidenceItems.name: Staff emergency response training records
evidenceItems.description: Records of staff training on fire emergency response procedures including training content, attendance, and drill participation
whatGoodLooksLike: Training records showing all data centre personnel completed fire emergency response training within the past 12 months
Training content covering fire alarm response, evacuation routes, suppression system operation, emergency power shutdown procedures, and first response actions
Fire drill records with dates, participant lists, observed response times, and identified improvements
New employee induction records showing fire safety training completed before unescorted data centre access is granted
Competency assessment records for key personnel responsible for suppression system manual activation
commonGaps: Training records incomplete; not all data centre staff have received fire emergency training
No fire drills conducted in the past 12 months; training is classroom-only without practical exercises
Training content generic and not specific to the data centre fire suppression systems in use
New employees not trained on fire procedures before being granted data centre access
suggestedSources: HR learning management system (LMS) training records
Data centre operations training registers
Fire safety officer drill reports

evidenceItems.name: Fire detection system configuration
evidenceItems.description: Configuration documentation for fire detection systems including zone definitions, alarm thresholds, notification routing, and integration with building systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Zone map showing fire detection zones aligned with data centre room layout and suppression zones
VESDA sensitivity settings appropriate for the environment type (server room, office, storage)
Alarm notification routing showing how fire alarms are communicated to building management, security, and fire services
Integration configuration with HVAC (automatic shutdown), access control (door release for evacuation), and power systems (emergency shutdown if required)
Regular calibration records for detection system sensors
commonGaps: Detection zones too large; a single zone covers multiple rooms, making it impossible to identify the specific area of a fire
VESDA sensitivity levels not calibrated for the specific environment, leading to false alarms or missed detections
No automatic HVAC shutdown on fire detection; manual intervention required to stop smoke spread
Fire alarm notifications do not reach 24/7 monitoring personnel outside business hours
suggestedSources: Fire alarm control panel configuration exports
VESDA system configuration interface
BMS fire system integration documentation

auditTips: Have the most recent annual fire system test report readily available, dated within the past 12 months, conducted by a qualified fire protection engineer
Prepare training attendance records showing 100% completion of fire emergency training for all data centre personnel
Ensure the fire system vendor maintenance contract is current and includes annual testing obligations
Be prepared to demonstrate that gas-based (not water-based) suppression is installed in all server rooms during any site visit
Have room integrity test results available showing adequate gas retention for the suppression hold time required

10.28.title: Data Centre Activity Segregation
10.28.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify multi-factor physical access controls with biometric authentication for sensitive areas, quarterly access review records with documented evidence of revocations, and that terminated employees have their access immediately revoked with timestamp evidence.

evidenceItems.name: Physical access control system configuration
evidenceItems.description: Configuration documentation of the physical access control system showing multi-factor authentication requirements, zone definitions, and access policies
whatGoodLooksLike: System configuration showing multi-factor authentication (card + biometric) required for server rooms and sensitive areas
Zone hierarchy documented with escalating authentication requirements at each level
Anti-passback configuration preventing a single credential from being used for multiple consecutive entries
Integration with HR systems for automated access provisioning and deprovisioning workflows
Comprehensive logging configuration capturing all access events with individual identification
commonGaps: Card-only access to server rooms without biometric requirement
Biometric readers installed but configured in fallback mode allowing card-only entry
Anti-passback not enabled, allowing card sharing between personnel
Access events not logged or log retention insufficient for investigation purposes
suggestedSources: Physical access control system administration console (HID, Lenel, Honeywell)
Data centre security operations documentation
System integrator configuration reports

evidenceItems.name: Biometric system deployment records
evidenceItems.description: Records of biometric system deployment including device inventory, enrolment records, and system health monitoring
whatGoodLooksLike: Biometric device inventory showing model, location, authentication type (fingerprint, iris, palm vein), and firmware version
Enrolment records showing all authorised personnel have active biometric enrolments
System availability and health monitoring reports showing biometric devices operational at 99%+ uptime
False rejection rate (FRR) monitoring to ensure biometric systems are not causing excessive access delays
Regular maintenance and calibration records for biometric devices
commonGaps: Biometric devices deployed at some but not all server room entry points
High false rejection rates causing staff to use override procedures that bypass biometric authentication
Biometric enrolment not completed for all authorised personnel; some rely on card-only bypass
No monitoring of biometric system health; device failures go undetected until staff report issues
suggestedSources: Biometric system management console
Data centre operations maintenance records
Access control system integration logs

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly access review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of quarterly reviews of data centre physical access rights including the access list reviewed, decisions made, and approvals
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly review reports showing complete access list reviewed against current employee roster and role requirements
Documented decisions for each access holder: retain (with current justification), revoke (no longer required), or modify (zone changes)
Sign-off by the data centre manager or designated approving authority on each review cycle
Evidence of access removals actioned within the review cycle for personnel who no longer require access
Reconciliation between the access control system and the approved access list to confirm no discrepancies
commonGaps: Access reviews not conducted quarterly; gaps of 6+ months between reviews
Reviews conducted but only verifying a sample of access holders rather than the complete list
No documented sign-off or approval on review outcomes
Reviews identify stale access but revocations are not actioned promptly after the review
suggestedSources: Data centre access management team records
Access control system reports (current access list exports)
HR system employee status reports for reconciliation

evidenceItems.name: Access revocation records for terminations and role changes
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating immediate access revocation upon employee termination or role change, with timestamps showing time between notification and revocation
whatGoodLooksLike: Revocation records showing HR notification timestamp and access removal timestamp for each termination event
Time-to-revoke metric consistently within 24 hours of termination notification
Access control system audit trail showing the specific access rights removed and the operator who performed the revocation
Process for role changes showing access reviewed and adjusted (added or removed zones) based on new role requirements
Automated integration between HR termination workflow and access control system for immediate flagging
commonGaps: Revocation delays exceeding 24 hours, sometimes days or weeks after termination
No timestamp evidence; revocations recorded but without precise timing to demonstrate promptness
Role changes not triggering access reviews; employees who move roles retain their previous access in addition to new access
Contract and temporary worker access not subject to the same revocation processes as permanent employees
suggestedSources: Access control system audit logs
HR termination and role change notification records
IT service desk revocation request tickets

auditTips: Prepare a report showing all four quarterly access reviews conducted in the past 12 months with sign-off evidence and revocation actions taken
Have a sample of termination revocation records ready showing timestamps that demonstrate revocation within 24 hours of notification
Ensure biometric system configuration evidence is current, showing biometric authentication is required (not optional) for server room entry
Reconcile the current access list against the HR active employee roster before the audit to identify and remove any stale access proactively
Be prepared to demonstrate the access request and approval workflow, showing how new access requests are justified and approved before provisioning

10.29.title: System Capacity Planning
10.29.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that SLAs are formally defined for all critical technology services with specific availability and performance targets, that performance is continuously monitored, and that monthly reports are presented to senior management with evidence of breach management.

evidenceItems.name: Service level agreement documents
evidenceItems.description: Formally documented SLAs for all critical technology services defining availability targets, performance metrics, incident response times, and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual SLA documents for each critical service (core banking, internet banking, mobile banking, ATM, payments) with specific measurable targets
Availability targets expressed as percentages (e.g. 99.9% uptime) with clear measurement methodology and exclusion windows
Performance metrics including transaction response times, throughput targets, and error rate thresholds
Incident response time commitments by severity level (P1: 15 minutes, P2: 30 minutes, P3: 2 hours, P4: 8 hours)
Joint sign-off by business owner and technology owner with review date
commonGaps: SLAs exist only for externally provided services (vendor SLAs) but not for internally delivered technology services
Availability targets defined but performance metrics (response times, throughput) not specified
SLAs not signed off by business owners; treated as IT-internal targets without business agreement
No SLA defined for newer digital channels (mobile banking, API platform) while legacy channels are covered
suggestedSources: IT service management platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy)
CTO or IT operations office documentation
Business unit agreements repository

evidenceItems.name: SLA monitoring dashboards or reports
evidenceItems.description: Real-time or near-real-time monitoring dashboards showing SLA compliance status for all critical technology services
whatGoodLooksLike: Live dashboards showing current availability, response times, and throughput against SLA targets for each critical service
Red/amber/green status indicators making SLA compliance immediately visible
Historical trend views showing SLA performance over time (daily, weekly, monthly)
Automated alerting configured when services approach or breach SLA thresholds
Both synthetic monitoring (proactive checks) and real-user monitoring (actual customer experience) data included
commonGaps: No real-time SLA monitoring; compliance calculated retrospectively from logs after the fact
Monitoring covers availability only, missing performance metrics (response time, throughput)
Dashboards not accessible to business stakeholders; only IT operations teams can view SLA status
Synthetic monitoring only, without real-user experience measurement
suggestedSources: Application performance monitoring tools (Dynatrace, AppDynamics, New Relic, Datadog)
Infrastructure monitoring platforms (SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix)
IT service management platform SLA module

evidenceItems.name: Monthly SLA performance reports to management
evidenceItems.description: Monthly reports on SLA performance for all critical technology services presented to senior management
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly reports with SLA achievement percentages for each critical service against defined targets
Trend analysis showing month-over-month SLA performance trajectory
Detailed breakdown of any SLA breaches including root cause, duration, customer impact, and corrective actions
Evidence of presentation to senior management (meeting minutes, email distribution records, management portal access logs)
Action items arising from management review with tracked follow-up
commonGaps: Monthly reports not consistently produced; gaps in the reporting cycle over the past 12 months
Reports produced but not presented to senior management; filed without management review
Reports show SLA achievement percentages without analysis of breaches or corrective actions
No evidence of management discussion or action items arising from SLA performance reviews
suggestedSources: IT operations monthly reporting pack
IT steering committee or management meeting minutes
Executive dashboard or management reporting portal

evidenceItems.name: SLA breach records and remediation actions
evidenceItems.description: Records of SLA breach events including root cause analysis, business impact, remediation actions, and preventive measures
whatGoodLooksLike: Breach register listing each SLA breach with service affected, breach duration, root cause, business impact, and assigned remediation owner
Root cause analysis for each significant breach with identified preventive measures
Remediation actions tracked to completion with evidence of effectiveness verification
Trend analysis showing whether similar breaches are recurring or whether remediation actions are reducing breach frequency
Escalation records showing timely notification of breaches to appropriate management levels
commonGaps: SLA breaches not formally recorded; known informally but not tracked in a breach register
Root cause analysis not conducted for SLA breaches; same issues recur without investigation
Remediation actions identified but not tracked to completion; open items accumulate over time
No trend analysis; management cannot determine whether SLA compliance is improving or deteriorating
suggestedSources: IT service management platform incident and problem modules
SLA breach register or risk event log
Problem management root cause analysis reports

auditTips: Prepare 12 consecutive monthly SLA performance reports to demonstrate consistent reporting to management, with evidence of management receipt (meeting minutes or distribution records)
Have the current SLA monitoring dashboard ready to demonstrate real-time visibility of service performance against targets
Prepare a summary of all SLA breaches in the past 12 months with root cause analysis and remediation evidence for each
Ensure all SLA documents have current business and technology owner sign-off within the past 12 months
Be ready to demonstrate how SLA targets were derived from business requirements and risk appetite, not just set arbitrarily by IT

10.30.title: Real-Time Performance Monitoring
10.30.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that IT service continuity plans exist for all critical services, are aligned with business continuity management, address multiple disruption scenarios (natural disasters, cyber attacks, technology failures), and have been tested with documented results.

evidenceItems.name: IT service continuity plan documents
evidenceItems.description: Documented IT service continuity plans for all critical technology services detailing recovery procedures, resource requirements, and activation criteria
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual continuity plans for each critical service with specific recovery procedures, resource requirements, and prerequisites
Clear activation criteria defining when continuity plans are invoked and who has authority to activate them
Technology dependency mapping showing all upstream and downstream dependencies for each critical service
Plans covering multiple disruption scenarios: site loss, partial infrastructure failure, cyber attack (ransomware, DDoS), and pandemic
Version control with annual review dates and formal approval by technology and business owners
commonGaps: Continuity plans exist for some critical services but not all; newer services or recently outsourced services lack plans
Plans are generic templates that have not been customised to the specific technology stack and dependencies of each service
Plans do not address cyber attack scenarios (e.g. ransomware recovery from clean backups, DDoS mitigation procedures)
Plans stored only in electronic format on systems that would be unavailable during a major disruption
suggestedSources: IT operations or BCM team documentation repository
Business continuity management system (Fusion, Castellan)
CTO office governance documentation

evidenceItems.name: Alignment mapping with business continuity plans
evidenceItems.description: Mapping document demonstrating alignment between IT service continuity plans and the institution's business continuity plans
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix mapping each critical business process (from BCP) to its corresponding IT service continuity plan, showing complete coverage
RTO/RPO alignment verification showing IT recovery targets meet or exceed business continuity requirements
Evidence of joint planning sessions between BCM and IT continuity teams
Identified gaps where business continuity requirements exceed current IT recovery capabilities, with remediation plans
Cross-reference between BIA findings and IT continuity plan scope
commonGaps: No formal mapping; BCM and IT continuity planning conducted independently without coordination
IT recovery targets set without reference to business continuity requirements, resulting in misalignment
BCP updated but IT continuity plans not updated to reflect changes in business priorities
New business processes or products launched without corresponding IT continuity plan updates
suggestedSources: BCM programme documentation
IT-BCM alignment workshop minutes and outputs
Enterprise risk management framework documents

evidenceItems.name: Disruption scenario analysis
evidenceItems.description: Analysis of various disruption scenarios and their potential impact on critical technology services, informing continuity plan design
whatGoodLooksLike: Scenario library covering natural disasters (floods, earthquakes), cyber attacks (ransomware, DDoS, data breach), technology failures (hardware, software, network, cloud provider outage), and workforce disruption (pandemic)
Impact assessment for each scenario showing affected services, estimated downtime, data loss risk, and financial impact
Likelihood assessment based on historical incidents, threat intelligence, and geographic risk factors relevant to Malaysia
Scenario-specific recovery strategies and resource requirements documented for each scenario type
Annual review of scenario library to incorporate new threats and lessons from real incidents
commonGaps: Scenario analysis covers only natural disasters without addressing cyber attack or technology-specific failure scenarios
No likelihood or impact assessment; scenarios listed but not analysed for probability or severity
Scenarios not specific to the institution's geography and infrastructure; generic templates used without customisation
No consideration of cascading failures across dependent systems
suggestedSources: IT risk management scenario planning documentation
BCM programme scenario analysis
Threat intelligence reports informing scenario development

evidenceItems.name: Continuity plan testing records
evidenceItems.description: Records of IT service continuity plan testing including test objectives, scenarios exercised, results, and lessons learned
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual test schedule showing all critical services tested with defined objectives and success criteria
Test reports documenting scenario exercised, participants, timeline of events, issues encountered, and overall pass/fail assessment
Mix of test types conducted: tabletop exercises, partial failover tests, and full failover tests on a rotating schedule
Lessons learned documented with specific improvement actions assigned to owners with target completion dates
Evidence that previous test findings have been remediated before the next test cycle
commonGaps: No continuity plan testing conducted in the past 12 months
Testing limited to tabletop exercises without any technical failover validation
Test results not documented beyond a pass/fail outcome; no detailed analysis of issues or lessons learned
Lessons learned identified but not tracked to remediation; same issues discovered in successive test cycles
suggestedSources: IT operations or BCM team test documentation
DR test reports (overlap with clause 10.32)
Post-test review meeting minutes

auditTips: Prepare a coverage matrix showing every critical service listed in the BIA has a corresponding, current IT service continuity plan
Have the BCP-to-IT alignment mapping ready, demonstrating that every critical business process has IT recovery coverage within its required RTO
Ensure continuity plans are stored in locations accessible during a major disruption (printed copies, offline devices, or geographically separate cloud storage)
Prepare the scenario analysis documentation showing consideration of multiple scenario types including cyber attack scenarios
Have test reports from the past 12 months available with evidence of lessons learned and remediation actions completed

10.31.title: Digital Services Resilience Enhancement
10.31.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that DR plans exist for every critical system with detailed recovery procedures, defined roles and responsibilities, communication plans, and escalation procedures, and that plans are accessible to relevant personnel during emergencies through out-of-band means.

evidenceItems.name: Disaster recovery plan documents for critical systems
evidenceItems.description: Detailed DR plans for each critical system including step-by-step recovery procedures, prerequisites, and validation steps
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual DR plans for each critical system (core banking, payment processing, internet banking, ATM, SWIFT) with system-specific recovery procedures
Step-by-step procedures that can be followed by any qualified engineer, not just the original author
Prerequisites clearly listed including infrastructure requirements, data sources, credentials, and vendor contacts
Failback procedures covering the return to normal operations after DR site activation
Version control with review within the past 12 months and sign-off by technical and business owners
commonGaps: Generic DR plan template used across all systems without customisation to each system's specific technology stack and dependencies
Plans too high-level to be executable; lacking specific commands, configuration details, and validation steps
Plans not updated after infrastructure changes, referencing outdated server names, IP addresses, or software versions
Failback procedures missing or incomplete, covering only the failover direction
suggestedSources: IT operations or DR team documentation repository
DR plan management system
System-specific technical documentation maintained by application teams

evidenceItems.name: DR roles and responsibilities matrix
evidenceItems.description: RACI matrix or equivalent document defining roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths for disaster recovery operations
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI matrix for each critical system identifying DR coordinator, technical recovery leads, business validation owners, communication leads, and vendor contacts
Named individuals (not just roles) with current contact details including out-of-hours mobile numbers
Backup personnel identified for each critical role to address availability during holidays, illness, or simultaneous incidents
Clear escalation paths with time-based triggers defining when to escalate to the next management level
Regular update (at least quarterly) to reflect personnel changes
commonGaps: Roles defined in generic terms (e.g. 'system administrator') without naming specific individuals
No backup personnel identified for critical roles; single points of failure in the recovery team
Contact details outdated; personnel have changed roles or left the organisation without matrix updates
No escalation paths defined; recovery teams work without management visibility until they request help
suggestedSources: DR plan appendices or annexes
IT operations on-call roster and contact lists
BCM programme role assignment documentation

evidenceItems.name: Communication and escalation procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented communication protocols and escalation procedures for use during disaster recovery activation
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication plan defining notification sequences for internal stakeholders (IT teams, management, board), external stakeholders (BNM, customers), and vendors
Pre-drafted notification templates for different DR scenarios reducing communication preparation time during a crisis
Escalation procedures with time-based triggers (e.g. if RTO milestone not met within 50% of target time, escalate to CTO)
Out-of-band communication channels identified (mobile phones, satellite phones, personal email) for use when primary systems are affected
Contact trees tested and validated within the past quarter
commonGaps: Communication procedures limited to internal IT notification; no external stakeholder communication plan
No pre-drafted notification templates; communication must be composed from scratch during a high-stress incident
Escalation triggers not time-bound; escalation depends on subjective judgement rather than predefined milestones
Communication plan relies on email and internal messaging which may be unavailable during the disaster
suggestedSources: Crisis communication plan (overlap with clause 10.35)
DR plan communication annexes
IT operations escalation procedures

evidenceItems.name: DR plan distribution and accessibility evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that DR plans are distributed to all relevant personnel and accessible through out-of-band means during emergencies
whatGoodLooksLike: Distribution records showing all named personnel in the DR RACI matrix have received current versions of relevant plans
Acknowledgement receipts confirming recipients have read and understood their DR responsibilities
Out-of-band storage evidence: printed copies in secure DR packs, offline copies on encrypted USB drives, or plans stored in geographically separate cloud storage
DR plan access tested during exercises to verify personnel can retrieve plans when primary systems are unavailable
Plan accessibility verified as part of new employee onboarding for DR-critical roles
commonGaps: DR plans stored only on internal file servers or SharePoint which would be unavailable if the primary data centre is affected
No evidence of plan distribution; plans exist centrally but are not pushed to individual recipients
Personnel in DR roles unaware of their specific responsibilities due to lack of plan familiarisation
Printed DR packs stored at the primary site rather than at the DR site or off-site location
suggestedSources: Document management system distribution and acknowledgement logs
DR pack inventory and location records
DR exercise observations noting plan accessibility

auditTips: Prepare a coverage matrix showing that every system classified as critical in the BIA has a corresponding current DR plan
Have sample DR plans ready that demonstrate system-specific, step-by-step procedures rather than generic templates
Demonstrate out-of-band plan accessibility by showing where printed copies or offline versions are stored, separate from primary infrastructure
Ensure the RACI matrix is current with named individuals (not just roles), current contact details, and backup personnel assignments
Be prepared to explain how DR plans are kept current after infrastructure changes, including the trigger for plan updates in the change management process

10.32.title: Critical System High Availability Requirements
10.32.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will specifically look for annual full failover test evidence for all critical systems, measured RTO and RPO validation results, and documented deficiency remediation with tracked progress. Tabletop exercises alone are insufficient.

evidenceItems.name: DR test schedule and completion records
evidenceItems.description: Annual DR test schedule covering all critical systems and records confirming test completion
whatGoodLooksLike: Approved annual schedule listing each critical system with planned test dates, test type (tabletop, partial failover, full failover), and assigned test leads
Completion records confirming all scheduled tests were conducted, with any rescheduling documented and justified
Coverage report showing 100% of critical systems tested within the 12-month cycle
Test types rotated across years to cover different failure scenarios (site loss, component failure, cyber attack recovery)
Schedule approved by CTO or IT Risk Committee
commonGaps: No formal test schedule; testing conducted on an ad hoc basis without comprehensive coverage
Schedule exists but multiple critical systems not tested within the 12-month cycle due to postponements
Only tabletop exercises scheduled; no full failover tests planned or conducted
Testing focused on the same systems each year while other critical systems are never tested
suggestedSources: DR programme management documentation
IT operations annual planning records
IT Risk Committee meeting minutes approving the test schedule

evidenceItems.name: Full failover test results
evidenceItems.description: Detailed results from full failover tests to the DR site including timeline, observations, and outcomes
whatGoodLooksLike: Detailed test reports for each critical system showing the complete failover timeline from activation to service restoration at the DR site
Measured recovery times compared against defined RTO targets with pass/fail determination
Business user validation confirming recovered services support actual business operations (transaction processing, reporting, customer functions)
Screenshots or system evidence showing services operational at the DR site
Failback results documenting the return to normal operations after the test
commonGaps: No full failover conducted; only paper-based or simulated tests performed
Full failover attempted but critical issues prevented successful completion, with no clear remediation plan
Recovery times not measured or not compared against RTO targets
Technical recovery confirmed but no business user validation of functional correctness
suggestedSources: DR test execution reports from IT operations
Business validation sign-off forms from test exercises
DR site system monitoring data during failover tests

evidenceItems.name: RTO and RPO validation records
evidenceItems.description: Records specifically documenting the validation of RTO and RPO achievement during DR tests for each critical system
whatGoodLooksLike: Table showing each critical system with defined RTO, actual recovery time achieved in the latest test, and pass/fail status
RPO validation showing data loss measurement: timestamp of last committed transaction before failover versus first available transaction at DR site
Trend data showing RTO/RPO performance across multiple test cycles demonstrating consistency or improvement
For any systems failing to meet targets, documented gap analysis with remediation actions and target dates
Business owner sign-off accepting the validated RTO/RPO results
commonGaps: RTO measured from DR site activation rather than from the point of disruption, understating actual recovery time
RPO not measured; data loss not quantified during failover tests
Multiple critical systems failing RTO targets with no documented remediation plan
Validation conducted under ideal conditions (pre-staged, pre-warmed) that do not reflect real disaster scenarios
suggestedSources: DR test results database or reporting system
RTO/RPO register with validation history
Business owner validation sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Deficiency remediation plans and tracking
evidenceItems.description: Documented plans for remediating deficiencies identified during DR testing with assigned owners, timelines, and progress tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: Deficiency register listing each finding from DR tests with description, risk rating, assigned owner, target remediation date, and current status
Remediation plans with specific actions for each deficiency, not just generic statements of intent
Progress tracking showing deficiencies being closed within defined timelines
Verification testing evidence confirming remediated deficiencies are resolved in subsequent tests
Reporting to IT Risk Committee on deficiency status and remediation progress
commonGaps: Deficiencies identified in test reports but not formally tracked in a remediation register
Remediation timelines repeatedly extended without justification or management approval
Same deficiencies found in successive test cycles, indicating remediation actions were ineffective or not implemented
No verification testing to confirm remediation effectiveness
suggestedSources: DR deficiency tracking register or project management tool
IT risk register entries for DR deficiencies
IT Risk Committee reporting packs showing deficiency status

auditTips: Prepare the annual DR test schedule alongside completion evidence to demonstrate 100% coverage of all critical systems within the past 12 months
Have the RTO/RPO validation summary table ready showing each critical system with defined targets, actual results, and pass/fail status
Ensure full failover test reports include business user validation, not just technical recovery confirmation
Prepare the deficiency remediation tracker showing current status of all open items with evidence that critical deficiencies are being actively addressed
Be prepared to explain any systems that failed to meet RTO/RPO targets, including the remediation plan and expected resolution date

10.33.title: Non-NCII Entity Service Availability Guidance
10.33.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that RTO and RPO targets are defined for all critical systems, are based on business impact analysis, have been formally approved by business owners, and are validated through regular DR testing with documented results.

evidenceItems.name: RTO and RPO definitions for critical systems
evidenceItems.description: Documented RTO and RPO targets for all critical systems with their basis in business impact analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Register listing every critical system with its defined RTO (maximum tolerable downtime) and RPO (maximum tolerable data loss) targets
Clear linkage between each RTO/RPO target and the corresponding BIA finding that informed the target
Tiered classification showing different RTO/RPO targets based on system criticality (Tier 1: RTO 2 hours/RPO near-zero, Tier 2: RTO 8 hours/RPO 1 hour, Tier 3: RTO 24 hours/RPO 24 hours)
Targets expressed in specific, measurable terms (hours and minutes) rather than qualitative descriptions
Defined measurement methodology specifying when the RTO clock starts (point of disruption vs point of DR declaration)
commonGaps: RTO/RPO defined only for major systems; newer or less visible critical systems lack defined targets
Targets not linked to BIA; set based on technical capability rather than business requirement
All systems given the same RTO/RPO without differentiation based on criticality
Ambiguous measurement methodology; unclear when the RTO timer starts and stops
suggestedSources: DR programme documentation or RTO/RPO register
BIA report with linked recovery targets
IT Risk Committee approved recovery targets documentation

evidenceItems.name: Business owner approval records
evidenceItems.description: Formal approval records from business owners accepting the defined RTO and RPO targets for their systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed approval forms or email confirmations from designated business owners for each critical system's RTO and RPO targets
Evidence that business owners understand the implications of the targets (e.g. a 4-hour RTO means up to 4 hours of service unavailability)
Approval records refreshed at least annually, reflecting current business owner understanding and acceptance
Documentation of any business owner requests for more aggressive targets with corresponding impact assessment on technology investment required
Clear identification of the approving business owner by name and role
commonGaps: RTO/RPO targets set by IT without business owner consultation or approval
Approval obtained years ago and never refreshed despite business changes
Business owners approved targets without understanding the practical implications
No formal approval records; verbal agreements without documentation
suggestedSources: Business owner sign-off forms or approval emails
BIA workshop attendance and decision records
IT-business alignment meeting minutes

evidenceItems.name: DR test results validating RTO/RPO compliance
evidenceItems.description: DR test results specifically demonstrating that defined RTO and RPO targets are achievable with current recovery infrastructure and procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Test result summary table for each critical system showing defined RTO vs actual recovery time and defined RPO vs actual data loss
Pass/fail determination for each system based on whether actual results met defined targets
Multiple test cycle results showing consistent achievement of targets (not just a single successful test)
For systems that failed validation, documented gap analysis identifying root cause and remediation actions
Business user validation confirming recovered services are functionally complete within the measured recovery time
commonGaps: DR tests conducted but RTO/RPO specifically not measured or reported
Only RTO validated; RPO (data loss) not measured during failover tests
Test conditions do not reflect realistic disaster scenarios (pre-staged environments, advance preparation)
Systems failing validation in multiple test cycles without effective remediation
suggestedSources: DR test reports with RTO/RPO measurement data (overlap with clause 10.32)
RTO/RPO validation register with historical results
Business user acceptance testing records from DR exercises

evidenceItems.name: Business impact analysis supporting RTO/RPO decisions
evidenceItems.description: BIA documentation showing the analysis that informed RTO and RPO target setting for each critical system
whatGoodLooksLike: BIA report sections showing impact assessment for each critical business process at various downtime intervals (1 hour, 4 hours, 8 hours, 24 hours, 48 hours)
Clear derivation of RTO from the maximum tolerable downtime identified in the BIA
RPO derived from analysis of data loss tolerance for each critical process
Financial, operational, regulatory, and reputational impact quantification supporting target decisions
Annual review evidence showing BIA findings refreshed to reflect current business conditions
commonGaps: BIA conducted but RTO/RPO targets not clearly derived from BIA findings; targets appear arbitrary
BIA impact assessment limited to financial impact only, missing regulatory, reputational, and operational dimensions
BIA not refreshed annually; targets based on outdated business impact assumptions
No consideration of interdependencies between systems when setting individual system RTO/RPO targets
suggestedSources: BIA report (overlap with clause 10.34)
RTO/RPO derivation methodology documentation
IT Risk Committee meeting minutes where RTO/RPO targets were approved

auditTips: Prepare a single-page summary showing all critical systems with their BIA-derived RTO/RPO targets, business owner approval status, and latest DR test validation results
Ensure business owner approvals are current (within the past 12 months) and refreshed following any BIA updates
Be ready to trace any RTO/RPO target from the business owner approval through the BIA finding that informed it to the DR test that validated it
Have explanations ready for any systems where actual DR test recovery times exceeded defined RTOs, including remediation plans
Prepare a comparison showing how RTO/RPO targets have evolved over time, demonstrating they are actively managed rather than static numbers

10.34.title: Technology Diversity for Resilience
10.34.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a comprehensive BIA has been conducted within the past 12 months, covers all critical business processes and their technology dependencies, quantifies the impact of disruption, and directly informs recovery priorities and continuity planning.

evidenceItems.name: Business impact analysis report
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive annual BIA report identifying critical business processes, quantifying disruption impacts, and establishing recovery priorities
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive report covering all business divisions with process-level impact analysis
Impact quantification at multiple time intervals (1 hour, 4 hours, 8 hours, 24 hours, 48 hours, 1 week) across financial, operational, regulatory, and reputational dimensions
Clear criticality classification of each business process (Tier 1 mission-critical, Tier 2 important, Tier 3 standard) based on impact analysis
Recovery priority recommendations driven by impact assessment results
Dated within the past 12 months with evidence of broad stakeholder engagement across business units
commonGaps: BIA not conducted within the past 12 months; relying on outdated analysis that does not reflect current business conditions
BIA conducted by IT only without meaningful engagement from business process owners across divisions
Impact assessment limited to financial impact; missing regulatory (BNM reporting obligations, licence conditions) and reputational dimensions
BIA covers only a subset of business processes; newer digital channels or products not included
suggestedSources: BCM programme documentation
IT risk management team BIA deliverables
Enterprise risk management framework documentation

evidenceItems.name: Critical business process inventory
evidenceItems.description: Structured inventory of all critical business processes identified through the BIA with assigned criticality tiers and business owners
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete inventory listing each critical business process with description, business owner, division, criticality tier, and maximum tolerable downtime
Coverage across all business divisions: retail banking, corporate banking, treasury, operations, compliance, risk management
Tiered classification with clear criteria for each tier and evidence that classification was reviewed with business owners
Linkage to supporting technology services showing which systems each process depends on
Regular update process ensuring new processes are added as the business evolves
commonGaps: Inventory limited to customer-facing processes; back-office and support processes not assessed despite their criticality
No tiered classification; all identified processes treated as equally critical
Business owners not assigned or not involved in criticality assessment
Inventory static; new products and services launched without BIA assessment
suggestedSources: BIA report process inventory section
Business process registry or CMDB service catalogue
Business division heads and process owner input records

evidenceItems.name: Technology service dependency mapping
evidenceItems.description: Mapping showing how each critical business process depends on specific technology services, infrastructure components, and third-party providers
whatGoodLooksLike: Detailed dependency map for each critical business process showing application services, middleware, databases, infrastructure, network, and third-party dependencies
Single points of failure identified and highlighted with risk assessment and mitigation recommendations
Third-party dependency analysis including cloud services, payment networks (PayNet, Visa, Mastercard), market data feeds, and SWIFT
Impact chain analysis showing how failure of individual technology components cascades to affect business processes
Regular validation (at least quarterly) to ensure mapping reflects current infrastructure
commonGaps: Mapping exists at a high level (process to application) without drilling down to infrastructure and third-party dependencies
Single points of failure not identified or assessed despite being present in the architecture
Third-party and cloud service dependencies not mapped, creating blind spots in continuity planning
Mapping not updated after infrastructure changes; references decommissioned systems
suggestedSources: Configuration management database (CMDB) and service catalogue
Enterprise architecture documentation and dependency diagrams
Infrastructure and application team dependency documentation

evidenceItems.name: Annual BIA review and update records
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the annual review and update of the BIA including stakeholder participation, methodology, findings, and changes from the previous year
whatGoodLooksLike: Review records dated within the past 12 months showing structured re-assessment of BIA findings
Stakeholder participation records showing engagement from business process owners across all divisions
Change log documenting what changed from the previous BIA: new processes added, criticality changes, new dependencies identified
Methodology documentation showing how impact was assessed and quantified
BIA findings formally presented to and accepted by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body
commonGaps: No evidence of annual review; BIA report date unchanged for multiple years
Review conducted as a rubber-stamp exercise without substantive re-assessment of business impact assumptions
Only IT involved in the review; business stakeholders not re-engaged to validate or update impact assessments
BIA findings not presented to senior management or board for endorsement
suggestedSources: BCM programme annual review documentation
BIA workshop attendance registers and facilitation materials
Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee meeting minutes

auditTips: Ensure the BIA report is dated within the past 12 months; an outdated BIA is one of the most common examination findings in this area
Prepare evidence of cross-divisional stakeholder participation in the BIA process (attendance registers, interview records, workshop materials)
Have the technology dependency map ready and ensure it is current, as examiners will cross-reference it against actual infrastructure during testing
Be prepared to demonstrate how BIA findings directly drove RTO/RPO targets (clause 10.33) and continuity plan priorities (clause 10.30)
Prepare a summary showing how BIA findings changed from the previous year and what actions were taken in response to any changes

10.35.title: Service Interruption Response
10.35.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a crisis communication plan exists with defined stakeholder notification procedures (including BNM notification timelines), designated spokespersons, media management strategies, and evidence that the plan has been tested as part of continuity exercises.

evidenceItems.name: Crisis communication plan document
evidenceItems.description: Formal crisis communication plan defining communication protocols, stakeholder notification sequences, and media management strategies during technology incidents
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive plan covering internal communication (IT teams, management, board), regulatory notification (BNM incident reporting), customer communication, and media management
Clear activation criteria defining when the crisis communication plan is triggered and who has activation authority
Notification sequences with defined timelines for each stakeholder group, aligned with BNM incident reporting requirements
Pre-drafted communication templates for different incident types (service outage, data breach, cyber attack, extended disruption)
Designated spokespersons with media training records and authority to communicate on behalf of the institution
commonGaps: Communication plan addresses internal stakeholders only; no procedures for customer, regulatory, or media communication
No defined BNM notification timeline; regulatory reporting requirements not referenced in the plan
Plan does not include pre-drafted templates; all communications must be composed from scratch during a crisis
No designated spokesperson or media management strategy; communication handled ad hoc by whoever is available
suggestedSources: Corporate communications or public relations team
BCM programme documentation
CISO office incident response communication procedures

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder notification procedures and contact lists
evidenceItems.description: Detailed notification procedures for each stakeholder group with maintained contact lists and out-of-band communication channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification matrix showing each stakeholder group with notification trigger criteria, responsible notifier, notification channel, and target timeline
Maintained contact lists with primary and alternate contact details for all stakeholders, validated within the past quarter
BNM contact details and incident reporting procedures specifically documented with reference to applicable regulatory circulars
Out-of-band contact mechanisms (mobile numbers, personal email, satellite phone) for use when primary systems are unavailable
Automated mass notification system configuration with delivery confirmation tracking
commonGaps: Contact lists outdated; personnel changes not reflected in crisis contact information
No out-of-band communication channels; notification relies on systems that may be affected by the incident
BNM notification procedures vague or not referencing specific regulatory reporting requirements and timelines
Mass notification capability not available; reliance on manual phone calls for large stakeholder groups
suggestedSources: Crisis management team contact lists and notification systems
Regulatory compliance team BNM reporting procedures
IT operations on-call and escalation contact lists

evidenceItems.name: Crisis communication testing records
evidenceItems.description: Records of crisis communication plan testing conducted as part of continuity exercises, including test objectives, results, and lessons learned
whatGoodLooksLike: Test records from at least one communication exercise in the past 12 months, conducted as part of a broader continuity or DR exercise
Test objectives covering notification speed, stakeholder reachability, message clarity, and escalation effectiveness
Measured results showing time from crisis declaration to completion of first notification wave
Lessons learned documented with specific improvement actions and assigned owners
Evidence that previous test findings have been remediated (e.g. updated contact lists, improved templates)
commonGaps: Crisis communication not tested; continuity exercises focus only on technical recovery without exercising communication
Communication tested informally but no structured test with defined objectives and measured results
Test identified issues (e.g. outdated contacts, slow notification) but no follow-up remediation
Only internal notification tested; external stakeholder communication (customers, regulators, media) not exercised
suggestedSources: BCM exercise reports including communication component
DR test reports with communication testing sections
Crisis management team exercise documentation

evidenceItems.name: Media management guidelines
evidenceItems.description: Guidelines for managing media enquiries and public communications during technology incidents and disruptions
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear guidelines on who is authorised to speak to media (designated spokesperson only) and how media enquiries should be routed
Holding statement templates for common incident scenarios that can be issued quickly while detailed communications are prepared
Social media monitoring and response procedures for managing public perception during incidents
Coordination procedures between IT, corporate communications, and legal teams for media response approval
Guidelines on what information can and cannot be disclosed during an ongoing incident
commonGaps: No media management guidelines; media response handled reactively without predefined procedures
No designated spokesperson; multiple individuals may communicate inconsistent messages to media
Social media not addressed; customer complaints on social media during outages go unmanaged
No legal review process for public statements, risking disclosure of investigation-sensitive information
suggestedSources: Corporate communications team media policy
Legal department communication guidelines
Public relations team crisis communication playbooks

auditTips: Ensure the crisis communication plan explicitly references BNM incident reporting requirements with specific timelines and contact procedures
Have evidence ready showing that stakeholder contact lists have been validated within the past quarter with any updates applied
Prepare test records demonstrating that communication was exercised as part of a continuity exercise in the past 12 months
Ensure designated spokespersons can be identified by name with evidence of media training or briefing
Be prepared to demonstrate out-of-band communication capability that does not depend on primary IT infrastructure

10.36.title: Enterprise Network Design
10.36.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the network architecture is documented with comprehensive diagrams showing all zones, data flows, and interconnections, is designed with defence-in-depth principles, and has been reviewed within the past 12 months or upon significant changes.

evidenceItems.name: Network architecture documentation and diagrams
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive documentation of the network architecture including logical and physical topology diagrams with all zones, segments, and external interconnections
whatGoodLooksLike: Logical network topology diagram showing all security zones (DMZ, internal, restricted, management, guest), inter-zone connections, and external connections
Physical network topology diagram showing device placement, redundancy paths, and geographic distribution
Complete coverage of all network infrastructure including on-premises, cloud environments, and hybrid connections
Version-controlled documentation with review date within the past 12 months
Diagrams at appropriate detail levels: high-level architecture overview plus detailed zone-level diagrams for each security zone
commonGaps: Network diagrams outdated and do not reflect current infrastructure (missing cloud connections, recent expansions, or decommissioned segments)
Only high-level diagrams available without zone-level detail showing individual connections and security controls
Cloud and hybrid network components not included in architecture documentation
Diagrams maintained informally by individual network engineers without version control or formal review process
suggestedSources: Network engineering team documentation (Visio, Lucidchart, draw.io diagrams)
Network configuration management tools (SolarWinds NCM, RANCID)
Enterprise architecture documentation repository

evidenceItems.name: Network zone definitions and data flow diagrams
evidenceItems.description: Documentation defining each network security zone with its purpose, trust level, and permitted data flows between zones
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal zone definition document listing each security zone with description, trust level, classification of hosted systems, and security controls at each boundary
Data flow diagrams showing permitted traffic flows between zones with protocol, port, and direction specifications
External interconnection inventory showing all connections to external networks (internet, SWIFT, PayNet, partner banks, cloud providers) with security controls at each boundary
Clear rationale for each permitted inter-zone flow with business justification
Regular review of data flows to identify and remove any that are no longer required
commonGaps: Zone definitions informal or undocumented; zone boundaries exist in firewall rules but are not formally defined
Data flow documentation incomplete; only major flows documented while smaller or legacy flows are undocumented
External interconnections not comprehensively inventoried; shadow connections unknown to the network security team
No regular review of permitted data flows; stale flows remain active long after the business need has ended
suggestedSources: Network security team documentation
Firewall rule analysis tool reports (Tufin, AlgoSec)
Network flow analysis tools (NetFlow, sFlow analysers)

evidenceItems.name: Annual architecture review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of the annual network architecture review including participants, findings, recommendations, and resulting actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Review report dated within the past 12 months conducted by qualified network security professionals
Assessment of architecture alignment with current best practices and threat landscape
Review of defence-in-depth control adequacy at each network layer and zone boundary
Findings with risk ratings and specific remediation recommendations
Evidence that previous review findings have been addressed or have documented risk acceptance
commonGaps: No formal architecture review conducted in the past 12 months
Review conducted internally only without independent or external perspective
Review focused on availability and performance without assessing security architecture adequacy
Review findings not tracked to remediation; recommendations documented but not acted upon
suggestedSources: Network security team review documentation
External network security assessment reports
IT Risk Committee meeting minutes discussing architecture review findings

evidenceItems.name: Defence-in-depth design principles documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation articulating the defence-in-depth design principles applied to the network architecture and how they are implemented at each layer
whatGoodLooksLike: Design principles document specifying the defence-in-depth strategy: multiple security layers, redundant controls, and fail-secure design

Mapping showing which security controls operate at each layer: perimeter (firewalls, DDoS protection), network (IDS/IPS, NAC), host (endpoint protection, HIDS), application (WAF, API gateway), and data (encryption, DLP)
Evidence that no single control failure compromises the security of critical systems
Control overlap analysis showing which controls provide compensating protection if another control fails
Regular assessment of control effectiveness at each layer
commonGaps: No documented defence-in-depth principles; security controls deployed tactically without a cohesive strategic framework
Defence-in-depth stated as a principle but not demonstrably implemented; single points of security failure exist
Security controls concentrated at the perimeter with insufficient internal monitoring and segmentation
No assessment of what happens when individual security controls fail; no compensating control analysis
suggestedSources: Security architecture documentation
CISO office strategic security design documents
Network security standards and guidelines

auditTips: Ensure network architecture diagrams are current (reviewed within the past 12 months) and include all network zones, data flows, and external interconnections including cloud
Prepare a zone definition summary showing each zone's purpose, trust level, and boundary controls for quick reference during examination
Have the annual architecture review report ready with evidence of findings being tracked to remediation
Be prepared to explain the defence-in-depth strategy and demonstrate multiple layers of security controls at each boundary
Ensure diagrams accurately reflect the actual network configuration; examiners may cross-reference diagrams against firewall rules or scanning results

10.37.title: Network Service Reliability
10.37.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that network segmentation isolates critical systems and sensitive data environments, that segmentation controls restrict lateral movement between zones, and that segmentation effectiveness has been validated through penetration testing.

evidenceItems.name: Network segmentation design documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the network segmentation design including zone definitions, trust levels, and the rationale for segmentation boundaries
whatGoodLooksLike: Segmentation design document showing all network zones with their trust levels and hosted system classifications
Rationale for each segmentation boundary explaining what is being protected and from what threats
Zone diagram showing critical system zones (core banking, payment processing), sensitive data zones (customer databases, card data), and lower-trust zones (development, guest, internet-facing)
Micro-segmentation design for the most sensitive environments where applicable
Formal approval of the segmentation design by the CISO with review date within the past 12 months
commonGaps: Network segmentation exists in firewall rules but is not formally documented as a design with rationale and trust levels
Segmentation design does not separate development/test environments from production networks
Critical systems and non-critical systems share the same network segment due to legacy architecture
PCI cardholder data environment not segmented from the general corporate network
suggestedSources: Network security architecture documentation
CISO office security design documents
Network engineering team segmentation design records

evidenceItems.name: VLAN and firewall rule configurations
evidenceItems.description: Technical configurations implementing network segmentation including VLAN assignments, firewall rules between zones, and access control lists
whatGoodLooksLike: VLAN inventory showing each VLAN with its zone assignment, purpose, IP addressing, and hosted systems
Firewall rule extracts showing inter-zone traffic restrictions with specific source/destination and port definitions (no 'any' rules between zones)
Access control lists on network devices restricting traffic flow between segments
Evidence that default-deny is in place between zones with only explicitly permitted flows allowed
Configuration compliance check results showing segmentation rules match the approved design
commonGaps: Overly permissive firewall rules between zones allowing broad traffic flows that undermine segmentation purpose
VLANs defined but no firewall or ACL enforcement between them, providing only logical separation without security controls
Management VLAN not isolated from user and server VLANs, creating a path for lateral movement to network management
Inter-zone rules accumulated over time without regular review, with many stale or unjustified entries
suggestedSources: Network switch VLAN configuration exports
Firewall management platform (Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon)
Network device configuration backups

evidenceItems.name: Penetration test reports validating segmentation
evidenceItems.description: Penetration test reports with specific scope items testing segmentation effectiveness and documenting any successful lateral movement between zones
whatGoodLooksLike: Penetration test report with network segmentation validation explicitly included in the scope of work
Testing methodology describing how segmentation was tested (attempted lateral movement from each zone to adjacent zones)
Findings showing whether testers could breach segmentation boundaries with specific technical details
No successful lateral movement across zone boundaries, or all successful crossings documented with risk rating and remediation recommendations
Remediation evidence for any segmentation bypass findings from previous tests
commonGaps: Penetration testing conducted but segmentation validation not included in the scope
Segmentation testing limited to internet-to-internal; internal zone-to-zone segmentation not validated
Successful segmentation bypass findings from previous tests not remediated before the next test cycle
Penetration test focused on application vulnerabilities only without testing network-layer segmentation controls
suggestedSources: External penetration testing firm reports
Internal red team assessment reports
IT risk register entries for segmentation-related findings

evidenceItems.name: Segmentation policy and standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy and standards governing network segmentation including zone classification criteria, inter-zone traffic rules, and change management requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defining segmentation requirements including mandatory isolation for critical systems, sensitive data, and different trust environments
Standards specifying how segmentation is implemented technically (VLAN, firewall, micro-segmentation) with minimum requirements for each zone type
Inter-zone connection request process requiring documented business justification, security review, and CISO approval
Policy on temporary inter-zone connections requiring defined expiry dates and automatic removal
Regular review cycle (at least annual) with evidence of policy currency
commonGaps: No formal segmentation policy; segmentation implemented based on individual engineer decisions without governance
Policy exists but no formal process for approving new inter-zone connections, leading to ad hoc rule additions
Temporary connections approved but never removed after the business need expires
Policy not reviewed or updated to address new segmentation technologies (micro-segmentation, zero trust networking)
suggestedSources: CISO office policy library
Network security standards documentation
IT governance documentation repository

auditTips: Ensure the most recent penetration test report explicitly includes segmentation validation in its scope and documents the testing methodology used
Prepare a segmentation summary diagram showing all zones, their trust levels, and the security controls at each boundary for quick examiner reference
Have VLAN and firewall configurations ready to demonstrate that default-deny is in place between zones with only justified, documented traffic flows permitted
Be prepared to demonstrate that critical systems (core banking, payments) and sensitive data (customer databases, card data) are in isolated segments with validated boundary controls
Ensure any segmentation bypass findings from previous penetration tests have been remediated with evidence of verification

10.38.title: Network Fault Prevention Measures
10.38.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that firewalls are deployed at all network boundaries and between security zones, that rules follow the principle of least privilege with no 'any-any' rules, that semi-annual rule reviews are conducted and documented, and that overly permissive or unused rules are actively identified and remediated.

evidenceItems.name: Firewall management policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy governing firewall deployment, rule management, review cycles, and change management processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive policy covering firewall deployment requirements (at all boundaries and between zones), rule design principles (default-deny, least privilege), and rule lifecycle management
Minimum semi-annual rule review requirement clearly stated with defined review process and documentation requirements
Change management procedures requiring business justification, security review, and approval before any rule modification
Temporary rule management requiring defined expiry dates and automatic alerting when temporary rules expire
Board or senior management approval with version control and annual review date
commonGaps: No formal firewall management policy; firewall rules managed according to informal practices that vary between teams
Policy does not mandate minimum review frequency; reviews conducted ad hoc or not at all
No temporary rule management process; 'temporary' rules become permanent without review
Policy not reviewed or updated within the past 12 months
suggestedSources: CISO office policy library
Network security team governance documentation
IT governance documentation repository

evidenceItems.name: Firewall rule review records (semi-annual)
evidenceItems.description: Records of semi-annual firewall rule reviews including rules assessed, findings, and remediation actions taken
whatGoodLooksLike: At least two review records in the past 12 months demonstrating semi-annual cadence
Review scope covering all firewalls in the estate (perimeter, internal, cloud security groups)
Review findings documenting unused rules, overly permissive rules (source/destination/port 'any'), shadowed rules, and rules without documented business justification
Remediation actions for each finding with assigned owner, target date, and completion evidence
Rule base metrics showing total rules, rules reviewed, findings by category, and trend comparison with previous review
commonGaps: Rule reviews not conducted semi-annually; last review more than 6 months ago
Review conducted on perimeter firewalls only; internal firewalls and cloud security groups not reviewed
Reviews identify overly permissive rules but do not track remediation to completion
No metrics or trend analysis; each review is standalone without comparison to previous reviews
suggestedSources: Firewall rule analysis tool reports (Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon)
Network security team review documentation
IT Risk Committee reporting packs showing review findings

evidenceItems.name: Firewall rule base audit reports
evidenceItems.description: Technical audit reports analysing the firewall rule base for compliance with policy, identifying rule base quality issues and security risks
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated rule base analysis report from firewall management tools identifying rule base issues by category
Identification of specific high-risk rules: rules allowing 'any' source or destination, rules allowing 'any' port, and rules with no hit count (unused)
Compliance scoring showing percentage of rules meeting policy requirements (specific source/destination, specific ports, documented justification)
Shadow rule analysis identifying rules that are never matched due to being overridden by higher-priority rules
Trend analysis showing rule base quality improving over successive review cycles
commonGaps: No automated rule base analysis; manual reviews of large rule bases are incomplete and error-prone
Audit reports identify issues but no risk rating or prioritisation of findings
Rule hit count analysis not performed; unused rules remain active indefinitely
Cloud security group rules not included in the audit scope
suggestedSources: Firewall management platform reports (Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon)
Firewall vendor native rule analysis tools
External firewall audit reports from security consultants

evidenceItems.name: Remediation records for overly permissive rules
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the identification, analysis, and remediation of overly permissive firewall rules
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation tracker listing each overly permissive rule identified with original rule details, risk assessment, remediation plan, and current status
Evidence of rules being tightened from broad 'any' rules to specific source/destination/port combinations based on actual traffic analysis
Traffic analysis evidence (NetFlow, firewall logs) used to determine the minimum required access before tightening rules
Completion evidence showing before and after rule configurations for remediated rules
Metrics showing decreasing number of overly permissive rules over successive review cycles
commonGaps: Overly permissive rules identified in reviews but not remediated due to fear of causing service disruption
Remediation deferred indefinitely; same rules flagged in multiple review cycles without action
Rules tightened without traffic analysis, risking service disruption from overly aggressive rule changes
No tracking of remediation progress; findings from reviews not centrally managed
suggestedSources: Firewall change management records
Network security team remediation tracking
Firewall management platform rule change history

auditTips: Prepare evidence of at least two semi-annual rule reviews completed in the past 12 months with documented findings and remediation actions
Have the firewall rule base audit report ready showing the current state of rule base hygiene including percentage of rules with 'any' entries
Demonstrate the firewall change management process by providing examples of recent rule changes with business justification, security review, and approval evidence
Prepare metrics showing the trend of overly permissive rules being reduced over successive review cycles as evidence of active rule base management
Ensure all firewalls are accounted for in the review scope including perimeter firewalls, internal segmentation firewalls, and cloud security groups

10.39.title: Network Bandwidth Monitoring
10.39.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that IDS/IPS is deployed at all critical network points with current signatures, and that alerts are actively monitored and investigated rather than merely collected. They will look for evidence of timely triage and documented investigation outcomes.

evidenceItems.name: IDS/IPS deployment architecture
evidenceItems.description: Network architecture diagram showing IDS/IPS sensor placement at all critical network points including perimeter, DMZ, and internal segment boundaries
whatGoodLooksLike: Network diagram clearly marking IDS/IPS sensor locations at every critical network point
Sensor placement covers internet gateways, DMZ, core banking segments, and inter-VLAN boundaries
Document is version-controlled with last review date within 12 months
Coverage mapping shows alignment between critical asset register and sensor deployment
commonGaps: IDS/IPS deployed only at the perimeter with no coverage of internal east-west traffic
Architecture diagram outdated and does not reflect current network topology
No coverage of cloud or hybrid network segments
Sensor placement not aligned with critical asset locations
suggestedSources: Network architecture team (Visio/draw.io diagrams)
IDS/IPS management console (Snort, Suricata, Palo Alto, Fortinet)
IT asset register for critical network points

evidenceItems.name: Signature and rule update records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating regular and timely updates to IDS/IPS signatures and detection rules
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated signature update logs showing updates applied within 24 hours of vendor release
Change records for custom rules with documented justification
Validation testing records confirming updates do not disrupt legitimate traffic
No gaps exceeding 48 hours between signature releases and deployment
commonGaps: Signature updates are manual and applied inconsistently across sensors
No validation testing after signature updates leading to service disruptions
Subscription lapsed resulting in outdated signatures for extended periods
Custom rules not documented or reviewed
suggestedSources: IDS/IPS management console update logs
Vendor subscription portal (Cisco Talos, Emerging Threats, Fortiguard)
Change management system records

evidenceItems.name: Alert monitoring and investigation records
evidenceItems.description: Records of IDS/IPS alert monitoring, triage decisions, and investigation outcomes demonstrating timely response
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM dashboard showing real-time IDS/IPS alert monitoring with defined triage SLAs
Investigation records with timestamps showing response within defined SLA windows
Escalation records for high-severity alerts with management notification evidence
Monthly alert volume and investigation metrics reports
commonGaps: Alerts generated but no evidence of triage or investigation
High volume of uninvestigated alerts indicating alert fatigue
No defined SLAs for alert investigation and response
Investigation records lack detail on findings and remediation actions
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel)
SOC ticketing system (ServiceNow, Jira)
Incident response records

evidenceItems.name: IDS/IPS policy and configuration standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy document defining IDS/IPS deployment standards, operational procedures, and configuration baselines
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy document with clear ownership, version control, and annual review sign-off
Defined deployment standards specifying sensor placement requirements
Configuration baselines for IDS/IPS sensors with hardening standards
Alert triage SLAs and escalation procedures clearly documented
commonGaps: Policy exists but has not been reviewed or updated for over 12 months
No configuration baseline standards leading to inconsistent sensor configurations
Policy does not define alert investigation SLAs or escalation procedures
No linkage between policy requirements and actual IDS/IPS configuration
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
CISO office for policy sign-off records
IDS/IPS configuration management database

auditTips: Prepare a mapping document showing each critical network point and its corresponding IDS/IPS sensor with deployment evidence
Extract signature update logs for the past 12 months demonstrating consistent and timely updates across all sensors
Compile a sample of alert investigation records showing end-to-end triage from detection to resolution
Ensure the IDS/IPS policy has a current review date and is signed off by the CISO
Prepare metrics on alert volume, investigation rates, and mean time to investigate for the review period

10.40.title: Network Security for Critical Systems
