Just-in-time access not implemented; standing privileged access granted permanently
suggestedSources: PAM platform (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea, HashiCorp Vault)
PAM system administration console and configuration
PAM deployment project documentation

evidenceItems.name: Log retention policy and configuration
evidenceItems.description: Policy and technical configuration evidence demonstrating that privileged activity logs are retained for a minimum of three years in tamper-proof storage
whatGoodLooksLike: Log retention policy specifying minimum three-year retention for privileged activity logs
SIEM or log management configuration showing retention settings meeting policy requirements
Tamper-proof log storage configured (WORM storage, immutable buckets, or equivalent)
Log integrity verification mechanisms in place
commonGaps: Log retention period set to less than three years due to storage constraints
Privileged activity logs not forwarded to centralised log management
Logs stored in mutable storage accessible to administrators being monitored
No log integrity verification or tamper detection mechanisms
suggestedSources: SIEM platform retention configuration (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh)
Log storage infrastructure configuration
Information security log retention policy

evidenceItems.name: Shared account exception register
evidenceItems.description: Register of all approved shared privileged accounts with documented business justification, approving authority, and compensating controls
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete register of all shared privileged accounts across all systems
Each entry includes business justification, risk assessment, and approving authority
Compensating controls documented for each shared account (e.g., session recording, dual control)
Register reviewed at least quarterly with evidence of reduction in shared accounts over time
commonGaps: No register maintained; shared accounts exist without formal documentation
Shared accounts documented but without compensating controls specified
No regular review of shared account justifications
Shared account usage growing rather than being reduced over time
suggestedSources: PAM system shared account inventory
IAM team exception register
IT risk register for shared account risk entries

evidenceItems.name: Periodic access review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of quarterly privileged access reviews demonstrating that all privileged access rights are reviewed and inappropriate access is revoked
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly review records covering all privileged accounts across all critical systems
Each privileged access right reviewed and re-certified by the system or data owner
Inappropriate or excessive access identified and revoked with evidence of revocation
Review completion tracked with all four quarterly reviews completed in the past 12 months
commonGaps: Privileged access reviews not conducted quarterly as required
Reviews limited to a subset of systems rather than comprehensive coverage
Review findings not acted upon with revocation evidence
No sign-off from system or data owners on review outcomes
suggestedSources: IAM platform access review reports (SailPoint, Saviynt)
PAM system access reports
IT governance committee review sign-off records

auditTips: Present PAM system deployment evidence showing coverage across all critical systems with credential vaulting and session recording
Export SIEM or log management retention configuration demonstrating three-year retention for privileged activity logs
Compile the shared account exception register with compensating controls and evidence of quarterly review
Prepare quarterly privileged access review records for the past 12 months showing comprehensive coverage and revocation actions
Demonstrate log tamper-proof mechanisms and show that privileged users cannot modify or delete their own activity logs

10.57.title: Access Monitoring and Logging
10.57.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that privileged access is reviewed at least quarterly and standard user access at least semi-annually, that reviews are conducted by appropriate system or data owners, and that access identified as no longer required is promptly revoked with documented evidence.

evidenceItems.name: Access review schedule and procedure
evidenceItems.description: Formal access review procedure defining review frequency, scope, reviewer responsibilities, certification criteria, and revocation timelines
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure specifies quarterly reviews for privileged access and semi-annual reviews for standard access
Reviewer responsibilities clearly assigned to system or data owners
Certification criteria defined for reviewers to determine whether access should be retained or revoked
Revocation SLAs defined with escalation for overdue revocations
commonGaps: No formal procedure; access reviews conducted ad hoc or only before audits
Review frequency not aligned with clause requirements (quarterly for privileged, semi-annual for standard)
Reviews assigned to IT rather than system or data owners who understand business context
No defined SLAs for revoking decertified access
suggestedSources: IAM team procedure documentation
Information security standards and procedures
IT governance framework documentation

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly privileged access review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of quarterly access reviews for all privileged accounts showing reviewer decisions, certification outcomes, and revocation actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Four quarterly reviews completed in the past 12 months covering all privileged accounts
Each privileged account reviewed by the relevant system or data owner
Certification decisions documented for each account with retain or revoke outcome
Revocation actions completed within defined SLAs with evidence of access removal
commonGaps: Fewer than four quarterly reviews completed in the past 12 months
Reviews do not cover all systems with privileged accounts
Certification decisions are rubber-stamped without meaningful assessment
Revocation decisions made but access not actually removed in the system
suggestedSources: IAM platform access review reports (SailPoint, Saviynt, Microsoft Entra ID Governance)
PAM system access review outputs
IT governance committee sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Semi-annual standard access review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of semi-annual access reviews for all standard user accounts showing reviewer decisions, certification outcomes, and revocation actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Two semi-annual reviews completed in the past 12 months covering all standard user accounts
Reviews conducted by system or data owners with documented certification decisions
Scope covers all critical systems, applications, and information assets
Review completion rate above 95% with escalation for non-respondent reviewers
commonGaps: Semi-annual reviews not completed or completed less frequently than required
Reviews limited to a subset of systems rather than comprehensive coverage
Low review completion rates with many reviewers not responding
Review findings not tracked to revocation with completion evidence
suggestedSources: IAM platform access review reports
Application-level access review records
HR system cross-reference for role and department validation

evidenceItems.name: Access revocation records from reviews
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of access revocation resulting from review findings, showing that decertified access is promptly removed
whatGoodLooksLike: Revocation records for each access right decertified during reviews with timestamps
Time between decertification decision and actual access removal within defined SLAs
Evidence of access removal verified in the target system (AD, application, database)
Summary metrics showing total revocations per review cycle and average revocation time
commonGaps: No evidence that decertified access was actually revoked in the target system
Significant delays between review decisions and access removal
Revocation records incomplete or not linked to the originating review
Some decertified access never revoked due to process breakdowns
suggestedSources: IAM platform revocation workflow records
Active Directory account and group membership change logs
IT service management platform access revocation tickets

auditTips: Prepare a matrix showing all quarterly privileged access reviews for the past 12 months with completion dates and coverage metrics
Compile semi-annual standard access review records demonstrating comprehensive system coverage
Extract revocation evidence showing prompt access removal for all decertified access rights
Present review completion rate metrics and escalation records for non-respondent reviewers
Ensure access review records show that reviews were conducted by system or data owners, not just IT administrators

11.1.title: Enterprise-Wide Cyber Risk Management
11.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a comprehensive IRP exists covering all five phases, has been tested within the past 12 months, and has been updated following any major incidents. They will look for evidence of post-incident reviews and that lessons learned have been incorporated.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan document (version + date)
evidenceItems.description: Incident Response Plan document (version + date)
whatGoodLooksLike: IRP covers all five phases: identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activities
Clear RACI matrix with named roles and escalation paths including BNM notification within 4 hours
Version-controlled with review date within the past 12 months and CISO sign-off
Includes specific playbooks for common scenarios such as ransomware, data breach, DDoS, and insider threat
commonGaps: IRP exists but lacks one or more phases, typically eradication or post-incident review
No version control or review date, making it unclear whether the plan is current
Generic plan not tailored to the institution's technology environment or Malaysian regulatory context
BNM notification timeline not explicitly stated or exceeds the 4-hour requirement
suggestedSources: Information Security department (CISO office)
Document management system (SharePoint, Confluence)
Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC) platform

evidenceItems.name: IRP testing records (tabletop or simulation)
evidenceItems.description: IRP testing records (tabletop or simulation)
whatGoodLooksLike: At least one tabletop exercise and one technical simulation conducted within the past 12 months
Participation from cross-functional teams including IT, legal, communications, and senior management
Documented scenarios, participant observations, identified gaps, and remediation actions with deadlines
Evidence that findings from previous tests were addressed before the next test cycle
commonGaps: Testing not conducted within the past 12 months or only performed as a checkbox exercise
Only IT staff participated without involvement from business, legal, or communications teams
No documented outcomes or action items from the exercise
Same scenario repeated annually without adapting to current threat landscape
suggestedSources: SOC or Incident Response team records
Training and awareness department
External simulation service provider reports

evidenceItems.name: Post-incident review reports
evidenceItems.description: Post-incident review reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Completed within 14 days of incident closure with detailed root cause analysis
Includes full incident timeline from detection to resolution with timestamps
Documents lessons learned, specific improvement actions, assigned owners, and completion deadlines
Evidence that improvement actions have been tracked to completion and incorporated into the IRP
commonGaps: Post-incident reviews not conducted or completed months after the incident
Reports lack root cause analysis and focus only on symptoms rather than systemic issues
Improvement actions identified but not tracked or implemented
Reviews conducted only for major incidents while recurring minor incidents go unreviewed
suggestedSources: Incident management system (ServiceNow, Jira)
SOC incident log and case management records
CISO office post-incident review register

evidenceItems.name: Annual review sign-off
evidenceItems.description: Annual review sign-off
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal sign-off from the CISO and relevant senior management within the past 12 months
Review covers changes to the threat landscape, organisational structure, technology environment, and regulatory requirements
Documented comparison between current IRP and previous version highlighting what changed and why
Sign-off includes confirmation that the IRP aligns with current BNM RMiT requirements
commonGaps: No formal sign-off or approval trail for the annual review
Review conducted but changes not reflected in the IRP document itself
Sign-off by junior staff without appropriate authority
Review does not consider lessons from incidents or test exercises conducted during the year
suggestedSources: CISO office governance records
Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee minutes
Document management system version history

auditTips: Request the IRP document and verify it covers all five phases with specific procedures for each, not just high-level descriptions
Cross-reference the IRP review date against the date of the last major incident to confirm the plan was updated post-incident
Ask for the participant list from the most recent tabletop exercise and verify cross-functional representation including senior management
Review two or three post-incident reports and trace improvement actions through to completion in subsequent IRP updates
Verify that the BNM 4-hour notification requirement is explicitly documented in the IRP with a named responsible officer

11.2.title: Cyber Resilience Framework Development
11.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a board-approved cybersecurity strategy exists, is aligned with the business strategy and risk appetite, includes a multi-year roadmap, and has been reviewed within the past 12 months with documented board sign-off.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity strategy document
evidenceItems.description: Cybersecurity strategy document
whatGoodLooksLike: Clearly articulates the cybersecurity vision, strategic objectives, and a phased multi-year roadmap with milestones
Explicitly references and aligns with the institution's business strategy and stated risk appetite
Covers all security domains including detection, prevention, response, recovery, and emerging technology risks
Includes measurable KPIs and resource requirements with budget allocation
commonGaps: Strategy document is overly generic without institution-specific context or Malaysian banking relevance
No clear linkage between cybersecurity objectives and business strategy priorities
Missing roadmap or roadmap lacks timelines, milestones, and resource commitments
Strategy has not been updated to reflect recent technology changes such as cloud adoption or open banking
suggestedSources: CISO office strategic planning documentation
Board Risk Committee papers and presentations
Enterprise risk management function

evidenceItems.name: Board approval records
evidenceItems.description: Board approval records
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal board resolution or minutes recording approval of the cybersecurity strategy
Approval date within the past 12 months demonstrating annual review cycle
Evidence of board discussion, questions raised, and management responses
Board papers include strategy summary, risk assessment, and investment requirements
commonGaps: No formal board approval record — strategy approved only at management level
Board approval older than 12 months indicating the annual review was missed
Board papers lack sufficient detail for meaningful board oversight
No evidence of board challenge or discussion — appears as rubber-stamp approval
suggestedSources: Board secretariat records and minutes
Board Risk Committee or IT Committee meeting minutes
Corporate governance function

evidenceItems.name: Strategy alignment with business strategy
evidenceItems.description: Strategy alignment with business strategy
whatGoodLooksLike: Mapping document showing each business strategic initiative linked to corresponding cybersecurity requirements
Risk assessment of new digital initiatives with cybersecurity controls identified
Cybersecurity budget allocation proportionate to the scale and complexity of digital transformation programmes
Evidence of joint planning sessions between business and cybersecurity teams
commonGaps: No documented alignment — cybersecurity strategy developed independently of business strategy
Alignment is superficial, referencing business strategy in the introduction but not in specific initiatives
New business initiatives launched without corresponding cybersecurity workstreams
Cybersecurity budget not linked to business risk exposure or growth plans
suggestedSources: CISO office and business strategy team
Enterprise architecture function
Digital transformation programme office

evidenceItems.name: Annual strategy review records
evidenceItems.description: Annual strategy review records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented annual review with assessment of strategy effectiveness against KPIs
Analysis of changes to the threat landscape, regulatory environment, and business priorities since the last review
Identified strategy adjustments with rationale and updated roadmap
Review conducted with input from multiple stakeholders including risk, compliance, and business units
commonGaps: Annual review not conducted or not documented despite the strategy document being reissued
Review focuses only on completed activities without assessing effectiveness or outcomes
No consideration of changes in the threat landscape or new BNM regulatory requirements
Review conducted by the CISO alone without cross-functional input
suggestedSources: CISO office governance and planning records
Cybersecurity steering committee minutes
Board Risk Committee annual review papers

auditTips: Compare the cybersecurity strategy document date against the board approval date to confirm the latest version has been formally approved
Request the business strategy document and verify explicit cross-references between business and cybersecurity strategic objectives
Check whether the strategy roadmap has been updated to reflect emerging risks such as cloud, AI, and open banking security
Review quarterly board reporting packs to verify that strategy progress is being reported with measurable KPIs, not just activity lists
Ask for the cybersecurity maturity assessment results and verify they are being used to inform roadmap priorities

11.3.title: CRF Minimum Elements
11.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a clear cybersecurity governance structure exists with defined roles across the three lines of defence, adequate segregation of duties between security operations, risk management, and audit, and that the CISO has appropriate organisational positioning and board access.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity governance framework document
evidenceItems.description: Cybersecurity governance framework document
whatGoodLooksLike: Defines the cybersecurity governance structure with clear reporting lines and committee structures
Maps responsibilities across the three lines of defence with explicit separation of duties
CISO reporting line ensures independence from technology operations with direct board access
Reviewed and approved within the past 12 months by senior management
commonGaps: Governance framework exists but does not explicitly address the three lines of defence model
CISO reports to the CTO or CIO without independent access to the board, compromising independence
Framework is outdated and does not reflect current organisational structure or team composition
No clear escalation path for cybersecurity matters to reach the board
suggestedSources: CISO office governance documentation
Organisational chart and reporting line documentation
Board Risk Committee charter and terms of reference

evidenceItems.name: Three lines of defence model documentation
evidenceItems.description: Three lines of defence model documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear documentation of first-line responsibilities (SOC, IT security operations, system administrators)
Second-line risk management function with independent cybersecurity risk assessment capability
Third-line internal audit with documented cybersecurity audit plan and qualified auditors
No individual or team holding responsibilities across multiple lines that would create conflicts of interest
commonGaps: Three lines of defence model documented for enterprise risk but not specifically applied to cybersecurity
Second-line cybersecurity risk function does not exist or is part of the first-line security team
Internal audit lacks cybersecurity expertise and relies entirely on external specialists
Segregation exists on paper but is not enforced in practice due to resource constraints
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management framework
Internal audit cybersecurity audit plan
HR records confirming separate reporting lines

evidenceItems.name: Roles and responsibilities matrix
evidenceItems.description: Roles and responsibilities matrix
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI matrix covering all cybersecurity functions with named roles and clear R/A/C/I assignments
Covers key processes including incident response, vulnerability management, risk assessment, policy development, and compliance
No individual assigned both operational and oversight roles for the same process
Matrix is current and reflects actual personnel assignments, not just planned structure
commonGaps: RACI matrix is generic and not tailored to the institution's actual cybersecurity team structure
Same individual or team is both Responsible and Accountable for control execution and oversight
Matrix does not cover all cybersecurity processes, particularly emerging areas like cloud security or threat intelligence
Matrix is outdated with departed staff still listed or new functions not included
suggestedSources: CISO office organisational documentation
GRC platform role assignments
HR department organisational records

evidenceItems.name: Segregation of duties evidence
evidenceItems.description: Segregation of duties evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Access control reports showing separate permissions for security operations, risk, and audit users in security platforms
Organisational chart confirming different reporting lines for each line of defence
Evidence that second-line risk function independently challenges first-line security assessments
Internal audit reports demonstrating independent testing of cybersecurity controls
commonGaps: Segregation exists in documentation but system access permissions do not enforce it
Risk management staff also perform first-line security operations due to resource constraints
Internal audit has not conducted an independent cybersecurity audit in the past 12 months
No evidence of second-line challenge or independent risk assessment of cybersecurity controls
suggestedSources: Identity and access management system reports
Internal audit cybersecurity audit reports
Risk management independent assessment records

auditTips: Review the CISO's reporting line on the organisational chart and verify they have direct access to the board or Board Risk Committee without being filtered through the CIO/CTO
Request the RACI matrix and cross-reference against actual system access permissions to verify segregation is enforced technically, not just documented
Ask for the most recent internal audit report on cybersecurity and verify findings were reported to the Audit Committee with management responses
Interview second-line risk management staff to confirm they independently assess cybersecurity controls rather than simply relying on first-line self-assessments
Check whether the governance structure has been reviewed and updated following any organisational changes in the past 12 months

11.4.title: NCII Compliance Requirements
11.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on the incident classification framework to verify materiality criteria are clearly defined, the four-hour BNM notification procedure is documented and tested, and that the institution can demonstrate actual compliance with reporting timelines for any material incidents that occurred.

evidenceItems.name: Incident classification framework
evidenceItems.description: Incident classification framework
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear severity levels (e.g., P1-P4) with specific criteria for each level covering customer impact, financial exposure, data compromise, and service availability
Explicit materiality thresholds that trigger BNM reporting requirements
Classification criteria are specific and measurable, not subjective (e.g., 'more than 1,000 customers affected' rather than 'significant customer impact')
Framework is aligned with BNM's expectations and references applicable regulatory requirements
commonGaps: Classification criteria are vague or subjective, leaving materiality determination to individual judgement
Framework exists for IT incidents generally but does not specifically address cybersecurity incident classification
BNM materiality thresholds not explicitly defined within the framework
Framework has not been updated to address new incident types such as ransomware, supply chain compromise, or cloud-specific incidents
suggestedSources: CISO office incident management documentation
IT service management framework (ServiceNow, BMC)
Regulatory compliance team BNM correspondence files

evidenceItems.name: SIEM or detection tool evidence
evidenceItems.description: SIEM or detection tool evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM deployed with log ingestion from all critical systems and active detection rules for material incident indicators
Detection coverage spans network, endpoint, application, cloud, and identity layers
Alert triage procedures ensure potential material incidents are escalated within 30 minutes of detection
Detection tools are regularly tuned with evidence of rule updates in response to new threats
commonGaps: SIEM deployed but critical log sources are missing from ingestion, creating detection blind spots
Detection rules are not tuned for the institution's specific environment, generating excessive false positives
No automated correlation — analysts must manually investigate individual alerts
Detection coverage does not extend to cloud environments or third-party hosted systems
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, QRadar, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel)
SOC operations team and detection engineering records
EDR console (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender)

evidenceItems.name: BNM reporting procedure
evidenceItems.description: BNM reporting procedure
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented procedure specifying the four-hour reporting timeline starting from detection, not from confirmation
Named designated reporting officer with backup contacts for 24/7 coverage
Pre-approved notification template with BNM-required information fields
Procedure tested through simulation at least annually with documented results
commonGaps: Procedure exists but the four-hour timeline is measured from incident confirmation rather than detection as required by BNM
No designated backup reporting officer for weekends, public holidays, or leave periods
Notification template not pre-approved, requiring drafting during an active incident
Procedure has never been tested through a timed simulation
suggestedSources: Regulatory compliance and affairs team
CISO office incident response documentation
BNM correspondence and reporting records

evidenceItems.name: Sample incident report / notification record
evidenceItems.description: Sample incident report / notification record
whatGoodLooksLike: Completed incident reports with timestamps showing detection-to-notification within four hours for material incidents
Full incident timeline from detection through containment, eradication, and recovery
BNM notification records with submission timestamps and acknowledgement receipts
Follow-up reports submitted to BNM as required with investigation findings and remediation status
commonGaps: Incident records lack precise timestamps making it impossible to verify four-hour compliance
BNM notification was submitted but no acknowledgement or receipt evidence is retained
Material incidents occurred but were not reported to BNM due to classification errors
Follow-up reports to BNM were not submitted or were significantly delayed
suggestedSources: Incident management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
BNM reporting portal or email correspondence records
SOC incident case management records

auditTips: Request the incident register for the past 12 months and identify any incidents classified as material — verify each was reported to BNM within four hours of detection
Test the classification framework by presenting hypothetical scenarios and asking staff to classify them to check for consistency
Verify that the four-hour clock starts from detection, not from confirmation or management approval, as per BNM's requirement
Check whether the BNM reporting procedure has been tested through a timed drill and whether the drill results demonstrated four-hour compliance
Review SIEM log source coverage against the critical systems inventory to identify any detection blind spots

11.5.title: Cybersecurity Control Measures
11.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a cyber crisis management plan exists with defined governance and escalation processes, that executive management roles are clearly assigned, and that at least one crisis simulation has been conducted within the past 12 months with documented outcomes and improvements.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber crisis management plan
evidenceItems.description: Cyber crisis management plan
whatGoodLooksLike: Clearly defines what constitutes a cyber crisis versus a routine incident with specific escalation triggers
Documents the crisis governance structure with decision-making authority assigned to named executive roles
Covers roles for executive management, legal counsel, corporate communications, technology, and business continuity teams
Includes provisions for BNM regulatory notification and coordination with law enforcement
commonGaps: Plan does not clearly differentiate between a cyber crisis and a routine incident, leading to confusion on when to activate
Decision-making authority is vague or requires too many layers of approval, slowing crisis response
Legal and communications roles are mentioned but lack specific procedures or assigned personnel
Plan is a standalone document not integrated with the institution's broader business continuity management framework
suggestedSources: CISO office crisis management documentation
Business continuity management team
Corporate communications department

evidenceItems.name: Crisis governance and escalation procedures
evidenceItems.description: Crisis governance and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Step-by-step escalation procedure with time-bound triggers at each level
Clear decision-making authority matrix specifying who can authorise system isolation, service shutdown, ransom decisions, and public statements
Defined interface between the cyber crisis team and the institution's broader crisis management or business continuity governance
Pre-approved communication templates for BNM, customers, media, and staff
commonGaps: Escalation procedures are too high-level without specific time triggers or action steps
No clear authority for critical decisions such as network isolation or service shutdown during an active attack
Cyber crisis governance operates independently of the institution's overall crisis management framework
Communication templates not pre-approved, requiring legal review during an active crisis
suggestedSources: CISO office and executive management team
Legal and compliance department
Corporate communications crisis management protocols

evidenceItems.name: Annual crisis simulation records
evidenceItems.description: Annual crisis simulation records
whatGoodLooksLike: At least one simulation conducted within the past 12 months with a realistic scenario such as ransomware, data exfiltration, or destructive malware
Executive management participation documented with attendance records
Simulation report includes timeline, decisions made, identified weaknesses, and improvement actions with owners and deadlines
Evidence that improvement actions from the previous simulation have been implemented
commonGaps: No simulation conducted within the past 12 months or simulation was too simplistic to test crisis governance
Executive management did not participate, limiting the exercise to technical teams only
Simulation report does not document identified weaknesses or improvement actions
Improvement actions from previous simulations remain unaddressed
suggestedSources: CISO office simulation planning and records
External simulation facilitator reports
Business continuity management team exercise records

evidenceItems.name: Crisis team roles and contact information
evidenceItems.description: Crisis team roles and contact information
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive roster listing all crisis team members with primary and alternate contacts
24/7 contact details including personal mobile numbers and alternative communication channels
Defined alternates for every key role to ensure coverage during leave or unavailability
Roster reviewed and updated at least quarterly to reflect personnel changes
commonGaps: Contact roster is outdated with departed staff still listed or new team members missing
No alternates defined for key roles, creating single points of failure
Only office contact numbers provided without personal or out-of-hours contact details
Roster does not include external parties such as legal counsel, forensics providers, or regulatory contacts
suggestedSources: CISO office crisis management documentation
HR department for current personnel records
Business continuity management contact registers

auditTips: Request the attendance list from the most recent crisis simulation and verify that executive management (CEO, CFO, or their delegates) participated, not just IT staff
Ask to see the improvement action tracker from the last two simulations and verify that identified gaps have been remediated
Test the crisis team contact roster by checking whether key contacts are still with the institution and whether alternates are defined for all roles
Verify that the crisis management plan addresses ransomware specifically, including decision-making authority for ransom payment considerations
Check whether the institution has pre-engaged external incident response and forensics providers with documented SLAs for crisis mobilisation

11.6.title: Red Team Simulation Attacks
11.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the SOC provides genuine 24/7 monitoring coverage with qualified analysts, documented triage and escalation procedures, and measurable performance metrics demonstrating effective detection and response capabilities.

evidenceItems.name: SOC operational procedures
evidenceItems.description: SOC operational procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive procedures covering alert triage, investigation workflows, escalation criteria, and shift handover protocols
Documented runbooks for common attack scenarios including phishing, malware, DDoS, and unauthorised access
Clear escalation matrix with severity-based timelines and named escalation contacts
Version-controlled with evidence of regular review and updates
commonGaps: Procedures exist but are high-level without specific step-by-step instructions for analysts
Runbooks do not cover the institution's specific technology environment or common alert types
No shift handover procedure, leading to dropped investigations between shifts
Procedures have not been updated to reflect changes in tooling or the threat landscape
suggestedSources: SOC operations team documentation
SOAR platform playbook configurations
ITSM knowledge base (Confluence, SharePoint)

evidenceItems.name: 24/7 staffing schedule and analyst qualifications
evidenceItems.description: 24/7 staffing schedule and analyst qualifications
whatGoodLooksLike: Published shift schedule demonstrating 24/7 coverage with no gaps including weekends and public holidays
Minimum two analysts per shift to prevent single-point-of-failure monitoring
Analyst qualification records showing relevant certifications (GCIA, GCIH, CEH, CySA+)
Annual training plan with evidence of completion for all SOC analysts
commonGaps: 24/7 coverage claimed but staffing schedule shows single-analyst shifts or gaps during Malaysian public holidays
Analyst certifications are expired or only held by senior staff while junior analysts have none
No formal training plan — analysts rely on on-the-job learning without structured skill development
High turnover with vacant positions reducing effective coverage below minimum requirements
suggestedSources: SOC shift management system or roster
HR training and certification records
MSSP service level agreements (if outsourced)

evidenceItems.name: Alert triage and escalation procedures
evidenceItems.description: Alert triage and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Defined severity classification for alerts with corresponding triage time targets (e.g., P1 within 15 minutes, P2 within 1 hour)
Clear escalation criteria specifying when and to whom alerts are escalated based on severity and type
Automated escalation workflows in SOAR or ticketing system to prevent missed escalations
Regular review of escalation accuracy with feedback loop to improve triage quality
commonGaps: Triage time targets exist but are not tracked or enforced, with no visibility into compliance rates
Escalation relies on analyst judgement without defined criteria, leading to inconsistent escalation decisions
No automated escalation — critical alerts can sit unactioned during shift transitions
No feedback mechanism to identify and correct recurring triage errors
suggestedSources: SOAR platform (Phantom, XSOAR, Swimlane)
SIEM alert management console
SOC ticketing and case management system

evidenceItems.name: SOC performance metrics and reports
evidenceItems.description: SOC performance metrics and reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly SOC performance reports tracking MTTD, MTTR, alert volume, escalation rates, and false positive rates
Trend analysis showing improvement over time with explanations for any degradation
Metrics benchmarked against industry standards or SLTs with variance analysis
Reports reviewed by the CISO with documented actions for underperforming metrics
commonGaps: SOC performance metrics not formally tracked or reported to management
Metrics limited to alert volume without measuring detection and response effectiveness
No trend analysis — metrics reported as point-in-time snapshots without historical context
CISO does not review SOC performance reports or take action on underperformance
suggestedSources: SIEM and SOAR dashboards and reports
SOC management reporting to the CISO
MSSP service performance reports (if outsourced)

auditTips: Request the SOC shift schedule for the past three months and verify there are no coverage gaps, particularly during weekends and Malaysian public holidays
Ask for SOC performance metrics for the past 12 months and look for MTTD consistently under 15 minutes and MTTR trends improving over time
Review analyst certification records and verify that at least 70% of SOC staff hold relevant security certifications
Request a sample of five recent high-severity alerts and trace them through the triage, investigation, and escalation process to verify procedure adherence
If the SOC is outsourced to an MSSP, review the SLA and verify performance against contractual metrics

11.7.title: Crowdsourced Security Testing
11.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the SIEM collects logs from all critical systems, detection rules are regularly tuned to address evolving threats, log source completeness is validated, and the SIEM provides effective correlation and alerting capability.

evidenceItems.name: SIEM architecture and deployment documentation
evidenceItems.description: SIEM architecture and deployment documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented architecture covering SIEM platform, log collectors, storage, and high-availability configuration
Data flow diagrams showing log ingestion paths from all critical systems
Defined log retention periods that meet regulatory requirements (minimum 12 months online, 7 years archive)
Capacity planning documentation demonstrating the platform can handle current and projected log volumes
commonGaps: Architecture documentation is outdated and does not reflect the current deployment
No data flow diagrams making it difficult to identify log collection gaps or single points of failure
Log retention policies not defined or not meeting BNM's regulatory retention requirements
No capacity planning — SIEM performance degrades during peak periods causing log drops
suggestedSources: SOC engineering team documentation
SIEM platform administration records (Splunk, QRadar, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel)
Security architecture team

evidenceItems.name: Log source inventory and coverage analysis
evidenceItems.description: Log source inventory and coverage analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory of all log sources feeding into the SIEM mapped to the critical asset register
Coverage analysis showing percentage of critical systems with active SIEM integration
Automated log source health monitoring with alerting for ingestion failures
Quarterly validation records showing cross-reference against critical asset register and network discovery
commonGaps: Log source inventory is incomplete — new systems deployed without SIEM integration
No automated monitoring for log source health, meaning silent failures go undetected for days or weeks
Coverage analysis shows significant gaps in cloud, mobile, or third-party system log collection
No regular validation process — coverage analysis is only performed during audit preparation
suggestedSources: SIEM administration console (log source management)
IT asset management system (CMDB)
SOC operations team log source tracking

evidenceItems.name: SIEM rule tuning records
evidenceItems.description: SIEM rule tuning records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented rule tuning activities performed at least monthly with rationale for changes
Detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques with coverage analysis
Records of new rules created in response to emerging threats or threat intelligence
False positive analysis showing reduction trends and specific tuning actions taken
commonGaps: Detection rules deployed at SIEM installation and never tuned or updated
No mapping to MITRE ATT&CK framework making it impossible to assess detection coverage
Rules tuned to suppress alerts rather than improve detection accuracy, masking genuine threats
No formal change management for rule changes — rules modified ad hoc without documentation
suggestedSources: SIEM rule management and change log
SOC detection engineering team records
Change management system records for SIEM rule changes

evidenceItems.name: SIEM alert and correlation reports
evidenceItems.description: SIEM alert and correlation reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly SIEM performance reports showing alert volumes, true positive rates, and correlation effectiveness
Examples of multi-source correlation detecting threats that individual log sources would miss
Alert quality metrics showing improvement trends through ongoing tuning efforts
Dashboard screenshots demonstrating real-time monitoring and alerting capability
commonGaps: No regular reporting on SIEM alert quality or correlation effectiveness
Alerts based on single log sources without cross-source correlation
Excessive alert volume with high false positive rate indicating poor tuning
Correlation rules are vendor defaults without customisation for the institution's environment
suggestedSources: SIEM platform reporting and dashboard exports
SOC performance reports to the CISO
SOAR platform case management statistics

auditTips: Request the log source inventory and cross-reference it against the critical asset register to identify systems not sending logs to the SIEM
Ask for evidence of SIEM rule tuning in the past three months and verify it includes new rules for recent threats, not just threshold adjustments
Check whether the SIEM has automated log source health monitoring by asking what happens when a critical log source stops sending data
Request MITRE ATT&CK coverage analysis to identify detection gaps for techniques commonly used against financial institutions
Verify that log retention meets BNM requirements with at least 12 months online and archived logs accessible for at least 7 years

11.8.title: Cybersecurity Operations Responsibilities
11.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that EDR is deployed on all endpoints including servers and mobile devices, provides real-time detection and automated response, has daily signature updates, and is centrally managed with complete coverage visibility.

evidenceItems.name: EDR deployment documentation and coverage
evidenceItems.description: EDR deployment documentation and coverage
whatGoodLooksLike: EDR deployment report showing 100% coverage across all endpoint types with zero unprotected endpoints
Coverage mapped against the endpoint asset inventory with reconciliation evidence
Deployment includes servers, workstations, laptops, and mobile devices as required by BNM
Coverage report generated within the past 30 days demonstrating current state
commonGaps: EDR deployed on workstations but not on servers, mobile devices, or virtual desktop infrastructure
Coverage percentage below 100% with no documented exceptions or risk acceptances for gaps
No reconciliation between EDR deployment and the endpoint asset inventory, leaving unknown gaps
Legacy or specialised systems excluded from EDR without compensating controls
suggestedSources: EDR management console (CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)
IT asset management system (CMDB)
Mobile device management platform (Intune, MobileIron)

evidenceItems.name: EDR configuration and policy settings
evidenceItems.description: EDR configuration and policy settings
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration shows real-time behavioural detection enabled alongside traditional signature-based scanning
Automated response actions configured including process isolation, file quarantine, and network containment
Policy settings consistent across all endpoint groups with documented justification for any variations
Anti-tamper protection enabled to prevent users or malware from disabling the EDR agent
commonGaps: EDR configured in detection-only mode without automated response actions enabled
Behavioural detection disabled due to performance concerns, leaving only signature-based protection
Inconsistent policies across endpoint groups with some having weaker protection settings
Excessive exclusions configured, often from legacy implementations, creating detection blind spots
suggestedSources: EDR management console policy configuration exports
SOC endpoint protection configuration documentation
Change management records for EDR policy modifications

evidenceItems.name: Signature update schedule and compliance records
evidenceItems.description: Signature update schedule and compliance records
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated daily signature updates configured with evidence of successful deployment across all endpoints
Compliance dashboard showing 95% or higher of endpoints with signatures updated within the past 24 hours
Monitoring and alerting for endpoints that fail to receive signature updates within the required timeframe
Historical compliance data showing sustained daily update adherence over the past 12 months
commonGaps: Signature updates configured but not monitored for successful deployment to all endpoints
Endpoints frequently offline or disconnected from the update infrastructure, falling behind on signatures
Update frequency less than daily, not meeting BNM's minimum requirement
No alerting or remediation process for endpoints with outdated signatures
suggestedSources: EDR management console update compliance reports
Patch management system update verification records
SOC endpoint health monitoring dashboards

evidenceItems.name: EDR alert and response records
evidenceItems.description: EDR alert and response records
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly summary of EDR detections, automated containment actions, and analyst investigations
Evidence of automated response actions successfully containing threats before manual analyst intervention
Investigation and response records for all high-severity EDR detections with documented outcomes
Trend analysis showing detection effectiveness and most common threat categories encountered
commonGaps: EDR alerts not integrated with SOC workflows, resulting in detections going uninvestigated
Automated response actions disabled, requiring manual analyst action for all detections
No trending or analysis of EDR detection data to identify patterns or emerging threats
Alert fatigue from excessive false positives leading to genuine threats being overlooked
suggestedSources: EDR management console detection and response logs
SOC case management system (SOAR, ServiceNow, Jira)
Monthly SOC performance reports

auditTips: Pull the EDR coverage report during the examination and compare total deployed agents against the endpoint asset inventory to verify 100% coverage
Request evidence of daily signature update compliance for the past 30 days and check whether endpoints consistently receive updates within 24 hours
Verify that automated response actions are enabled by reviewing the EDR policy configuration, not just relying on documentation claims
Ask for the EDR exclusion list and review each exclusion for valid business justification and documented compensating controls
Check whether EDR telemetry is integrated with the SIEM by requesting examples of correlated alerts that combine endpoint and network data

11.9.title: Continuous Security Monitoring and SOC
11.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that comprehensive email security controls are deployed including spam filtering, malware scanning, and phishing detection, and that SPF, DKIM, and DMARC are correctly configured with enforcement on all organisational domains.

evidenceItems.name: Email security architecture documentation
evidenceItems.description: Email security architecture documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented architecture showing multi-layered email security controls including gateway, cloud, and endpoint protections
Covers spam filtering, malware scanning, URL protection, attachment sandboxing, and advanced phishing detection
Architecture includes DLP integration for outbound email content inspection
Includes data flow diagrams showing email routing through security controls
commonGaps: Architecture documentation is outdated and does not reflect the current email security stack
Missing coverage for one or more control layers such as attachment sandboxing or URL rewriting
Email DLP not integrated or configured, leaving data exfiltration via email uncontrolled
No protection for internal email traffic — security controls only applied to external email
suggestedSources: Email security gateway administration (Proofpoint, Mimecast, Microsoft Defender for Office 365)
Security architecture team documentation
IT infrastructure team email system documentation

evidenceItems.name: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration records
evidenceItems.description: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration records
whatGoodLooksLike: SPF records configured for all organisational domains with appropriate sender inclusion and hard fail (-all) mechanism
DKIM signing enabled for all outbound email with regular key rotation (at least annually)
DMARC policy set to reject or quarantine (not 'none') with aggregate (rua) and forensic (ruf) reporting enabled
Complete domain inventory showing authentication configuration status for every domain including parked domains
commonGaps: DMARC policy set to 'none' which provides monitoring only without preventing spoofing
SPF record includes too many mechanisms or uses a soft fail (~all) which does not block spoofed messages
DKIM not enabled or using weak key lengths (below 2048-bit)
Parked or legacy domains not protected with email authentication, allowing them to be spoofed
suggestedSources: DNS management records and zone files
DMARC reporting service (dmarcian, Valimail, Agari)
Email gateway configuration export

evidenceItems.name: Email filtering and scanning configuration
evidenceItems.description: Email filtering and scanning configuration
whatGoodLooksLike: Spam filtering with multi-engine scanning showing high catch rates (above 99%) with low false positive rates
Malware scanning with attachment detonation and sandboxing for suspicious file types
URL rewriting and time-of-click analysis for embedded links in emails
Impersonation protection configured for key executives and high-value targets
commonGaps: Spam filtering relies on a single engine without multi-layered scanning
No attachment sandboxing — only signature-based malware scanning is performed
URL protection limited to blocking known malicious URLs without time-of-click analysis
No impersonation or business email compromise protection configured
suggestedSources: Email security gateway configuration and policy exports
Advanced threat protection service configuration (ATP, SEG sandbox)
Email security vendor service reports

evidenceItems.name: Phishing detection and response records
evidenceItems.description: Phishing detection and response records
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly metrics showing phishing emails detected and blocked at the gateway versus those reported by users
Documented phishing response procedure with evidence of execution for reported phishing attempts
Containment actions documented including mailbox purge, credential resets, and affected user notification
Trend analysis showing phishing attack patterns and effectiveness of security awareness training
commonGaps: No formal user reporting mechanism for suspected phishing emails
Phishing reports not investigated by the SOC or investigated with significant delays
Containment actions such as mailbox purge not performed after confirmed phishing campaigns
No trending or analysis of phishing data to inform security awareness programme improvements
suggestedSources: Email security gateway reporting dashboard
SOC phishing investigation records and case management
Security awareness platform phishing simulation reports

auditTips: Perform a live DMARC record check for all organisational domains during the examination to verify enforcement is set to reject or quarantine
Request the domain inventory and verify SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration for every domain, including parked and inactive domains
Review the email security gateway configuration to verify that attachment sandboxing and URL rewriting are enabled, not just signature-based scanning
Ask for phishing detection metrics for the past 12 months and verify that user-reported phishing attempts are investigated and resolved by the SOC
Check whether email DLP is configured and aligned with the data classification framework, particularly for customer PII and financial data

11.10.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence
11.10.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that DLP controls are deployed across all required channels (email, web, endpoint, cloud), aligned with the data classification framework, and that DLP incidents are investigated with documented outcomes and remediation actions.

evidenceItems.name: DLP policy and configuration documentation
evidenceItems.description: DLP policy and configuration documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented DLP policy specifying protected data types mapped to the institution's data classification scheme
Configuration covering all four BNM-required channels: email, web, endpoint, and cloud
Response actions defined by severity level from monitoring to automatic blocking
Policy reviewed and updated within the past 12 months with CISO approval
commonGaps: DLP policy exists but only covers email, leaving web, endpoint, and cloud channels unmonitored
No mapping between DLP rules and the data classification framework, resulting in inconsistent protection
All DLP policies set to monitor-only mode without blocking, allowing data loss to proceed
Policy has not been updated to address cloud storage services adopted since initial DLP deployment
suggestedSources: DLP management console (Symantec DLP, Microsoft Purview, Forcepoint, Digital Guardian)
CISO office data protection policy documentation
Information security policy framework

evidenceItems.name: Data classification framework alignment evidence
evidenceItems.description: Data classification framework alignment evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Mapping document showing each data classification level linked to corresponding DLP rules and response actions
DLP rules configured to detect data patterns for each classification level including customer PII, financial data, and restricted information
Evidence that DLP policies were updated when the data classification framework was revised
Sample DLP test results demonstrating accurate detection of classified data across all categories
commonGaps: Data classification framework exists but DLP rules are not aligned with the classification levels
DLP rules focus only on regulatory data (e.g., NRIC numbers) without covering all classification categories
No evidence that DLP policies were updated following changes to the data classification framework
DLP rule testing not performed to validate detection accuracy for different data types
suggestedSources: Data governance and classification team
DLP policy management records
Information risk management function

evidenceItems.name: DLP rule configuration across channels
evidenceItems.description: DLP rule configuration across channels
whatGoodLooksLike: DLP rules deployed and active across email, web, endpoint, and cloud channels with evidence from each
Channel-specific configurations appropriate to the data flow patterns of each channel
Consistent policy enforcement across all channels preventing circumvention by switching channels
Regular testing of DLP rules across each channel with documented test results
commonGaps: DLP deployed on email only without coverage of web uploads, USB transfers, or cloud storage
Cloud DLP not integrated with CASB or does not cover unsanctioned cloud applications
Endpoint DLP agent not deployed on all endpoints, creating gaps in removable media control
Inconsistent policies across channels — strict on email but permissive on web and cloud
suggestedSources: DLP management console showing channel-specific policy configurations
CASB platform (Netskope, Zscaler, Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps)
Endpoint DLP agent deployment reports

evidenceItems.name: DLP incident and alert records
evidenceItems.description: DLP incident and alert records
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly DLP incident summary showing alert volumes, investigation outcomes, and confirmed data loss events
Investigation records for each DLP alert with documented triage, analysis, and resolution
Trend analysis identifying repeat offenders, common data types involved, and preferred exfiltration channels
Evidence that confirmed incidents led to corrective actions including policy updates and user training
commonGaps: DLP alerts generated but not investigated by the SOC or data protection team
No documentation of investigation outcomes — alerts are closed without analysis
High false positive rate leading to alert fatigue and genuine incidents being overlooked
No trend analysis or management reporting on DLP incident patterns
suggestedSources: DLP incident management console and reports
SOC case management system for DLP investigations
Monthly CISO report on data protection incidents

auditTips: Request the DLP channel coverage matrix and verify that all four BNM-required channels (email, web, endpoint, cloud) have active DLP policies
Ask for DLP incident records for the past six months and verify that alerts are being investigated, not just logged
Cross-reference DLP rules against the data classification framework to verify alignment for each classification level
Test DLP effectiveness by asking the institution to demonstrate detection of sample sensitive data patterns across different channels
Check whether cloud DLP covers both sanctioned and unsanctioned cloud applications, particularly consumer file-sharing services

11.11.title: Anomalous Activity Response
11.11.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that internet-facing systems are scanned at least monthly and internal systems at least quarterly using industry-recognised tools, that vulnerabilities are risk-rated and prioritised, and that remediation is tracked against defined timeframes.

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability assessment schedule and policy
evidenceItems.description: Vulnerability assessment schedule and policy
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented policy specifying monthly scans for internet-facing systems and quarterly for internal systems
Scanning scope explicitly covers all internet-facing systems including web applications, APIs, and payment gateways
Approved scanning tools listed with evidence of current licensing and vulnerability database subscriptions
Risk-rating methodology defined using CVSS with contextual enrichment for asset criticality
commonGaps: Policy exists but scanning frequency does not meet BNM's minimum requirements (monthly/quarterly)
Scanning scope excludes certain internet-facing systems such as partner portals or development environments
Risk-rating methodology relies solely on raw CVSS scores without considering asset criticality or exploit availability
Policy has not been updated to include cloud infrastructure and containerised workloads in scope
suggestedSources: CISO office vulnerability management documentation
Vulnerability scanner configuration (Qualys, Nessus, Rapid7 InsightVM, Tenable)
IT risk management policy framework

evidenceItems.name: Monthly scan results for internet-facing systems
evidenceItems.description: Monthly scan results for internet-facing systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Scan reports available for every month of the past 12 months with no gaps
Reports show all internet-facing assets in scope with scan completion confirmation
Vulnerabilities risk-rated with severity distribution trending downward over time
Critical and high vulnerabilities highlighted with remediation status and assigned owners
commonGaps: Monthly scans missed for one or more months, creating compliance gaps
Scan reports show incomplete scans with assets that failed to be scanned or timed out
Vulnerability counts increasing over time without evidence of effective remediation
Scan results not reviewed or actioned in a timely manner
suggestedSources: Vulnerability scanner reporting console (Qualys, Nessus, Rapid7)
Vulnerability management team scan records
CISO monthly reporting pack

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly scan results for internal systems
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly scan results for internal systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly scan reports available for the past 12 months covering all internal system categories
Authenticated scanning performed to provide comprehensive vulnerability detection
Internal system scope includes servers, databases, network devices, and internal applications
Results segmented by system type and business unit for targeted remediation ownership
commonGaps: Quarterly scans not performed consistently with missed quarters in the past 12 months
Scans performed unauthenticated only, missing a significant portion of detectable vulnerabilities
Internal scope does not cover all system types, particularly network devices or database servers
Results not segmented or distributed to system owners for remediation
suggestedSources: Vulnerability scanner reporting console
IT infrastructure team for internal system inventory
Vulnerability management team quarterly reports

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability remediation tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Vulnerability remediation tracking records
whatGoodLooksLike: Tracking register showing all identified vulnerabilities with assigned owners, deadlines, and current remediation status
Remediation compliance metrics showing percentage of vulnerabilities remediated within defined SLTs
Evidence that critical vulnerabilities are prioritised and remediated first with expedited timelines
Ageing analysis showing no critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days without documented risk acceptance
commonGaps: Vulnerabilities identified by scans but not tracked in a remediation register
No defined SLTs for remediation, with vulnerabilities remaining open indefinitely
Critical vulnerabilities open for extended periods without formal risk acceptance or escalation
Remediation tracking is manual and inconsistent, with no single source of truth
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform tracking dashboard
IT change management and patching records
Risk acceptance register for deferred remediations

auditTips: Request scan reports for the past 12 months and verify monthly scans for internet-facing systems and quarterly for internal systems with no gaps
Compare the scanning scope against the internet-facing asset inventory to identify any systems excluded from regular scanning
Review the vulnerability remediation tracking register and check whether critical vulnerabilities are being remediated within 7 days as per industry best practice
Verify that authenticated scanning is being used for internal systems by checking the scanner configuration, not just the policy
Ask for the vulnerability trend report and verify that the overall vulnerability count is decreasing over time, indicating effective remediation

11.12.title: Cyber Crisis Management
11.12.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that penetration testing of critical systems is conducted at least annually by qualified independent testers, findings are risk-rated and reported to management, and remediation is tracked with evidence of re-testing for critical findings.

evidenceItems.name: Penetration testing policy and schedule
evidenceItems.description: Penetration testing policy and schedule
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented policy specifying annual testing for all critical systems and pre-release testing for major deployments
Testing scope covers internet-facing systems, core banking, mobile applications, and APIs
Policy defines tester independence and qualification requirements including accepted certifications
Annual testing schedule with defined scope for each test engagement
commonGaps: No documented penetration testing policy — tests conducted ad hoc without consistent scope or methodology
Testing scope limited to internet-facing web applications only, excluding core banking, mobile, and API systems
No requirement for pre-release testing, allowing vulnerabilities to be introduced with major deployments
Policy does not define tester independence or qualification requirements
suggestedSources: CISO office vulnerability management documentation
Procurement records for penetration testing engagements
IT risk management policy framework

evidenceItems.name: Penetration test reports from qualified testers
evidenceItems.description: Penetration test reports from qualified testers
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive test reports from the past 12 months covering all critical systems in scope
Reports include detailed findings with evidence of exploitation, risk ratings, and specific remediation recommendations
Testing methodology documented including tools used, techniques applied, and attack scenarios attempted
Executive summary suitable for board and management reporting
commonGaps: No penetration test conducted within the past 12 months
Report is a high-level summary without detailed findings, exploitation evidence, or specific remediation guidance
Testing methodology not documented, making it impossible to assess test thoroughness
Report covers only a subset of critical systems, leaving high-value systems untested
suggestedSources: External penetration testing provider deliverables
CISO office test engagement records
Vulnerability management team report repository

evidenceItems.name: Tester independence and qualification evidence
evidenceItems.description: Tester independence and qualification evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Statement of independence confirming testers are not involved in development or administration of tested systems
Tester CVs or profiles showing relevant certifications (OSCP, CREST CRT, GPEN, GXPN)
Testing firm accreditation evidence (CREST, CHECK, or equivalent)
Conflict of interest declaration for the testing engagement
commonGaps: Penetration testing performed by internal IT staff who also develop or administer the tested systems
No evidence of tester qualifications or certifications provided with the test report
Same testing firm used for consecutive years without rotation or justification
No independence declaration or conflict of interest assessment for the testing engagement
suggestedSources: Penetration testing firm proposal and engagement documents
Tester certification and qualification records
Procurement and vendor management records

evidenceItems.name: Remediation plans and tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Remediation plans and tracking records
whatGoodLooksLike: All penetration test findings tracked in the remediation register with assigned owners, agreed deadlines, and current status
Remediation timeframes aligned with finding severity: critical within 7 days, high within 30 days
Re-testing evidence confirming critical and high findings have been effectively remediated
Management reporting on remediation progress with escalation for overdue items
commonGaps: Findings documented in the test report but not entered into the remediation tracking register
No agreed remediation timeframes resulting in findings remaining open indefinitely
Re-testing not conducted to verify that critical findings have been effectively fixed
Remediation ownership not assigned, with findings treated as the security team's responsibility rather than the system owner's
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management tracking system
IT change management records for remediation patches and fixes
Re-test reports from the penetration testing provider

auditTips: Verify that the most recent penetration test was conducted within the past 12 months and covered all critical systems identified in the asset register
Request tester CVs or certification evidence and confirm they hold recognised certifications such as OSCP, CREST CRT, or GPEN
Confirm tester independence by verifying the testing firm is not the same organisation that developed, administers, or manages the tested systems
Review the remediation tracking register for penetration test findings and verify that critical findings have been remediated and re-tested
Check whether penetration testing was conducted prior to any major system releases in the past 12 months as required by the policy

11.13.title: Cyber Incident Response Plan
11.13.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution conducts red team exercises to validate defences against advanced threats, whether exercises simulate realistic attack scenarios with clear rules of engagement, and whether findings have been used to improve detection and response capabilities.

evidenceItems.name: Red team exercise plan and rules of engagement
evidenceItems.description: Red team exercise plan and rules of engagement
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented rules of engagement signed by both the institution and the red team provider before the exercise begins
Clear scope definition including in-scope and out-of-scope systems, network segments, and attack techniques
Safety mechanisms defined including emergency stop procedures and communication channels with exercise coordinators
Exercise objectives aligned with specific security concerns or threat scenarios relevant to the institution
commonGaps: Rules of engagement not documented or signed before the exercise begins
Scope too narrow, excluding critical systems that should be tested or too broad without appropriate safety controls
No emergency stop procedure defined, risking unintended impact to production systems
Exercise objectives are generic rather than tailored to the institution's specific threat landscape
suggestedSources: Red team provider engagement documents
CISO office exercise planning records
Legal department contract and liability documentation

evidenceItems.name: Red team exercise reports
evidenceItems.description: Red team exercise reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Detailed report documenting the attack path from initial access through to objective achievement with timestamps
Findings mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques with risk ratings and specific remediation recommendations
Assessment of the SOC's detection and response performance during the exercise including what was and was not detected
Executive summary with strategic recommendations for improving the institution's overall security posture
commonGaps: Report lacks detail on the attack path and techniques used, reading more like a penetration test than a red team exercise
No assessment of detection and response performance — report focuses only on vulnerabilities exploited
Findings not mapped to MITRE ATT&CK framework making it difficult to assess detection gaps
No executive summary suitable for board-level reporting
suggestedSources: Red team provider deliverables
CISO office exercise documentation
SOC detection logs from the exercise period (for blue team assessment)

evidenceItems.name: Lessons learned and improvement plans
evidenceItems.description: Lessons learned and improvement plans
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured lessons learned document covering detection gaps, response weaknesses, and control failures identified
Prioritised improvement plan with specific actions, assigned owners, deadlines, and progress tracking
Evidence that improvement actions from previous exercises have been completed before the next exercise
New detection rules or playbooks created as a direct result of red team findings
commonGaps: No formal lessons learned documented — findings remain only in the red team report
Improvement plan created but not tracked or implemented before the next exercise cycle
Improvements limited to fixing specific vulnerabilities without addressing systemic detection or response gaps
No evidence of red team findings being operationalised into new SIEM rules or SOC playbooks
suggestedSources: CISO office improvement action tracker
SOC detection engineering change records
Security architecture improvement project records

evidenceItems.name: Red team provider qualifications
evidenceItems.description: Red team provider qualifications
whatGoodLooksLike: Provider company profile demonstrating experience in financial sector red team engagements
Individual tester CVs showing advanced offensive security certifications (OSCP, OSCE, GXPN, CREST)
References or case studies from similar engagements with other financial institutions
Provider holds relevant accreditations such as CREST membership or equivalent
commonGaps: Provider qualifications not verified or documented as part of the engagement process
Testers hold basic certifications only without the advanced skills needed for realistic threat simulation
Provider has no financial sector experience and may not simulate relevant threat scenarios
Same provider used for both penetration testing and red teaming without considering different skill requirements
suggestedSources: Procurement and vendor management records
Red team provider proposals and capability statements
CISO office vendor assessment documentation

auditTips: Verify that the red team exercise simulated realistic adversary behaviour, not just vulnerability exploitation — look for evidence of multi-phase attack simulation
Request the SOC detection assessment from the exercise to understand which attack phases were detected and which were missed
Check whether red team findings have been operationalised into new SIEM detection rules or SOC playbooks since the exercise
Review the improvement plan from the last exercise and verify that critical actions have been completed before any subsequent exercise
Confirm that the red team provider holds relevant certifications and has demonstrated financial sector experience

11.14.title: CERT Member Readiness
11.14.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that defined remediation SLTs exist (critical: 7 days, high: 30 days, medium: 90 days), SLT compliance is tracked and reported, and any exceptions have documented risk acceptance with compensating controls and expiry dates.

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability remediation policy and SLTs
evidenceItems.description: Vulnerability remediation policy and SLTs
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented policy specifying SLTs by severity: critical within 7 days, high within 30 days, medium within 90 days
Clear remediation workflow from vulnerability identification through to closure with defined roles
Exception handling process requiring system owner and CISO sign-off with compensating controls
Escalation procedures for overdue remediations with defined escalation points and timeframes
commonGaps: No documented SLTs or SLTs that exceed BNM's expected timeframes
Remediation workflow does not define clear ownership — vulnerabilities treated as the security team's problem
Exception process exists but does not require compensating controls or define maximum exception duration
No escalation process for overdue remediations, allowing critical vulnerabilities to remain open indefinitely
suggestedSources: CISO office vulnerability management documentation
IT risk management policy framework
Information security policy suite

evidenceItems.name: Remediation tracking dashboard or reports
evidenceItems.description: Remediation tracking dashboard or reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboard or report showing all open vulnerabilities with severity, assigned owner, deadline, and days open
Ageing analysis highlighting vulnerabilities approaching or exceeding SLT deadlines
Trend reporting showing remediation volumes, completion rates, and average time-to-remediate by severity
Reports generated at least monthly and distributed to the CISO and IT Risk Committee
commonGaps: No centralised tracking — vulnerabilities tracked in spreadsheets or not tracked at all
Dashboard exists but is not regularly reviewed by management or used to drive remediation priorities
No ageing analysis to identify vulnerabilities that have exceeded their SLT deadlines
Tracking does not cover all vulnerability sources (scans, penetration tests, threat intelligence)
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform dashboard (Qualys, Rapid7, Tenable)
IT service management system (ServiceNow, Jira)
CISO monthly reporting pack

evidenceItems.name: SLT compliance metrics
evidenceItems.description: SLT compliance metrics
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly SLT compliance report showing percentage of vulnerabilities remediated within target for each severity level
Compliance rates above 90% for critical and high vulnerabilities demonstrating effective remediation
