Trend analysis showing improvement in SLT compliance over the past 12 months
Root cause analysis for SLT breaches with corrective actions to prevent recurrence
commonGaps: SLT compliance not measured or reported, making it impossible to demonstrate adherence to BNM's expectations
Compliance rates below 70% for critical vulnerabilities indicating systemic remediation challenges
No root cause analysis for SLT breaches — overdue items simply rolled over without investigation
Metrics not segmented by severity, masking poor performance on critical vulnerabilities
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform compliance reports
CISO office KPI dashboard
IT Risk Committee reporting pack

evidenceItems.name: Risk acceptance records for exceptions
evidenceItems.description: Risk acceptance records for exceptions
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal risk acceptance forms signed by the system owner and CISO for each exception
Each acceptance documents the specific vulnerability, reason for exception, compensating controls in place, and residual risk
Defined expiry date not exceeding 90 days with mandatory review and re-approval
Risk acceptance register maintained centrally with periodic review of active exceptions
commonGaps: Vulnerabilities left unpatched without formal risk acceptance documentation
Risk acceptances signed but without compensating controls or residual risk assessment
No expiry date on risk acceptances, allowing indefinite deferral of remediation
Risk acceptances approved by personnel without appropriate authority (not the system owner or CISO)
suggestedSources: Risk acceptance register (GRC platform or manual register)
CISO office exception management records
IT risk management function

auditTips: Request the remediation SLT compliance report for the past 12 months and verify compliance rates for critical vulnerabilities are above 90%
Pull a sample of 10 critical vulnerabilities from the past year and verify each was remediated within 7 days or has a documented risk acceptance
Review the risk acceptance register and verify that all exceptions have compensating controls, CISO sign-off, and expiry dates not exceeding 90 days
Check whether the institution uses automated patch management by requesting deployment success rates for the most recent critical security patches
Ask for evidence of compensating controls (such as virtual patching) deployed for critical vulnerabilities that could not be immediately remediated

11.15.title: Out-of-Band Communication Infrastructure
11.15.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has considered establishing a vulnerability disclosure or bug bounty programme, and if one exists, whether it has clear scope, safe harbour provisions, and operational procedures for triaging and remediating reported vulnerabilities.

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability disclosure policy or bug bounty program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Vulnerability disclosure policy or bug bounty program documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Published vulnerability disclosure policy accessible from the institution's website
Clear programme scope defining which systems and vulnerability types are in scope
Safe harbour provisions protecting researchers from legal action for good-faith vulnerability research
Defined reward structure (if bug bounty) or acknowledgement process (if coordinated disclosure)
commonGaps: No vulnerability disclosure programme considered or implemented despite BNM's guidance
Policy exists internally but is not published or accessible to external researchers
No safe harbour provisions, discouraging researchers from submitting reports due to legal risk
Programme scope too narrow, excluding critical systems such as mobile banking or APIs
suggestedSources: CISO office programme documentation
Institution's public website (security.txt, vulnerability disclosure page)
Bug bounty platform (HackerOne, Bugcrowd, YesWeHack)

evidenceItems.name: Program scope and rules of engagement
evidenceItems.description: Program scope and rules of engagement
whatGoodLooksLike: Detailed scope document listing in-scope domains, applications, APIs, and vulnerability types
Rules of engagement specifying what researchers can and cannot do during testing
Clear boundaries for testing to prevent impact on production systems or customer data
Regular scope updates reflecting new systems and services added to the institution's digital footprint
commonGaps: Scope not clearly defined, leading to confusion about what systems can be tested
Rules of engagement too restrictive, preventing meaningful security testing by researchers
No boundaries defined, risking unintended impact on production systems
Scope has not been updated to include new systems deployed since the programme launched
suggestedSources: Bug bounty platform scope configuration
CISO office programme management records
Security architecture team for system inventory alignment

evidenceItems.name: Reported vulnerability records and remediation
evidenceItems.description: Reported vulnerability records and remediation
whatGoodLooksLike: Register of all vulnerabilities reported through the programme with risk ratings and remediation status
Evidence of timely initial response to researchers (within 3 business days)
Reported vulnerabilities tracked through the standard remediation pipeline with SLT compliance
Trend analysis showing the volume and severity of reported vulnerabilities over time
commonGaps: No tracking of externally reported vulnerabilities separate from internal scan findings
Slow or no response to researcher submissions, undermining programme credibility
Reported vulnerabilities not integrated into the standard remediation tracking process
No analysis of reported vulnerability trends to identify systemic security weaknesses
suggestedSources: Bug bounty platform submission and triage records
Vulnerability management tracking system
CISO office programme metrics and reports

evidenceItems.name: Researcher acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.description: Researcher acknowledgement records
whatGoodLooksLike: Records of researcher acknowledgement or reward payments for valid vulnerability submissions
Hall of fame or acknowledgement page recognising contributing researchers (where agreed)
Response time metrics showing consistent adherence to published SLTs
Researcher feedback or satisfaction data used to improve the programme
commonGaps: No acknowledgement or reward process, providing no incentive for researchers to participate
Inconsistent researcher communication with some reports receiving no response
Response time metrics not tracked, with no visibility into researcher experience
No feedback mechanism to improve the programme based on researcher input
suggestedSources: Bug bounty platform payment and acknowledgement records
CISO office researcher correspondence records
Programme annual review documentation

auditTips: Check whether the institution has published a security.txt file or vulnerability disclosure page on their website, indicating a mature approach to external researcher engagement
If a programme exists, request submission records for the past 12 months and verify that reported vulnerabilities were triaged and remediated within standard SLTs
Verify that safe harbour provisions exist in the disclosure policy to protect researchers from legal action for good-faith testing
If no programme exists, ask the institution to explain their rationale and whether they have considered BNM's guidance on this topic
Review whether the programme scope covers the institution's full internet-facing attack surface including APIs and mobile applications

11.16.title: Annual Cyber Drill Exercise
11.16.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal threat intelligence programme exists with multiple intelligence sources, produces actionable intelligence relevant to the financial sector, and can demonstrate evidence of intelligence-driven security improvements.

evidenceItems.name: Threat intelligence program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Threat intelligence program documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented programme charter defining objectives, scope, intelligence requirements, and operational processes
Clear intelligence cycle covering collection, processing, analysis, dissemination, and feedback
Programme aligned with the institution's threat profile and the Malaysian financial sector threat landscape
Defined roles and responsibilities for the threat intelligence function with qualified personnel
commonGaps: No formal programme documentation — threat intelligence activities are ad hoc and unstructured
Programme focuses only on tactical intelligence (IoCs) without strategic or operational intelligence production
Intelligence requirements not defined, resulting in unfocused collection and analysis efforts
No dedicated threat intelligence personnel — function performed part-time by SOC analysts
suggestedSources: CISO office threat intelligence programme documentation
Threat intelligence team operating procedures
Security operations programme documentation

evidenceItems.name: Intelligence source inventory
evidenceItems.description: Intelligence source inventory
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory listing all intelligence sources including commercial feeds, open-source, government, and industry sharing arrangements
Source quality ratings based on relevance, accuracy, timeliness, and coverage
Mix of strategic, operational, and tactical intelligence sources appropriate for the institution's needs
Sources include MyCERT advisories, BNM circulars, regional ISAC feeds, and financial sector-specific intelligence
commonGaps: Reliance on a single commercial threat intelligence feed without supplementary sources
No government or regulator intelligence sources (MyCERT, BNM) included in the source portfolio
Source inventory not maintained or reviewed, with expired subscriptions or inactive feeds
No dark web or underground forum monitoring for threats targeting the institution or Malaysian financial sector
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform source configuration (MISP, Recorded Future, ThreatConnect, Anomali)
Procurement records for commercial intelligence subscriptions
MyCERT and BNM correspondence records

evidenceItems.name: Threat intelligence reports and analysis
evidenceItems.description: Threat intelligence reports and analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular threat intelligence reports (weekly tactical, monthly operational, quarterly strategic) tailored to different audiences
Reports include analysis of threats specific to the institution's technology environment and the Malaysian financial sector
Intelligence assessments include confidence levels, source reliability indicators, and recommended actions
Evidence that reports are disseminated to relevant teams including SOC, vulnerability management, and senior management
commonGaps: No regular threat intelligence reporting — information shared ad hoc when major threats emerge
Reports are raw intelligence feeds forwarded without analysis or contextualisation for the institution
Intelligence reports do not include actionable recommendations or recommended defensive actions
Reports produced but not disseminated beyond the threat intelligence team
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence team report repository
CISO monthly and quarterly reporting packs
SOC intelligence briefing records

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of intelligence-driven security actions
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of intelligence-driven security actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented examples of SIEM rules or detection use cases created from threat intelligence analysis
Evidence of security controls updated in response to specific threat intelligence findings
Vulnerability scanning priorities adjusted based on actively exploited vulnerabilities identified through intelligence
Security awareness campaigns triggered by intelligence on phishing or social engineering threats targeting the sector
commonGaps: No documented linkage between threat intelligence and security operations actions
Intelligence consumed but not operationalised into detection rules, control updates, or priority changes
Actions taken in response to intelligence but not documented, making it impossible to demonstrate the programme's value
Intelligence-driven actions limited to IoC blocking without broader defensive improvements
suggestedSources: SOC detection engineering change records linked to threat intelligence
SIEM rule change logs referencing intelligence sources
Vulnerability management priority adjustment records

auditTips: Request the threat intelligence source inventory and verify that multiple sources are in use, including government sources (MyCERT, BNM) and financial sector-specific intelligence
Ask for three examples of intelligence-driven security actions in the past six months and trace them from the intelligence report through to the operational change
Review threat intelligence reports and verify they include institution-specific analysis and actionable recommendations, not just forwarded vendor alerts
Check whether the threat intelligence platform is integrated with the SIEM and EDR to enable automated detection of newly identified indicators of compromise
Verify that the programme produces intelligence at strategic, operational, and tactical levels, not just tactical IoC feeds

11.17.title: Cyber Insurance and Loss Provision
11.17.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution actively participates in threat information sharing with peers, government agencies, and ISACs, that shared information is handled in accordance with TLP or equivalent protocols, and that sharing arrangements are supported by formal agreements.

evidenceItems.name: Threat sharing agreements and memberships
evidenceItems.description: Threat sharing agreements and memberships
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed sharing agreements with industry peers, government agencies (MyCERT, NACSA), and relevant ISACs
Active membership in at least one financial sector ISAC or sharing community
Agreements define sharing scope, handling requirements, confidentiality obligations, and dispute resolution
Current memberships and agreements — not expired or lapsed subscriptions
commonGaps: No formal sharing agreements in place — intelligence shared informally without defined terms
Membership in sharing groups but no active participation or contribution
Agreements signed but expired or not renewed, with no current active arrangements
Sharing limited to government agencies only without industry peer participation
suggestedSources: CISO office partnership and sharing agreement records
Legal department contract management records
MyCERT and NACSA membership correspondence

evidenceItems.name: TLP or equivalent handling procedures
evidenceItems.description: TLP or equivalent handling procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented TLP handling procedures covering all four levels: TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, TLP:GREEN, and TLP:CLEAR
Procedures specify who can receive information at each TLP level and how it must be stored and transmitted
Staff awareness training on TLP handling completed by all personnel who handle shared intelligence
Technical controls enforcing TLP handling restrictions in the threat intelligence platform and email systems
commonGaps: No documented TLP handling procedures despite receiving TLP-marked intelligence from sharing partners
Procedures exist but staff are unaware of TLP classifications and handling requirements
TLP:AMBER intelligence forwarded broadly within the organisation without restriction
No technical controls enforcing TLP restrictions — compliance relies entirely on manual processes
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence team operational procedures
Information classification and handling policy
Security awareness training records

evidenceItems.name: Records of information shared and received
evidenceItems.description: Records of information shared and received
whatGoodLooksLike: Log of threat intelligence shared with and received from sharing partners over the past 12 months
Evidence of both contribution and consumption demonstrating active bilateral participation
Shared intelligence includes indicators of compromise, threat reports, and adversary analysis relevant to the financial sector
Records show regular sharing activity, not just one or two exchanges per year
commonGaps: Institution consumes shared intelligence but does not contribute, acting as a passive participant
No records of sharing activity maintained, making it impossible to demonstrate participation to BNM
Sharing activity is sporadic with long gaps between exchanges indicating a dormant programme
Shared information limited to IoCs without broader threat context or analysis
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform sharing logs (MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali)
Email correspondence records with sharing partners
ISAC portal activity logs and contribution records

evidenceItems.name: ISAC participation evidence
evidenceItems.description: ISAC participation evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Active membership in a financial sector ISAC with documented participation in meetings and working groups
Evidence of intelligence received through ISAC channels that was operationalised by the institution
Contribution of threat intelligence or incident data back to the ISAC community
Attendance records for ISAC meetings, workshops, or collaborative exercises
commonGaps: ISAC membership exists on paper but no active participation in meetings or intelligence exchange
No financial sector-specific ISAC membership — participation limited to general cybersecurity forums
Intelligence received from ISAC not integrated into the institution's threat intelligence programme
No contribution of intelligence back to the ISAC, undermining the collaborative model
suggestedSources: ISAC membership documentation and correspondence
ISAC meeting attendance records
Threat intelligence team ISAC contribution records

auditTips: Request evidence of active ISAC membership and verify participation through meeting attendance records and intelligence contribution logs
Ask for the TLP handling procedures and test staff awareness by asking how TLP:AMBER intelligence should be handled
Review the sharing log for the past 12 months and verify the institution is contributing intelligence, not just consuming it
Check whether shared intelligence is automatically ingested into the SIEM and threat intelligence platform or requires manual processing
Verify that formal sharing agreements exist with defined terms, particularly for confidentiality and permitted use of shared information

11.18.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.18.title: Cyber Incident Notification to BNM
11.18.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has established proactive threat hunting capabilities informed by threat intelligence, whether hunts follow structured methodologies, and whether findings are operationalised into improved detection rules and security controls.

evidenceItems.name: Threat hunting program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Threat hunting program documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented programme with clear objectives, methodology, hunting cadence, and resource allocation
Methodology defined as hypothesis-driven or intelligence-driven with structured approach to each hunt
Dedicated or designated threat hunting personnel with appropriate skills and certifications
Programme integrated with the broader threat intelligence and detection engineering functions
commonGaps: No formal threat hunting programme — hunting performed informally or not at all
Programme documented but no hunts actually conducted in the past 12 months
No dedicated hunting resources — expected to be performed by SOC analysts alongside alert triage duties
Hunting methodology not defined, resulting in ad hoc and unrepeatable investigations
suggestedSources: CISO office threat hunting programme documentation
SOC operations programme documentation
Threat intelligence team operating procedures

evidenceItems.name: Threat hunting methodology and playbooks
evidenceItems.description: Threat hunting methodology and playbooks
whatGoodLooksLike: Hunting playbooks covering key adversary techniques from MITRE ATT&CK including lateral movement, credential access, persistence, and exfiltration
Each playbook defines the hypothesis, data sources to query, analytical techniques, expected findings, and escalation procedures
Playbooks tailored to the institution's technology environment and common threat actor profiles
Playbooks regularly updated based on new threat intelligence and findings from previous hunts
commonGaps: No documented hunting playbooks — hunts performed ad hoc without repeatable methodology
Playbooks are generic and not tailored to the institution's specific technology environment
Playbooks cover only basic techniques without addressing advanced adversary behaviours
Playbooks not updated following new threat intelligence or completed hunts
suggestedSources: Threat hunting team playbook repository
SOC knowledge base (Confluence, SharePoint)
Threat intelligence team TTP analysis records

evidenceItems.name: Threat hunting reports and findings
evidenceItems.description: Threat hunting reports and findings
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular hunting reports documenting each hunt with the hypothesis tested, data analysed, and findings
Evidence of hunts conducted at least monthly with documented outcomes
Findings include both positive results (threats found) and negative results (no threats found but coverage validated)
Actionable recommendations from each hunt with follow-up actions tracked to completion
commonGaps: No documented hunting reports — hunts conducted but not recorded for audit or knowledge retention
Hunts conducted infrequently with long gaps between activities
Reports only document positive findings without recording hunts that found no threats, losing validation evidence
Findings not actioned or tracked, with recommendations remaining as report items without follow-through
suggestedSources: Threat hunting team report repository
SOC case management system hunting records
CISO quarterly reporting on threat hunting activities

evidenceItems.name: Hunting-to-detection pipeline records
evidenceItems.description: Hunting-to-detection pipeline records
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented pipeline process for converting hunting findings into automated SIEM detection rules or EDR policies
Records showing specific detection rules created as a direct result of threat hunting discoveries
Tracking of detection rules from hunt origin through development, testing, and production deployment
Measurable improvement in detection coverage attributable to threat hunting findings
commonGaps: No hunt-to-detection pipeline — hunting findings remain in reports without being operationalised
Detection rules created from hunts but not tracked back to the originating hunt for programme measurement
Pipeline exists on paper but no detection rules have actually been created from hunting findings
Detection rules created but not properly tested before deployment, leading to false positives
suggestedSources: SOC detection engineering change records
SIEM rule management and change log
Threat hunting programme metrics and KPIs

auditTips: Request the threat hunting log for the past 12 months and verify that hunts are being conducted regularly with documented hypotheses and findings
Ask for examples of detection rules created from threat hunting findings and verify they are active in the SIEM
Verify that hunting activities are informed by current threat intelligence by checking whether hunt hypotheses reference specific intelligence sources
Review the hunting playbook collection and assess whether it covers the most relevant adversary techniques for the financial sector
If no formal programme exists, ask the institution to explain what proactive measures they take to detect threats beyond automated monitoring

11.19.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.19.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing
11.19.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has a structured process for managing indicators of compromise covering collection, validation, enrichment, and integration with security monitoring tools, maintained in a threat intelligence platform and kept current with the evolving threat landscape.

evidenceItems.name: IoC management procedure
evidenceItems.description: IoC management procedure
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented IoC management procedure covering the full lifecycle: collection, validation, enrichment, dissemination, and retirement
Procedure defines IoC types managed (IP addresses, domains, file hashes, URLs, email addresses, YARA rules) with handling requirements for each
Validation process includes confidence scoring and source reliability assessment before IoCs are operationalised
Enrichment process adds context such as threat actor attribution, campaign association, and relevance to the Malaysian financial sector
commonGaps: No documented IoC management procedure — IoCs are consumed from feeds and pushed to tools without validation or enrichment
IoCs collected but never validated, leading to false positives from stale or inaccurate indicators blocking legitimate traffic
No confidence scoring or source reliability assessment, treating all IoCs as equally trustworthy regardless of origin
IoC retirement process absent, with indicators remaining active indefinitely and degrading detection quality over time
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence team standard operating procedures
Information security policy framework — threat intelligence section
SOC operational procedures manual

evidenceItems.name: Threat intelligence platform configuration
evidenceItems.description: Threat intelligence platform configuration
whatGoodLooksLike: Deployed threat intelligence platform (e.g., MISP, OpenCTI, ThreatConnect, Anomali) with documented configuration and architecture
Platform configured with taxonomies and tagging aligned to MITRE ATT&CK, STIX/TAXII standards, and the institution's internal classification scheme
Role-based access controls implemented with audit logging of all platform activities
Platform integrated with external feed sources including MyCERT advisories, commercial feeds, and ISAC sharing communities
commonGaps: No dedicated threat intelligence platform — IoCs managed in spreadsheets or ad hoc email distribution lists
Platform deployed but poorly configured with default taxonomies that do not align with the institution's threat model
No STIX/TAXII integration capability, preventing automated ingestion from and sharing with external partners
Platform access not restricted by role, allowing unauthorised staff to modify or delete IoC records
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform administration console and configuration exports
IT infrastructure architecture documentation — TIP deployment diagram
Platform vendor support and licensing records

evidenceItems.name: IoC feed integration evidence
evidenceItems.description: IoC feed integration evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented integrations between the threat intelligence platform and security monitoring tools (SIEM, EDR, firewall, proxy, email gateway)
Automated IoC dissemination pipeline pushing validated indicators to blocking and detection tools with minimal manual intervention
Integration health monitoring in place with alerts for feed failures or synchronisation delays
Evidence of IoC-triggered detections or blocks in security tool logs over the past 12 months
commonGaps: IoCs collected in the TIP but not automatically pushed to security monitoring tools — requiring manual copy-paste into SIEM rules
Integration exists but is one-directional, with no feedback loop from detection tools back to the TIP on IoC hit rates
Feed integration failures go undetected for extended periods, leaving gaps in automated detection coverage
Only partial integration — IoCs pushed to SIEM but not to firewall, proxy, or email gateway for blocking
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform integration configuration and API connection logs
SIEM correlation rule library showing IoC-based detection rules
Firewall and proxy block list management records

evidenceItems.name: IoC lifecycle management records
evidenceItems.description: IoC lifecycle management records
whatGoodLooksLike: Records demonstrating active IoC lifecycle management including creation dates, last validation dates, and retirement dates
Regular review cycles (at least monthly) to age out stale IoCs and update confidence scores based on new intelligence
Dashboard or metrics showing total active IoCs, IoCs added and retired per period, and false positive rates
Evidence that IoC retirement decisions are informed by threat intelligence analysis rather than arbitrary time-based expiry
commonGaps: No IoC retirement process — indicators accumulate indefinitely, with stale IoCs from years ago still active in detection tools
No regular review cycle for IoC validity, leading to growing false positive rates that erode analyst trust
No metrics or reporting on IoC lifecycle, making it impossible to demonstrate programme health to BNM examiners
IoCs retired based solely on age without considering whether the associated threat actor or campaign is still active
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform IoC statistics and lifecycle reports
SOC false positive analysis records linked to IoC-based detections
Threat intelligence team monthly operational reports

auditTips: Request a walkthrough of the IoC lifecycle from collection through to retirement, asking the team to demonstrate each stage with a recent real-world example
Verify automated integration by selecting a recently ingested IoC in the TIP and confirming it appears in SIEM correlation rules and firewall block lists
Check IoC validation processes by asking how the team distinguishes between high-confidence and low-confidence indicators before operationalising them
Review IoC retirement records and ask how the team determines when an indicator is no longer relevant — look for evidence-based decisions rather than arbitrary expiry
Examine feed health monitoring by asking what happens when an IoC feed fails and how quickly the team is alerted to integration disruptions

11.20.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.20.title: Stakeholder Collaboration on Cyber Threats
11.20.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution maintains continuous awareness of the external threat landscape and its own attack surface, with regular situational awareness reports provided to senior management and security operations to inform risk decisions.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber situational awareness program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Cyber situational awareness program documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Formally documented cyber situational awareness programme with defined objectives, scope, information sources, and reporting cadence approved by the CISO
Programme covers three domains: external threat landscape monitoring, institutional attack surface monitoring, and financial sector-specific threat tracking
Defined roles and responsibilities for producing, reviewing, and distributing situational awareness outputs
Programme references relevant Malaysian threat sources including MyCERT advisories, NACSA alerts, and BNM communications on cyber threats
commonGaps: No documented situational awareness programme — threat monitoring is ad hoc and dependent on individual analyst initiative
Programme covers external threats but neglects the institution's own attack surface and exposure monitoring
No defined information sources or collection strategy, resulting in inconsistent and incomplete threat coverage
Programme does not address financial sector-specific threats, relying solely on generic cybersecurity news
suggestedSources: CISO office programme documentation and governance records
Threat intelligence team charter and operational procedures
Annual cybersecurity strategy document submitted to the board

evidenceItems.name: Attack surface monitoring evidence
evidenceItems.description: Attack surface monitoring evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Continuous external attack surface monitoring using dedicated tooling (e.g., ASM platforms such as Mandiant Advantage, Censys, or Shodan Monitor)
Regular attack surface discovery scans identifying internet-facing assets, exposed services, shadow IT, and certificate issues
Findings tracked through to remediation with defined SLAs for addressing newly discovered exposures
Attack surface inventory reconciled against the institution's approved asset register to identify unauthorised or unknown assets
commonGaps: No external attack surface monitoring — the institution relies solely on internal vulnerability scanning without visibility of its external footprint
Attack surface scans conducted but findings not tracked through to remediation, leaving identified exposures unaddressed
Monitoring is periodic (e.g., quarterly) rather than continuous, missing newly exposed assets between scan cycles
Shadow IT and cloud assets excluded from attack surface monitoring, creating blind spots in the institution's external exposure
suggestedSources: Attack surface management platform dashboards and reports
Vulnerability management team remediation tracking records
IT asset management system reconciliation reports

evidenceItems.name: External threat landscape reports
evidenceItems.description: External threat landscape reports
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular (at least monthly) threat landscape reports covering emerging threats, active campaigns, and vulnerabilities relevant to the Malaysian financial sector
Reports reference credible intelligence sources including MyCERT advisories, NACSA bulletins, vendor threat reports, and ISAC intelligence
Analysis includes relevance assessment specific to the institution's technology stack, geography, and business operations
Reports include actionable recommendations for security operations and risk management, not merely informational summaries
commonGaps: No regular threat landscape reporting — awareness is limited to ad hoc forwarding of vendor alerts and news articles
Reports produced but are generic summaries without relevance assessment to the institution's specific environment
No inclusion of Malaysia-specific threat intelligence from MyCERT, NACSA, or local financial sector sharing communities
Reports lack actionable recommendations, providing information without guidance on what the institution should do in response
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence team report repository
MyCERT and NACSA advisory tracking records
Commercial threat intelligence vendor portal (e.g., Recorded Future, Mandiant, CrowdStrike)

evidenceItems.name: Situational awareness reports to management
evidenceItems.description: Situational awareness reports to management
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular (at least monthly) cyber situational awareness reports provided to senior management and the board risk committee
Reports summarise the current threat posture, significant incidents in the financial sector, attack surface changes, and recommended actions
Evidence of management acknowledgement, discussion, or decisions taken based on situational awareness reports (e.g., meeting minutes, action items)
Reporting format tailored for executive audience with risk-based language, avoiding overly technical jargon
commonGaps: No regular situational awareness reporting to senior management — cyber threat updates provided only after major incidents
Reports produced for the SOC but not escalated to senior management or the board, failing to meet the RMiT requirement
Reports provided but no evidence of management engagement, discussion, or action taken in response
Reporting is overly technical and not accessible to senior management, reducing its value as a risk governance input
suggestedSources: Board risk committee and IT steering committee meeting packs and minutes
CISO monthly or quarterly report to senior management
Enterprise risk management reporting portal

auditTips: Request the last three months of situational awareness reports provided to senior management and verify they cover external threats, attack surface findings, and financial sector-specific intelligence
Ask for evidence of management acknowledgement or decisions taken in response to situational awareness reports — meeting minutes or action items from board risk committee sessions
Review the attack surface monitoring setup by asking the team to demonstrate the tooling and show recently discovered exposures with remediation tracking evidence
Check whether MyCERT advisories and NACSA bulletins are systematically tracked and assessed for relevance to the institution, not merely received and filed

Verify the situational awareness programme covers all three domains required by this clause: external threat landscape, institutional attack surface, and emerging threats targeting the financial sector

12.1.title: Digital Services Security Controls
12.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that comprehensive security controls are implemented across all digital channels (internet banking, mobile banking, e-payment) and that these controls are regularly tested. Expect requests for security architecture documents, vulnerability assessment results, and evidence of security controls covering authentication, authorisation, encryption, and session management for each channel.

evidenceItems.name: Digital channel security architecture
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive security architecture documentation for all digital banking channels showing defence-in-depth controls, data flow diagrams, and security component placement
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams cover all active digital channels (internet banking, mobile banking, e-payment) individually
Security controls are mapped to specific threats and OWASP Top 10 categories for each channel
Defence-in-depth layers are clearly illustrated including WAF, API gateway, authentication services, and encryption points
Document is version-controlled with review dates within the last 12 months
Data flow diagrams show encryption boundaries and trust zones for each channel
commonGaps: Architecture documentation exists only for internet banking but not mobile banking or e-payment channels
Security controls are described generically without mapping to specific threats or vulnerabilities
Documentation has not been updated after significant platform changes or new channel launches
Missing data flow diagrams showing where encryption is applied and where sensitive data is processed
suggestedSources: Enterprise architecture team (ArchiMate/TOGAF repositories)
IT Security department security design documents
Digital banking platform vendor documentation

evidenceItems.name: Security control implementation evidence per channel
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that authentication, authorisation, encryption, and session management controls are implemented and operational for each digital channel
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration screenshots or exports showing TLS 1.2+ enforcement, HSTS headers, and certificate configurations per channel
Authentication mechanism documentation with multi-factor implementation details for each channel
Authorisation matrix showing role-based access controls for digital banking administrative functions
Session management configuration evidence including timeout values and concurrent session limits
commonGaps: Evidence shows configuration for one channel but not all active digital channels
TLS configuration allows deprecated cipher suites or protocol versions
No evidence of session management controls such as timeout settings or concurrent session restrictions
Authentication controls documented in policy but no evidence of actual implementation configuration
suggestedSources: Digital banking platform configuration exports
Web server and application server configuration files
WAF and API gateway configuration dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Web application vulnerability assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Results from vulnerability assessments and penetration tests conducted against all internet-facing digital channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly vulnerability scans and annual penetration tests covering all digital channels with no outstanding critical or high findings
Reports from qualified security assessors (CREST, OSCP certified) with clear scope covering all digital channel endpoints
Remediation tracking showing all identified vulnerabilities addressed within defined SLAs (critical: 7 days, high: 30 days)
Testing covers OWASP Top 10 categories with specific test cases documented for each
commonGaps: Penetration testing scope excludes mobile banking APIs or e-payment endpoints
Vulnerability assessment performed only annually rather than quarterly as required
Critical or high vulnerabilities remain open beyond defined remediation timelines with no documented risk acceptance
Testing performed by internal team without independent external validation
suggestedSources: Security testing vendor reports (e.g., Qualys, Rapid7, HackerOne)
Internal vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable, Nessus)
Penetration testing engagement reports from CREST-certified providers

evidenceItems.name: Digital channel security standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal security standards document defining security requirements for all digital banking channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards cover authentication, authorisation, encryption, session management, and vulnerability management for each channel type
Document is approved by CISO and reviewed within the last 12 months
Standards reference industry frameworks (OWASP ASVS, PCI DSS, NIST) and are mapped to RMiT requirements
Clear compliance requirements with measurable criteria for each security control area
Standards include secure coding requirements for digital channel development teams
commonGaps: Standards document is generic and does not address channel-specific security requirements
No formal approval or review process; document is outdated
Standards lack measurable criteria making compliance assessment subjective
Missing coverage of newer channels such as QR payments or e-wallet features
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
CISO office governance document library
Digital banking team standards documentation

auditTips: Prepare a channel-by-channel security control matrix mapping each RMiT requirement to specific implemented controls with configuration evidence
Ensure vulnerability assessment reports cover all active digital channels and that remediation evidence is readily available for any identified findings
Verify that the security architecture documentation reflects the current production environment, not an outdated or aspirational state
Have session management configuration exports readily available showing timeout values and concurrent session settings for each channel
Prepare a timeline showing when security standards were last reviewed and any changes made in response to new threats or regulatory guidance

12.2.title: Alternative Controls and Liability
12.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will specifically look for evidence that mobile banking apps implement code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, and secure local storage. They will also verify that security testing is performed before each app store release, with formal sign-off records.

evidenceItems.name: Mobile application security standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal standards document defining mandatory security features and testing requirements for mobile banking applications
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards explicitly mandate code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, and secure local storage as required for all mobile banking apps
OWASP MASVS (Mobile Application Security Verification Standard) is referenced as the baseline framework
Standards define pre-release security testing requirements and acceptance criteria
Document is approved by CISO, reviewed within the last 12 months, and distributed to all mobile development teams
Standards cover both Android and iOS platform-specific security requirements
commonGaps: Standards exist but do not explicitly list all RMiT-mandated security features (obfuscation, root detection, pinning, secure storage)
No reference to OWASP MASVS or equivalent mobile security framework
Standards have not been updated to reflect latest mobile platform security capabilities
Missing platform-specific guidance for Android vs iOS implementations
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
Mobile development team documentation
CISO office governance document library

evidenceItems.name: Security feature implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that each mandated mobile security feature is implemented and functional in the production mobile banking application
whatGoodLooksLike: Code obfuscation configuration evidence (ProGuard/R8 rules for Android, build settings for iOS) with before/after decompilation comparison
Root/jailbreak detection implementation evidence showing detection logic and response actions (restrict operations, log events)
Certificate pinning configuration showing pinned certificates and pin update mechanism
Secure storage implementation evidence showing use of Android Keystore/iOS Keychain for sensitive data
Anti-tampering and anti-debugging protections documented with test results
commonGaps: Code obfuscation enabled but with default minimal rules that are easily reversible
Root/jailbreak detection implemented but only logs events without restricting high-risk operations
Certificate pinning implemented but no mechanism for pin rotation, risking app lockout during certificate changes
Sensitive data found in shared preferences or local files instead of platform secure storage
suggestedSources: Mobile development team build configuration repositories
Mobile application security scan reports (e.g., NowSecure, Checkmarx)
Source code repository showing security feature implementations

evidenceItems.name: Mobile application security test reports
evidenceItems.description: Security testing reports from independent assessors validating mobile application security for each production release
whatGoodLooksLike: Independent MAST (Mobile Application Security Testing) report for every production release within the last 12 months
Testing scope covers OWASP MASVS L1 and L2 requirements including all RMiT-mandated security features
Reports produced by qualified mobile security assessors (e.g., CREST, NowSecure certified)
All critical and high findings remediated before app store release with evidence of retesting
Both static analysis (SAST) and dynamic analysis (DAST) results included
commonGaps: Security testing performed only for major releases but skipped for minor or patch releases
Testing scope does not cover all OWASP MASVS requirements relevant to banking applications
Critical findings identified but app released before remediation with informal risk acceptance
Only automated scanning performed without manual security testing by qualified assessors
suggestedSources: Mobile security testing vendor reports (NowSecure, Synopsys, Checkmarx)
Internal application security testing team reports
CI/CD pipeline security scan outputs

evidenceItems.name: App store release security sign-off records
evidenceItems.description: Formal security sign-off records documenting IT Security approval before each mobile app release to app stores
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed security checklist completed for every app store release covering all RMiT-mandated features
Sign-off by IT Security, QA Lead, and release manager with dates and approval signatures
Security test results summary attached to each sign-off showing pass/fail status for each security requirement
Clear traceability from security test findings to remediation actions and retesting confirmation
Records maintained for all releases within the retention period including rejected releases
commonGaps: No formal sign-off process; security approval is verbal or via informal email
Sign-off checklist exists but does not cover all RMiT-mandated mobile security features
Sign-off records missing for some releases, particularly emergency or hotfix releases
No evidence linking the sign-off to specific security test results
suggestedSources: Release management system (Jira, ServiceNow, Azure DevOps)
IT Security approval workflow records
Mobile app release management documentation

auditTips: Prepare a release-by-release matrix showing security test date, test results summary, and sign-off record for each mobile app version released in the past 12 months
Have technical evidence ready demonstrating each specific RMiT security feature (obfuscation, root detection, pinning, secure storage) with configuration screenshots
Ensure security test reports clearly state OWASP MASVS coverage and that the testing scope aligns with RMiT requirements
Verify that emergency and hotfix releases also have security sign-off records, even if through an expedited process
Prepare evidence showing how certificate pinning is managed during certificate rotation to demonstrate operational maturity

12.3.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.3.title: CRF Enhancement for Digital Fraud
12.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that all APIs are inventoried, documented, and secured with authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption. They will also check that deprecated APIs are decommissioned promptly and that regular security assessments are performed on APIs.

evidenceItems.name: API security standards and policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal API security standards defining requirements for authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption across all APIs
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards aligned with OWASP API Security Top 10 covering authentication, broken object-level authorisation, rate limiting, and injection prevention
Clear requirements for OAuth 2.0 implementation, API key management, and mutual TLS for server-to-server communications
Rate limiting and throttling thresholds defined per API tier (public, partner, internal)
Document approved by CISO and reviewed within the last 12 months
Input validation and output encoding requirements specified for all API endpoints
commonGaps: API security standards focus only on authentication but lack rate limiting, input validation, and encryption requirements
Standards do not differentiate between public-facing, partner, and internal API security requirements
No reference to OWASP API Security Top 10 or equivalent API security framework
Standards have not been updated to address new API patterns such as GraphQL or WebSocket APIs
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
API platform team documentation
Enterprise architecture standards library

evidenceItems.name: API inventory and documentation
evidenceItems.description: Complete inventory of all APIs with security classification, documentation status, and lifecycle stage
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory covering all APIs (internal, partner, public) with unique identifiers, owners, and security classifications
Each API has OpenAPI/Swagger documentation showing endpoints, parameters, authentication requirements, and data types
Lifecycle stage clearly marked for each API (active, deprecated, decommissioning, retired)
Inventory updated within the last quarter with evidence of regular review process
Data sensitivity classification for each API indicating what customer or financial data is accessible
commonGaps: Inventory exists for public APIs but internal and partner APIs are not tracked
Deprecated APIs identified but no decommissioning timeline or plan documented
API documentation is incomplete or outdated, not reflecting current production endpoints
No data sensitivity classification for APIs making it impossible to apply risk-proportionate controls
suggestedSources: API management platform (Apigee, Kong, AWS API Gateway) dashboards
API developer portal documentation
Enterprise service registry or API catalogue

evidenceItems.name: API security assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Results from regular security assessments of APIs including automated scanning and manual penetration testing
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly automated API security scans covering all production APIs with OWASP API Top 10 test coverage
Annual manual API penetration testing by qualified assessors covering authentication bypass, injection, and authorisation flaws
Remediation tracking showing all identified vulnerabilities addressed within defined SLAs
Testing includes both functional API security tests and business logic abuse scenarios
CI/CD pipeline integration for automated API security testing on every deployment
commonGaps: API security testing limited to web application scans that do not cover API-specific vulnerabilities
No testing for business logic abuse such as broken object-level authorisation (BOLA) or excessive data exposure
Security assessment scope does not include partner or internal APIs
Automated scanning only without manual penetration testing by qualified security assessors
suggestedSources: API security testing tools (Postman, Burp Suite, 42Crunch)
Penetration testing vendor reports
CI/CD pipeline security scan outputs

evidenceItems.name: Deprecated API decommissioning records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating timely identification, communication, and decommissioning of deprecated APIs
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented decommissioning timeline for each deprecated API with consumer notification records
Evidence of consumer migration support including migration guides and alternative API documentation
Confirmation of API shutdown with traffic monitoring showing zero usage before final decommissioning
Post-decommission verification that endpoints no longer respond and are removed from API gateways
commonGaps: Deprecated APIs identified but remain active with no decommissioning timeline
No evidence of consumer notification before API retirement
Decommissioned APIs still responding with error messages rather than being fully removed
No monitoring to verify deprecated APIs are not still receiving traffic from unknown consumers
suggestedSources: API management platform lifecycle reports
Change management records for API decommissioning
API gateway traffic analytics showing deprecated API usage trends

auditTips: Prepare the complete API inventory with evidence that it includes all APIs, not just public-facing ones; examiners often ask about internal and partner APIs
Have API gateway configuration exports showing rate limiting, authentication, and encryption settings for representative APIs
Ensure deprecated API decommissioning records show timely action with documented consumer communication trails
Prepare a mapping of each API to its security assessment coverage demonstrating no APIs are excluded from testing
Show evidence of CI/CD pipeline API security scanning to demonstrate shift-left security practices

12.4.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.4.title: Fraud Detection Capabilities
12.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that customer authentication strength is proportionate to transaction risk, that MFA is implemented for high-risk transactions, and that authentication effectiveness is regularly assessed. Expect detailed questions about the risk-based authentication model and how it adapts to emerging threats.

evidenceItems.name: Customer authentication policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining risk-based authentication requirements, acceptable authentication methods, and MFA mandates for high-risk transactions across all digital channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy clearly defines transaction risk tiers (low, medium, high, critical) with corresponding authentication requirements for each tier
MFA is explicitly mandated for high-risk transactions including fund transfers above defined thresholds, payee additions, and limit changes
Acceptable authentication factors are defined for each category (knowledge, possession, inherence) with approved implementations
Policy is approved by CISO and board risk committee, reviewed within the last 12 months
Coverage spans all digital channels (internet banking, mobile banking, e-wallet)
commonGaps: Policy mandates MFA generically without defining which specific transactions require it based on risk classification
Transaction risk tiers not formally defined, leaving authentication requirements ambiguous
Policy does not address emerging authentication methods such as biometrics or FIDO2
Missing coverage of some digital channels, particularly newer channels like e-wallet or QR payments
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
Digital banking product documentation
Risk management framework documentation

evidenceItems.name: Risk-based authentication configuration
evidenceItems.description: Technical configuration evidence showing how authentication requirements are dynamically adjusted based on transaction risk and user behaviour
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration exports showing risk scoring rules including device fingerprint, geolocation, transaction amount, and behavioural factors
Step-up authentication triggers documented for high-risk scenarios with specific threshold values
Risk engine decision flow diagrams showing how authentication level is determined in real time
Evidence of adaptive authentication adjusting requirements based on anomaly detection
commonGaps: Static authentication requirements applied uniformly regardless of transaction risk level
Risk-based authentication described in policy but not actually implemented in the technical platform
Risk scoring rules have not been updated to address current attack patterns such as SIM swapping or device emulation
No evidence of adaptive behaviour; same authentication for all transactions regardless of context
suggestedSources: Authentication platform configuration (e.g., ForgeRock, Ping Identity, custom IAM)
Risk engine rule configuration exports
Digital banking platform authentication settings

evidenceItems.name: MFA implementation for high-risk transactions
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that multi-factor authentication is implemented and enforced for all high-risk digital banking transactions
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration evidence showing MFA enforcement for fund transfers above defined thresholds, payee additions, and account settings changes
MFA methods deployed include at least two independent factor categories (e.g., password + OTP, biometric + device binding)
Transaction logs showing MFA challenge issuance and completion rates for high-risk transactions
Fallback authentication procedures documented for MFA delivery failures with appropriate security controls
commonGaps: MFA implemented only for login but not enforced for high-risk transactional operations
Single SMS OTP used as the sole second factor without alternative methods for SIM swap scenarios
MFA bypass mechanisms exist for customer convenience that undermine the security intent
No monitoring of MFA completion rates to detect potential bypass or social engineering attacks
suggestedSources: Authentication platform MFA configuration
Transaction processing system authentication logs
Digital banking application MFA flow documentation

evidenceItems.name: Authentication effectiveness assessment records
evidenceItems.description: Records from periodic assessments evaluating the effectiveness of customer authentication mechanisms against current threats
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual authentication effectiveness assessment report covering all digital channels and authentication methods
Assessment evaluates resistance to current threats including credential stuffing, SIM swapping, phishing, and device emulation
Recommendations from assessments are tracked through to implementation with evidence of control improvements
Assessment includes analysis of authentication-related fraud cases and security incidents from the review period
Benchmarking against industry standards and peer institution practices in Malaysian banking
commonGaps: No formal authentication effectiveness assessment conducted; reliance on general penetration testing only
Assessment does not evaluate resistance to current threat vectors specific to Malaysian digital banking
Recommendations from previous assessments remain unimplemented without documented risk acceptance
Assessment does not analyse actual authentication-related fraud and incident data
suggestedSources: IT Security assessment reports
Fraud analytics team incident analysis
Authentication platform vendor security advisories

auditTips: Prepare a clear mapping of transaction types to risk tiers and corresponding authentication requirements to demonstrate the risk-based approach
Have evidence ready showing MFA is enforced for specific high-risk transactions, not just at login
Ensure the authentication effectiveness assessment explicitly addresses current threats relevant to Malaysian digital banking (SIM swap, social engineering)
Prepare authentication failure rate and fraud correlation data to demonstrate monitoring of authentication effectiveness
Be ready to explain how adaptive authentication rules have been updated in response to new threats identified in the assessment period

12.5.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.5.title: Digital Service Delivery Risk Mitigation
12.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that high-value and high-risk transactions are verified through an independent channel from the initiation channel, that customers see full transaction details before confirming, and that verification mechanisms cannot be easily bypassed or intercepted.

evidenceItems.name: Transaction verification policy and procedures
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining which transactions require out-of-band verification, acceptable verification channels, and customer notification requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines specific monetary thresholds and transaction types requiring out-of-band verification (e.g., transfers above RM10,000, new payee registration)
Acceptable independent verification channels are explicitly listed (SMS OTP, push notification, hardware token) with channel independence requirements
Customer transaction detail display requirements are specified including amount, payee name, and account number
Policy is approved by CISO and Head of Digital Banking, reviewed within the last 12 months
Procedures for handling verification failures and customer disputes are documented
commonGaps: No clearly defined monetary or risk thresholds for triggering out-of-band verification
Verification channel is not truly independent (e.g., in-app verification for in-app transactions)
Policy does not specify what transaction details must be shown to the customer during verification
Missing procedures for handling verification delivery failures and customer escalations
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
Digital banking product procedures
Transaction processing system documentation

evidenceItems.name: Out-of-band verification mechanism configuration
evidenceItems.description: Technical configuration evidence showing the implementation of independent channel verification for high-risk transactions
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration showing verification triggers linked to defined monetary thresholds and high-risk transaction types
Evidence of channel independence (e.g., SMS/push sent via separate infrastructure from the transaction channel)
Time-limited verification codes with maximum validity period (e.g., 5 minutes) and single-use enforcement
Cryptographic binding of transaction details to verification codes preventing modification after verification request
commonGaps: Verification uses the same channel as transaction initiation (e.g., in-app OTP for mobile banking transactions)
Verification codes have excessively long validity periods or can be reused
Transaction details are not cryptographically bound to verification codes, allowing modification between verification and execution
No configuration evidence for automatic transaction cancellation when verification expires
suggestedSources: Transaction verification system configuration
OTP/push notification gateway configuration
Digital banking platform security settings

evidenceItems.name: Transaction verification flow documentation
evidenceItems.description: Flow diagrams and technical documentation showing the end-to-end transaction verification process with channel independence highlighted
whatGoodLooksLike: Flow diagrams clearly showing transaction initiation channel and verification channel as separate, independent paths
Sequence diagrams illustrating the full verification lifecycle from trigger to execution or cancellation
Channel independence architecture showing separate infrastructure for transaction and verification channels
Edge cases documented including verification timeout, delivery failure, and concurrent transaction handling
commonGaps: Flow diagrams exist but do not clearly demonstrate channel independence
Documentation shows the happy path only without error handling and edge case flows
Architecture does not demonstrate true infrastructure separation between channels
Missing documentation of fallback mechanisms when the primary verification channel is unavailable
suggestedSources: Digital banking solution architecture documentation
Transaction processing system design documents
Integration architecture team documentation

evidenceItems.name: Customer notification evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that customers receive complete transaction details through the independent verification channel before final authorisation
whatGoodLooksLike: Sample verification messages showing full transaction details (amount, payee name, account number, reference) displayed to the customer
Customer journey screenshots showing transaction summary screen with verification prompt before final authorisation
Transaction logs showing verification challenge issuance with transaction details and customer confirmation timestamps
Evidence of real-time transaction notifications sent to customers after successful verification and execution
commonGaps: Verification messages contain only OTP codes without transaction details for customer verification
Transaction details shown are incomplete (e.g., amount shown but payee name masked or missing)
No post-transaction notification to alert customers of completed transactions
Customer confirmation screen does not display all relevant transaction details before final execution
suggestedSources: Digital banking application screenshots and user flow documentation
SMS/push notification gateway message templates
Transaction processing system notification logs

auditTips: Prepare flow diagrams that clearly demonstrate channel independence between transaction initiation and verification for each digital channel
Have sample verification messages ready showing that full transaction details are included, not just OTP codes
Be prepared to demonstrate how the system handles verification failures, timeouts, and delivery issues
Ensure configuration evidence shows time-limited and single-use verification codes with automatic transaction cancellation on expiry
Prepare evidence of threshold review history showing how verification triggers have been updated based on fraud trend analysis

12.6.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.6.title: Customer Fraud Risk Empowerment
12.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that session management controls include automatic timeouts, secure token generation, session fixation prevention, and concurrent session controls. They will specifically check that session tokens are not transmitted in URLs and are properly invalidated upon logout.

evidenceItems.name: Session management policy and standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal standards defining session management requirements including timeout values, token handling, and concurrent session policies for each digital channel
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards define specific timeout values (idle and absolute) for each digital channel with justification based on risk assessment
Secure session token requirements specified including minimum entropy, HTTP-only and Secure flags, and prohibition of URL-based transmission
Session fixation prevention requirements documented including token regeneration triggers
Concurrent session policies defined per channel with clear rules for session displacement or blocking
Standards approved by CISO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Standards define generic timeout values without channel-specific differentiation or risk-based justification
No explicit prohibition of session tokens in URLs or requirement for HTTP-only and Secure cookie flags
Session fixation prevention not addressed in standards
Concurrent session controls not defined, allowing unlimited simultaneous sessions
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
Digital banking platform security documentation
Application security standards library

evidenceItems.name: Session timeout configuration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Configuration exports demonstrating session timeout values implemented in production for all digital channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration exports from each digital channel showing idle timeout (e.g., 5-15 minutes) and absolute timeout (e.g., 30-60 minutes) values
Evidence that timeout values align with the approved session management standards
Server-side timeout enforcement evidence showing sessions are invalidated server-side, not just client-side
Different timeout configurations for different risk contexts (e.g., shorter timeouts for transaction-enabled sessions)
commonGaps: Timeout configured client-side only with no server-side enforcement, allowing bypass
Excessively long timeout values (e.g., 60+ minutes idle timeout) without risk-based justification
Same timeout values applied across all channels without risk-based differentiation
No absolute timeout configured, allowing sessions to persist indefinitely with activity
suggestedSources: Application server configuration (e.g., web.xml, application.properties)
Digital banking platform session configuration dashboards
Load balancer and reverse proxy session configuration

evidenceItems.name: Session security testing results
evidenceItems.description: Security testing results specifically validating session management controls including token security, fixation resistance, and timeout enforcement
whatGoodLooksLike: Penetration test results covering session fixation, session hijacking, and concurrent session bypass test cases
Verification that session tokens are not exposed in URLs, browser history, or server logs
Testing confirms session invalidation on logout with server-side verification
Evidence of session token entropy analysis confirming cryptographically secure generation
Testing covers all digital channels with channel-specific session management test cases
commonGaps: Session management testing limited to timeout verification without testing fixation, hijacking, or token security
Testing performed on internet banking only without covering mobile banking session management
Session token entropy and randomness not tested or verified
No test case verifying server-side session invalidation on logout
suggestedSources: Penetration testing vendor reports (session management section)
Internal application security testing results
Automated security scanning tool outputs (Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP)

evidenceItems.name: Secure token implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating secure session token generation, transmission, and storage practices across all digital channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence of cryptographically secure random number generator (CSPRNG) usage for session token generation with minimum 128-bit entropy
Cookie configuration showing HTTP-only, Secure, and SameSite flags enabled for all session cookies
Verification that session tokens are never transmitted in URL parameters or query strings
Session token regeneration evidence upon authentication and privilege changes
Token binding or device binding mechanisms for additional session security
commonGaps: Session tokens generated with predictable patterns or insufficient entropy
HTTP-only or Secure flags not set on session cookies, allowing client-side script access
Session tokens found in URL parameters in web server access logs
No session token regeneration after authentication, enabling fixation attacks
suggestedSources: Application framework session configuration (Spring Security, Express.js, ASP.NET)
HTTP response header analysis from security testing tools
Source code review of session management implementation

auditTips: Prepare configuration exports showing session timeout values for each digital channel with clear mapping to the approved standards
Have HTTP response headers readily available showing Secure, HTTP-only, and SameSite flags on session cookies
Ensure penetration test reports include specific session management test cases covering fixation, hijacking, and token analysis
Be prepared to demonstrate server-side session invalidation on logout, not just client-side cookie deletion
Prepare evidence showing concurrent session controls are configured and enforced across all digital channels

12.7.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.7.title: Customer Fraud Awareness Programmes
12.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that real-time fraud detection systems are deployed for digital banking transactions, employing behavioural analytics and anomaly detection. They will look for evidence of detection effectiveness metrics, investigation procedures, and the ability to flag and block suspicious transactions in real time.

evidenceItems.name: Fraud detection system architecture and configuration
evidenceItems.description: Architecture documentation and configuration evidence for the real-time fraud detection system deployed across digital banking channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagram showing real-time fraud detection integrated into the transaction processing pipeline with sub-second decision latency
Configuration evidence showing detection rules cover digital banking channels including internet banking, mobile banking, and e-wallet transactions
Integration points documented with transaction monitoring, anti-money laundering, and SIEM platforms
System capacity and performance metrics showing the system handles peak transaction volumes without degradation
Failover and resilience architecture ensuring fraud detection remains operational during system failures
commonGaps: Fraud detection operates in batch mode rather than real-time, analysing transactions after completion
Detection coverage excludes some digital channels such as QR payments or e-wallet transactions
No documented integration with SIEM or anti-money laundering systems for correlated risk analysis
Missing capacity planning evidence for fraud detection systems during peak transaction periods
suggestedSources: Fraud detection platform documentation (e.g., NICE Actimize, SAS, Featurespace)
Enterprise architecture repository
IT operations capacity management reports

evidenceItems.name: Fraud detection rules and models documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of fraud detection rules, machine learning models, and behavioural analytics employed by the fraud detection system
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented rule library categorised by fraud type (account takeover, social engineering, card-not-present, authorised push payment)
Machine learning model documentation including training data scope, feature engineering, and model performance metrics
Behavioural analytics profiles documented showing what customer behaviour baselines are monitored (transaction patterns, device usage, login times)
Rule and model change history showing regular updates based on fraud trend analysis
Threshold calibration records showing how detection sensitivity is balanced against false positive rates
commonGaps: Detection relies solely on static rules without behavioural analytics or machine learning as required by RMiT
Rules have not been updated in over 12 months and do not address current fraud patterns in Malaysian digital banking
No documentation of model performance metrics or threshold calibration rationale
Behavioural analytics scope is limited to transaction amount only without considering device, location, or timing patterns
suggestedSources: Fraud operations team rule documentation
Data science team model documentation and performance reports
Fraud detection platform rule management interface

evidenceItems.name: Fraud alert monitoring and investigation records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that fraud alerts are monitored, investigated, and resolved in a timely manner with documented outcomes
whatGoodLooksLike: Alert monitoring dashboard showing 24/7 coverage with response time SLAs for different alert severity levels
Investigation records showing structured triage, analysis, and resolution for flagged transactions
Evidence of real-time transaction blocking for confirmed fraud cases with customer notification records
Escalation records showing timely escalation of confirmed fraud to management and regulatory reporting where required
Case management system records demonstrating end-to-end investigation lifecycle tracking
commonGaps: Alerts generated but not monitored outside business hours, creating gaps in coverage
Investigation records are informal (email-based) without structured case management
No evidence of real-time transaction blocking capability; all suspicious transactions processed and reviewed after the fact
Missing regulatory reporting evidence for confirmed fraud cases exceeding BNM notification thresholds
suggestedSources: Fraud case management system (e.g., NICE Actimize, SAS Case Management)
Security operations centre alert dashboards
Regulatory fraud reporting submissions

evidenceItems.name: Fraud detection effectiveness metrics
evidenceItems.description: Regular reports on fraud detection system effectiveness including detection rates, false positive rates, and financial impact metrics
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly effectiveness reports showing detection rate (percentage of fraud caught), false positive rate, and mean time to detect
Financial impact metrics including amounts blocked, losses prevented, and actual fraud losses for the reporting period
Trend analysis comparing current period metrics against previous quarters and industry benchmarks
Model performance metrics including precision, recall, and F1 scores for machine learning models
Reports reviewed by senior management with documented actions for any performance concerns
commonGaps: No formal effectiveness metrics tracked; effectiveness claims based on anecdotal evidence
High false positive rates (above 95%) indicating poor rule calibration and alert fatigue risk
No comparison against industry benchmarks or previous period performance
Effectiveness reports not reviewed by senior management or audit committee
suggestedSources: Fraud operations team quarterly reports
Data science team model performance dashboards
