Management information system (MIS) fraud reporting

auditTips: Prepare quarterly fraud detection effectiveness metrics demonstrating detection rates, false positive management, and financial impact for the past 12 months
Have evidence ready showing that fraud detection operates in real time within the transaction processing flow, not as a post-transaction batch process
Ensure investigation records demonstrate structured case management with clear triage, investigation, and resolution workflows
Be prepared to explain how detection rules and models have been updated in response to fraud trends specific to Malaysian digital banking
Prepare evidence of 24/7 alert monitoring coverage and escalation procedures for after-hours fraud detection

12.8.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.8.title: Customer Account Suspension and Kill Switch
12.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether digital customer onboarding processes include appropriate identity verification, liveness detection, document verification, and fraud screening controls that are proportionate to the risk level of the products offered. Evidence of control effectiveness and alignment with BNM e-KYC guidelines will be examined.

evidenceItems.name: Digital onboarding security standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal standards defining security control requirements for digital customer onboarding processes, differentiated by product risk tier
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards define onboarding security requirements by product risk tier (e.g., basic savings vs. credit facilities) with proportionate controls
Identity verification, liveness detection, document verification, and fraud screening requirements are explicitly specified
Alignment with BNM e-KYC guidelines and AMLATFPUAA requirements is documented
Standards cover both mobile and web-based onboarding channels
Document approved by CISO and Head of Compliance, reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Standards apply the same verification requirements to all products regardless of risk level
Liveness detection not addressed in standards despite being explicitly mentioned in RMiT
No alignment mapping to BNM e-KYC guidelines or anti-money laundering requirements
Standards cover only one onboarding channel without addressing mobile-specific considerations
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
Compliance department e-KYC documentation
Digital banking onboarding project documentation

evidenceItems.name: Identity verification process documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the end-to-end digital identity verification process including MyKad/identity document validation, biometric comparison, and sanctions screening
whatGoodLooksLike: Process flow diagrams showing the complete digital identity verification journey from document capture to verification decision
Integration with JPN (Jabatan Pendaftaran Negara) or equivalent identity validation services documented
Facial recognition comparison process with match score thresholds and manual review triggers
Sanctions, PEP, and adverse media screening integration with documented hit review procedures
Error handling and manual fallback procedures documented for verification failures
commonGaps: Identity verification relies solely on OCR document capture without backend validation against government databases
No facial recognition comparison between submitted photo and identity document
Sanctions and PEP screening not integrated into the digital onboarding flow
Manual review process for edge cases not documented or resourced
suggestedSources: Digital onboarding vendor documentation (Jumio, Onfido, iProov)
Compliance team KYC process documentation
Identity verification system integration architecture

evidenceItems.name: Liveness detection and document verification evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that liveness detection and document verification controls are implemented and effective against spoofing attempts
whatGoodLooksLike: Liveness detection technology documentation showing resistance to photo, video, and 3D mask attacks with testing evidence
Document verification capabilities including watermark detection, hologram validation, and MRZ/barcode cross-checking
Vendor-provided liveness detection accuracy metrics (false acceptance rate and false rejection rate)
Regular testing results showing liveness detection bypass resistance against current spoofing techniques
Integration evidence showing liveness and document verification are mandatory steps in the onboarding flow
commonGaps: Basic liveness detection (e.g., blink detection only) that is easily spoofed by video replay
Document verification limited to OCR without authenticity checks for watermarks, holograms, or security features
No regular testing of liveness detection against evolving spoofing techniques
Liveness detection implemented but can be bypassed through alternative onboarding paths
suggestedSources: Liveness detection vendor documentation and accuracy reports
Document verification vendor capability documentation
Internal security testing reports for onboarding controls

evidenceItems.name: Fraud screening integration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that fraud screening databases and risk signals are integrated into the digital onboarding process to detect and prevent fraudulent applications
whatGoodLooksLike: Integration evidence showing real-time fraud screening during onboarding including device risk scoring, velocity checks, and fraud database lookups
Connection to industry fraud databases (e.g., CTOS, RAM, internal fraud blacklists) for applicant screening
Onboarding fraud detection rules documented with evidence of regular review and update
Fraud screening decision audit trail showing screening results for each onboarding application
Metrics showing fraud screening effectiveness including application rejection rates and false positive analysis
commonGaps: Fraud screening performed only after account opening rather than integrated into the onboarding decision flow
No device risk scoring or velocity checks during digital onboarding
Fraud databases are checked but results are not integrated into the automated onboarding decision
No metrics tracked for onboarding fraud screening effectiveness
suggestedSources: Fraud screening platform configuration (e.g., LexisNexis, TransUnion, CTOS)
Digital onboarding application backend logs
Fraud operations team onboarding screening reports

auditTips: Prepare a product risk tier matrix showing how onboarding security controls scale with product risk level
Have liveness detection testing evidence ready demonstrating resistance to common spoofing attacks (photo, video, mask)
Ensure alignment with BNM e-KYC guidelines is explicitly documented and demonstrable
Prepare onboarding fraud metrics including application rejection rates, fraud detection rates, and false positive analysis
Be ready to demonstrate the end-to-end onboarding verification flow including fallback procedures for verification failures

12.9.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.9.title: Post-Incident Customer Protection
12.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that QR code payments and e-wallet services are protected against tampering, overlay attacks, and forgery. They will look for QR code validation mechanisms, transaction encryption, integrity controls, and evidence of security testing specifically targeting QR and e-wallet attack vectors.

evidenceItems.name: QR code and e-wallet security standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal security standards for QR code-based payments and e-wallet services covering validation, encryption, and anti-tampering requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Standards cover QR code generation, validation, display security, and transaction processing for both merchant-presented and consumer-presented QR codes
Anti-tampering and anti-overlay requirements explicitly defined for e-wallet applications
Alignment with PayNet DuitNow QR specifications and Malaysian Payment Systems Act requirements documented
Encryption requirements for QR transaction data at rest and in transit specified
Standards approved by CISO and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Standards address general digital channel security but lack QR-specific and e-wallet-specific requirements
No reference to PayNet DuitNow QR security specifications
Anti-overlay and anti-screen-capture requirements not addressed for e-wallet applications
Standards do not differentiate between merchant-presented and consumer-presented QR code security requirements
suggestedSources: IT Security policy repository
PayNet DuitNow QR technical specifications
E-wallet product security documentation

evidenceItems.name: QR code validation implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that QR codes are cryptographically validated before processing payment transactions
whatGoodLooksLike: QR code generation process documented showing cryptographic signing of QR code data to ensure integrity and authenticity
Validation logic evidence showing QR codes are verified against the signing authority before processing transactions
Merchant QR code registration process documented with validation of merchant identity and account details
Evidence of QR code expiry mechanisms preventing replay of time-sensitive QR codes
Error handling for invalid or tampered QR codes documented with user notification flow
commonGaps: QR codes contain plain text data without cryptographic integrity protection
No merchant QR code registration process, allowing anyone to generate payment QR codes
QR codes have no expiry mechanism, enabling indefinite reuse of intercepted codes
Invalid QR codes are rejected but no security alert is generated for potential tampering detection
suggestedSources: QR payment platform technical documentation
Merchant onboarding system QR registration records
Payment gateway QR validation configuration

evidenceItems.name: Transaction encryption and integrity controls
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that QR code and e-wallet transactions are encrypted end-to-end with integrity verification throughout the transaction lifecycle
whatGoodLooksLike: End-to-end encryption evidence showing transaction data encrypted from customer device to payment processing backend using TLS 1.2+ and application-layer encryption
Transaction integrity verification mechanisms documented including digital signatures or HMAC on transaction payloads
Evidence of secure key management for QR transaction encryption and signing keys
Payment tokenisation implementation evidence replacing sensitive account data in QR transaction flows
commonGaps: TLS encryption at transport layer only without application-layer encryption of sensitive transaction data
No transaction integrity verification allowing potential modification of transaction details in transit
Encryption keys stored in application configuration without proper key management procedures
Sensitive account numbers transmitted in plain text within QR code data without tokenisation
suggestedSources: Payment platform encryption architecture documentation
Key management system configuration
Security testing reports covering transaction encryption validation

evidenceItems.name: Security testing results for QR and e-wallet features
evidenceItems.description: Security testing results from assessments specifically targeting QR code payment and e-wallet security controls
whatGoodLooksLike: Penetration testing scope includes QR code tampering, substitution, and replay attack test cases
E-wallet application testing covers overlay attack simulations and screen-capture prevention validation
Testing validates QR code validation logic including handling of modified, expired, and forged QR codes
Results from security assessments performed by qualified assessors with QR payment security expertise
All findings remediated before production release with retesting evidence
commonGaps: General mobile app security testing performed but no QR-specific or e-wallet-specific attack scenarios tested
Overlay attack resistance not tested for the e-wallet application
QR code tampering and substitution attack scenarios not included in penetration testing scope
Security testing performed at initial launch only without retesting for subsequent feature updates
suggestedSources: Penetration testing vendor reports (QR/e-wallet section)
Mobile application security testing reports
Internal security team QR-specific test results

auditTips: Prepare evidence showing QR code cryptographic validation is implemented, not just format validation
Have security testing reports ready that specifically cover QR tampering, overlay attacks, and e-wallet-specific threat scenarios
Ensure alignment with PayNet DuitNow QR specifications is documented and demonstrable
Prepare evidence of both merchant-presented and consumer-presented QR code security controls
Be ready to demonstrate the end-to-end encrypted transaction flow from QR scan to payment completion

13.1.title: Technology Audit Scope and Frequency
13.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a risk-based IT audit plan exists covering all critical technology domains, that it has been formally approved by the audit committee, and that it is executed by qualified IT auditors. They will check the risk assessment methodology underpinning audit priorities and the completeness of coverage across cybersecurity, IT operations, change management, access control, and business continuity.

evidenceItems.name: IT audit plan document (annual)
evidenceItems.description: The approved annual IT audit plan showing planned audits, scope, timeline, resource allocation, and risk-based prioritisation across all critical technology domains
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual plan covers all critical technology domains mandated by RMiT: cybersecurity, IT operations, change management, access control, and business continuity
Each planned audit has defined scope, objectives, methodology, timeline, and assigned qualified auditors
Risk-based prioritisation is clearly evidenced with higher-risk domains receiving more frequent or deeper coverage
Plan includes coverage of digital banking channels, cloud services, and third-party technology providers
Plan document shows version control, preparation date, and formal audit committee approval
commonGaps: Audit plan covers traditional IT domains but omits digital banking, cloud, or third-party technology assessments
No evidence of risk-based prioritisation; all domains receive identical audit frequency and depth
Plan lacks resource allocation details making it unclear whether sufficient qualified auditors are assigned
Audit plan has not been updated to reflect new technology deployments or emerging risk areas
suggestedSources: Internal audit department annual planning documentation
Audit management system (TeamMate, AuditBoard, Galvanize)
Head of Internal Audit planning files

evidenceItems.name: Audit committee approval records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating formal audit committee review and approval of the annual IT audit plan
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit committee meeting minutes showing discussion and formal approval of the IT audit plan with resolution number and date
Presentation materials submitted to the audit committee showing the plan, risk rationale, and resource requirements
Evidence of audit committee challenge and input on the plan scope and priorities
Approval records for any mid-year amendments to the audit plan with rationale for changes
commonGaps: Audit plan presented to audit committee for information only without formal approval resolution
Meeting minutes do not specifically record discussion or approval of the IT audit plan as a separate agenda item
No evidence of audit committee input or challenge on IT audit plan priorities and coverage
Mid-year changes to the audit plan made without audit committee approval
suggestedSources: Board secretariat records and committee meeting minutes
Audit committee meeting papers and presentations
Internal audit department records of committee submissions

evidenceItems.name: Risk-based audit scoping methodology
evidenceItems.description: Documented methodology explaining how technology risk assessments are used to determine IT audit priorities, scope, and frequency
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology document describes how technology risk factors (inherent risk, control effectiveness, regulatory impact) are scored and weighted
Risk assessment results for the current audit cycle clearly showing how scores translate into audit priorities
Methodology references the institution's enterprise risk framework and aligns with IPPF (IIA) standards
Evidence of annual methodology review and update to incorporate new risk factors and technology changes
Coverage analysis showing percentage of the IT audit universe addressed by the current year plan
commonGaps: No documented risk-based methodology; audit plan appears to be based on historical rotation without risk assessment
Risk assessment performed but the link between risk scores and audit plan priorities is not documented
Methodology has not been updated to address current technology risks such as cloud, API, and digital banking
No coverage analysis showing gaps between the total IT audit universe and the current year plan
suggestedSources: Internal audit methodology documentation
IT risk assessment reports and registers
Internal audit quality assurance documentation

evidenceItems.name: Audit plan coverage analysis
evidenceItems.description: Analysis showing the completeness of IT audit plan coverage across all critical technology domains and the IT audit universe
whatGoodLooksLike: Coverage matrix mapping all auditable technology entities in the IT audit universe to planned audit activities and cycle frequencies
Gap analysis identifying any critical technology domains not covered in the current year plan with risk-based justification
Multi-year coverage view showing how the full IT audit universe is addressed over a rolling 3-year cycle
Coverage comparison against RMiT requirements demonstrating all mandated technology domains are addressed
commonGaps: No formal IT audit universe defined making coverage analysis impossible
Coverage analysis limited to the current year without multi-year view of universe coverage
Critical technology domains such as digital banking or cloud infrastructure not included in the audit universe
No mapping of audit plan coverage to specific RMiT clause requirements
suggestedSources: Internal audit planning documentation and audit universe register
Audit management system coverage reports
Risk-based audit planning worksheets

auditTips: Prepare the audit committee approval records showing formal approval (not just noting) of the IT audit plan with clear resolution and date
Have the risk-based scoping methodology ready with a clear linkage between risk scores and audit plan priorities
Ensure the audit plan coverage analysis maps to all critical technology domains specified in RMiT clause 13.1
Prepare evidence of quarterly progress reporting to the audit committee showing execution against the approved plan
Be ready to explain how the audit plan was adjusted for any new technology deployments or emerging risks during the year

13.2.title: Annual Technology Audit Plan
13.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the IT audit function is structurally independent from IT management, reports directly to the audit committee, and has unrestricted access to all systems and records. They will scrutinise reporting lines, access rights, and any situations where audit independence may have been compromised.

evidenceItems.name: Audit independence policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy establishing the independence of the IT audit function from IT management and the activities it audits
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly states that internal audit function is independent from IT management with no dotted or solid reporting line to CTO/CIO
Conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit auditors from auditing areas where they previously held operational responsibilities within a defined cooling-off period
Policy defines the audit function's right to unrestricted access to all systems, records, and personnel
Policy aligned with IIA International Standards and approved by the audit committee
Annual independence declaration requirement documented for all IT audit personnel
commonGaps: No formal independence policy; independence is assumed but not documented
Policy exists but does not specifically address IT audit independence from IT management
No conflict-of-interest provisions or cooling-off period requirements for auditors with previous IT operational roles
Policy does not explicitly guarantee unrestricted access to systems and records
suggestedSources: Internal audit department policy repository
Audit committee charter and governance documents
Human resources organisational policies

evidenceItems.name: Audit function reporting line documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the internal audit function reports directly to the audit committee, not through IT or operational management
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart showing Head of Internal Audit reporting directly to the Board Audit Committee with a dotted administrative line to CEO only
Audit committee charter explicitly stating that internal audit reports functionally to the audit committee
Evidence of direct communication between Head of Internal Audit and audit committee without management filtering
Performance evaluation of Head of Internal Audit conducted by or with input from the audit committee, not by management
Evidence of private sessions between internal audit and the audit committee without management present
commonGaps: Head of Internal Audit reports to CFO or CRO rather than directly to the audit committee
Reporting line shown correctly on paper but in practice all audit reports go through management before reaching the committee
No evidence of private sessions between internal audit and the audit committee
Performance evaluation of Head of Internal Audit conducted solely by management without audit committee involvement
suggestedSources: HR organisational charts and reporting structure documentation
Audit committee charter and terms of reference
Board secretariat records of audit committee sessions

evidenceItems.name: Audit access rights documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of IT audit personnel's access rights to systems, records, and personnel necessary for audit activities
whatGoodLooksLike: Access rights register showing IT audit personnel have read-only access to all critical technology systems, databases, and log repositories
Evidence that audit access is provisioned independently from IT operations team approval processes
Access logs showing audit personnel exercising their access rights during audit engagements
Documented process for requesting additional access when needed for specific audits without IT management gatekeeping
No instances of audit access requests being denied or delayed by IT management
commonGaps: Audit access requires approval from IT management who may have conflicts of interest in granting access to systems they manage
Audit personnel lack direct access to critical systems and must request data extracts from IT teams being audited
Access rights are granted on a per-audit basis rather than maintained as standing rights
No records of access requests or evidence that access is truly unrestricted as required by RMiT
suggestedSources: Identity and access management system (IAM) records
IT audit access request and provisioning records
Internal audit department access rights register

evidenceItems.name: Audit committee charter covering IT audit
evidenceItems.description: Audit committee charter with specific provisions covering IT audit oversight, independence, and reporting requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter explicitly includes oversight of IT audit within the audit committee's mandate and responsibilities
IT audit independence provisions embedded in the charter including direct reporting and unrestricted access guarantees
Charter defines audit committee's role in approving IT audit plan, reviewing IT audit results, and monitoring finding remediation
Charter reviewed and approved by the board within the last 12 months
Provisions for the audit committee to engage external IT audit specialists when needed
commonGaps: Charter covers financial audit oversight but does not specifically address IT audit responsibilities
No provisions for IT audit independence or unrestricted access in the charter
Charter does not define the audit committee's role in IT audit plan approval or finding review
Charter has not been updated to reflect RMiT requirements for IT audit oversight
suggestedSources: Board secretariat governance document repository
Audit committee charter and terms of reference
Corporate governance documentation

auditTips: Prepare the organisation chart clearly showing the Head of Internal Audit reporting to the audit committee, not through IT management
Have evidence of private audit committee sessions without management present to demonstrate genuine independence
Ensure access rights documentation shows standing read-only access to critical systems without requiring IT management approval
Prepare annual independence declarations from all IT audit personnel demonstrating no conflicts of interest
Be ready to address any instances where audit independence may have been challenged and how they were resolved

13.3.title: Technology Audit Competency
13.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether IT auditors hold relevant professional certifications, maintain current knowledge of technology risks, and participate in continuous professional development. Expect requests for certification records, CPD logs, and training plans for all IT audit personnel.

evidenceItems.name: IT auditor certification and qualification records
evidenceItems.description: Records of professional certifications and relevant qualifications held by all IT audit personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete register showing each IT auditor's professional certifications (CISA, CISSP, CRISC, CISM, CIA) with certification dates and expiry status
100% of senior IT auditors hold at least one relevant professional certification
Certification verification evidence (e.g., ISACA registry confirmation) for each claimed certification
Relevant academic qualifications documented for all audit team members
Gap analysis showing any certification deficiencies and plans to address them
commonGaps: No centralised register of IT auditor certifications; records scattered across HR files
Some IT auditors lack relevant professional certifications with no plan for obtaining them
Certifications have lapsed due to missed CPD requirements with no monitoring process
No verification of claimed certifications against issuing body registries
suggestedSources: Human resources certification tracking records
ISACA, ISC2, IIA professional registry confirmations
Internal audit department personnel records

evidenceItems.name: Continuous professional development records
evidenceItems.description: Records tracking CPD hours and professional development activities completed by each IT audit team member
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual CPD logs for each IT auditor showing training courses, conferences, self-study, and professional activities completed
Each IT auditor meets minimum CPD requirements (e.g., 40 hours annually, 20 hours in IT-related topics)
CPD activities cover current technology risks including cloud security, cybersecurity threats, digital banking, and regulatory developments
Records maintained in a structured format with dates, topics, providers, and hours for each activity
CPD completion tracked against certification body requirements (ISACA, ISC2)
commonGaps: CPD records are incomplete or not systematically tracked
CPD activities focus on general audit topics without sufficient coverage of technology-specific subjects
Some auditors fall short of minimum CPD requirements without documented remediation plans
No evidence that CPD topics align with the institution's technology risk profile and audit plan requirements
suggestedSources: Internal audit department training records
HR learning management system (LMS) records
Professional certification body CPD portals (ISACA, ISC2, IIA)

evidenceItems.name: Auditor competency assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Results from periodic competency assessments evaluating IT auditors' technical knowledge and audit methodology proficiency
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual competency assessment results for each IT auditor covering technical knowledge, audit methodology, and professional skills
Assessment framework includes evaluation criteria for cybersecurity knowledge, IT risk assessment, data analytics, and regulatory awareness
Individual development plans created based on assessment results with specific actions and timelines
Assessment results reviewed by Head of Internal Audit with sign-off on development plans
Trend analysis showing competency improvements year over year
commonGaps: No formal competency assessment process for IT auditors; reliance on annual performance reviews only
Assessment criteria are generic and do not evaluate technology-specific competencies
Assessment results do not lead to targeted development plans for individual skill gaps
No tracking of competency trends to identify systemic team-wide capability gaps
suggestedSources: Internal audit department performance management records
Competency assessment frameworks and scoring templates
HR performance management system

evidenceItems.name: Training plan for IT audit personnel
evidenceItems.description: Annual training and development plan identifying training needs, planned activities, and certification targets for the IT audit team
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual training plan aligned with competency assessment findings and the technology risk landscape
Plan covers emerging technology areas relevant to the institution (cloud, digital banking, API security, AI/ML)
Budget allocation documented for external training, conferences, and certification exam fees
Mix of training methods including formal courses, on-the-job mentoring, conference attendance, and self-directed learning
Training plan approved by Head of Internal Audit with execution tracked quarterly
commonGaps: No formal training plan; training is ad hoc and reactive rather than planned and proactive
Training plan does not address emerging technology risks relevant to the institution's current and planned technology deployments
Insufficient budget allocation leading to cancelled or deferred training activities
Training plan not linked to competency assessment results, resulting in generic training rather than targeted skill development
suggestedSources: Internal audit department annual planning documentation
HR training and development records
Internal audit budget documentation

auditTips: Prepare a summary table showing each IT auditor, their certifications, CPD hours completed, and competency assessment results for quick examiner review
Ensure CPD records demonstrate coverage of technology-specific topics, not just general audit methodology training
Have the annual training plan ready with evidence of execution against plan, including any deviations and reasons
Be prepared to explain how competency gaps are identified and addressed through targeted development activities
If external specialists were engaged for technically complex audits, have the engagement records and qualification evidence ready

13.4.title: Technology Audit Advisory Role
13.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a systematic process exists for tracking IT audit findings to remediation completion, that management provides action plans with target dates, and that overdue findings are escalated to senior management and the audit committee. They will review the finding register, ageing analysis, and escalation records.

evidenceItems.name: Audit finding tracking process documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure for tracking IT audit findings from issuance through remediation to closure
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure defines the complete finding lifecycle: issuance, action plan agreement, progress monitoring, closure verification, and reporting
Finding severity classification criteria defined (critical, high, medium, low) with corresponding remediation timelines
Escalation triggers and timelines documented for overdue findings at each severity level
Closure verification requirements specified including independent validation for high-risk findings
Procedure approved by Head of Internal Audit and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: No formal follow-up procedure; finding tracking is ad hoc and dependent on individual auditor initiative
Escalation triggers and timelines not defined, leading to inconsistent escalation of overdue findings
Closure verification criteria not specified, allowing management self-certification without independent validation
Procedure does not define reporting requirements to the audit committee for finding status
suggestedSources: Internal audit department methodology documentation
Audit management system (TeamMate, AuditBoard) workflow configuration
Internal audit quality assurance documentation

evidenceItems.name: Audit finding register with action plans and target dates
evidenceItems.description: Complete register of all IT audit findings with management action plans, assigned owners, target dates, and current status
whatGoodLooksLike: Register captures all IT audit findings with unique identifiers, severity ratings, descriptions, and root causes
Each finding has a management action plan with specific remediation steps, assigned owner, and realistic target date
Current status is maintained with progress notes and evidence of ongoing monitoring
Ageing analysis available showing findings by age, severity, and technology domain
Register is accessible to audit committee members and updated at least monthly
commonGaps: Finding register exists but action plans are vague (e.g., 'will address') without specific remediation steps or accountable owners
Target dates are unrealistic or repeatedly extended without documented justification
Register not consistently updated, with stale status information for many findings
No ageing analysis or trend reporting available from the register
suggestedSources: Audit management system (TeamMate, AuditBoard, Galvanize) finding register
Internal audit department finding tracking spreadsheets (if no dedicated system)
Audit committee reporting packs

evidenceItems.name: Overdue finding escalation records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that overdue IT audit findings are escalated to senior management and the audit committee as required
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented escalation records showing overdue findings escalated to senior management within defined timelines
Audit committee meeting minutes showing discussion of overdue findings with management responses recorded
Evidence of management action following escalation, such as additional resource allocation or revised remediation plans
Escalation tracking showing resolution of previously escalated items
Systematic escalation across all overdue findings, not just selected high-profile items
commonGaps: Overdue findings exist but no evidence of formal escalation to senior management or audit committee
Escalation is informal (verbal or email) without documented records
Escalation occurs selectively for high-profile findings only, with many overdue items remaining unescalated
No evidence that escalation results in management action or accelerated remediation
suggestedSources: Internal audit escalation records and correspondence
Audit committee meeting minutes and reporting packs
Senior management meeting minutes addressing audit findings

evidenceItems.name: Audit finding closure evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that completed IT audit finding remediation actions have been verified before closure
whatGoodLooksLike: Closure evidence package for each remediated finding including description of actions taken and supporting documentation
Independent verification by internal audit for all high and critical findings confirming effective remediation
Before-and-after evidence showing the control gap has been resolved (e.g., configuration changes, policy updates, process improvements)
Closure sign-off by the auditor who raised the finding or a designated follow-up auditor
Retesting evidence for findings that require technical validation of remediation effectiveness
commonGaps: Findings closed based on management assertion only without independent verification by internal audit
Closure evidence is perfunctory (e.g., email stating 'done') without substantive proof of remediation
No retesting performed for technical findings where verification of control effectiveness is necessary
Closure process inconsistent with some findings closed without any evidence and others requiring extensive documentation
suggestedSources: Audit management system closure records
Internal audit follow-up testing work papers
Management-provided remediation evidence packages

auditTips: Prepare the finding register with current ageing analysis showing findings by severity, age, and technology domain
Have escalation records ready demonstrating systematic escalation of all overdue findings, not just selected items
Ensure closure evidence for high-risk findings includes independent verification by internal audit, not just management sign-off
Prepare trend analysis showing finding volumes, closure rates, and average remediation times across audit cycles
Be ready to explain any findings that have been open for extended periods with documented justification for delays

14.1.title: Data Centre Resilience Assessment
14.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that independent security assessments are obtained for all critical third-party service providers at least annually, that assessments cover security controls, data protection, and contractual compliance, and that results are reviewed by the risk management function. They will request the vendor register, assessment reports, and risk management review records.

evidenceItems.name: Third-party assessment policy and schedule
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining requirements for independent security assessments of third-party service providers and the assessment schedule for critical vendors
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines assessment frequency requirements by vendor criticality tier (minimum annual for critical providers)
Assessment scope requirements cover security controls, data protection, incident response, and business continuity
Criteria for qualifying independent assessors are defined (e.g., CREST, ISO 27001 lead auditor, Big Four)
Annual assessment schedule showing all critical vendors with planned assessment dates and status
Policy approved by CISO and Chief Risk Officer, reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: No formal policy; vendor assessments performed ad hoc without defined frequency or scope requirements
Assessment policy does not define what constitutes an 'independent' assessor, allowing vendor self-assessments to be accepted
Assessment schedule does not cover all critical vendors or has significant gaps in coverage
Policy does not define assessment scope requirements, leading to inconsistent and incomplete assessments
suggestedSources: Vendor management office policy repository
IT Security third-party risk management documentation
Procurement and vendor management procedures

evidenceItems.name: Independent security assessment reports for critical vendors
evidenceItems.description: Security assessment reports from independent assessors for each critical third-party service provider
whatGoodLooksLike: Independent assessment reports available for 100% of critical vendors within the last 12 months
Reports produced by qualified independent assessors (not the vendor's own internal audit or parent company audit)
Assessment scope covers security controls, data protection practices, and compliance with contractual security requirements
Reports include findings with severity ratings, recommendations, and vendor management responses
SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 audit reports, or equivalent independent assurance reports for each critical vendor
commonGaps: Assessment reports missing for some critical vendors or reports older than 12 months
Vendor-provided self-assessment questionnaires accepted in lieu of independent security assessments
Assessment scope does not cover the specific services provided to the institution or the institution's data
Reports provided are SOC 2 Type I (point-in-time) rather than Type II (period-of-time) for operational effectiveness
suggestedSources: Vendor management office assessment report repository
Third-party risk management platform (e.g., BitSight, SecurityScorecard, OneTrust)
Vendor-provided SOC 2/ISAE 3402 reports and ISO 27001 certificates

evidenceItems.name: Risk management review records of assessments
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the risk management function has formally reviewed third-party security assessment results and documented risk acceptance decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk management review records for each critical vendor assessment showing review date, reviewer, and risk assessment conclusions
Risk acceptance documentation for any identified control gaps with compensating controls and residual risk classification
Evidence of risk management challenging vendor responses to assessment findings where remediation plans are insufficient
Risk management recommendations tracked through to implementation or formal acceptance by appropriate authority
Quarterly risk management reporting on third-party risk posture to senior management
commonGaps: Assessment reports received by vendor management but not formally reviewed by the risk management function
Risk management review is perfunctory (rubber-stamp) without substantive analysis of assessment findings
No documented risk acceptance for vendor control gaps; risks are implicitly accepted without formal decision
Risk management recommendations not tracked or followed up for implementation
suggestedSources: Risk management department vendor risk assessment records
Third-party risk management platform risk assessment outputs
Risk management committee meeting minutes and reporting packs

evidenceItems.name: Contractual security requirement compliance evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that third-party security assessments evaluate vendor compliance with contractual security requirements specific to the institution
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment reports include specific evaluation of vendor compliance against contractual security requirements and SLAs
Contractual security requirements clearly defined in vendor agreements with measurable compliance criteria
Compliance matrix mapping contractual requirements to assessment findings for each critical vendor
Non-compliance instances documented with vendor remediation plans and agreed timelines
Annual compliance reports from vendors addressing all contractual security obligations
commonGaps: Assessment scope covers general vendor security but does not evaluate compliance with institution-specific contractual requirements
Vendor contracts lack specific measurable security requirements making compliance assessment impossible
No compliance matrix linking contractual requirements to assessment results
Vendor non-compliance with contractual security requirements not formally documented or addressed
suggestedSources: Legal department vendor contract repository
Vendor management office compliance tracking records
Third-party assessment reports (contractual compliance section)

auditTips: Prepare the critical vendor register with assessment dates showing 100% coverage within the last 12 months for all critical providers
Ensure independent assessment reports are from truly independent assessors, not the vendor's own audit function or parent company
Have risk management review records ready for each assessment demonstrating substantive analysis, not just acknowledgement
Prepare a compliance matrix mapping contractual security requirements to assessment findings for your most critical vendors
Be ready to explain the process for handling vendors who refuse to provide independent assessment reports or cooperate with assessments

14.2.title: Network Resilience Assessment
14.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that critical third-party providers are required to maintain recognised security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, or equivalent), that certification status is actively monitored, and that certification lapses trigger risk reassessments. They will request the certification tracking register, monitoring evidence, and any lapse response records.

evidenceItems.name: Third-party certification requirements in contracts
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that vendor contracts include mandatory security certification requirements for critical third-party service providers
whatGoodLooksLike: Contract clauses explicitly requiring maintenance of ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, or equivalent certifications as a condition of engagement
Certification requirements specified for all critical vendor contracts with clear definition of acceptable certifications
Contractual obligation for vendors to notify the institution immediately of any certification status changes or lapses
Consequences of certification lapse defined in contract including right to reassess, additional monitoring, or termination
Certification requirements apply to all critical vendors including those onboarded before the policy was established (through contract renewal)
commonGaps: Vendor contracts do not include specific security certification requirements
Certification requirements included in newer contracts but legacy contracts lack these provisions
Contract specifies certification at time of engagement but no ongoing maintenance requirement
No contractual obligation for vendors to notify the institution of certification changes or lapses
suggestedSources: Legal department vendor contract repository
Procurement standard contract templates and schedules
Vendor management office contract review records

evidenceItems.name: Vendor certification tracking register
evidenceItems.description: Centralised register tracking the security certification status of all critical third-party service providers
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all critical vendors with certification type, certifying body, certificate number, scope, issue date, and expiry date
Register updated within the last quarter with evidence of regular review and maintenance
Certification scope verified to ensure it covers the services provided to the institution
Register includes certification verification evidence (e.g., certifying body confirmation or accredited registry check)
Dashboard view available showing certifications approaching expiry within 90 days
commonGaps: No centralised certification tracking; certification information scattered across vendor files
Register exists but is incomplete, missing some critical vendors or lacking expiry date tracking
Certification scope not verified; vendor holds ISO 27001 but the certificate scope excludes the services provided to the institution
Register not regularly updated; contains stale or inaccurate certification information
suggestedSources: Vendor management office certification tracking system
Third-party risk management platform (OneTrust, Archer, ServiceNow VRM)
Vendor management spreadsheets and databases

evidenceItems.name: Certification lapse monitoring evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that vendor certification status is actively monitored on an ongoing basis with alerts for certifications approaching expiry
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated monitoring system or calendar-based alerting configured for 90-day, 60-day, and 30-day pre-expiry notifications
Evidence of proactive vendor engagement when certifications approach expiry, requesting renewal confirmation
Monitoring covers all critical vendors without exceptions
Records of vendor responses confirming certification renewal or explaining delays
Monitoring frequency is at minimum quarterly for all critical vendors
commonGaps: No proactive monitoring; certification lapses discovered only during annual vendor reviews
Monitoring relies on vendor self-reporting without independent verification
Automated alerts configured but not actioned when triggered
Monitoring excludes some critical vendors, particularly smaller or specialist providers
suggestedSources: Vendor management system alert and notification logs
Vendor correspondence records regarding certification renewal
Calendar or task management system certification review reminders

evidenceItems.name: Risk reassessment records for certification lapses
evidenceItems.description: Records of risk reassessments triggered by vendor security certification lapses, including interim controls and service continuity decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk reassessment report for each certification lapse event including assessment of increased risk exposure
Interim compensating controls documented and implemented during the certification gap period
Escalation to senior management for decision on continued engagement with uncertified vendor
Documented decision outcome (continued engagement with conditions, enhanced monitoring, service transition planning, or termination)
Follow-up tracking showing certification reinstatement or agreed alternative assurance measures
commonGaps: No risk reassessment performed when vendor certifications lapse; business as usual continues
Reassessment performed informally without documented analysis or senior management involvement
No interim compensating controls identified or implemented during the certification gap period
No documented decision on whether to continue engagement with the uncertified vendor
suggestedSources: Risk management department vendor risk reassessment records
Vendor management office lapse response documentation
Senior management meeting minutes addressing vendor certification issues

auditTips: Prepare the vendor certification tracking register with current status for all critical vendors, highlighting any approaching expiries
Have contract extracts ready showing certification maintenance clauses for your top critical vendors
If any certification lapses have occurred, prepare the complete risk reassessment package including interim controls and senior management decisions
Ensure certification scope verification is documented, showing that vendor certifications cover the relevant services
Be ready to explain the process for legacy vendors whose contracts predate the certification requirement policy and how you are bringing them into compliance

15.1.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Education
15.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal security awareness programme exists covering all personnel categories, that mandatory annual training is completed with high participation rates, and that completion is tracked with evidence of management reporting.

evidenceItems.name: Security awareness program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Formal, board-approved security awareness programme document defining scope, objectives, target audience, content areas, delivery methods, and annual training calendar
whatGoodLooksLike: Programme document is version-controlled with clear approval signatures from CISO and board risk committee
Scope explicitly covers employees, contractors, and third-party personnel with system access
Content areas include cybersecurity risks, data protection (PDPA), social engineering, and institutional security policies
Annual review cycle is documented with evidence of content updates based on emerging threats
commonGaps: Programme document exists but has not been updated for over two years
Scope excludes contractors or third-party personnel with system access
No board or senior management approval documented on the programme
suggestedSources: Information Security department programme files
Board risk committee meeting minutes showing programme approval
LMS platform (Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors, KnowBe4)

evidenceItems.name: Annual training content and materials
evidenceItems.description: Actual training modules, slides, videos, and assessment materials used for the annual security awareness training cycle
whatGoodLooksLike: Training content is current and references recent threat trends relevant to Malaysian financial institutions
Materials include interactive elements and post-training knowledge assessments with minimum passing scores
Content is available in Bahasa Malaysia and English to ensure accessibility across all staff
Modules cover all required topics: cyber risks, data protection, security policies, and incident reporting
commonGaps: Training content is generic vendor material not tailored to the institution's environment or Malaysian regulatory context
No post-training assessment to verify knowledge retention
Content has not been refreshed since initial deployment
suggestedSources: LMS platform content repository (KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness)
Information Security team training material archives
HR Learning and Development department

evidenceItems.name: Training completion tracking records
evidenceItems.description: Detailed records showing individual completion status for all personnel required to complete security awareness training, including timestamps and assessment scores
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual-level records with name, employee ID, personnel category (employee/contractor/third-party), completion date, and assessment score
Records reconciled against the full population of personnel with system access to identify non-completions
Automated tracking via LMS with tamper-evident audit trails
Non-completion escalation records showing follow-up actions taken
commonGaps: Tracking is limited to employees and excludes contractors or third-party personnel
Manual spreadsheet tracking with no audit trail or reconciliation against HR records
No mechanism to identify personnel who joined mid-year and missed the training cycle
suggestedSources: LMS platform completion reports (Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors)
HR system personnel roster for reconciliation
Third-party vendor management system for contractor records

evidenceItems.name: Training completion rate reports
evidenceItems.description: Management reports showing aggregate training completion rates by department, personnel category, and trend over time, presented to senior management or board
whatGoodLooksLike: 95%+ completion rate across all personnel categories with clear breakdown by department and staff type
Trend analysis showing year-over-year improvement in completion rates and assessment scores
Reports presented to board risk committee with documented discussion and action items for low-performing areas
Clear identification of non-compliant personnel with documented escalation and remediation actions
commonGaps: Completion rate below 90% with no documented escalation or remediation plan
Reports generated but no evidence of presentation to senior management or board
No breakdown by personnel category, masking low completion rates among contractors
suggestedSources: LMS platform analytics and reporting module
Board risk committee meeting packs and minutes
CISO quarterly reporting dashboard

auditTips: Prepare a reconciliation between the total number of personnel with system access (from HR and vendor management systems) and the number who completed training to demonstrate full coverage
Ensure training completion records include contractors and third-party personnel, not just permanent employees, as BNM specifically requires coverage of all personnel with system access
Have board risk committee meeting minutes readily available showing the most recent presentation of training completion metrics and any resulting action items
Demonstrate that training content has been reviewed and updated within the past 12 months with evidence of changes reflecting current threat landscape
Prepare a sample of post-training assessment results to show that knowledge retention is being measured, not just course completion

15.2.title: Technology Staff Training
15.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that phishing simulations are conducted at least quarterly, that personnel who fail receive follow-up training, and that metrics are tracked over time and reported to management demonstrating measurable improvement.

evidenceItems.name: Phishing simulation program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documented phishing simulation procedure defining simulation types, frequency, target population, scenario design approach, follow-up training requirements, and escalation for repeat offenders
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure is version-controlled and approved by CISO with clear annual review date
Defines minimum quarterly frequency with provisions for additional simulations targeting high-risk departments
Specifies scenario categories including email phishing, spear phishing, and business email compromise
Includes escalation pathway for personnel who fail multiple consecutive simulations
commonGaps: Procedure exists but does not specify minimum quarterly frequency as required by BNM
No defined escalation process for repeat offenders
Scope limited to head office staff, excluding branch and remote personnel
suggestedSources: Information Security department procedure repository
Phishing simulation platform documentation (KnowBe4, Proofpoint, Cofense)
CISO office programme files

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly simulation execution records
evidenceItems.description: Records of each quarterly phishing simulation execution including date, scenario description, target population, and aggregate results
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear evidence of at least four simulations per year with distinct dates in separate quarters
Each simulation record includes scenario type, number of targets, click rate, credential submission rate, and reporting rate
Target population covers all personnel with email access, not just a subset
Simulation scenarios vary across quarters to test different social engineering techniques
commonGaps: Only two or three simulations conducted in the past 12 months, falling short of quarterly requirement
Simulations target only a random sample rather than the full population
No variation in scenario types across quarters, reducing effectiveness of the programme
suggestedSources: Phishing simulation platform campaign reports (KnowBe4, Proofpoint, Cofense)
Information Security team simulation execution logs
Email gateway logs confirming simulation delivery

evidenceItems.name: Follow-up training records for failed simulations
evidenceItems.description: Records showing that personnel who failed phishing simulations were assigned and completed follow-up training within the required timeframe
whatGoodLooksLike: Individual records linking specific simulation failures to assigned follow-up training modules
Completion timestamps showing follow-up training was finished within 10 business days of simulation results
Follow-up training content is targeted to the specific failure type (e.g., link clicking, credential submission)
Records of escalation for personnel who failed follow-up training or failed multiple simulations
commonGaps: Follow-up training assigned but completion not tracked or enforced
Significant delay between simulation failure and follow-up training assignment (over 30 days)
Generic follow-up training not tailored to the specific failure type
suggestedSources: LMS platform follow-up training assignment and completion records
Phishing simulation platform remediation module reports
HR escalation records for repeat offenders

evidenceItems.name: Phishing simulation metrics and trend reports
evidenceItems.description: Management reports showing phishing simulation metrics over time including click rates, reporting rates, repeat failure rates, and trend analysis presented to senior management
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly trend showing declining click rates and increasing phishing reporting rates over a 12-month period
Metrics broken down by department, role, and personnel category to identify high-risk groups
Benchmarking against industry averages for Malaysian financial services sector
Evidence of board risk committee presentation with documented discussion and action items
commonGaps: Metrics collected but no trend analysis or management reporting
Reports show persistently high click rates (above 15%) with no documented remediation action plan
No benchmarking against industry norms to contextualise performance
suggestedSources: Phishing simulation platform analytics dashboard (KnowBe4, Proofpoint)
CISO quarterly reporting pack to board risk committee
Board risk committee meeting minutes

auditTips: Prepare a calendar view showing simulation dates across all four quarters to immediately demonstrate compliance with the quarterly requirement
Have follow-up training completion records pre-linked to specific simulation failures to show the remediation loop is closed
Prepare trend charts showing click rate reduction over time as this is a key indicator BNM examiners use to assess programme effectiveness
Ensure records demonstrate that simulations cover the full email-enabled population, not just a sample or subset of departments
Document any escalation actions taken for repeat offenders to demonstrate that consequences exist for persistent non-compliance

15.3.title: Board Technology Training
15.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has identified specialised roles requiring tailored training, whether training content is differentiated by role and updated for emerging threats, and whether completion is tracked by role category.

evidenceItems.name: Role-based training program documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documented programme identifying specialised roles, mapped security competencies, training requirements per role, and the annual training calendar for role-specific modules
whatGoodLooksLike: Programme clearly identifies IT administrators, developers, security analysts, and senior management as distinct training cohorts
Each role has mapped security competencies with defined learning objectives and minimum proficiency standards
Training frequency and delivery method specified for each role category
Programme is reviewed and updated annually with version control and approval signatures
commonGaps: Programme exists only for IT security staff but excludes developers and senior management
No mapping of specific competencies to roles; all specialised staff receive the same training
Programme document has not been reviewed or updated in over 18 months
suggestedSources: CISO office programme documentation
HR Learning and Development department training framework
IT department role classification matrix

evidenceItems.name: Role-specific training materials and curricula
evidenceItems.description: Actual training content tailored to each specialised role, including secure coding training for developers, system hardening for administrators, threat hunting for analysts, and cyber risk governance for senior management
whatGoodLooksLike: Distinct training modules for each role category with content relevant to that role's specific responsibilities
Developer training includes OWASP Top 10, secure coding practices, and code review techniques
IT administrator training covers system hardening, patch management, and privileged access security
Senior management training addresses cyber risk governance, incident response decision-making, and regulatory obligations
commonGaps: All specialised roles receive identical generic cybersecurity training with no role differentiation
Developer secure coding training not provided despite in-house software development
Senior management receives no tailored training on their governance responsibilities
suggestedSources: LMS platform course catalogue (Cornerstone, Pluralsight, SANS)
Secure coding training platform (HackEDU, Secure Code Warrior)
External training provider certificates and course materials

evidenceItems.name: Training completion records by role
evidenceItems.description: Training completion records segmented by role category showing which specialised personnel have completed their required role-specific training modules
whatGoodLooksLike: Completion records grouped by role category with individual-level detail including completion date and assessment score
High completion rates (90%+) across all specialised role categories
Records reconciled against the current list of personnel in each specialised role
Assessment scores demonstrating competency attainment above defined thresholds
commonGaps: Completion tracked at aggregate level only, with no breakdown by specialised role
Several specialised roles show completion rates below 70% with no documented follow-up
New personnel in specialised roles not assigned training promptly after role assignment
suggestedSources: LMS platform role-based completion reports
HR system role classification records for reconciliation
Department heads confirming personnel role assignments

evidenceItems.name: Training content update records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that role-based training content has been reviewed and updated to reflect emerging threats, recent incidents, and technology changes
whatGoodLooksLike: Version history showing content updates within the past 12 months for each role-specific module
Change log referencing specific emerging threats or incidents that prompted content updates
Evidence of subject matter expert review and sign-off on updated content
Incorporation of lessons learned from the institution's own security incidents
commonGaps: Training content unchanged for over two years despite significant threat landscape evolution
No formal review process or change log for training material updates
Content updates driven solely by vendor schedule rather than institutional threat intelligence
suggestedSources: LMS platform content version history
Information Security team training content review records
Threat intelligence reports used to inform content updates

auditTips: Prepare a role-to-training mapping matrix showing which specific training modules are assigned to each specialised role category
Have sample training content available for each role to demonstrate genuine differentiation, not just relabelled generic material
Ensure completion records can be filtered by role category so examiners can quickly assess coverage for IT administrators, developers, security analysts, and senior management separately
Prepare evidence of content update history including the specific threats or incidents that triggered changes
If senior management training is delivered via briefings rather than formal courses, ensure attendance records and briefing materials are documented

16.1.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.1.title: Digital Services Notification Requirement
16.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the institution has a defined notification procedure with clear materiality criteria, whether past incidents were reported within the mandatory four-hour window, and whether detailed follow-up reports were submitted within fourteen days.

evidenceItems.name: BNM incident notification procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining the end-to-end process for notifying BNM of material technology and cybersecurity incidents, including materiality criteria, escalation paths, approval authorities, and submission channels
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure clearly defines materiality thresholds for customer impact, financial loss, data breach scope, and service disruption duration
End-to-end process mapped with specific time allocations to ensure the four-hour deadline is met
Named roles and alternates for each step including out-of-hours contact details
Procedure has been tested through tabletop exercises with documented results
commonGaps: Materiality criteria are vague or undefined, leading to inconsistent reporting decisions
Procedure exists but has never been tested through simulation exercises
No out-of-hours escalation contacts defined, creating risk of missed deadlines for incidents detected outside business hours
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department procedure repository
Incident response plan documentation
CISO office operational procedures

evidenceItems.name: Incident notification template
evidenceItems.description: Pre-approved notification template containing all fields required by BNM including incident nature, affected systems, estimated impact, initial response actions, and contact details
whatGoodLooksLike: Template includes all mandatory fields specified by BNM notification requirements
Pre-populated fields for institution details, contact information, and submission metadata
Guidance notes embedded within the template to assist rapid completion under pressure
Template aligned with BNM LINK portal submission format where applicable
commonGaps: Template is a generic incident report form not tailored to BNM's specific requirements
Required fields missing such as estimated customer impact or initial response actions
Template not accessible within the incident management system, requiring manual retrieval
suggestedSources: Incident management system template repository (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy)
Regulatory Affairs department document repository
BNM LINK portal notification format guidance

evidenceItems.name: Sample incident notification records
evidenceItems.description: Actual BNM notification submissions for past material incidents with timestamps demonstrating compliance with the four-hour notification deadline
whatGoodLooksLike: Timestamps showing notification submitted within four hours of incident detection for every material incident
Notification content covers all required fields: nature, affected systems, impact estimate, and initial response
BNM acknowledgement or receipt confirmation for each notification submitted
Clear audit trail from incident detection to notification submission with timestamped intermediate steps
commonGaps: BNM notification submitted five or more hours after incident detection, exceeding the four-hour requirement
Notification content incomplete with missing impact assessment or response actions
No BNM acknowledgement retained as proof of timely submission
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Incident management system notification workflow logs
Regulatory Affairs department correspondence files

evidenceItems.name: Follow-up report records
evidenceItems.description: Detailed follow-up reports submitted to BNM within fourteen days of the initial notification, covering root cause analysis, full impact assessment, remediation actions, and lessons learned
whatGoodLooksLike: Follow-up report submitted within fourteen days of initial notification for every material incident
Report contains comprehensive root cause analysis, not just a restatement of the initial notification
Remediation actions are specific with assigned owners and target completion dates
Lessons learned section identifies systemic improvements to prevent recurrence
commonGaps: Follow-up report submitted beyond the fourteen-day deadline without documented extension approval from BNM
Root cause analysis is superficial, stating only proximate cause without identifying systemic factors
Remediation actions are generic with no specific owners or timelines
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department follow-up report files
BNM LINK portal submission records
Incident management system post-incident review documentation

auditTips: Prepare a timeline for each past material incident showing detection time, classification time, notification preparation time, and BNM submission time to demonstrate four-hour compliance
If no material incidents occurred during the audit period, present tabletop exercise results demonstrating the institution's ability to meet the four-hour deadline
Ensure the materiality criteria document is readily available as examiners will test whether the institution's classification decisions are consistent and defensible
Have BNM acknowledgement records or LINK portal submission confirmations available as proof of timely notification
Prepare a register of all notifications with follow-up report submission dates to demonstrate compliance with the fourteen-day follow-up requirement

16.2.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.2.title: First-Time Digital Services Notification
16.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has defined criteria for major projects, that notifications were submitted at least three months before implementation, and that notifications include adequate project scope, risk assessment, and implementation timelines.

evidenceItems.name: Technology project notification procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining criteria for identifying major technology projects requiring BNM notification, notification content requirements, approval process, and three-month advance submission workflow
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear, measurable criteria for major project identification covering customer impact, operational significance, investment threshold, and technology risk
Step-by-step notification process with named roles and specific time allocations ensuring three-month advance submission
Mandatory Compliance Officer sign-off integrated into project approval gates
Procedure reviewed and updated annually with version control
commonGaps: Major project criteria are subjective with no quantitative thresholds, leading to inconsistent classification
Notification procedure exists but is not integrated into the project approval workflow
No designated Compliance Officer checkpoint in the project approval process
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department procedure repository
Project Management Office (PMO) governance documentation
Compliance department policy files

evidenceItems.name: Project notification records to BNM
evidenceItems.description: Actual BNM project notification submissions with submission dates, project details, and BNM acknowledgement records demonstrating three-month advance compliance
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification submissions show dates at least three months prior to planned go-live for every major project
Each notification includes complete project scope, risk assessment summary, and implementation timeline
BNM acknowledgement or receipt confirmation retained for each submission
Register of all notifications maintained with tracking of BNM feedback or conditions
commonGaps: Notification submitted less than three months before implementation, breaching the advance requirement
Major projects implemented without any BNM notification due to failure to apply classification criteria
No BNM acknowledgement records retained as proof of submission
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Regulatory Affairs department correspondence files
PMO project portfolio register

evidenceItems.name: Major project risk assessments
evidenceItems.description: Risk assessments prepared for major technology projects and submitted as part of BNM notifications, covering technology, operational, customer impact, and implementation risks
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessments follow a structured methodology covering technology, operational, customer, and implementation risk dimensions
Identified risks have documented mitigation strategies with assigned owners
Risk assessments are signed off by the CRO or risk management function
Assessments are specific to the project, not generic templates with minimal customisation
commonGaps: Risk assessments are superficial checklists rather than substantive analysis of project-specific risks
No sign-off from the risk management function on project risk assessments
Mitigation strategies are generic without project-specific detail
suggestedSources: Risk Management department project risk assessment files
PMO project documentation repository
CRO office risk assessment records

evidenceItems.name: Three-month advance notification evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentary evidence demonstrating the timeline between BNM notification submission and project implementation date, proving compliance with the three-month advance requirement
whatGoodLooksLike: Side-by-side comparison of notification submission date and actual implementation date showing three-month or greater gap
Project timeline documents corroborating the planned and actual implementation dates
Evidence that implementation did not commence until the three-month notification period had elapsed
Any BNM conditions or feedback received during the notification period documented and addressed
commonGaps: Project implementation commenced before the three-month notification period elapsed
Notification date documented but implementation date not verifiable from project records
BNM feedback received but not documented or addressed before implementation
suggestedSources: PMO project timeline and go-live records
BNM LINK portal submission timestamps
Change management system implementation records

auditTips: Prepare a timeline view for each major project showing notification date versus implementation date to immediately demonstrate three-month compliance
Cross-reference the project portfolio register with BNM notification records to confirm no major projects were implemented without notification
Have the major project classification criteria document ready, as examiners will test whether classification decisions are consistent across projects
If any projects were implemented with less than three months advance notification, prepare a documented explanation and any BNM dispensation obtained
Ensure risk assessments submitted to BNM are substantive and project-specific, not generic templates

16.3.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.3.title: Simplified Enhancement Notification
16.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that material technology outsourcing arrangements were notified to BNM before contracts were executed, that notifications include adequate risk assessments and oversight mechanisms, and that materiality criteria are clearly defined and consistently applied.

evidenceItems.name: Outsourcing notification procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining materiality criteria for technology outsourcing, notification content requirements, risk assessment standards, and the end-to-end process from materiality assessment to BNM submission
whatGoodLooksLike: Materiality criteria cover service criticality, customer data exposure, operational dependency, concentration risk, and substitutability
Notification content requirements aligned with BNM expectations including scope, risk assessment, and oversight mechanisms
Mandatory Compliance Officer sign-off integrated into procurement and vendor onboarding workflows
Procedure reviewed annually with evidence of updates reflecting regulatory guidance changes
commonGaps: Materiality criteria do not address concentration risk or single-provider dependency
