Procedure is documented but not integrated into procurement workflows, allowing bypass
No Compliance Officer checkpoint in the outsourcing approval process
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department procedure repository
Vendor Management Office governance documentation
Procurement department policy files

evidenceItems.name: BNM outsourcing notifications records
evidenceItems.description: Actual BNM notification submissions for material outsourcing arrangements with submission dates, arrangement details, and evidence that notification was made prior to contract execution
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification submission date precedes the contract execution date for every material outsourcing arrangement
Each notification includes complete scope description, risk assessment, and oversight mechanisms
BNM acknowledgement or receipt confirmation retained for each submission
Register of all outsourcing notifications maintained with tracking of BNM feedback
commonGaps: Contract executed before BNM notification was submitted, breaching the prior notification requirement
Material outsourcing arrangements entered into without any BNM notification due to failure in materiality assessment
Notification content incomplete, missing oversight mechanism details
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Regulatory Affairs department correspondence files
Vendor Management Office outsourcing register

evidenceItems.name: Outsourcing risk assessments
evidenceItems.description: Risk assessments prepared for material technology outsourcing arrangements covering operational risk, data security risk, concentration risk, vendor viability, and regulatory compliance risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessments follow a structured methodology covering all relevant risk dimensions for outsourcing
Assessments consider data residency, cross-border data transfer implications under Malaysian law, and PDPA compliance
Risk ratings are justified with supporting analysis, not just assigned without rationale
CRO or risk management function sign-off documented on each assessment
commonGaps: Risk assessments are generic templates with minimal customisation for the specific outsourcing arrangement
Data residency and cross-border transfer risks not addressed despite offshore service delivery
No risk management function involvement in outsourcing risk assessments
suggestedSources: Risk Management department outsourcing risk assessment files
Vendor Management Office due diligence documentation
CRO office risk assessment records

evidenceItems.name: Oversight mechanism documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of oversight mechanisms established for material outsourcing arrangements including service level agreements, monitoring procedures, audit rights, and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Service level agreements with measurable KPIs covering availability, performance, security, and compliance
Regular vendor performance monitoring reports with documented escalation of SLA breaches
Contractual audit rights exercised periodically with documented audit results
Exit and transition provisions documented and tested
commonGaps: SLAs defined but no evidence of regular monitoring or enforcement
Audit rights included in contract but never exercised
No documented exit strategy or transition plan for the outsourced service
suggestedSources: Vendor Management Office contract and SLA repository
Vendor performance monitoring reports
Internal audit vendor assessment reports

auditTips: Prepare a timeline for each material outsourcing arrangement showing BNM notification date versus contract execution date to demonstrate prior notification compliance
Cross-reference the outsourcing register with BNM notification records to confirm no material arrangements were entered into without notification
Have oversight mechanism evidence readily available for each material arrangement, as examiners will assess whether oversight is effective, not just documented
If any arrangements were entered into before BNM notification, prepare a documented explanation and any retrospective notification submitted
Ensure risk assessments address data residency and cross-border transfer implications, which are high-priority areas for BNM in the Malaysian context

16.4.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.4.title: Full Enhancement Notification Requirements
16.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution notified BNM prior to deploying critical systems or customer data to the cloud, that notifications include comprehensive cloud risk assessments with data residency arrangements and shared responsibility models, and that no cloud deployment proceeded without prior notification.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud adoption notification procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining which cloud deployments require BNM notification, notification content requirements, risk assessment standards, and the process ensuring notification precedes deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear criteria for identifying cloud deployments requiring notification: critical systems and customer data processing/storage/transmission
Notification content checklist covering risk assessment, data residency, shared responsibility model, and security controls
Mandatory sign-off gates from CRO, CISO, and Compliance Officer before notification submission
Procedure integrated into the cloud adoption governance framework
commonGaps: Procedure does not clearly define what constitutes a critical system for notification purposes
Cloud deployments by individual business units bypass the central notification procedure
No integration between cloud adoption workflow and regulatory notification process
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department procedure repository
Cloud Centre of Excellence governance documentation
Compliance department policy files

evidenceItems.name: BNM cloud adoption notification records
evidenceItems.description: Actual BNM notification submissions for cloud adoptions with submission dates and evidence that notifications were made prior to cloud deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification submission date precedes cloud deployment date for every notifiable cloud adoption
Each notification includes complete risk assessment, data residency arrangements, and shared responsibility model
BNM acknowledgement or receipt confirmation retained for each submission
Register tracking all cloud notifications with status of BNM feedback or conditions
commonGaps: Cloud services deployed before BNM notification was submitted
Critical systems or customer data migrated to cloud without any BNM notification
Notification content does not include data residency or shared responsibility details
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Regulatory Affairs department correspondence files
Cloud governance team notification register

evidenceItems.name: Cloud risk assessments submitted to BNM
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive cloud risk assessments prepared for BNM notifications covering data security, data residency, vendor lock-in, concentration risk, business continuity, and regulatory compliance
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment follows a structured methodology addressing all cloud-specific risk dimensions
Data residency analysis confirms customer data storage locations and addresses cross-border transfer implications under PDPA
Concentration risk analysis considers dependency on the cloud service provider and impact of provider failure
Risk mitigation strategies are specific and actionable with assigned owners and implementation timelines
commonGaps: Risk assessment is a generic cloud risk checklist not tailored to the specific cloud deployment
Data residency analysis missing or does not address backup data locations and disaster recovery regions
Concentration risk not assessed despite significant dependency on a single cloud provider
suggestedSources: Risk Management department cloud risk assessment files
Cloud Centre of Excellence risk documentation
CRO office risk assessment records

evidenceItems.name: Data residency and shared responsibility documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of data residency arrangements and shared responsibility model mapping for each cloud deployment, showing where customer data resides and which party is responsible for each security domain
whatGoodLooksLike: Data residency documentation specifies primary, backup, and disaster recovery data locations with confirmation they are within approved jurisdictions
Shared responsibility model clearly maps security domains (IAM, encryption, network, patching, monitoring) to either institution or provider
Technical evidence of data residency configuration (cloud region settings, data sovereignty controls)
Cross-border data transfer assessment completed where data may transit through non-Malaysian jurisdictions
commonGaps: Data residency documentation states region but does not address backup or disaster recovery data locations
Shared responsibility model is the generic provider document without institution-specific mapping
No technical validation that data residency configurations match documented arrangements
suggestedSources: Cloud management platform configuration (AWS Organizations, Azure Management Groups, Google Cloud Resource Manager)
Cloud service provider shared responsibility documentation
Cloud architecture team data residency configuration evidence

auditTips: Prepare a timeline for each cloud adoption showing BNM notification date versus deployment date to demonstrate prior notification compliance
Have data residency configuration screenshots or exports from the cloud management console ready to corroborate documented arrangements
Ensure the shared responsibility model mapping is institution-specific, not just the generic cloud provider document
Cross-reference the cloud service inventory with BNM notification records to confirm no critical systems or customer data were deployed without notification
If using multiple cloud providers or regions, ensure data residency documentation covers all deployment locations including backup and disaster recovery sites

16.5.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.5.title: External Assurance Competency
16.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has defined criteria for significant findings, that significant technology audit findings were reported to BNM within thirty days of audit report finalisation, and that management responses include concrete remediation commitments.

evidenceItems.name: Audit finding reporting procedure for BNM
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining the process for identifying, classifying, and reporting significant technology audit findings to BNM within the thirty-day deadline
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure clearly defines the end-to-end process from audit report finalisation to BNM submission
Roles and responsibilities specified for finding classification, report preparation, approval, and submission
Timeline mapped with specific milestones ensuring the thirty-day deadline is met
Procedure reviewed annually and aligned with current BNM reporting expectations
commonGaps: Procedure exists but does not specify the thirty-day deadline or timeline milestones
No clear ownership assigned for BNM audit finding report preparation and submission
Procedure is not integrated into the standard audit report finalisation workflow
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department procedure repository
Regulatory Affairs department policy files
Board Audit Committee charter and terms of reference

evidenceItems.name: Significant finding identification criteria
evidenceItems.description: Documented criteria defining what constitutes a significant technology audit finding requiring BNM reporting, including materiality thresholds and classification guidance
whatGoodLooksLike: Criteria cover material weaknesses in technology risk management, cybersecurity control failures, and regulatory non-compliance
Quantitative and qualitative thresholds defined for finding significance (e.g., control failures affecting critical systems, findings rated High or Critical)
Examples provided to guide consistent classification across different audit teams
Criteria approved by the Head of Internal Audit and aligned with BNM expectations
commonGaps: Criteria are vague, stating only 'material findings' without defining materiality
No distinction between technology audit findings and general operational audit findings in the criteria
Criteria not reviewed or updated to reflect evolving BNM expectations
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department methodology documentation
Audit finding classification framework
Board Audit Committee-approved audit methodology

evidenceItems.name: BNM audit finding notification records
evidenceItems.description: Actual BNM submissions for significant audit findings with submission dates demonstrating compliance with the thirty-day deadline, including finding details and management responses
whatGoodLooksLike: Submission dates within thirty days of audit report finalisation dates for every significant finding reported
Each submission includes finding description, affected systems, risk rating, management response, and remediation timeline
BNM acknowledgement or receipt confirmation retained for each submission
Subsequent updates submitted to BNM on remediation progress where required
commonGaps: Significant findings reported to BNM more than thirty days after audit report finalisation
Significant findings identified by external auditors but not reported to BNM
Management responses in submissions are vague with no specific remediation actions or timelines
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Internal Audit department BNM reporting files
Regulatory Affairs department correspondence records

evidenceItems.name: Thirty-day reporting compliance records
evidenceItems.description: Records tracking the timeline from audit report finalisation to BNM submission for each significant finding, demonstrating compliance with the thirty-day reporting requirement
whatGoodLooksLike: Timeline log for each significant finding showing audit report finalisation date, finding classification date, report preparation date, and BNM submission date
All significant findings submitted within thirty days with no exceptions
Board Audit Committee meeting minutes showing presentation of BNM reporting status
Remediation tracking records for all reported findings showing progress against committed timelines
commonGaps: No timeline tracking between audit report finalisation and BNM submission, making compliance verification impossible
Some significant findings submitted late with no documented justification or BNM communication
Remediation progress not tracked after initial BNM submission
suggestedSources: Audit management system timeline reports (TeamMate, AuditBoard, Galvanize)
Internal Audit department BNM reporting tracker
Board Audit Committee meeting minutes and packs

auditTips: Prepare a timeline table for each significant finding showing the audit report finalisation date and BNM submission date side by side to demonstrate thirty-day compliance
Have the significant finding criteria document readily available, as examiners will test whether the institution's classification decisions are consistent and defensible
Cross-reference the internal audit finding register with BNM notification records to confirm all significant findings were reported
Ensure management responses in BNM submissions include specific remediation actions, assigned owners, and target completion dates, not just acknowledgements
If external audit findings were classified as significant, demonstrate that these were also included in BNM reporting

16.6.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.6.title: Document Provision to Bank
16.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution responded to previous examination findings within specified timeframes, that action plans were developed for all findings with accountable owners, that progress was reported to both the board and BNM, and that the institution cooperated fully during examinations.

evidenceItems.name: Regulatory examination response procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure defining the end-to-end process for receiving, triaging, responding to, and remediating BNM examination findings, including response timelines and escalation paths
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure clearly defines roles for each step: receipt, triage, action plan development, board presentation, response submission, and remediation tracking
Response timeline mapped with milestones ensuring BNM-specified deadlines are met
Escalation procedures defined for findings requiring cross-departmental remediation or significant investment
Procedure tested and refined based on experience from previous examination cycles
commonGaps: Procedure is generic and does not specify response timelines aligned with BNM expectations
No defined escalation path when remediation requires cross-departmental coordination
Procedure has not been updated to reflect lessons learned from previous examinations
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department procedure repository
Compliance department governance documentation
CEO office regulatory engagement protocols

evidenceItems.name: Examination finding action plans
evidenceItems.description: Detailed action plans developed for every BNM examination finding, including specific remediation actions, accountable senior management owners, target completion dates, and required resources
whatGoodLooksLike: Every examination finding has a corresponding action plan with specific, measurable remediation actions
Each action plan has a named senior management owner with clear accountability for delivery
Target completion dates are realistic and aligned with the severity and complexity of each finding
Resource requirements identified and approved for each remediation action
commonGaps: Action plans are generic with vague actions such as 'improve controls' without specific deliverables
No named senior management owner assigned to examination findings
Target completion dates are unrealistic or have been repeatedly extended without justification
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department examination response files
Examination finding tracking system or register
Board meeting papers presenting examination findings and action plans

evidenceItems.name: Progress reports to BNM and board
evidenceItems.description: Regular progress reports on examination finding remediation submitted to BNM and presented to the board, showing status of each action plan, completed actions, and any timeline changes
whatGoodLooksLike: Progress reports submitted to BNM at the frequency specified by the Bank, with finding-by-finding status updates
Board papers showing examination finding remediation progress presented at least quarterly
Status reporting includes percentage completion, milestone achievements, and any revised target dates with justification
Evidence of board discussion and challenge on remediation progress documented in meeting minutes
commonGaps: Progress reports to BNM submitted late or at irregular intervals
Board reporting on examination findings is infrequent or lacks finding-level detail
Timeline extensions not communicated to BNM or justified in progress reports
suggestedSources: BNM correspondence files showing progress report submissions
Board meeting packs and minutes showing examination finding reporting
Examination finding tracking system status reports

evidenceItems.name: Cooperation and access provision records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating full cooperation with BNM examination teams, including timely provision of requested documentation, system access, and personnel availability
whatGoodLooksLike: Log of all BNM information requests with response dates showing timely provision
Records of system access provided to examination teams with appropriate controls
Evidence of dedicated examination workspace and support personnel assigned during examination periods
No outstanding or overdue information requests from BNM examination teams
commonGaps: Significant delays in providing requested documentation to BNM examination teams
No log of information requests and responses to demonstrate cooperation
System access requests handled on an ad-hoc basis without proper tracking
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs department examination coordination files
IT Service Desk access request records for BNM examiners
Document management system retrieval logs

auditTips: Prepare a summary table of all outstanding and recently closed examination findings with their current status, action plan owners, and target dates
Have board meeting minutes available showing presentation and discussion of examination findings, as examiners will verify board-level oversight
Ensure progress reports submitted to BNM are consistent with board reporting to avoid discrepancies that could raise concerns
If any examination finding timelines have been extended, have documented justifications and evidence of BNM communication ready
Demonstrate a culture of cooperation by showing proactive information provision rather than reactive responses to repeated requests

16.7.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.7.title: Immediate Service Offering Upon Notification
16.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the annual technology risk report was submitted on time, that it provides a comprehensive and honest assessment of the institution's technology risk posture, includes all significant incidents with lessons learned, presents meaningful KRIs, and outlines concrete planned initiatives for the coming year.

evidenceItems.name: Annual technology risk report submitted to BNM
evidenceItems.description: The comprehensive annual technology risk report as submitted to BNM, covering risk posture assessment, significant incidents, key risk indicators, and planned initiatives for the coming year
whatGoodLooksLike: Report submitted within BNM's required timeframe with acknowledgement receipt retained
Covers all four required content areas: risk posture, incidents, KRIs, and planned initiatives
Board approval documented prior to submission with evidence in board meeting minutes
Consistent format enabling year-on-year comparison of technology risk posture
commonGaps: Report submitted late or without evidence of BNM receipt acknowledgement
Report covers only two or three of the four required content areas
No evidence of board review and approval before submission to BNM
suggestedSources: BNM LINK portal submission records
Regulatory Affairs department annual report files
Board meeting packs showing report approval

evidenceItems.name: Technology risk posture assessment
evidenceItems.description: Detailed assessment of the institution's current technology risk posture covering infrastructure resilience, cybersecurity maturity, third-party risk, data protection, and regulatory compliance status
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment covers all key technology risk domains with evidence-based ratings
Honest identification of both strengths and areas requiring improvement
Risk ratings supported by quantitative data from vulnerability scans, incident trends, and compliance assessments
Year-on-year comparison showing improvement trajectory or candid explanation of deterioration
commonGaps: Risk posture assessment is overly optimistic, omitting known weaknesses or open audit findings
Assessment based on subjective opinions rather than data-driven metrics
No year-on-year comparison to demonstrate progress or identify regression
suggestedSources: Risk Management department technology risk assessment
CISO cybersecurity maturity assessment reports
Vulnerability management and patch management dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Annual incident summary
evidenceItems.description: Summary of all significant technology and cybersecurity incidents during the reporting period, including categorisation, root causes, impacts, lessons learned, and improvements implemented
whatGoodLooksLike: All significant incidents from the reporting period included with consistent categorisation and severity rating
Root cause analysis documented for each significant incident, not just proximate cause
Lessons learned section identifies systemic improvements implemented as a result of incidents
Incident metrics presented with trend analysis showing year-on-year comparison
commonGaps: Not all significant incidents included in the annual summary, with some omitted or understated
Root cause analysis limited to proximate cause without identifying systemic factors
Lessons learned are generic without evidence of specific improvements implemented
suggestedSources: Incident management system annual reports (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy)
CISO incident trend analysis reports
Post-incident review documentation

evidenceItems.name: Planned initiatives for the coming year
evidenceItems.description: Documented technology risk management initiatives planned for the coming year, including objectives, timelines, resource commitments, and expected risk reduction outcomes
whatGoodLooksLike: Initiatives are specific and actionable with clear objectives, milestones, and timelines
Resource commitments (budget and personnel) documented for each initiative
Initiatives address identified weaknesses from the risk posture assessment and incident lessons learned
Expected outcomes defined in measurable terms enabling future assessment of delivery
commonGaps: Planned initiatives are vague aspirations without specific timelines or resource commitments
Initiatives do not address the weaknesses identified in the risk posture assessment
No progress tracking against previous year's planned initiatives to demonstrate follow-through
suggestedSources: CTO office technology risk initiative planning documents
Annual IT budget and resource allocation papers
Board-approved technology risk strategy and roadmap

auditTips: Prepare both the current year's report and the previous year's report side by side to demonstrate year-on-year comparability and progress tracking
Ensure board meeting minutes show substantive discussion of the annual report before submission, not just a rubber-stamp approval
Cross-reference the incident summary in the annual report with the incident management system records to verify completeness
Review the previous year's planned initiatives and prepare evidence of delivery against those commitments, as BNM may test follow-through
Ensure the risk posture assessment is honest and consistent with known issues; overly optimistic assessments undermine credibility during examinations

17.1.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.1.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.1.title: First-Time Cloud and Emerging Technology Consultation
17.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has a board-approved cloud strategy aligned with overall technology strategy and risk appetite, that governance structures are defined for cloud decisions, and that approved service models and providers are documented with appropriate assessment evidence.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud strategy document
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved cloud strategy document defining the institution's approach to cloud adoption, strategic objectives, approved service models, target workloads, and multi-year roadmap
whatGoodLooksLike: Strategy document is board-approved with documented approval in board meeting minutes
Clearly defines approved cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and deployment models (public, private, hybrid)
Multi-year roadmap with specific milestones and investment plans
Reviewed and updated at least annually to reflect evolving cloud capabilities and regulatory guidance
commonGaps: Cloud strategy exists as an informal technology team document without board approval
Strategy is a generic industry document not tailored to the institution's specific context and risk appetite
No multi-year roadmap or investment plan, indicating ad-hoc rather than strategic cloud adoption
suggestedSources: CTO office strategic planning documentation
Board meeting packs showing cloud strategy approval
Technology strategy alignment documentation

evidenceItems.name: Cloud governance framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented governance framework defining decision rights, roles and responsibilities, approval processes, and oversight mechanisms for cloud adoption and management
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear definition of cloud governance committee or equivalent body with terms of reference and membership
Decision rights matrix specifying approval authorities for different cloud adoption scenarios
Roles and responsibilities defined for cloud architect, security, risk, compliance, and business stakeholders
Integration with existing enterprise governance structures including IT steering committee and board committees
commonGaps: No dedicated cloud governance structure; cloud decisions made within general IT governance without cloud-specific considerations
Decision rights not clearly defined, leading to inconsistent approval processes across departments
Governance framework does not include compliance and risk management stakeholders in cloud decision-making
suggestedSources: Cloud Centre of Excellence governance documentation
IT governance framework documentation
Cloud governance committee meeting minutes and terms of reference

evidenceItems.name: Approved cloud service models and providers
evidenceItems.description: Register of approved cloud service providers and services with documented assessments of security posture, compliance certifications, data residency capabilities, and suitability for institutional use
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive register listing all approved providers with their service offerings, regions, and certifications
Each provider assessed against institutional security, compliance, and data residency requirements
Assessment includes review of provider certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, CSA STAR) relevant to Malaysian regulatory context
Unapproved providers explicitly excluded with documented rationale
commonGaps: No formal approved provider list; cloud services adopted from any provider without assessment
Provider assessments limited to marketing material review without independent verification
Register does not include data residency capabilities or Malaysian data protection compliance
suggestedSources: Cloud management platform provider inventory (AWS Organizations, Azure Management Groups)
Vendor Management Office cloud provider assessment files
Cloud Centre of Excellence approved provider register

evidenceItems.name: Strategy alignment with technology strategy
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating alignment between the cloud strategy and the institution's overall technology strategy, risk appetite framework, and business objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Explicit cross-references between cloud strategy objectives and overall technology strategy goals
Cloud risk appetite statements aligned with and derived from the institutional risk appetite framework
Cloud investment plans integrated into overall technology investment and budget planning
Board or senior management confirmation that cloud strategy is aligned with institutional direction
commonGaps: Cloud strategy developed independently without reference to overall technology strategy
No explicit link between cloud risk appetite and institutional risk appetite framework
Cloud investment managed separately from overall technology budget, indicating strategic misalignment
suggestedSources: CTO office strategy alignment documentation
Risk appetite framework showing cloud-specific risk statements
IT steering committee minutes showing strategy alignment discussions

auditTips: Have the board meeting minutes showing cloud strategy approval readily available, as examiners will verify board-level endorsement
Prepare a mapping document showing how the cloud strategy objectives link to overall technology strategy goals
Ensure the approved provider register is current and includes assessment evidence, not just a list of provider names
Demonstrate that the cloud governance committee or equivalent body is active with regular meeting records and documented decisions
If shadow IT cloud adoption exists, demonstrate what controls have been implemented to identify and bring it under governance

17.2.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.2.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.2.title: Subsequent Cloud and Emerging Technology Notification
17.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has implemented security controls across all five required domains (IAM, data protection, network security, logging, configuration management), that controls are aligned with the shared responsibility model, and that effectiveness is validated through regular assessments.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud security control framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework defining minimum security control requirements across IAM, data protection, network security, logging and monitoring, and configuration management for each cloud service model in use
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework addresses all five security domains with specific control requirements for each
Controls differentiated by cloud service model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) reflecting different shared responsibility boundaries
Framework aligned with industry standards such as CSA Cloud Controls Matrix and CIS Cloud Benchmarks
Annual review cycle with version control showing updates for new cloud services and evolving threats
commonGaps: Framework covers only IAM and encryption, omitting network security, logging, or configuration management
No differentiation of controls by service model; same requirements applied to IaaS and SaaS deployments
Framework has not been updated since initial cloud adoption, missing controls for newly adopted services
suggestedSources: CISO office cloud security policy and standards documentation
Cloud Centre of Excellence security standards repository
Cloud security assessment reports referencing control framework

evidenceItems.name: Shared responsibility model mapping
evidenceItems.description: Documented shared responsibility model mappings for each cloud service provider and service model, clearly assigning control ownership for every security domain between the institution and the provider
whatGoodLooksLike: Separate mapping for each cloud service provider (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) and service model in use
Every security control explicitly assigned to either institution, provider, or shared responsibility
Institutional controls have named owners responsible for implementation and maintenance
Mappings reviewed when new services are adopted or provider shared responsibility models change
commonGaps: Generic provider shared responsibility document used without institution-specific mapping
Shared responsibility mapping exists only at the IaaS level, not for PaaS or SaaS services
No named institutional owners assigned for controls on the institution's side of the boundary
suggestedSources: Cloud provider shared responsibility documentation (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)
Cloud architecture team institutional responsibility mapping
CISO office shared responsibility assessment documentation

evidenceItems.name: Cloud security configuration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence of implemented cloud security controls including IAM configurations, encryption settings, network security rules, logging configurations, and CSPM scan results
whatGoodLooksLike: IAM configuration evidence showing MFA enforcement, least-privilege policies, and privileged access controls
Encryption configuration showing data encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+) for all cloud services
CSPM scan results showing compliance rate above 95% against defined security baselines
Logging configuration showing centralised log aggregation with SIEM integration
commonGaps: MFA not enforced for all cloud accounts, particularly service accounts or API access
Encryption at rest not enabled for all data stores, especially non-production environments containing copies of customer data
CSPM tools not deployed or showing significant non-compliance findings without remediation plans
suggestedSources: Cloud management platform configuration exports (AWS Security Hub, Azure Security Center, Google Cloud Security Command Center)
CSPM tool compliance reports (Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Orca Security)
SIEM cloud log ingestion configuration evidence

evidenceItems.name: Cloud security assessment reports
evidenceItems.description: Reports from regular cloud security assessments including penetration testing, configuration reviews, and compliance scanning that validate the effectiveness of implemented controls
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual penetration testing report specifically targeting cloud environments with cloud-specific attack scenarios
Assessment covers all cloud accounts and services, not just a subset
Findings are risk-rated with remediation recommendations and assigned owners
Evidence of remediation for findings from previous assessments demonstrating continuous improvement
commonGaps: Penetration testing covers only on-premises infrastructure, not cloud environments
Assessment limited to one cloud account while other accounts remain untested
Previous assessment findings remain open without remediation evidence
suggestedSources: External penetration testing firm cloud assessment reports
CSPM continuous compliance scan reports
Internal cloud security review documentation

auditTips: Prepare a control coverage matrix showing which controls are implemented across each of the five required security domains for each cloud service in use
Have CSPM dashboard screenshots or exports ready to demonstrate continuous monitoring and current compliance posture
Ensure penetration testing scope explicitly includes cloud environments; a generic infrastructure test that happens to touch cloud is insufficient
Prepare shared responsibility mapping documents that are institution-specific, showing named owners for every control on the institution's side
If any CSPM findings show non-compliance, have remediation plans and timelines ready to demonstrate active management of security gaps

17.3.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.3.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.3.title: Enhancement Notification Exemption
17.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has documented exit strategies for all cloud services, that architectures are designed to avoid excessive vendor dependency, that data extraction procedures are tested, and that vendor lock-in risks are assessed and reported.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud portability architecture documentation
evidenceItems.description: Architecture documentation demonstrating design decisions that support portability and reduce vendor lock-in, including use of open standards, containerisation, and cloud-agnostic tooling
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams showing use of containers (Kubernetes), infrastructure-as-code (Terraform), and standard APIs
Provider-specific dependencies clearly identified and justified for each cloud deployment
Design decision records showing portability was considered as a requirement during architecture reviews
Comparison of provider-specific versus cloud-agnostic alternatives with documented rationale for choices
commonGaps: Architecture heavily reliant on proprietary provider services with no documentation of portability considerations
No containerisation or infrastructure-as-code adoption despite being available
Provider-specific dependencies not identified or documented
suggestedSources: Cloud architecture team design documentation
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
Infrastructure-as-code repositories (Terraform, CloudFormation)

evidenceItems.name: Cloud exit strategy documents
evidenceItems.description: Documented exit strategies for each cloud service covering data extraction procedures, transition timelines, contingency arrangements, resource requirements, and estimated costs
whatGoodLooksLike: Separate exit strategy for each cloud service or provider with service-specific detail
Data extraction procedures documented with specific steps, tools, formats, and estimated extraction timeframes
Transition timeline covering notification period, data extraction, validation, service migration, and provider decommissioning
Cost and resource estimates for executing the exit strategy
commonGaps: Exit strategies exist only for the primary cloud provider, not for all cloud services in use
Exit strategies are generic templates without service-specific data extraction or transition details
No estimated timeline or cost for executing the exit, making feasibility assessment impossible
suggestedSources: Cloud governance team exit strategy documentation
CTO office cloud provider management files
Vendor Management Office contract exit provisions

evidenceItems.name: Data extraction procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented and tested data extraction procedures for each cloud service, specifying how customer data and institutional data can be fully extracted in standard formats
whatGoodLooksLike: Step-by-step extraction procedures for each cloud data store with specific tools and commands
Data extracted in open, standard formats (CSV, JSON, Parquet) rather than proprietary formats
Evidence of annual testing with documented results confirming data completeness and integrity post-extraction
Extraction timeline validated against exit strategy requirements
commonGaps: Data extraction procedures documented but never tested
Data can only be extracted in proprietary formats requiring additional conversion
Testing reveals data completeness issues that have not been resolved
suggestedSources: Cloud architecture team data migration documentation
Data extraction test results and validation reports
Disaster recovery team data restoration test records

evidenceItems.name: Vendor lock-in risk assessments
evidenceItems.description: Risk assessments evaluating the degree of vendor dependency for each cloud service, availability of alternatives, migration complexity, and risk mitigation measures
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment covers all cloud services with dependency ratings (low, medium, high, critical)
Factors assessed include proprietary API usage, data format portability, contractual lock-in, and switching costs
Alternative providers identified for high-dependency services with feasibility analysis
Risk mitigation actions documented for services with high vendor lock-in ratings
commonGaps: No formal vendor lock-in risk assessment conducted despite significant cloud usage
Assessment limited to commercial factors (cost) without considering technical lock-in
High lock-in ratings identified but no mitigation actions planned or implemented
suggestedSources: Risk Management department cloud vendor risk assessments
Cloud governance team vendor dependency analysis
Board technology committee papers on cloud concentration risk

auditTips: Prepare a summary table of all cloud services showing their vendor lock-in risk rating and exit strategy status to demonstrate comprehensive coverage
Have data extraction test results available to show that exit procedures have been validated, not just documented
Ensure exit strategy cost and timeline estimates are realistic and defensible, as examiners may challenge unrealistic assumptions
Demonstrate that portability was a design consideration by presenting architecture decision records or design review documentation
If the institution uses proprietary cloud services, prepare a rationale explaining why the lock-in risk was accepted and what mitigations are in place

17.4.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.4.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.4.title: Bank Discretionary Direction
17.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has established governance over AI and ML use, whether customer-facing models are subject to validation for accuracy and bias, whether model decisions are explainable, and whether clear accountability exists for AI-driven outcomes.

evidenceItems.name: AI and ML governance framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented governance framework defining requirements for AI and ML model development, deployment, monitoring, and retirement, covering model risk, data quality, bias, explainability, and accountability
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework covers the full model lifecycle from development through deployment, monitoring, and retirement
Specific requirements for data quality, bias testing, explainability, and accountability clearly articulated
Framework differentiates governance requirements by model risk tier (e.g., customer-facing versus internal-only models)
Reviewed and updated annually to reflect evolving regulatory expectations and industry best practices
commonGaps: No formal AI/ML governance framework; models developed and deployed without structured oversight
Framework addresses model accuracy but omits bias detection and explainability requirements
Framework does not differentiate governance intensity based on model risk or customer impact
suggestedSources: CRO office model risk management documentation
Data Science team governance documentation
Model risk committee terms of reference and meeting records

evidenceItems.name: Model risk management procedures
evidenceItems.description: Detailed procedures for managing model risk across the AI and ML model lifecycle, including development standards, validation requirements, deployment gates, and ongoing monitoring obligations
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures define model risk tiers with corresponding governance requirements for each tier
Independent model validation required before deployment for all customer-facing models
Defined trigger conditions for model re-validation (performance degradation, data drift, material changes)
Clear escalation procedures when models breach performance or fairness thresholds
commonGaps: Model validation performed by the same team that developed the model, lacking independence
No defined triggers for model re-validation; validation is a one-time pre-deployment exercise
Procedures do not address model retirement or decommissioning
suggestedSources: Model risk management function procedures
Data Science team development standards
Model validation reports and review records

evidenceItems.name: Bias detection and explainability documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of bias detection methodologies, fairness testing results, and explainability approaches for each AI model, particularly those affecting customer-facing decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Bias testing results for each customer-facing model showing analysis across protected characteristics (race, gender, age)
Defined fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalised odds) with documented thresholds
Explainability approach documented for each model (SHAP values, LIME, feature importance, decision rules)
Evidence that bias testing is repeated regularly, not just during initial development
commonGaps: No bias testing conducted despite models affecting customer creditworthiness or product eligibility
Bias testing limited to a single protected characteristic without intersectional analysis
Explainability not addressed; model decisions are treated as black boxes
suggestedSources: Data Science team bias testing reports (using Fairlearn, AI Fairness 360, What-If Tool)
Model documentation including explainability methodology
Model risk committee review records of bias and fairness assessments

evidenceItems.name: Model validation reports
evidenceItems.description: Independent validation reports for customer-facing AI models assessing accuracy, stability, fairness, and fitness for purpose, with recommendations and management responses
whatGoodLooksLike: Validation performed by a function independent of the model development team
Reports assess model accuracy, stability over time, sensitivity to input changes, and fairness across population segments
Clear recommendation on model fitness for deployment or requirement for modifications
Evidence of annual re-validation for models in production
commonGaps: Validation performed by the development team rather than an independent function
Validation focuses only on accuracy without assessing fairness or stability
No evidence of ongoing re-validation after initial deployment
suggestedSources: Model risk management function validation reports
External model validation firm reports
Model risk committee approval records

auditTips: Prepare a complete AI model inventory showing all models in use, their risk tiers, validation status, and accountable owners
For customer-facing models, have bias testing results and explainability documentation readily available as these are high-priority areas for examiners
Demonstrate that model validation is independent from model development; organisational charts showing separate reporting lines are helpful
If the institution uses third-party or vendor-provided AI models, demonstrate how governance requirements are applied to vendor models
Prepare examples of how model decisions can be explained to a customer or regulator in plain language

17.5.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.5.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.5.title: Cloud and Emerging Technology Roadmap
17.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has a structured process for evaluating emerging technology risks before adoption, whether quantum computing preparedness is being addressed, and whether risk assessments cover security, privacy, regulatory, and operational dimensions.

evidenceItems.name: Emerging technology risk assessment framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework defining the methodology for assessing risks of emerging technology adoption, required risk dimensions, assessment criteria, and approval processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework defines a structured assessment methodology applicable to diverse emerging technologies
Required risk dimensions include security, privacy, regulatory compliance, operational, and strategic risks
Assessment criteria are specific to the Malaysian financial services regulatory context
Framework is approved by CRO and reviewed annually
commonGaps: No formal framework; emerging technology risk assessments are ad-hoc and inconsistent
Framework focuses only on technical risks without addressing regulatory and privacy dimensions
Framework is generic and does not account for Malaysian-specific regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: Risk Management department emerging technology assessment documentation
CTO office technology innovation governance files
IT steering committee emerging technology discussion records

evidenceItems.name: Completed risk assessments for adopted emerging technologies
evidenceItems.description: Completed risk assessment reports for each emerging technology the institution has adopted or is evaluating, covering all required risk dimensions with documented risk acceptance decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment completed before technology adoption with documented approval from CRO and CISO
All five risk dimensions (security, privacy, regulatory, operational, strategic) addressed with substantive analysis
Risk mitigation measures documented with assigned owners and implementation status
Assessment reviewed and updated post-adoption based on operational experience
commonGaps: Technology adopted before risk assessment was completed or without formal risk acceptance
Risk assessment is superficial, covering only one or two risk dimensions
No post-adoption risk review despite operational issues emerging
suggestedSources: Risk Management department technology risk assessment files
CTO office proof-of-concept evaluation reports
IT project documentation for emerging technology initiatives

evidenceItems.name: Quantum computing readiness assessment
evidenceItems.description: Assessment of the institution's preparedness for quantum computing impacts on cryptographic security, including inventory of quantum-vulnerable algorithms, migration roadmap, and timeline for transition to quantum-resistant cryptography
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory of cryptographic algorithms in use across systems, certificates, and protocols
Identification of quantum-vulnerable algorithms (RSA, ECC) and the systems that depend on them
Migration roadmap to post-quantum cryptographic standards with prioritised timeline based on system criticality
Monitoring of NIST post-quantum cryptography standardisation progress and vendor quantum-safe product roadmaps
commonGaps: No quantum readiness assessment conducted despite reliance on quantum-vulnerable cryptography
Assessment limited to awareness of quantum computing without specific institutional impact analysis
No migration roadmap or timeline for transitioning to quantum-resistant algorithms
suggestedSources: CISO office quantum readiness assessment documentation
Cryptographic algorithm inventory from certificate and key management systems
Technology vendor quantum-safe product roadmaps

evidenceItems.name: Emerging technology risk register
evidenceItems.description: Centralised register tracking all emerging technologies under evaluation or adopted by the institution, their risk assessment status, risk ratings, and mitigation actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all emerging technologies including DLT/blockchain, IoT, quantum computing preparedness, and any other novel technologies
Each entry includes risk assessment status, risk rating, mitigation actions, and accountable owner
Register is reviewed and updated at least quarterly
Presented to board technology committee as part of annual technology risk reporting
commonGaps: No centralised register; emerging technologies evaluated in isolation without consolidated risk view
Register exists but is not maintained or updated, with stale entries
Register not reported to governance bodies
suggestedSources: Risk Management department technology risk register
CTO office innovation portfolio tracking
Board technology committee meeting papers

auditTips: Prepare the emerging technology risk register showing all technologies under evaluation or adopted, with their risk assessment status
Have the quantum computing readiness assessment available even if it is an initial assessment, as this demonstrates proactive engagement with a forward-looking regulatory expectation
For any emerging technology adopted, ensure the risk assessment pre-dates the adoption decision with documented sign-off
If the institution has not adopted any emerging technologies, demonstrate that a framework exists and that quantum readiness is being addressed proactively
Ensure risk assessments reference the Malaysian regulatory context, including any BNM guidance or restrictions on specific emerging technologies

18.1.title: Gap Analysis and Action Plan
18.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution conducts a comprehensive annual self-assessment covering all applicable RMiT clauses, that compliance ratings are supported by evidence, that the assessment is honest about gaps, and that results are reviewed and signed off by the board or senior management.

evidenceItems.name: Annual RMiT self-assessment report
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive annual self-assessment report covering all applicable RMiT policy requirements with clause-by-clause compliance ratings, supporting evidence references, and overall compliance posture summary
whatGoodLooksLike: Report covers all applicable RMiT clauses with no gaps in assessment scope
Each clause rated as fully compliant, partially compliant, or non-compliant with consistent application of rating criteria
Overall compliance posture summary with executive-level dashboard showing distribution of ratings across sections
Assessment completed within a defined annual cycle with consistent timing year on year
commonGaps: Assessment scope excludes certain RMiT sections or clauses without documented justification
All clauses rated as fully compliant despite known gaps, indicating lack of assessment rigour
Assessment conducted on an ad-hoc schedule rather than as a defined annual process
suggestedSources: Compliance department annual RMiT self-assessment report
GRC platform compliance assessment reports (ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer)
Board meeting packs containing self-assessment presentation

evidenceItems.name: Compliance status for each RMiT clause
evidenceItems.description: Detailed clause-by-clause compliance status showing the rating for each RMiT requirement, the rationale for the rating, and reference to supporting evidence
whatGoodLooksLike: Every applicable clause has a documented compliance rating with clear rationale
Partially compliant and non-compliant ratings include specific description of the gap and remaining work required
Evidence references are specific and traceable (not just 'see documentation' but specific document names and versions)
Rating methodology is consistent across clauses, with calibration exercises documented
commonGaps: Compliance ratings assigned without documented rationale or evidence references
Partially compliant ratings lack specificity about what is missing versus what is in place
No calibration of ratings across different assessors, leading to inconsistent standards
suggestedSources: GRC platform clause-level compliance tracking
Compliance department self-assessment working papers
Internal audit RMiT compliance review reports

evidenceItems.name: Supporting evidence for compliance ratings
evidenceItems.description: Evidence packages supporting the compliance ratings for each RMiT clause, including policy documents, procedure evidence, technical configurations, reports, and other artefacts demonstrating compliance
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence packages organised by RMiT clause with a clear index linking evidence to specific compliance requirements
Evidence is current (within the assessment period) and directly relevant to the clause requirements
Mix of evidence types including policies, procedures, technical configurations, reports, and meeting minutes
Evidence demonstrates operational effectiveness, not just documentation existence
commonGaps: Evidence consists solely of policy documents without evidence of operational implementation
Evidence is outdated, predating the current assessment period
Evidence is poorly organised, making it difficult to link specific artefacts to specific clause requirements
suggestedSources: GRC platform evidence repository
Document management system policy and procedure library
Technical systems providing configuration and operational evidence

evidenceItems.name: Board or senior management review of assessment results
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of board or senior management review and sign-off of the annual self-assessment results, including meeting minutes showing discussion of findings and direction for remediation
whatGoodLooksLike: Board meeting minutes showing substantive discussion of self-assessment results, not just a notation of receipt
Board or senior management sign-off documented with date and signatories
Discussion includes challenge of compliance ratings and direction for addressing material gaps
Follow-up actions assigned by the board with accountability and timelines for remediation
commonGaps: Self-assessment results presented to board but no evidence of discussion or challenge in meeting minutes
Board sign-off obtained after significant delay, indicating the assessment is a box-ticking exercise
No follow-up actions assigned by the board for identified gaps
suggestedSources: Board risk committee meeting minutes and packs
Senior management committee meeting records
Board sign-off sheet or resolution on self-assessment results

auditTips: Prepare the self-assessment report with a clear executive summary dashboard showing the distribution of compliance ratings to give examiners an immediate overview
Ensure evidence packages are well-organised and indexed by clause so that examiners can quickly verify compliance claims for any selected clause
Have board meeting minutes showing substantive discussion of self-assessment results, not just a rubber-stamp approval
If any clauses are rated as non-compliant, ensure corresponding remediation plans exist (linking to clause 18.2) to demonstrate proactive gap management
Be prepared for examiners to select specific clauses and request the supporting evidence to validate the compliance rating; have the evidence readily accessible

18.2.title: Gap Analysis Submission
18.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that remediation plans exist for all identified compliance gaps, that plans include specific actions with accountable owners and target dates, that progress is reported to the board at least quarterly, and that closed gaps have evidence of completion and independent verification.

evidenceItems.name: Gap remediation plan with actions and owners
evidenceItems.description: Detailed remediation plan documenting all identified RMiT compliance gaps with specific remediation actions, accountable senior management owners, and required resources for each gap
whatGoodLooksLike: Every gap identified in the annual self-assessment has a corresponding remediation action plan
Actions are specific and measurable, not vague statements like 'improve compliance'
Each action has a named senior management owner with clear accountability
Resource requirements (budget, personnel, technology) documented for each remediation action
commonGaps: Some identified gaps have no corresponding remediation plan, particularly for lower-priority items
Remediation actions are vague without specific deliverables or success criteria
Owners assigned at operational level without senior management accountability
suggestedSources: GRC platform remediation tracking module (ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer)
Compliance department gap remediation plan documentation
Board meeting packs containing remediation plan approval

evidenceItems.name: Target completion dates and resource requirements
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of target completion dates for each remediation action, including milestones for longer-term remediation efforts, and approved resource allocations
whatGoodLooksLike: Realistic target dates set for each action based on complexity and resource availability
Milestones defined for remediation efforts spanning more than three months
Resource allocations approved and documented, including budget and personnel commitments
Priority-based timeline with critical gaps (S-marker clauses) targeted for earliest completion
commonGaps: Target dates are unrealistic given the complexity of remediation required
No milestones for long-running remediation, making progress monitoring impossible
Resource requirements identified but no budget approval obtained, stalling remediation
suggestedSources: GRC platform remediation timeline reports
Budget approval documentation for remediation initiatives
Project management system timeline records for remediation projects

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly progress reports to the board
evidenceItems.description: Quarterly board reports showing remediation progress across all compliance gaps, including status of each action, completed items, overdue items, and any timeline revisions with justification
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports presented to the board at least quarterly with evidence in board meeting minutes
Status dashboard showing overall remediation progress (on track, at risk, overdue, completed) for all gaps
Individual status for each remediation action with clear explanation of any delays or timeline revisions
Board meeting minutes showing discussion, challenge, and direction on remediation progress
commonGaps: Progress reported less frequently than quarterly, indicating insufficient board oversight
Reports show aggregate progress only without individual action-level detail
Overdue actions not flagged or escalated in board reporting
suggestedSources: Board risk committee meeting packs and minutes
GRC platform quarterly remediation status reports
Compliance department quarterly reporting documentation

evidenceItems.name: Remediation completion evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of completed remediation actions for closed gaps, including artefacts demonstrating the gap has been addressed and independent verification of closure by compliance or internal audit
whatGoodLooksLike: Each closed gap has specific completion evidence demonstrating the remediation action was implemented
Evidence demonstrates operational effectiveness, not just documentation creation
Independent verification by compliance function or internal audit documented with sign-off
Updated compliance rating in the self-assessment reflecting the closed gap
commonGaps: Gaps marked as closed without completion evidence, based solely on owner assertion
Completion evidence is a policy document without evidence of operational implementation
No independent verification of gap closure; closure is self-certified by the remediation owner
suggestedSources: GRC platform remediation closure evidence repository
Internal audit verification reports for closed remediation items
Compliance department gap closure review records

auditTips: Prepare a summary dashboard showing total gaps, completed remediations, in-progress items, and overdue actions to give examiners an immediate overview of remediation status
Have board meeting minutes from all quarterly progress reports readily available to demonstrate consistent governance oversight
For closed gaps, ensure completion evidence is readily accessible and demonstrates operational effectiveness, not just policy documentation
Prepare a reconciliation between the self-assessment gap list and the remediation plan to confirm every identified gap has a corresponding action plan
If any remediation timelines have been extended, have documented justifications and board approval for the extensions
